topics.title: Tensile Properties of Ductile Materials Using UTM with Extensometer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMEL308 - MATERIALS TESTING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Tensile Properties of Ductile Materials Using UTM with Extensometer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module Experiment 2: Tensile Properties of Ductile Materials Using UTM with Extensometer

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Uniaxial Tensile Test** (also called the Tension Test) is a fundamental, standardized destructive mechanical test conducted on a carefully machined cylindrical or flat specimen to determine a wide spectrum of **strength** and **ductility** characteristics of a ductile engineering material. The test is performed on a **Universal Testing Machine (UTM)** — a hydraulically or screw-driven apparatus capable of applying a controlled, monotonically increasing axial tensile load until specimen fracture.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> "A tensile test on a ductile metallic specimen (typically mild steel) is carried out using a Universal Testing Machine (UTM) fitted with an extensometer to obtain the load–elongation curve, from which key mechanical properties — Young's modulus, yield stress (upper/lower), ultimate tensile strength, percentage elongation, percentage reduction in area, and modulus of resilience — are evaluated."

The **Extensometer** is a precision electromechanical or optical transducer clamped onto the specimen's gauge length to continuously measure the *local* elongation $\Delta L$ in the parallel reduced section, completely isolated from machine frame compliance, grip slip, and elastic stretch of the loading train.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Stretchy Rubber Band vs. Toffee" Analogy

Imagine pulling a **soft toffee** slowly apart between your two hands:

- **Initial stretch (low load):** The toffee deforms elastically — let go, and it springs back to its original length. This is the **elastic region**.
- **A small sudden "give"**: After a certain pull, you feel a tiny drop in resistance — the toffee starts yielding plastically; this is the **yield point**.
- **Sustained pull with thinning**: The toffee elongates more but you must pull harder (until a maximum). The cross-section visibly necks down.
- **Sudden snap**: Finally, at the necked region, it snaps. This is **fracture**.

The UTM does exactly this scientifically — it *measures* the force, and the extensometer measures the *exact stretch* of the middle portion, letting us draw the **Stress–Strain Curve** which is the "fingerprint" of the material.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why use an extensometer?**
> Without an extensometer, we only know the *crosshead displacement*, which includes elastic stretch of the machine frame, grips, and threads. These contaminations make the initial linear slope (Young's Modulus $E$) highly inaccurate. The extensometer isolates the **true specimen elongation** in the gauge length.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard reference temperature:** **23 °C (293 K)** (per **ASTM E8 / IS 1608**)
- **Standard strain rate:** between **0.001 and 0.005 per second** for the elastic portion
- **Standard specimen gauge length $L_0$:** proportional specimen ($L_0 = 4\sqrt{A_0}$, where $A_0$ is original cross-sectional area) → gives a 5× specimen, or sub-size proportional specimens (10× or 8×)
- **Circular specimens:** preferred gauge diameter $d_0 = 12.5$ mm or $20$ mm
- **Ductility benchmark:** mild steel is the canonical *ductile* material used in this experiment at KTU labs
- **Young's Modulus of mild steel:** **$E \approx 200 \text{ GPa}$** (often accepted as **$2 \times 10^5$ N/mm²**)

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Standard Round Specimen Geometry (per IS 1608 / ASTM E8)

A tensile specimen has three distinct zones:
1. **Reduced parallel section (gauge section):** uniform cross-section over the gauge length $L_0$
2. **Shoulder (transition / fillet):** smooth taper to avoid stress concentration
3. **Grip section (heads/threads):** for clamping in the UTM crossheads

The gauge length $L_0$ and original cross-section $A_0$ govern the entire experiment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Specimen proportions (Proportional Round Specimen):**
> $L_0 = 4\sqrt{A_0} \approx 5.65\sqrt{S_0}$ where $S_0$ is the original cross-sectional area.
> For a circular bar of diameter $d_0$: $L_0 = 4 \cdot \dfrac{\pi d_0^2}{4\sqrt{\pi}}^{1} = 4\sqrt{\dfrac{\pi d_0^2}{4}} = \pi \sqrt{d_0} \approx 5.65 d_0$ (since $A_0 = \frac{\pi d_0^2}{4}$).
> For example, for $d_0 = 12.5$ mm: $L_0 \approx 56.25$ mm.

### 2.2 Engineering Stress and Engineering Strain

Two fundamental derived quantities drive every tensile property:

$$
\sigma = \frac{P}{A_0} \quad \text{[MPa or N/mm²]}
$$

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \frac{L - L_0}{L_0} \quad \text{[dimensionless, often in \% or mm/mm]}
$$

where:
- $P$ = axial tensile load at any instant (N)
- $A_0$ = *original* cross-sectional area (mm²)
- $\Delta L$ = increase in gauge length (mm)
- $L_0$ = original gauge length (mm)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "$A_0$" and not "$A$" (current area)?**
> Because *engineering* stress and strain use the original dimensions throughout. *True* stress and strain use instantaneous dimensions. For ductile materials with large deformations, true values differ significantly near fracture.

### 2.3 Key Mechanical Properties — The Complete List (KTU Board High-Yield)

| # | Property | Symbol | How it is read / computed | Unit |
|---|----------|--------|--------------------------|------|
| 1 | Proportional Limit | $\sigma_{PL}$ | End of linear portion of $\sigma$–$\varepsilon$ curve | MPa |
| 2 | Elastic Limit | $\sigma_{el}$ | Maximum stress recoverable on unloading | MPa |
| 3 | Young's Modulus | $E$ | Slope of linear elastic portion $E = \sigma / \varepsilon$ | GPa |
| 4 | Upper Yield Point | $\sigma_{yU}$ | First peak in $\sigma$–$\varepsilon$ curve (mild steel) | MPa |
| 5 | Lower Yield Point | $\sigma_{yL}$ | Plateau value after upper yield | MPa |
| 6 | Yield Strength (0.2% offset) | $\sigma_{0.2}$ | Stress where plastic strain = 0.2 % offset | MPa |
| 7 | Ultimate Tensile Strength | $\sigma_{UTS}$ | Maximum stress on engineering curve | MPa |
| 8 | Fracture Stress | $\sigma_f$ | Stress at which specimen breaks | MPa |
| 9 | Percentage Elongation | $\%EL$ | $\frac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100$ | % |
| 10 | Percentage Reduction in Area | $\%RA$ | $\frac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100$ | % |
| 11 | Modulus of Resilience | $U_r$ | Area under elastic portion = $\dfrac{\sigma_y^2}{2E}$ | MPa or J/m³ |
| 12 | Modulus of Toughness | $U_t$ | Total area under $\sigma$–$\varepsilon$ curve up to fracture | MPa or J/m³ |

### 2.4 The Detailed Stress–Strain Curve for Mild Steel (Ductile)

The $\sigma$–$\varepsilon$ diagram for low-carbon steel exhibits all canonical features and is therefore the **most-examined** curve in KTU lab viva/ESE:

1. **OA — Linear elastic region:** Hooke's law valid; $E = \tan(\theta)$ where $\theta$ is the slope angle.
2. **A — Proportional limit:** departure from linearity begins.
3. **B — Elastic limit:** recoverable strain ends.
4. **C — Upper yield point ($\sigma_{yU}$):** sudden load drop occurs; Lüders bands initiate.
5. **D — Lower yield point ($\sigma_{yL}$):** plateau of nearly constant stress during yielding.
6. **DE — Strain hardening region:** beyond yielding, the material work-hardens and stress rises again.
7. **E — Ultimate Tensile Strength ($\sigma_{UTS}$):** maximum engineering stress; diffuse necking begins here.
8. **EF — Necking region:** local cross-section reduces rapidly; engineering stress *falls* (because we still divide by $A_0$).
9. **F — Fracture point:** specimen breaks; final elongation $L_f$ and final neck diameter $d_f$ are measured.

### 2.5 Young's Modulus from Extensometer Data

The extensometer gives highly accurate $\Delta L$ readings in the elastic zone (typically up to $\sim 0.5\%$ strain). Using two points on the linear portion:

$$
E = \frac{\Delta \sigma}{\Delta \varepsilon} = \frac{(P_2 - P_1) / A_0}{(\Delta L_2 - \Delta L_1) / L_0}
$$

### 2.6 0.2 % Offset Method (for materials without a sharp yield point)

For aluminium, copper, brass etc., a straight line parallel to the elastic slope is offset by $\varepsilon = 0.002$ (i.e. $0.2\%$). The intersection with the stress–strain curve gives $\sigma_{0.2}$, the **proof stress** / **offset yield strength**.

$$
\varepsilon_{offset} = 0.002 \implies \sigma_{y} = \sigma \big|_{\varepsilon = 0.002 + \sigma/E}
$$

### 2.7 Modulus of Resilience & Toughness

$$
U_r = \frac{1}{2} \sigma_y \cdot \varepsilon_y = \frac{\sigma_y^{\,2}}{2E}
$$

$$
U_t = \int_0^{\varepsilon_f} \sigma \, d\varepsilon \quad \text{(area under entire $\sigma$–$\varepsilon$ curve)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **$U_r$** measures a material's capacity to absorb energy elastically (think spring).
> **$U_t$** measures total energy absorbed up to fracture — a key indicator for crash-resistant design.

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Design of structural steel members** (beams, columns, bridges): uses $\sigma_y$ and $E$.
- **Pressure vessel and boiler design**: requires $\sigma_{UTS}$ with safety factor.
- **Sheet-metal forming** (automotive panels): needs $\sigma_{0.2}$ and $\%EL$.
- **Wire drawing, forging, rolling**: relies on strain-hardening behaviour.
- **Failure analysis**: $\%RA$ and $\%EL$ distinguish ductile vs. brittle fracture.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Examples

### 3.1 Derivation: Engineering Stress from Raw Load Data

**Given:** Tensile load $P$ applied axially on a round specimen of original diameter $d_0$.

**Step 1 — Original cross-sectional area:**

$$
A_0 = \frac{\pi d_0^{\,2}}{4}
$$

**Step 2 — Engineering stress (force per unit original area):**

$$
\sigma = \frac{P}{A_0} = \frac{4P}{\pi d_0^{\,2}}
$$

**Logic:** Since $A_0$ is a constant for the entire test, dividing the load by it converts "force" into a material-specific stress, allowing comparison between specimens of different sizes.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Engineering Strain from Extensometer Output

**Given:** Extensometer reading at instant $i$ is $\Delta L_i = L_i - L_0$.

**Step 1 — Engineering strain:**

$$
\varepsilon_i = \frac{\Delta L_i}{L_0}
$$

**Step 2 — Percentage strain (often used in lab reports):**

$$
\%\varepsilon_i = \varepsilon_i \times 100
$$

**Logic:** The extensometer directly outputs length *change*, so dividing by the original gauge length $L_0$ normalizes strain to a per-unit-length quantity, independent of specimen size.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Young's Modulus from the Linear Region

Take two points $(P_1, \Delta L_1)$ and $(P_2, \Delta L_2)$ from the linear region:

**Step 1 — Stress at each point:**

$$
\sigma_1 = \frac{P_1}{A_0}, \quad \sigma_2 = \frac{P_2}{A_0}
$$

**Step 2 — Strain at each point:**

$$
\varepsilon_1 = \frac{\Delta L_1}{L_0}, \quad \varepsilon_2 = \frac{\Delta L_2}{L_0}
$$

**Step 3 — Slope = Young's modulus:**

$$
E = \frac{\sigma_2 - \sigma_1}{\varepsilon_2 - \varepsilon_1} = \frac{(P_2 - P_1)\, L_0}{A_0\, (\Delta L_2 - \Delta L_1)}
$$

**Step 4 — Units check:** $\dfrac{\text{N} \cdot \text{mm}}{\text{mm}^2 \cdot \text{mm}} = \dfrac{\text{N}}{\text{mm}^2} = \text{MPa}$ ✓ (convert to GPa by dividing by 1000).

---

### 3.4 Derivation: 0.2 % Offset Yield Strength

**Step 1 — Equation of the offset line (parallel to elastic slope, shifted by $\varepsilon = 0.002$):**

$$
\sigma_{off} = E(\varepsilon - 0.002)
$$

**Step 2 — Equating offset line to stress–strain curve $\sigma(\varepsilon)$ and solving for $\varepsilon$:**

$$
E(\varepsilon - 0.002) = \sigma(\varepsilon)
$$

**Step 3 — At the intersection point, the stress value is $\sigma_{0.2}$:**

$$
\sigma_{0.2} = E(\varepsilon^* - 0.002)
$$

where $\varepsilon^*$ is the root of Step 2.

**Logic:** The offset method standardises a way to define "yielding" for materials without a sharp yield point (e.g., aluminium alloys, stainless steels).

---

### 3.5 Derivation: Percentage Elongation and Reduction in Area

After fracture, the two halves are carefully fitted together and the final gauge length $L_f$ measured; the neck diameter $d_f$ is measured with vernier calipers.

$$
\%EL = \frac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100
$$

$$
A_f = \frac{\pi d_f^{\,2}}{4}
$$

$$
\%RA = \frac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100
$$

---

### 3.6 Worked Numerical Example — Typical KTU Exam Problem

> **[Worked Example 1]**
> A mild-steel tensile specimen has $d_0 = 12.5$ mm and $L_0 = 62.5$ mm. The upper yield load is $P_{yU} = 28$ kN, ultimate load $P_{max} = 38$ kN, fracture load $P_f = 26$ kN, final gauge length $L_f = 80$ mm and final neck diameter $d_f = 8.5$ mm. Determine $\sigma_{yU}$, $\sigma_{UTS}$, $\sigma_f$, $\%EL$, $\%RA$, and the modulus of resilience if $E = 200$ GPa.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Original area:**

$$
A_0 = \frac{\pi (12.5)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 156.25}{4} = 122.72 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Upper yield strength:**

$$
\sigma_{yU} = \frac{P_{yU}}{A_0} = \frac{28\,000}{122.72} = 228.16 \text{ MPa} \approx 228 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 3 — Ultimate tensile strength:**

$$
\sigma_{UTS} = \frac{P_{max}}{A_0} = \frac{38\,000}{122.72} = 309.65 \text{ MPa} \approx 310 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 4 — Fracture stress:**

$$
\sigma_f = \frac{P_f}{A_0} = \frac{26\,000}{122.72} = 211.87 \text{ MPa} \approx 212 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 5 — Percentage elongation:**

$$
\%EL = \frac{80 - 62.5}{62.5} \times 100 = \frac{17.5}{62.5} \times 100 = 28\%
$$

**Step 6 — Percentage reduction in area:**

$$
A_f = \frac{\pi (8.5)^2}{4} = 56.745 \text{ mm}^2
$$

$$
\%RA = \frac{122.72 - 56.745}{122.72} \times 100 = 53.76\% \approx 54\%
$$

**Step 7 — Modulus of resilience** (using $\sigma_y = \sigma_{yU} = 228.16$ MPa, $E = 200$ GPa $= 200\,000$ MPa):

$$
U_r = \frac{\sigma_y^{\,2}}{2E} = \frac{(228.16)^2}{2 \times 200\,000} = \frac{52\,057}{400\,000} = 0.1301 \text{ MPa} = 130.1 \text{ kJ/m}^3
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result sanity check:** Mild steel typically has $E \approx 200$ GPa, $\sigma_y \approx 230$ MPa, $\sigma_{UTS} \approx 410$ MPa, $\%EL \approx 25\%-30\%$, $\%RA \approx 50\%-60\%$. Our values are perfectly consistent with textbook expectations. ✓

---

### 3.7 Worked Numerical Example — Young's Modulus from Extensometer

> **[Worked Example 2]**
> For the same specimen ($d_0 = 12.5$ mm, $L_0 = 62.5$ mm, $A_0 = 122.72$ mm²), the load–elongation data in the elastic region yields: at $P_1 = 6$ kN, $\Delta L_1 = 0.015$ mm; at $P_2 = 12$ kN, $\Delta L_2 = 0.030$ mm. Find Young's modulus.

**Step 1:**

$$
E = \frac{(P_2 - P_1)\,L_0}{A_0 (\Delta L_2 - \Delta L_1)} = \frac{(12\,000 - 6\,000) \times 62.5}{122.72 \times (0.030 - 0.015)}
$$

**Step 2:**

$$
E = \frac{6\,000 \times 62.5}{122.72 \times 0.015} = \frac{375\,000}{1.8408} = 203\,722 \text{ N/mm}^2 \approx 204 \text{ GPa}
$$

**Step 3 — Cross-check:** Theoretical $E$ for mild steel is **200 GPa**; the experimental value of **204 GPa** is within typical lab accuracy (≤ 5 % error). ✓

---

### 3.8 Typical Lab Observation Table Format (for record book)

| Sl. No. | Load $P$ (kN) | Extensometer reading $\Delta L$ (mm) | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2 | 0.005 | Linear elastic region |
| 2 | 4 | 0.010 | Linear elastic region |
| ... | ... | ... | ... |
| 12 | 28 | 0.090 | Upper yield reached |
| 13 | 27 | 0.110 | Yielding / Lüders band propagation |
| ... | ... | ... | ... |
| 25 | 38 | — | Ultimate load reached (extensometer removed near failure) |
| 26 | 26 | — | Fracture |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lab Tip:** The extensometer is *removed* before fracture (typically just after ultimate load) to prevent damage. After removal, the crosshead displacement is used until fracture — but at that stage, only fracture load and post-fracture dimensions are required for engineering calculations.

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Tensile Specimen Geometry (Round, Proportional)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Grip Head Threaded Shoulder for Clamping] --> B[Shoulder Fillet Smooth Radius Transition]
    B --> C[Reduced Parallel Section Gauge Length L0]
    C --> D[Symmetric Tapered Transition Zone]
    D --> E[Lower Grip Head Threaded]
    C --> F[Original Diameter d0 measured here]
```

> **Note:** The "Reduced Parallel Section" is where the extensometer is clamped and where elongation $\Delta L$ is measured. Grip heads transfer the load from the UTM crossheads to the specimen.

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of UTM + Extensometer Test Setup

```mermaid
graph TD
    PSU[Hydraulic Power Supply Unit] --> VALV[Servo Control Valve]
    VALV --> ACTU[Double Acting Hydraulic Actuator Cylinder]
    ACTU --> FRAME[Loading Frame Crosshead Assembly]
    FRAME --> GRPU[Upper Crosshead Grip]
    FRAME --> GRPL[Lower Crosshead Grip]
    GRPU --> SPEC[Specimen Reduced Gauge Section]
    GRPL --> SPEC
    SPEC --> EXT[Extensometer Attached to Gauge Length]
    EXT --> SIGCOND[Signal Conditioner and Amplifier]
    SIGCOND --> DAQ[Data Acquisition System]
    LOAD[Load Cell in Series with Actuator] --> SIGCOND
    ACTU --> LOAD
    DAQ --> PC[PC with Lab Software Plotting Stress Strain Curve]
    DAQ --> PRINT[Printer for Report Plot]
```

**Flow Logic:**
1. Hydraulic pressure from PSU is regulated by the servo valve.
2. The actuator pulls the crossheads apart, applying tension.
3. Load cell senses the instantaneous force $P$.
4. Extensometer senses the elongation $\Delta L$ in the gauge length.
5. Data Acquisition synchronously records $P$ and $\Delta L$ at high sampling rate.
6. Software plots $\sigma$ vs. $\varepsilon$ in real time.

### 4.3 Stress–Strain Curve — Labelled Regions

```mermaid
graph LR
    O[Origin] --> A[Point A Proportional Limit]
    A --> B[Point B Elastic Limit]
    B --> C[Point C Upper Yield Point sigma yU]
    C --> D[Point D Lower Yield Point sigma yL]
    D --> E[Point E Ultimate Tensile Strength sigma UTS]
    E --> F[Point F Fracture sigma f]
    O -.Slope equals E Young Modulus.-> A
    C -.Yield Plateau Plastic Flow.-> D
    E -.Diffuse Necking Begins.-> F
    O -.Region OA Linear Elastic.-> A
```

> **Reading guide:** Each labeled point corresponds to a transition the examiner loves to test. Memorise the order: **O → A (Proportional) → B (Elastic) → C (Upper Yield) → D (Lower Yield) → E (UTS) → F (Fracture)**.

### 4.4 Extensometer Types Commonly Used in KTU Labs

```mermaid
graph TD
    EXT[Extensometer Types Used in KTU Labs] --> MECH[Mechanical Knife Edge Lever Type]
    EXT --> LVDT[Electrical LVDT Based]
    EXT --> STRAIN[Gauge Type Strain Gauge Based]
    EXT --> AVG[Average Axial and Circumferential Type]
    MECH --> NOTE1[Cheapest Good Accuracy Up to 0.1 Percent Strain]
    LVDT --> NOTE2[High Resolution 0.0001 mm Range]
    STRAIN --> NOTE3[Attached Directly to Specimen]
    AVG --> NOTE4[Compensates for Bending Errors]
```

### 4.5 Test Procedure Flowchart (KTU Lab Manual Standard)

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Step 1 Prepare Specimen Measure d0 and L0] --> S2[Step 2 Mark Gauge Length with Centre Punch]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Fit Specimen in UTM Grips Align Axially]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Attach Extensometer to Gauge Section]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 Zero Load and Extensometer Readings]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 Apply Load Gradually at Standard Rate]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7 Record Load Elongation Data]
    S7 --> DECIDE{Specimen Yielded}
    DECIDE -->|Yes| S8[Note Upper and Lower Yield Loads]
    S8 --> S9[Continue Loading Through Strain Hardening]
    S9 --> S10[Remove Extensometer Before Necking Accelerates]
    S10 --> S11[Continue to Ultimate Load and Fracture]
    DECIDE -->|No| S11
    S11 --> S12[Measure Final Gauge Length Lf and Neck Diameter df]
    S12 --> S13[Compute All Mechanical Properties and Plot Stress Strain Curve]
```

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*What is an extensometer? Why is it preferred over measuring crosshead displacement for determining Young's modulus in a tensile test?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An extensometer is a precision strain-measuring instrument directly attached to the gauge length of a tensile specimen. It records the change in gauge length $\Delta L$ over the original gauge length $L_0$ as the specimen is loaded.

It is preferred over crosshead displacement because crosshead displacement includes unwanted elastic stretches from the machine frame, grips, and pull-rods. These contaminate the strain measurement, especially in the elastic region where strains are very small ($\sim 10^{-3}$). The extensometer isolates the true specimen elongation, giving a much more accurate slope and hence a reliable Young's modulus:

$$
E = \frac{\Delta \sigma}{\Delta \varepsilon} = \frac{(P_2 - P_1)/A_0}{(\Delta L_2 - \Delta L_1)/L_0}
$$

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Distinguish between the upper yield point and lower yield point in mild steel. What is the physical reason for the occurrence of these two yield points?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Upper Yield point ($\sigma_{yU}$) | Lower yield point ($\sigma_{yL}$) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | First peak stress observed at the onset of yielding | Plateau stress value immediately after the upper yield drop |
| Magnitude | Slightly higher (typically $5$–$10\%$ higher) | Lower, but more representative |
| Physical meaning | Stress required to unlock dislocations pinned by interstitial carbon and nitrogen atoms | Stress required to sustain dislocation motion once unlocked |
| Occurrence | Sharp, sudden | Prolonged over a small strain range |

**Physical Reason:** In low-carbon steel, interstitial carbon and nitrogen atoms segregate to dislocation cores, anchoring them (Cottrell atmospheres). The upper yield point is the stress needed to *tear* these dislocations free from their solute atmospheres, while the lower yield point corresponds to continued plastic flow *after* the dislocations have broken free. The propagation of Lüders bands across the gauge length is the visible evidence of this yielding.

---

### PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

---

**Q3. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] (Choice A — 14 Marks)**

**(a)** With the help of a neatly labelled stress–strain diagram for a ductile material (mild steel), explain the salient points: proportional limit, elastic limit, upper and lower yield points, ultimate tensile strength, necking, and fracture. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A mild-steel specimen of diameter $12$ mm and gauge length $60$ mm yielded at an upper yield load of $24$ kN, reached ultimate load of $36$ kN, fractured at $26$ kN, and after fracture the gauge length was $78$ mm while the neck diameter was $8.0$ mm. Compute the upper yield strength, ultimate tensile strength, percentage elongation, and percentage reduction in area. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Answer — Part (a):**

**Stress–Strain Curve for Mild Steel:**

```
   σ (MPa)
   ↑
   |          E (strain hardening)
   |         /
   |        /
   |    D  /      E (UTS)
   |   /  /      /
   |  /  /      /
   | /  /      /
   |C  /      /
   |\ /      / F (fracture)
   | \      /
   |  \    /
   |   \  /  F (necking drop)
   |    \/
   |    B
   |   /
   |  / E = slope
   | / 
   |/___________________→ ε
   O A
```

**Description of each labelled point:**

| Point | Description |
|---|---|
| **O** | Origin — zero stress, zero strain |
| **A** | Proportional Limit — end of linear region; beyond this, Hooke's law no longer holds |
| **B** | Elastic Limit — maximum stress at which the specimen returns to its original shape on unloading |
| **C** | Upper Yield Point ($\sigma_{yU}$) — first stress peak at which plastic deformation begins |
| **D** | Lower Yield Point ($\sigma_{yL}$) — nearly constant stress during which Lüders bands propagate |
| **E** | Ultimate Tensile Strength ($\sigma_{UTS}$) — maximum engineering stress the material can sustain; necking initiates |
| **F** | Fracture Point ($\sigma_f$) — final separation; engineering stress falls due to severe localized area reduction |

**Physical regions of the curve:**

1. **Elastic region (O–B):** Reversible deformation, governed by interatomic bond stretching.
2. **Yield plateau (C–D):** Dislocation glide through the lattice; Cottrell atmospheres in mild steel cause the upper/lower yield phenomenon.
3. **Strain-hardening region (D–E):** Dislocation multiplication and entanglement raise the flow stress.
4. **Necking region (E–F):** Geometric instability; localized reduction in cross-section accelerates until fracture.

**(7 marks awarded for: correct diagram with all points labelled + clear description of each point + identification of regions + physical explanation of yielding.)**

---

**Model Answer — Part (b):**

**Given:**
- $d_0 = 12$ mm, $L_0 = 60$ mm
- $P_{yU} = 24$ kN, $P_{max} = 36$ kN, $P_f = 26$ kN
- $L_f = 78$ mm, $d_f = 8.0$ mm

**Step 1 — Original cross-sectional area:**

$$
A_0 = \frac{\pi d_0^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 144}{4} = 113.10 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Upper yield strength:**

$$
\sigma_{yU} = \frac{P_{yU}}{A_0} = \frac{24\,000}{113.10} = 212.21 \text{ MPa} \approx 212 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 3 — Ultimate tensile strength:**

$$
\sigma_{UTS} = \frac{P_{max}}{A_0} = \frac{36\,000}{113.10} = 318.31 \text{ MPa} \approx 318 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 4 — Percentage elongation:**

$$
\%EL = \frac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100 = \frac{78 - 60}{60} \times 100 = 30\%
$$

**Step 5 — Percentage reduction in area:**

$$
A_f = \frac{\pi d_f^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 64}{4} = 50.27 \text{ mm}^2
$$

$$
\%RA = \frac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100 = \frac{113.10 - 50.27}{113.10} \times 100 = 55.55\% \approx 55.6\%
$$

**Final tabulated result:**

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| Upper yield strength $\sigma_{yU}$ | **212 MPa** |
| Ultimate tensile strength $\sigma_{UTS}$ | **318 MPa** |
| Percentage elongation $\%EL$ | **30 %** |
| Percentage reduction in area $\%RA$ | **55.6 %** |

**(7 marks awarded for: 5 separate calculations each carrying ~1–2 marks with proper units and clear steps.)**

---

**Q4. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] (Choice B — 14 Marks)**

**(a)** Describe with a neat sketch the construction and working of an extensometer used in tensile testing. Mention the significance of gauge length selection. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** In a tensile test on a mild steel specimen ($d_0 = 14$ mm, $L_0 = 70$ mm), the extensometer readings in the elastic region are: at $8$ kN load, elongation is $0.012$ mm; at $16$ kN load, elongation is $0.024$ mm. Calculate Young's modulus. Also, find the modulus of resilience if the upper yield strength of this material is $240$ MPa. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Answer — Part (a):**

**Construction of a typical (LVDT-type) Extensometer:**

An extensometer consists of two main parts:

1. **Two knife-edge arms (or pivoted arms)** clamped onto opposite sides of the specimen's gauge section. The two arms are held against the specimen by a light spring-loaded clamp.
2. **A displacement transducer** (LVDT, strain gauge bridge, or optical sensor) mounted between the arms. When the specimen elongates, the relative motion between the two arms is sensed by the transducer.
3. **A flexible cable** carries the signal to the data acquisition system.
4. **Gauge length setting**: The distance between the two knife edges is fixed at $L_0$ (e.g., $50$ mm or $62.5$ mm) using a calibration bar before mounting.

**Working Principle:**

As the specimen is stretched, the two knife edges move apart by exactly the local elongation $\Delta L$ over the gauge length $L_0$. This relative displacement is converted by the transducer into a proportional electrical voltage, which is calibrated to directly read elongation in mm. The output is plotted against load (or stress) by the data acquisition system.

**Significance of Gauge Length Selection:**

- **Standardisation:** A standard proportional gauge length ($L_0 = 4\sqrt{A_0} \approx 5.65 d_0$) ensures $\%EL$ values from different specimen sizes of the same material can be directly compared.
- **Uniformity of Strain:** The gauge length must lie entirely within the reduced parallel section where strain is uniform; otherwise shoulder/transition zones contaminate the reading.
- **Adequacy for Localisation:** Gauge length must be sufficiently long compared to the necking region so that uniform elongation dominates; too short a gauge length gives artificially high $\%EL$.
- **Specimen size economy:** Smaller gauge length = smaller specimen = less material required.

**(7 marks: sketch description — 3 marks, working — 2 marks, gauge length significance — 2 marks.)**

---

**Model Answer — Part (b):**

**Given:**
- $d_0 = 14$ mm, $L_0 = 70$ mm
- $P_1 = 8$ kN, $\Delta L_1 = 0.012$ mm
- $P_2 = 16$ kN, $\Delta L_2 = 0.024$ mm
- $\sigma_{yU} = 240$ MPa

**Step 1 — Original cross-sectional area:**

$$
A_0 = \frac{\pi d_0^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 196}{4} = 153.94 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Young's modulus:**

$$
E = \frac{(P_2 - P_1)\, L_0}{A_0\, (\Delta L_2 - \Delta L_1)} = \frac{(16\,000 - 8\,000) \times 70}{153.94 \times (0.024 - 0.012)}
$$

$$
E = \frac{8\,000 \times 70}{153.94 \times 0.012} = \frac{560\,000}{1.8473} = 303\,178 \text{ N/mm}^2 \approx 303 \text{ GPa}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's pitfall:** Many students incorrectly compute Young's modulus by directly dividing load by extension (without proper normalisation). Always write the formula symbolically first, then substitute.

**Step 3 — Modulus of resilience:**

$$
U_r = \frac{\sigma_y^{\,2}}{2E} = \frac{(240)^2}{2 \times 303\,178} = \frac{57\,600}{606\,356} = 0.0950 \text{ MPa} = 95.0 \text{ kJ/m}^3
$$

**Final tabulated result:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Young's modulus $E$ | **≈ 303 GPa** (theoretically 200 GPa for steel; high value suggests slight experimental deviation or non-standard specimen) |
| Modulus of resilience $U_r$ | **≈ 95 kJ/m³** |

**(7 marks: full derivation of $E$ from extensometer data — 4 marks; modulus of resilience — 3 marks.)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** in the final answer — always write **MPa** or **N/mm²** for stress, **GPa** for modulus, **%** for elongation/RA, **kJ/m³** for resilience/toughness.
> 2. **Using final neck area $A_f$ instead of original area $A_0$** to compute $\sigma_{UTS}$ and $\sigma_{yU}$ — engineering stress uses **$A_0$** throughout; true stress uses $A$.
> 3. **Not removing the extensometer** before fracture in the procedure write-up — examiners specifically test this safety/lab protocol point.
> 4. **Mixing up gauge length $L_0$** with total specimen length — they are different.
> 5. **Calculating Young's modulus from a single (load, extension) pair** instead of using *two* points to get the slope — examiners will deduct marks for this.
> 6. **Failing to mention 0.2 % offset method** when discussing yield strength for non-ferrous materials — important for full marks.
> 7. **Ignoring alignment of specimen in UTM grips** — bending stress causes premature yielding and inaccurate $E$ values.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ The **tensile test** is the most fundamental destructive mechanical test, giving a single specimen an entire suite of strength and ductility properties.
- ✅ **UTM** applies controlled uniaxial tension; **extensometer** accurately measures local elongation in the gauge length — both are essential for reliable Young's modulus.
- ✅ **Engineering stress** uses the original area $A_0$; **engineering strain** uses the original length $L_0$ — these are the "engineering" values plotted on the standard curve.
- ✅ Key stress-strain landmarks in order: **Proportional Limit → Elastic Limit → Upper Yield → Lower Yield → UTS → Fracture**.
- ✅ **$\sigma_{UTS}$ is the *maximum* engineering stress**, NOT the fracture stress — fracture stress is always lower because necking reduces the area but we still divide by $A_0$.
- ✅ **Young's modulus $E$** = slope of linear elastic portion; compute using **two points** from extensometer data.
- ✅ **0.2 % offset method** is used for materials *without* a sharp yield point (aluminium, brass, stainless steel).
- ✅ **Modulus of resilience** $U_r = \sigma_y^2 / 2E$ (area under the elastic triangle).
- ✅ **Modulus of toughness** $U_t$ = total area under $\sigma$–$\varepsilon$ curve up to fracture.
- ✅ **$\%EL$ and $\%RA$** are measured *after* fracture — careful re-fitting of broken halves is critical.
- ✅ **Proportional gauge length** $L_0 = 4\sqrt{A_0} \approx 5.65\, d_0$ for circular specimens (per IS 1608 / ASTM E8).
- ✅ **Extensometer is removed before fracture** to prevent damage.
- ✅ **Standard reference temperature:** 23 °C; **standard strain rate:** 0.001–0.005 per second (elastic region).
- ✅ For mild steel: $E \approx 200$ GPa, $\sigma_y \approx 230$ MPa, $\sigma_{UTS} \approx 410$ MPa, $\%EL \approx 25\%-30\%$, $\%RA \approx 50\%-60\%$ — these are your "sanity check" values.
- ✅ **Lower yield point** is taken as the design yield strength (more reproducible than the upper yield point).
- ✅ **Lüders bands** are the visible surface markings during the yield plateau — caused by localised plastic deformation from dislocation breakaway.
- ✅ **Necking** begins exactly at UTS and is a *geometric* instability, not a material change.
- ✅ Always present tabular raw data + final computed properties + a neatly drawn $\sigma$–$\varepsilon$ diagram in the lab record.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Compression Test on Ductile Materials Using UTM with Extensometer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMEL308 - MATERIALS TESTING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Compression Test on Ductile Materials Using UTM with Extensometer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Compression Test on Ductile Materials** is a fundamental destructive mechanical testing procedure conducted on a **Universal Testing Machine (UTM)** to determine the compressive mechanical response of ductile engineering materials (such as mild steel, aluminium, copper, and brass) under uniaxial, gradually increasing compressive loads applied along the longitudinal axis of a standardized specimen. The test characterizes key engineering properties including the **compressive yield strength**, **modulus of elasticity**, **proportional limit**, and the **load–deformation behaviour** until the material undergoes significant plastic deformation.

The **Extensometer (specifically a Compressometer/Compression Extensometer)** is a high-precision displacement-measuring transducer attached to the specimen to record minute changes in gauge length during the test, enabling accurate determination of the **elastic modulus (Young's Modulus)** and the **proportional limit**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Governing Standard:** The compression test on ductile metallic materials is governed by **ASTM E9 – Standard Test Methods of Compression Testing of Metallic Materials at Room Temperature**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Ductile vs. Brittle Distinction:**
> - **Ductile materials** (mild steel, aluminium) do **not fracture** under compression; they deform continuously and exhibit a characteristic "barrel" shape.
> - **Brittle materials** (cast iron, concrete) fracture along inclined planes (~45°) under compression.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine squeezing a soft, cylindrical piece of **clay or putty** between your palms. As you press harder:
- The clay **shortens** in the direction you are pushing (axial direction).
- It **bulges outwards** in the middle (lateral direction) — this bulging is called the **"Barrelling Effect."**
- If you use grease on your palms (to reduce friction), the clay deforms more uniformly without much bulging.

Now, replace your palms with the hardened steel platens of a **UTM**, and the clay with a precisely machined **cylindrical ductile specimen** (like mild steel). The **Extensometer** acts like a sensitive ruler that measures how much the specimen shortens at every instant — far more accurate than reading the crosshead movement, which includes machine frame deflection and platen seating errors.

### 1.3 Significance of the Test

| Property Determined | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|
| **Compressive Yield Strength (σ_yc)** | Design of columns, pillars, structural supports |
| **Young's Modulus (E)** | Stiffness calculation in columns, shafts under axial load |
| **Proportional Limit** | Transition from elastic to plastic behaviour |
| **Strain-hardening response** | Forming and forging process design |
| **Modulus of Resilience (U_r)** | Energy absorbed elastically per unit volume |

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants / Standard Metrics:**
> - **Poisson's ratio (ν) for ductile metals:** typically **0.25 to 0.35** (≈ 0.30 for steel, 0.33 for aluminium).
> - **Standard Height-to-Diameter (h/d) Ratio:** between **1.5 and 2.0** (per ASTM E9).
> - **Maximum strain recordable:** typically up to **~0.5 (50%)** for ductile materials in compression.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Background

In a uniaxial compression test, a cylindrical specimen is subjected to an axial compressive load **P** that increases monotonically. The specimen's response is captured simultaneously in the form of **load (P) vs. deformation (Δh)** data.

**Step-by-step conceptual flow:**

1. **Elastic Region (Hookean Behaviour):**  
   At low loads, the material deforms elastically. Strain is linearly proportional to stress. Atomic bonds stretch but do not break. The specimen returns to its original shape upon load removal.

2. **Proportional Limit (σ_p):**  
   The maximum stress at which the stress–strain curve remains a **straight line**. Beyond this, micro-plasticity (dislocation motion) initiates.

3. **Elastic Limit (σ_e):**  
   The maximum stress at which the material returns to its original dimensions upon unloading. Closely coincides with σ_p in ductile metals.

4. **Upper and Lower Yield Points (Ductile Steels only):**  
   - **Upper Yield Point (σ_yu):** Stress at which plastic deformation first begins suddenly.  
   - **Lower Yield Point (σ_yl):** Stress at which deformation continues at a nearly constant stress level (yielding plateau).

5. **Strain-Hardening Region:**  
   Beyond yielding, additional deformation requires increasing stress due to dislocation pile-up and entanglement.

6. **Barrelling and Continuous Deformation:**  
   Friction at the platen–specimen interface restrains lateral expansion at the ends, forcing material to flow outwards in the mid-region. Unlike tension, **no necking and no fracture** occur in ductile materials. The specimen merely shortens and bulges continuously.

### 2.2 Why an Extensometer is Required in Compression

The UTM crosshead displacement includes:
- **Specimen deformation** (what we want to measure).
- **Machine frame compliance** (elastic stretching of the load frame itself).
- **Platen seating and bedding errors** (irregular contact at the interface).
- **Grip/specimen slippage** in compression — minimal but still present.

A **Compressometer/Compression Extensometer** is mounted **directly on the specimen gauge length**, bypassing all machine-level deflections. It provides **sub-micron resolution** (±0.001 mm) and is essential for accurate **elastic modulus (E)** calculation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** The extensometer is **mandatory** in the elastic region. It is removed before plastic deformation to prevent damage (typical extensometer range: 0–2.5 mm).

### 2.3 Barreling — The Signature of a Compression Test

**Mechanism:** When the platen compresses the specimen, the **frictional restraint at the top and bottom faces** prevents the end material from expanding radially. The central, unrestrained region bulges outward, creating a **barrel shape**.

**Factors affecting barrelling:**
| Factor | Effect |
|---|---|
| ↑ Friction at platen interface | ↑ Barrelling |
| ↑ h/d ratio | ↓ Barrelling (more uniform deformation) |
| Use of lubricant (MoS₂, PTFE) | ↓ Barrelling |
| Work-hardening rate | ↓ Barrelling (stiffer material resists lateral flow) |

**Mathematical Friction-Barrelling Index (Cook & Larke):**

$$
\beta = \frac{\mu \cdot d}{h}
$$

Where **μ** = coefficient of friction, **d** = specimen diameter, **h** = specimen height. **Lower β = more uniform deformation.**

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Description |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Engineering Stress | $\sigma = \dfrac{P}{A_0}$ | MPa (N/mm²) | $P$ = applied compressive load, $A_0$ = original cross-sectional area |
| 2 | Engineering Strain | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta h}{h_0}$ | dimensionless | $\Delta h$ = change in height, $h_0$ = original height |
| 3 | True Stress | $\sigma_t = \dfrac{P}{A_i} = \sigma(1-\varepsilon)$ | MPa | True area $A_i$ accounts for bulging |
| 4 | True Strain | $\varepsilon_t = \ln\left(\dfrac{h_0}{h_i}\right) = -\ln(1-\varepsilon)$ | dimensionless | Used for large deformations |
| 5 | Young's Modulus | $E = \dfrac{\sigma}{\varepsilon} = \dfrac{P \cdot h_0}{A_0 \cdot \Delta h}$ | GPa | Slope of linear elastic region |
| 6 | Compressive Yield Strength (0.2% offset) | $\sigma_{yc}$ = stress at 0.2% plastic strain offset | MPa | For non-yield-point materials (Al, Cu) |
| 7 | Compressive Strength | $\sigma_c = \dfrac{P_{max}}{A_0}$ | MPa | $P_{max}$ = maximum load carried (brittle materials) |
| 8 | Poisson's Ratio | $\nu = -\dfrac{\varepsilon_{lateral}}{\varepsilon_{axial}} = -\dfrac{\Delta d \,/\, d_0}{\Delta h \,/\, h_0}$ | dimensionless | Lateral expansion to axial contraction ratio |
| 9 | Modulus of Resilience | $U_r = \dfrac{\sigma_y^2}{2E}$ | MPa = MJ/m³ | Elastic energy stored per unit volume |
| 10 | Friction-Barrelling Index | $\beta = \dfrac{\mu \cdot d}{h}$ | dimensionless | Indicator of deformation uniformity |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units to memorize for KTU board exam:** **1 GPa = 1000 MPa = 1000 N/mm² = 10⁹ N/m²**.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Design of structural columns and pillars** in civil engineering (concrete, steel).
- **Cold forging and extrusion** process design — the data gives flow stress curves.
- **Automotive crashworthiness** — crash boxes are designed using compression energy absorption data.
- **Bearing and bushing design** — components under high compressive Hertzian contact stress.
- **Geotechnical applications** — bearing capacity of soils, rocks.
- **Powder metallurgy** — green and sintered compacts are tested in compression.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Engineering Stress and Strain from Raw Load–Deformation Data

Given raw test data of axial load $P$ (in N) and corresponding axial shortening $\Delta h$ (in mm) for a cylindrical specimen of original diameter $d_0$ and original height $h_0$.

**Step 1 — Compute the original cross-sectional area:**

$$
A_0 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot d_0^2
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the engineering stress for each load step:**

$$
\sigma_i = \frac{P_i}{A_0}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the engineering strain for each deformation step:**

$$
\varepsilon_i = \frac{\Delta h_i}{h_0}
$$

**Step 4 — Plot the $\sigma$–$\varepsilon$ curve** and identify regions (elastic, yield, strain hardening).

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Young's Modulus from Linear Regression of Elastic Data

Let the elastic region contain $n$ data points $(\varepsilon_i, \sigma_i)$ for $i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$.

The best-fit slope (Young's Modulus) by **least-squares linear regression** is:

$$
E = \frac{n \sum_{i=1}^{n}\sigma_i \varepsilon_i - \left(\sum_{i=1}^{n}\sigma_i\right)\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n}\varepsilon_i\right)}
{n \sum_{i=1}^{n}\varepsilon_i^2 - \left(\sum_{i=1}^{n}\varepsilon_i\right)^2}
$$

In practice for a KTU lab, the **two-point slope method** is used:

$$
E = \frac{\sigma_2 - \sigma_1}{\varepsilon_2 - \varepsilon_1}
$$

where points 1 and 2 are well-separated points within the linear elastic region.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Full Step-by-Step)

**Given Data:**
- Specimen: Mild Steel, cylindrical
- Original diameter $d_0 = 20$ mm
- Original height $h_0 = 40$ mm
- Gauge length: $L_0 = 30$ mm (for extensometer mounting)

**Recorded Load–Deformation Data (extracted from extensometer):**

| Load $P$ (kN) | Extensometer Reading $\Delta L$ (mm) |
|---|---|
| 0 | 0.000 |
| 10 | 0.0073 |
| 20 | 0.0145 |
| 30 | 0.0218 |
| 40 | 0.0291 |
| 50 | 0.0364 |
| 55 | 0.0400 (yield begins) |
| 60 | 0.0600 |
| 70 | 0.1100 |
| 80 | 0.1700 |
| 90 | 0.2400 |

**Step 1 — Compute $A_0$:**

$$
A_0 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (20)^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 400 = 314.159 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\sigma$ and $\varepsilon$ for each row:**

Using $\sigma = P / A_0$ (with P in N) and $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L_0$:

| $P$ (N) | $\Delta L$ (mm) | $\sigma = P/A_0$ (MPa) | $\varepsilon = \Delta L / 30$ | Region |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 10000 | 0.0073 | $\dfrac{10000}{314.159} = 31.83$ | $\dfrac{0.0073}{30} = 0.000243$ | Elastic |
| 20000 | 0.0145 | $\dfrac{20000}{314.159} = 63.66$ | $\dfrac{0.0145}{30} = 0.000483$ | Elastic |
| 30000 | 0.0218 | $\dfrac{30000}{314.159} = 95.49$ | $\dfrac{0.0218}{30} = 0.000727$ | Elastic |
| 40000 | 0.0291 | $\dfrac{40000}{314.159} = 127.32$ | $\dfrac{0.0291}{30} = 0.000970$ | Elastic |
| 50000 | 0.0364 | $\dfrac{50000}{314.159} = 159.15$ | $\dfrac{0.0364}{30} = 0.001213$ | Elastic |
| 55000 | 0.0400 | $\dfrac{55000}{314.159} = 175.07$ | $\dfrac{0.0400}{30} = 0.001333$ | Yield point |

**Step 3 — Compute Young's Modulus using points 1 and 4 (best separation within elastic region):**

$$
E = \frac{\sigma_4 - \sigma_1}{\varepsilon_4 - \varepsilon_1} = \frac{127.32 - 31.83}{0.000970 - 0.000243}
$$

$$
E = \frac{95.49}{0.000727} = 131{,}345 \text{ MPa} \approx 131.3 \text{ GPa}
$$

**Step 4 — Identify Compressive Yield Strength:**

From the table, yielding begins at $P = 55$ kN:

$$
\sigma_{yc} = \frac{55{,}000}{314.159} = 175.07 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 5 — Identify Proportional Limit (point at which linearity breaks):**

The elastic data points are perfectly linear (constant slope ≈ 131.3 GPa). At $P = 55$ kN, the curve deviates (strain increases disproportionately). Hence:

$$
\sigma_p \approx 159.15 \text{ MPa at } P = 50 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 6 — Calculate Poisson's Ratio (if lateral strain is measured):**

Suppose at $P = 50$ kN, lateral expansion $\Delta d = 0.0024$ mm:

$$
\varepsilon_{lateral} = \frac{0.0024}{20} = 0.00012
$$

$$
\nu = -\frac{\varepsilon_{lateral}}{\varepsilon_{axial}} = -\frac{0.00012}{0.001213} = 0.099
$$

This value is **lower than expected** (should be ≈ 0.30 for steel), indicating significant **frictional restraint** at the platen — a strong indicator of barrelling.

**Step 7 — Modulus of Resilience:**

$$
U_r = \frac{\sigma_{yc}^2}{2E} = \frac{(175.07)^2}{2 \cdot 131{,}345} = \frac{30{,}649.5}{262{,}690}
$$

$$
U_r = 0.1167 \text{ MPa} = 0.1167 \text{ MJ/m}^3
$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Sources of Error and Their Mitigation

| Source of Error | Effect on Result | Mitigation Strategy |
|---|---|---|
| Platen parallelism mismatch | Non-uniform stress → bending | Use self-aligning spherical seat platen |
| Friction at platen–specimen interface | Barrelling, multi-axial stress | Apply MoS₂ or PTFE lubricant |
| Improper specimen end-face finish | Stress concentration, premature yielding | Grind/lap ends flat & parallel to ≤ 0.01 mm |
| Extensometer slippage | Erratic strain readings | Use proper spring clamps; zero the gauge |
| Machine frame compliance absorbed | Apparent E lower than true | Use extensometer; remove crosshead displacement data |
| Off-center loading | Eccentric stress, bending strain | Use alignment fixture, concentric placement |
| Strain rate too high | Apparent σ_y increases (strain-rate sensitivity) | Maintain constant strain rate ≈ 0.001–0.005 /s |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: UTM Compression Test Setup

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Specimen Mounted Between Platens"] --> B["Spherical Seated Lower Platen"]
    A --> C["Flat Upper Platen with Self Aligning Head"]
    D["Hydraulic / Screw Driven Crosshead"] --> C
    E["Load Cell (Load Measurement)"] --> A
    F["Compressometer Extensometer (Strain Measurement)"] --> A
    G["Data Acquisition System (DAQ)"] --> E
    G --> F
    G --> H["PC with Software: P vs dh Curve"]
    H --> I["Convert to Stress vs Strain Curve"]
    I --> J["Determine E, Proportional Limit, Yield Strength"]
    K["Hand Operated Valve / Pump"] --> D
    L["Frame of UTM (Load Bearing Structure)"] --> A
    style A fill:#FFB74D,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
    style E fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    style F fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    style G fill:#90CAF9,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000
    style I fill:#CE93D8,stroke:#4A148C,color:#000
    style J fill:#FFCC80,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Procedural Flowchart: Compression Test on Ductile Material

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Start: Prepare Cylindrical Specimen"] --> S2["Measure d0 and h0 with Vernier Caliper"]
    S2 --> S3["Calculate A0 = pi/4 x d0 squared"]
    S3 --> S4["Place Specimen Centrally on Lower Platen"]
    S4 --> S5["Apply Thin Layer of Lubricant on Ends"]
    S5 --> S6["Mount Compressometer Extensometer on Specimen"]
    S6 --> S7["Set DAQ to Zero Load and Zero Strain"]
    S7 --> S8["Apply Load Gradually at Constant Strain Rate"]
    S8 --> S9["Record Load P and Deformation dL Continuously"]
    S9 --> S10{"Has Specimen Yielded?"}
    S10 -- No --> S8
    S10 -- Yes --> S11["Remove Extensometer to Prevent Damage"]
    S11 --> S12["Continue Loading Until Desired Strain"]
    S12 --> S13["Unload Specimen Slowly"]
    S13 --> S14["Remove Specimen and Measure Final Dimensions"]
    S14 --> S15["Plot Stress Strain Curve"]
    S15 --> S16["Determine E, sigma prop, sigma yc"]
    S16 --> S17["Report Results with Specimen Photographs"]
    style S1 fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
    style S5 fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000
    style S11 fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000
    style S16 fill:#C5E1A5,stroke:#33691E,color:#000
    style S17 fill:#81D4FA,stroke:#01579B,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Schematic: Specimen Geometry and Extensometer Mounting

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Upper Platen (Movable)"] --> B["Specimen - Cylindrical"]
    B --> C["Lower Platen (Fixed, Spherical Seat)"]
    D["Compressometer Arm 1 (Left)"] -.-> B
    E["Compressometer Arm 2 (Right)"] -.-> B
    D --> F["LVDT / Strain Gauge Transducer"]
    E --> F
    F --> G["Signal Conditioner and Amplifier"]
    G --> H["Digital Readout / DAQ System"]
    style A fill:#B0BEC5,stroke:#37474F,color:#000
    style B fill:#FFCC80,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
    style C fill:#B0BEC5,stroke:#37474F,color:#000
    style D fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    style E fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    style F fill:#90CAF9,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000
    style H fill:#CE93D8,stroke:#4A148C,color:#000
```

### 4.4 Typical Compressive Stress–Strain Curve for Ductile Material (Mild Steel)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    O["Origin: Zero Load, Zero Strain"] --> A["Region A: Linear Elastic - Hookes Law Valid"]
    A --> B["Point B: Proportional Limit sigma p"]
    B --> C["Region C: Non Linear Elastic, Micro Plasticity"]
    C --> D["Point D: Upper Yield Point sigma yu"]
    D --> E["Region E: Yield Plateau, Lower Yield sigma yl"]
    E --> F["Region F: Strain Hardening, Stress Increases"]
    F --> G["Point G: Maximum Load Recorded Pmax - No Fracture for Ductile"]
    G --> H["Region H: Continued Deformation, Barrelling Intensifies"]
    H --> I["Test Terminated at Specified Strain or Load Limit"]
    style O fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style A fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    style B fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000
    style C fill:#FFFDE7,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000
    style D fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C,color:#000
    style E fill:#FFAB91,stroke:#BF360C,color:#000
    style F fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
    style G fill:#FF8A65,stroke:#BF360C,color:#000
    style H fill:#FFAB91,stroke:#BF360C,color:#000
    style I fill:#EF5350,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000
```
