<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
**Define the terms (a) Compressive Yield Strength, (b) Proportional Limit, and (c) Barreling Effect as applicable to a compression test on a ductile material.**

**Model Answer:**

(a) **Compressive Yield Strength (σ_yc):** The stress at which the material undergoes a specified amount of plastic deformation (usually 0.2% offset) under uniaxial compressive loading. For materials exhibiting a yield point (e.g., mild steel), it is the **lower yield point stress** in compression.

(b) **Proportional Limit (σ_p):** The maximum stress at which the stress–strain relationship remains **strictly linear** (i.e., obeys Hooke's law, $\sigma = E\varepsilon$). Beyond this stress, micro-plastic deformation begins and the curve deviates from linearity.

(c) **Barreling Effect:** The phenomenon in which a cylindrical compression specimen develops a **convex, barrel-shaped bulge** in its central region while the ends (in contact with the platens) remain relatively undeformed. This is caused by **frictional restraint** at the platen–specimen interface, which prevents lateral expansion at the ends and forces material to flow outwards at the unrestrained mid-section.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**  
**Why is a Compressometer/Extensometer used in the compression test, and why is it removed after the yield point?**

**Model Answer:**

A **Compressometer (compression extensometer)** is used to accurately measure the **small axial deformation** of the specimen in the elastic region, from which the **Young's Modulus (E)** and **proportional limit** are calculated. It is mounted directly on the specimen, thereby eliminating errors due to **machine frame compliance, platen bedding, and seating irregularities** that would otherwise contaminate the strain data if read from crosshead displacement.

It is **removed after the yield point** because:
1. **Strain range limit:** Typical extensometers have a limited travel (e.g., 2–5 mm) and would be damaged by the large plastic strains (5–50%) in the post-yield region.
2. **Physical safety:** The barrelling and possible surface buckling could physically harm the instrument.
3. **In the plastic region**, crosshead displacement data is sufficient because machine compliance becomes a small fraction of total deformation, and high-accuracy strain measurement is not required (only flow stress behaviour is of interest).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

**Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **procedure for conducting a compression test on a ductile material (mild steel) using a UTM equipped with a compressometer/extensometer**. Mention the relevant IS/ASTM standard. (7 Marks)

**(b)** The following data was recorded during a compression test on a cylindrical mild steel specimen of original diameter $d_0 = 25$ mm and original height $h_0 = 50$ mm. Using a compressometer gauge length of $L_0 = 40$ mm, calculate:
- (i) Young's Modulus (E)
- (ii) Compressive Yield Strength (σ_yc) at 0.2% offset
- (iii) Proportional Limit (σ_p)

| Load P (kN) | Compressometer Reading ΔL (mm) |
|---|---|
| 0 | 0.0000 |
| 20 | 0.0051 |
| 40 | 0.0102 |
| 60 | 0.0153 |
| 80 | 0.0205 |
| 100 | 0.0260 |
| 110 | 0.0290 |
| 120 | 0.1100 |
| 130 | 0.1800 |
| 140 | 0.2500 |

(7 Marks)

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Procedure:**

1. **Specimen Preparation:** Prepare a cylindrical specimen with $h/d$ ratio between **1.5 and 2.0** (per ASTM E9). Ensure end faces are **flat, parallel, and polished** (surface finish ≤ 0.8 μm Ra) to ensure uniform loading.

2. **Dimension Measurement:** Measure the original diameter $d_0$ (at three locations) and height $h_0$ using a Vernier caliper/micrometer. Record mean values.

3. **Initial Cross-sectional Area:**
$$
A_0 = \frac{\pi}{4} d_0^2
$$

4. **Specimen Mounting:**  
   - Clean the platens.  
   - Apply a thin film of **MoS₂ or PTFE grease** on both specimen end faces to **minimize barrelling** (friction reduction).  
   - Place specimen **centrally** on the lower platen.  
   - Use a **self-aligning spherical seat** to ensure uniform contact.

5. **Extensometer Mounting:** Attach the **compressometer (compression extensometer)** symmetrically on the specimen gauge length. Connect to the DAQ system and **zero the reading**.

6. **Loading:** Apply load gradually at a **constant strain rate** (typically 0.001 to 0.005 per second) until well into the plastic region. Record load $P$ and compressometer deformation $\Delta L$ continuously.

7. **Extensometer Removal:** At approximately **1.5× the expected yield load**, carefully remove the compressometer to prevent damage.

8. **Test Termination:** Continue loading up to a predefined strain (e.g., 25–30%) or load limit. Then unload gradually.

9. **Post-Test:** Measure final dimensions: $h_f$, $d_{f,\text{top}}$, $d_{f,\text{mid}}$, $d_{f,\text{bottom}}$. Note the **barrel shape** and any surface cracking.

10. **Data Analysis:** Convert to engineering stress $\sigma = P/A_0$ and strain $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L_0$. Plot the **stress–strain curve** and extract mechanical properties.

**Standard:** **ASTM E9 – Standard Test Methods of Compression Testing of Metallic Materials at Room Temperature.**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Errors to Avoid in Part A:**
> - Do **not** state the standard as "IS 5242" without confirming (most KTU questions expect ASTM E9).
> - Do **not** forget to mention the **lubricant** (often the most-missed sub-point).
> - Always mention **zeroing the extensometer** before loading.

---

**(b) Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute original cross-sectional area:
$$
A_0 = \frac{\pi}{4} (25)^2 = 490.874 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 2:** Compute stress $\sigma$ and strain $\varepsilon$ for each row:

| $P$ (N) | $\sigma = P/A_0$ (MPa) | $\varepsilon = \Delta L / 40$ |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.00 | 0.000000 |
| 20000 | $\dfrac{20000}{490.874} = 40.74$ | 0.000128 |
| 40000 | 81.49 | 0.000255 |
| 60000 | 122.23 | 0.000383 |
| 80000 | 162.97 | 0.000513 |
| 100000 | 203.72 | 0.000650 |
| 110000 | 224.09 | 0.000725 |
| 120000 | 244.46 | 0.002750 |
| 130000 | 264.83 | 0.004500 |
| 140000 | 285.20 | 0.006250 |

**Step 3: Young's Modulus (E)** — Slope of elastic region (using $P = 0$ to $P = 100$ kN):

$$
E = \frac{203.72 - 0}{0.000650 - 0} = \frac{203.72}{0.000650}
$$

$$
E = 313{,}415 \text{ MPa} \approx 313.4 \text{ GPa}
$$

**Cross-check using two points (40 kN and 100 kN):**

$$
E = \frac{203.72 - 81.49}{0.000650 - 0.000255} = \frac{122.23}{0.000395} = 309{,}418 \text{ MPa} \approx 309.4 \text{ GPa}
$$

Average $E \approx 311$ GPa. *(Note: A small variation from the textbook value of 200 GPa for steel indicates either specimen misalignment or extensometer slippage; in practice, this should be reported with a comment.)*

**Step 4: Proportional Limit (σ_p):**  
The elastic data (0 to 100 kN) is linear. At $P = 110$ kN, $\sigma = 224.09$ MPa, $\varepsilon = 0.000725$. Checking slope from 100 to 110 kN:

$$
E_{100 \to 110} = \frac{224.09 - 203.72}{0.000725 - 0.000650} = \frac{20.37}{0.000075} = 271{,}600 \text{ MPa}
$$

This is significantly less than 313 GPa, so linearity breaks between 100 and 110 kN.

$$
\boxed{\sigma_p \approx 203.72 \text{ MPa at } P = 100 \text{ kN}}
$$

**Step 5: Compressive Yield Strength (σ_yc) at 0.2% offset:**  
The 0.2% offset method:  
- Draw a line parallel to the elastic slope, offset by 0.002 (i.e., 0.2% strain) on the strain axis.  
- Intersection of this line with the stress–strain curve gives σ_yc.

Offset strain = 0.000650 (at 100 kN) + 0.002 = 0.002650.

Reading the table, at $\varepsilon \approx 0.002750$ (at $P = 120$ kN), we are just past the offset strain.

$$
\boxed{\sigma_{yc} \approx 244.46 \text{ MPa at } P = 120 \text{ kN}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Many students **forget to use $A_0$** (original area) for engineering stress; they incorrectly use instantaneous area.
> 2. The 0.2% offset is **strain offset, not stress offset** — read the question carefully.
> 3. Always express final answer in **GPa for E** and **MPa for stress**; mismatched units lose 1 mark.
> 4. Plotting the curve is **not required** in numerical sub-parts, but **conceptual curves in sub-part (a) carry marks**.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice — [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Question]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **differences between tension and compression testing of ductile materials**. Why is the ultimate compressive strength not well-defined for ductile materials? (7 Marks)

**(b)** During a compression test on a cylindrical aluminium specimen, the following observations were made:
- Original diameter: 30 mm; Original height: 45 mm.
- At a load of 60 kN, the compressometer reading (gauge length 35 mm) is 0.0245 mm.
- At yield, the load is 85 kN and the compressometer reading is 0.0385 mm.
- At maximum test load of 130 kN, the specimen has shortened by 5.5 mm and the central diameter is 36 mm.

Calculate:
- (i) Young's Modulus of aluminium
- (ii) Compressive Yield Strength
- (iii) Engineering strain at maximum load
- (iv) True stress at maximum load

(7 Marks)

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Differences between Tension and Compression Testing of Ductile Materials:**

| S.No. | Aspect | Tension Test | Compression Test |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Stress state | Uniaxial tensile | Uniaxial compressive |
| 2 | Failure mode | Necking → fracture | Continuous deformation, **no fracture** |
| 3 | Ultimate strength | Well-defined (UTS) | **Not well-defined** — load increases continuously as area grows |
| 4 | Specimen shape change | Necking (localized) | Barrelling (uniform) |
| 5 | Strain limit | ~30–50% (steel) | Up to 70–80% achievable |
| 6 | Cross-section | Decreases (A < A₀) | Increases (A > A₀) — bulging |
| 7 | Friction effect | Negligible (only at grips) | **Significant at platens** — causes barrelling |
| 8 | Standard specimen | Long, dog-bone | Short, uniform cylinder (h/d = 1.5–2) |
| 9 | Strain measurement | Tensile extensometer | Compressometer (compression extensometer) |
| 10 | Volume change | Increases slightly (negligible) | Conserves (incompressible plastic flow) |
| 11 | Governing standard | ASTM E8 | ASTM E9 |

**Why is the Ultimate Compressive Strength (UCS) not well-defined for ductile materials?**

In tension, the UTS corresponds to the onset of **necking (geometric instability)** — beyond this, the cross-sectional area reduces faster than the load-carrying capacity, so the load drops and the curve turns over. This gives a **distinct maximum** on the load–elongation curve.

In compression, the cross-sectional area of the specimen **continuously increases** due to barrelling and plastic flow. Since load $P = \sigma \cdot A$, and both $\sigma$ (via strain hardening) and $A$ increase with deformation, the load **rises monotonically** without a peak. There is **no instability** (no necking equivalent), so the **load–deformation curve has no maximum** for ductile materials. Therefore, UCS cannot be defined as in brittle compression (where fracture occurs on a shear plane at ~45°).

**(b) Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute original area:**
$$
A_0 = \frac{\pi}{4} (30)^2 = 706.858 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Young's Modulus (E):**

Using data at $P = 60$ kN: $\Delta L = 0.0245$ mm, $L_0 = 35$ mm.

$$
\sigma = \frac{60{,}000}{706.858} = 84.88 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{0.0245}{35} = 0.000700
$$

$$
E = \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon} = \frac{84.88}{0.000700} = 121{,}257 \text{ MPa} \approx 121.3 \text{ GPa}
$$

*(Note: Aluminium E = ~70 GPa. The higher value indicates friction-induced constraint at the platens increasing apparent stiffness.)*

**Step 3 — Compressive Yield Strength (σ_yc):**

At yield, $P_y = 85$ kN:

$$
\sigma_{yc} = \frac{85{,}000}{706.858} = 120.24 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 4 — Engineering Strain at Maximum Load:**

Total shortening $\Delta h = 5.5$ mm, original height $h_0 = 45$ mm:

$$
\varepsilon_{max} = \frac{5.5}{45} = 0.1222 \text{ (or 12.22%)}
$$

**Step 5 — True Stress at Maximum Load:**

At maximum load, $P_{max} = 130$ kN, central diameter $d_{f,mid} = 36$ mm.

True area:
$$
A_{true} = \frac{\pi}{4} (36)^2 = 1017.876 \text{ mm}^2
$$

True stress:
$$
\sigma_{true} = \frac{130{,}000}{1017.876} = 127.72 \text{ MPa}
$$

Alternatively, using the true-stress relationship:
$$
\sigma_{true} = \sigma_{eng} (1 - \varepsilon_{eng}) = \frac{130{,}000}{706.858} \times (1 - 0.1222)
$$

$$
\sigma_{true} = 183.92 \times 0.8778 = 161.45 \text{ MPa}
$$

*(The discrepancy between the two methods arises because the first uses the actual measured bulging diameter (more accurate), while the second assumes uniform deformation and uses engineering strain correction.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. The **0.2% offset yield strength** is required only if no distinct yield point is observed; aluminium is offset-yield.
> 2. Always state the **assumption of volume constancy** in plastic deformation if you use the relation $A_0 h_0 = A_i h_i$.
> 3. Do **not** confuse engineering stress with true stress — they are different by the factor $(1-\varepsilon)$ for compression.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ **Standard:** ASTM E9 governs compression testing of metallic materials.
- ✅ **Specimen:** Cylindrical, $h/d$ ratio = **1.5 to 2.0**, end faces flat and parallel.
- ✅ **Lubrication:** Use MoS₂ or PTFE grease on ends to **minimize barrelling**.
- ✅ **Engineering Stress:** $\sigma = P/A_0$ (use original area).
- ✅ **Engineering Strain:** $\varepsilon = \Delta h/h_0$ (negative sign omitted for magnitude).
- ✅ **Young's Modulus:** Slope of linear elastic region; **E_compression = E_tension** for ductile metals.
- ✅ **Poisson's Ratio:** $\nu = -\varepsilon_{lateral} / \varepsilon_{axial} \approx 0.30$ for steel.
- ✅ **Barrelling:** Caused by friction at platens; increases with $\mu$ and $d/h$.
- ✅ **UCS not defined for ductiles** — load increases monotonically; no instability.
- ✅ **Extensometer removed** at ~1.5× yield load to prevent damage.
- ✅ **0.2% Offset Method** is used for materials without a distinct yield point (e.g., aluminium, brass).
- ✅ **Units to use:** E in **GPa**, stress in **MPa** (1 GPa = 1000 MPa).
- ✅ **Modulus of Resilience:** $U_r = \sigma_y^2 / 2E$ — energy stored elastically per unit volume.
- ✅ **True Stress in compression** (volume constancy): $\sigma_t = \sigma(1-\varepsilon)$ — increases monotonically as area grows.
- ✅ **Specimen fails by barrelling and surface cracking**, **never by clean fracture** for ductile materials.
- ✅ **Strain rate:** Maintain 0.001 to 0.005 /s for quasi-static testing.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tensile Properties of Cast Iron Using UTM with Extensometer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMEL308 - MATERIALS TESTING LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Tensile Properties of Cast Iron Using UTM with Extensometer # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Tensile Test on Cast Iron** is a standardized destructive mechanical testing procedure (conforming to **IS 8978 / ASTM A48 / IS 2071** specifications) conducted on a Universal Testing Machine (UTM) equipped with a precision **Extensometer**, to determine the quasi-static uniaxial tensile response of grey cast iron, white cast iron, or ductile (nodular) cast iron under a monotonically increasing axial load until fracture occurs. Unlike ductile materials (mild steel, aluminium), **cast iron is fundamentally a brittle engineering material** because of the presence of **graphite flakes (in grey cast iron)** or **graphite nodules (in ductile iron)** embedded in a ferrous matrix. These graphite inclusions act as internal stress concentrators and pre-existing microcracks, causing the material to fracture with **negligible plastic deformation** (typically < 1% elongation). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The experiment is specifically designed to teach the student the *differences* in tensile behaviour between brittle (cast iron) and ductile (mild steel) materials, and to demonstrate **why an extensometer is mandatory** for accurate strain measurement in low-ductility materials. ## 1.2 Key Engineering Metrics Measured The following primary tensile properties are evaluated and reported: | # | Property | Symbol | Typical Magnitude (Grey Cast Iron) | |---|----------|--------|-----------------------------------| | 1 | Ultimate Tensile Strength | $\sigma_{UTS}$ | **150 – 400 MPa** | | 2 | Modulus of Elasticity (Tension) | $E_t$ | **80 – 170 GPa** | | 3 | Percentage Elongation | $\%EL$ | **0.2 – 1.5 %** | | 4 | Percentage Reduction in Area | $\%RA$ | **≈ 0 % (negligible)** | | 5 | Modulus of Rupture (transverse) | $MOR$ | **250 – 600 MPa** | | 6 | Fracture Load | $P_f$ | Application-dependent | | 7 | Fracture Stress | $\sigma_f$ | ≈ UTS (no necking) | ## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Imagine you are pulling apart two pieces of **dry clay (cast iron)** versus two pieces of **soft chewing gum (mild steel)**: - The **chewing gum** stretches a lot before snapping — you can see and measure the elongation easily with a simple ruler. Yielding is visible. - The **dry clay** barely stretches at all and then **cracks suddenly** — the stretch is so tiny (about the thickness of a human hair over 50 mm) that a normal ruler cannot detect it. > [!NOTE] > **The Extensometer is the "Super-Ruler":** A regular ruler has a least count of 1 mm. The strains in cast iron are of the order of **$10^{-3}$ to $10^{-4}$**, meaning elongations of just **0.025 mm to 0.005 mm** over a 50 mm gauge length. An **extensometer** is an electromechanical device with a least count of **0.001 mm (1 µm)** — it acts like a super-sensitive ruler that can detect these microscopic stretches and convert them into an electrical signal for the recording system. ## 1.4 Why Cast Iron Behaves This Way — Material Science Insight Grey cast iron contains **2.5 % to 4.0 % carbon** and **1.0 % to 3.0 % silicon**. During solidification, the excess carbon precipitates out as **interconnected graphite flakes** within a pearlitic or ferritic matrix. These flakes: 1. Act as **internal notches** that initiate cracks under tensile stress. 2. Have **negligible tensile strength** themselves — they simply separate under load. 3. Force the matrix to carry the entire tensile load across ever-smaller cross-sectional area. 4. Result in a stress–strain curve that is **almost perfectly linear up to fracture** — Hooke's law is obeyed almost to the breaking point. > [!TIP] > **KTU Practical Tip:** When you plot the load–elongation curve for cast iron, do NOT expect to see a yield point, an upper/lower yield plateau, or a plastic region. The curve will rise almost as a straight line and then **drop vertically** at the fracture point. The slope of this straight line gives the **Young's Modulus**. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Background — Step-by-Step Logic ### Step 1: The Governing Principle A tensile test applies a **uniaxial, monotonically increasing, quasi-static tensile load** $P$ along the longitudinal axis of a precisely machined cylindrical specimen. The original cross-sectional area is $A_0$ and the original gauge length is $L_0$. ### Step 2: Why Cast Iron Needs Special Treatment For **ductile materials**, the gauge length changes visibly, and a simple ruler or dial gauge is sufficient. For **cast iron**, the **failure strain is in the elastic range**. Hence: - An **extensometer** is mounted directly on the reduced gauge section. - It continuously records $\Delta L$ versus load $P$ in real time. - The UTM's crosshead displacement is **NOT used** for strain calculation in brittle materials, because it includes machine compliance, grip slippage, and jaw elongation errors. ### Step 3: The Stress–Strain Relationship From the recorded data, two derived quantities are computed: **Engineering Stress** — load per unit **original** area: $$\sigma = \frac{P}{A_0}$$ **Engineering Strain** — elongation per unit **original** gauge length: $$\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \frac{L - L_0}{L_0}$$ Since cast iron **does not neck**, engineering stress and **true stress are practically identical** at all points up to fracture. ### Step 4: Determination of Modulus of Elasticity Cast iron follows Hooke's law almost to fracture: $$\sigma = E_t \cdot \varepsilon \quad \Rightarrow \quad E_t = \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon} = \frac{P \cdot L_0}{A_0 \cdot \Delta L}$$ $E_t$ is obtained as the **slope of the initial linear portion** of the stress–strain curve. ### Step 5: Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS) The **maximum load** $P_{max}$ sustained by the specimen is divided by the original area: $$\sigma_{UTS} = \frac{P_{max}}{A_0}$$ > [!NOTE] > For cast iron, $P_{max}$ coincides with the **fracture load** $P_f$ because the curve is monotonically linear until sudden failure. ### Step 6: Ductility Indices Even though cast iron is brittle, the laboratory still measures: **Percentage Elongation:** $$\%EL = \frac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100$$ **Percentage Reduction in Area:** $$\%RA = \frac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100$$ For grey cast iron, both these values are **typically less than 1 %** and are often within the **measurement uncertainty** of the extensometer. ### Step 7: True Stress and True Strain (for completeness) $$\sigma_{true} = \frac{P}{A_i} = \sigma (1 + \varepsilon)$$ $$\varepsilon_{true} = \ln\left(\frac{L_i}{L_0}\right) = \ln(1 + \varepsilon)$$ For cast iron, since $\varepsilon \to 0$, we have $\sigma_{true} \approx \sigma$ and $\varepsilon_{true} \approx \varepsilon$. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Examination-Ready) | # | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Remarks / Boundary Condition | |---|----------|---------|------|------------------------------| | 1 | Original Area (circular) | $A_0 = \dfrac{\pi d_0^2}{4}$ | $\text{mm}^2$ | $d_0$ = original gauge diameter | | 2 | Engineering Stress | $\sigma = \dfrac{P}{A_0}$ | $\text{MPa} = \text{N/mm}^2$ | Valid until fracture for cast iron | | 3 | Engineering Strain | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0}$ | dimensionless (mm/mm) | $\Delta L$ from extensometer | | 4 | Young's Modulus | $E_t = \dfrac{\sigma}{\varepsilon} = \dfrac{P L_0}{A_0 \Delta L}$ | $\text{GPa}$ | Slope of linear elastic region | | 5 | Ultimate Tensile Strength | $\sigma_{UTS} = \dfrac{P_{max}}{A_0}$ | $\text{MPa}$ | $P_{max} = P_f$ for cast iron | | 6 | Fracture Stress | $\sigma_f = \dfrac{P_f}{A_0}$ | $\text{MPa}$ | Since no necking: $\sigma_f \approx \sigma_{UTS}$ | | 7 | Percentage Elongation | $\%EL = \dfrac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100$ | % | Reported to 2 decimal places | | 8 | Percentage Reduction in Area | $\%RA = \dfrac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100$ | % | For cast iron: $\approx 0$ | | 9 | True Stress | $\sigma_t = \sigma (1 + \varepsilon)$ | $\text{MPa}$ | ≈ Engineering stress for cast iron | | 10 | True Strain | $\varepsilon_t = \ln(1 + \varepsilon)$ | dimensionless | ≈ Engineering strain for cast iron | | 11 | Proportional Gauge Length | $L_0 = 5.65 \sqrt{A_0}$ | mm | IS 8978 / IS 2062 standard | | 12 | Standard Gauge Length (fixed) | $L_0 = 50 \text{ mm}$ | mm | Used for $d_0 = 12.5$ mm specimen | | 13 | Strain Rate | $\dot{\varepsilon} = \dfrac{\Delta \varepsilon}{\Delta t}$ | $\text{s}^{-1}$ | Held at $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3} \text{ s}^{-1}$ per IS 8978 | | 14 | Poisson's Ratio (typical) | $\nu = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{lateral}}{\varepsilon_{axial}}$ | dimensionless | Grey C.I. ≈ **0.22 – 0.27** | | 15 | Specific Modulus | $E_t / \rho$ | $\text{GPa} \cdot \text{cm}^3/\text{g}$ | Used in material selection charts | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Note:** Always state the **standard reference** (e.g., *as per IS 8978:1992 / ASTM A48*) in your practical record. Examiners award marks for citing the correct governing standard. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility The tensile test on cast iron is not an academic exercise — it has direct industrial consequences: - **Foundry Quality Control:** Foundries producing engine blocks, brake drums, manhole covers, and pipe fittings routinely perform this test to certify each batch. - **Design of Pressure Vessels:** Grey cast iron is used in **low-pressure pipelines and pump housings**. The test provides the **allowable tensile stress** $\sigma_{allow} = \sigma_{UTS} / \text{FOS}$. - **Comparative Material Selection:** Engineers use the test data to compare grey C.I. vs ductile C.I. vs malleable C.I. vs steel castings for the same application. - **Failure Analysis:** When a cast iron component fails in service (e.g., a cracked flywheel), the fractograph is compared with the standard tensile fracture to deduce the loading mode. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Specimen Geometry Derivation (Proportional Specimen) For a **circular cylindrical proportional specimen** as per **IS 8978**, the gauge length is related to the original area by: $$L_0 = k \cdot \sqrt{A_0}$$ For a circular cross-section, $A_0 = \dfrac{\pi d_0^2}{4}$, hence: $$\sqrt{A_0} = \sqrt{\frac{\pi d_0^2}{4}} = \frac{d_0 \sqrt{\pi}}{2}$$ $$L_0 = k \cdot \frac{d_0 \sqrt{\pi}}{2}$$ The standard proportionality constant is **$k = 5.65$**, giving: $$L_0 = 5.65 \times \frac{d_0 \sqrt{\pi}}{2} = 5.65 \times 0.8862 \cdot d_0 \approx 5.0 \cdot d_0$$ > Hence the **golden rule:** for a proportional cylindrical specimen, the **gauge length is exactly 5 times the gauge diameter**. If $d_0 = 12.5 \text{ mm}$, then $L_0 = 62.5 \text{ mm} \approx 50 \text{ mm}$ (rounded to the standard 50 mm gauge length used in most KTU labs). ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Lab Style ### Given Data (Typical KTU Lab Record) A grey cast iron tensile specimen is tested in a UTM fitted with an extensometer. The following observations are recorded: | Parameter | Value | |-----------|-------| | Gauge diameter, $d_0$ | **12.50 mm** | | Gauge length, $L_0$ | **50.00 mm** | | Extensometer gauge length, $L_e$ | **50.00 mm** | | Extensometer least count | **0.001 mm** | | Load range of UTM | **0 – 200 kN** | | Cross-head speed | **1 mm/min** | | Load $P$ (kN) | Extensometer reading $\Delta L$ (mm) | |----------------|--------------------------------------| | 0 | 0.000 | | 10 | 0.011 | | 20 | 0.022 | | 30 | 0.033 | | 40 | 0.044 | | 50 | 0.055 | | 60 | 0.067 | | 70 | 0.080 | | 80 (Fracture) | 0.094 | ### Step 1: Calculate Original Cross-Sectional Area $$A_0 = \frac{\pi d_0^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (12.50)^2}{4}$$ $$A_0 = \frac{\pi \times 156.25}{4} = \frac{490.874}{4} = 122.72 \text{ mm}^2$$ ### Step 2: Tabulate Stress and Strain for Each Load Point Using the formulas $\sigma = P/A_0$ and $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L_0$: | $P$ (kN) | $\sigma$ (MPa) | $\Delta L$ (mm) | $\varepsilon$ (×$10^{-3}$) | |----------|----------------|------------------|---------------------------| | 0 | 0.00 | 0.000 | 0.000 | | 10 | 81.49 | 0.011 | 0.220 | | 20 | 162.97 | 0.022 | 0.440 | | 30 | 244.46 | 0.033 | 0.660 | | 40 | 325.94 | 0.044 | 0.880 | | 50 | 407.43 | 0.055 | 1.100 | | 60 | 488.92 | 0.067 | 1.340 | | 70 | 570.40 | 0.080 | 1.600 | | 80 (Fracture) | 651.89 | 0.094 | 1.880 | **Sample calculation** for $P = 50$ kN: $$\sigma = \frac{P}{A_0} = \frac{50 \times 10^3 \text{ N}}{122.72 \text{ mm}^2} = 407.43 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 407.43 \text{ MPa}$$ $$\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \frac{0.055}{50.00} = 0.00110 = 1.10 \times 10^{-3}$$ ### Step 3: Determine Young's Modulus $E_t$ (Slope of Linear Region) Using the **origin** and the **fracture point** (since cast iron is linearly elastic up to fracture): $$E_t = \frac{\sigma_f - 0}{\varepsilon_f - 0} = \frac{\sigma_f}{\varepsilon_f}$$ $$\sigma_f = \frac{P_f}{A_0} = \frac{80 \times 10^3}{122.72} = 651.89 \text{ MPa}$$ $$\varepsilon_f = \frac{0.094}{50.00} = 1.88 \times 10^{-3}$$ $$E_t = \frac{651.89 \text{ MPa}}{1.88 \times 10^{-3}} = 346{,}750 \text{ MPa} = 346.75 \text{ GPa}$$ > [!NOTE] > A $E_t$ value of ~347 GPa is high for grey cast iron. This indicates a **high-quality pearlitic grey cast iron** or the example is hypothetical for illustration. Real grey C.I. typically shows $E_t = 80 - 170$ GPa. ### Step 4: Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS) $$\sigma_{UTS} = \frac{P_{max}}{A_0} = \frac{80{,}000 \text{ N}}{122.72 \text{ mm}^2} = 651.89 \text{ MPa} \approx 652 \text{ MPa}$$ ### Step 5: Ductility Calculations Assume the final gauge length after fracture is $L_f = 50.10$ mm and final diameter $d_f = 12.40$ mm (typical for slightly ductile cast iron): $$\%EL = \frac{50.10 - 50.00}{50.00} \times 100 = 0.20 \,\%$$ $$A_f = \frac{\pi \times (12.40)^2}{4} = 120.76 \text{ mm}^2$$ $$\%RA = \frac{122.72 - 120.76}{122.72} \times 100 = 1.60 \,\%$$ ### Step 3.6: True Stress and True Strain at Fracture $$\sigma_{true,f} = \sigma_f (1 + \varepsilon_f) = 651.89 \times (1 + 0.00188) = 653.12 \text{ MPa}$$ $$\varepsilon_{true,f} = \ln(1 + \varepsilon_f) = \ln(1.00188) = 0.001878$$ These values confirm that for cast iron, engineering and true quantities are **virtually identical**. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Reading the Curve | Observation on Load–Elongation / Stress–Strain Curve | Diagnosis | Action | |--------------------------------------------------|-----------|--------| | Curve is **linear up to sudden drop** | Typical brittle cast iron | Verify $E_t$ and $\sigma_{UTS}$ | | Curve shows **slight plastic region** before fracture | Ductile iron (SG iron) | Note $\sigma_y$ if present | | Curve **oscillates** near zero load | Grip slip / bedding error | Re-tighten grips, re-test | | Extensometer output **drifts** at low loads | Loose extensometer knife-edges | Re-mount, zero the device | | Fracture is **flat, perpendicular to axis** | Pure brittle cleavage | Valid test — record | | Fracture is **cup-and-cone** | Material was not cast iron | Specimen mislabelled — re-check | | Sudden load drop **before** extensometer reading | Pre-existing crack / flaw | Note as defect, re-test | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — UTM with Extensometer for Cast Iron Testing ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeA["POWER SUPPLY UNIT"] --> nodeB["HYDRAULIC ACTUATOR"] nodeB --> nodeC["LOADING FRAME WITH TWO COLUMNS"] nodeC --> nodeD["UPPER CROSSHEAD WITH FIXED GRIP"] nodeC --> nodeE["MOVABLE CROSSHEAD WITH HYDRAULIC GRIP"] nodeF["CAST IRON TENSILE SPECIMEN"] --> nodeD nodeF --> nodeE nodeG["EXTENSOMETER MOUNTED ON GAUGE LENGTH"] --> nodeF nodeH["LOAD CELL 0 TO 200 KN"] --> nodeC nodeI["STRAIN GAUGE BRIDGE INSIDE EXTENSOMETER"] --> nodeG nodeH --> nodeJ["DATA ACQUISITION SYSTEM DAQ"] nodeI --> nodeJ nodeJ --> nodeK["X Y PLOTTER OR PC SOFTWARE"] nodeJ --> nodeL["DIGITAL DISPLAY LOAD AND ELONGATION"] nodeK --> nodeM["LOAD ELONGATION CURVE"] nodeK --> nodeN["STRESS STRAIN CURVE"] nodeO["OPERATOR CONTROL PANEL"] --> nodeB nodeO --> nodeJ style nodeA fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style nodeF fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#1e8449,stroke-width:3px style nodeG fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#2874a6,stroke-width:2px style nodeJ fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#922b21,stroke-width:2px style nodeM fill:#d7bde2,stroke:#6c3483,stroke-width:2px style nodeN fill:#d7bde2,stroke:#6c3483,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.2 Cast Iron Specimen Geometry (Proportional, IS 8978) ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA["HEAD FOR THREADED GRIP
Diameter 20 mm
Length 30 mm"] --> nodeB["TRANSITION RADIUS R 12 mm
Length 15 mm"] nodeB --> nodeC["GAUGE LENGTH L0 50 mm
Diameter d0 12.5 mm"] nodeC --> nodeD["TRANSITION RADIUS R 12 mm
Length 15 mm"] nodeD --> nodeE["HEAD FOR THREADED GRIP
Diameter 20 mm
Length 30 mm"] style nodeC fill:#fdebd0,stroke:#b9770e,stroke-width:3px style nodeA fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#1f618d,stroke-width:1px style nodeE fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#1f618d,stroke-width:1px style nodeB fill:#f2f3f4,stroke:#566573,stroke-width:1px style nodeD fill:#f2f3f4,stroke:#566573,stroke-width:1px ``` ## 4.3 Typical Stress–Strain Curve Comparison (Cast Iron vs Mild Steel) ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeA["START ORIGIN"] --> nodeB["REGION A LINEAR ELASTIC
STRESS INCREASES LINEARLY WITH STRAIN"] nodeB --> nodeC{"MATERIAL TYPE"} nodeC -->|CAST IRON| nodeD["REGION B SUDDEN FRACTURE
NO YIELDING NO NECKING
STRESS DROPS TO ZERO"] nodeC -->|MILD STEEL| nodeE["REGION B ELASTIC LIMIT
PROPORTIONAL LIMIT REACHED"] nodeE --> nodeF["REGION C YIELD PLATEAU
UPPER AND LOWER YIELD POINTS"] nodeF --> nodeG["REGION D STRAIN HARDENING
STRESS RISES TO UTS"] nodeG --> nodeH["REGION E NECKING
STRESS DROPS UNTIL FRACTURE"] style nodeB fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#196f3d,stroke-width:2px style nodeD fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#922b21,stroke-width:3px style nodeF fill:#fcf3cf,stroke:#b9770e,stroke-width:2px style nodeG fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#1f618d,stroke-width:2px style nodeH fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#922b21,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.4 Experimental Procedure Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeA["STEP 1 MEASURE SPECIMEN
DIAMETER d0 AT 3 LOCATIONS
RECORD L0 AND L0"] --> nodeB["STEP 2 MOUNT SPECIMEN
IN UTM GRIPS"] nodeB --> nodeC["STEP 3 ATTACH EXTENSOMETER
AT GAUGE LENGTH MIDPOINT"] nodeC --> nodeD["STEP 4 ZERO LOAD AND EXTENSOMETER"] nodeD --> nodeE["STEP 5 APPLY LOAD AT
1 MM PER MIN CROSSHEAD SPEED"] nodeE --> nodeF["STEP 6 RECORD LOAD AND
EXTENSOMETER READINGS EVERY 5 KN"] nodeF --> nodeG{"FRACTURE OCCURRED"} nodeG -->|NO| nodeE nodeG -->|YES| nodeH["STEP 7 REMOVE EXTENSOMETER
BEFORE FINAL FAILURE TO PROTECT"] nodeH --> nodeI["STEP 8 RECOMBINE FRACTURED
SPECIMEN AND MEASURE Lf AND df"] nodeI --> nodeJ["STEP 9 COMPUTE STRESS STRAIN
YOUNGS MODULUS UTS PERCENT ELONGATION"] nodeJ --> nodeK["STEP 10 PLOT STRESS STRAIN CURVE
AND WRITE LAB REPORT"] style nodeA fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#1f618d,stroke-width:2px style nodeC fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#1e8449,stroke-width:2px style nodeH fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#cb4335,stroke-width:2px style nodeJ fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#9a7d0a,stroke-width:2px style nodeK fill:#d7bde2,stroke:#6c3483,stroke-width:2px ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Explain why an extensometer is mandatory for tensile testing of cast iron but not for mild steel. (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** Cast iron is a **brittle material** with fracture strain typically less than **1.5 %**. The corresponding elongation over a 50 mm gauge length is less than **0.75 mm**, often of the order of **0.05 mm**. Such small elongations cannot be measured accurately using the UTM crosshead displacement because the crosshead movement includes machine compliance, grip slippage, and elastic deformation of the frame, all of which are comparable in magnitude to the specimen elongation itself. An **extensometer** is a high-precision electromechanical device with a least count of **0.001 mm** that is mounted directly on the reduced gauge section of the specimen. It measures the **localized strain** of the specimen only, eliminating the machine errors. For ductile **mild steel**, elongations are 20–30 %, large enough to be measured accurately even with a simple ruler or dial gauge, so a crosshead-based measurement is sufficient. > **KTU Marking Tip:** Use the phrase *"machine compliance error"* — examiners love this term. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Why does cast iron fail without yielding in a tensile test? Explain in terms of its microstructure. (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** Grey cast iron contains **2.5 – 4.0 % carbon**, most of which is present as **interconnected graphite flakes** distributed throughout a **ferritic / pearlitic matrix**. These graphite flakes have negligible tensile strength and act as **internal stress concentrators** and pre-existing microcracks. Under a tensile load, the stress field around the flake tips intensifies according to Inglis's equation, $\sigma_{max} = \sigma(1 + 2\sqrt{a/\rho})$, where $a$ is the flake length and $\rho$ is the tip radius. The matrix is therefore unable to plastically deform (yield) to relieve this stress concentration. As a result, cracks initiate at the flake tips and propagate rapidly through the matrix, causing **sudden brittle fracture** at loads well below the matrix's theoretical strength, with **negligible macroscopic yielding**. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024] **(a)** Describe with a neat sketch the **preparation of a standard cast iron tensile specimen** as per IS 8978. List any **five** essential dimensional measurements to be taken before testing. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A grey cast iron specimen of diameter **15 mm** and gauge length **60 mm** is tested in a UTM. The extensometer readings and loads are tabulated below. Determine the **(i) Young's Modulus**, **(ii) Ultimate Tensile Strength**, and **(iii) Percentage Elongation**, given that the final gauge length after fracture is **60.25 mm** and the final diameter is **14.90 mm**. **(7 Marks)** | Load (kN) | 0 | 5 | 10 | 15 | 20 | 25 | 30 (Fracture) | |-----------|---|---|----|----|----|----|----------------| | Extensometer $\Delta L$ (mm) | 0.000 | 0.012 | 0.024 | 0.036 | 0.048 | 0.060 | 0.072 | --- #### Model Solution for Q-A(a) — 7 Marks **Specimen preparation steps (any 5):** 1. **Raw material selection:** Obtain a representative sample of cast iron from the foundry, ensuring it is free from surface defects, blowholes, or sand inclusions. 2. **Casting / Sectioning:** Either cast the specimen separately (keel-block or Y-block per IS 8978) or cut it from a larger casting by abrasive cutting. 3. **Rough machining:** Turn the casting on a lathe to obtain the basic cylindrical shape with thread-rolling allowance at the ends. 4. **Threading:** Cut **M16 × 1.5 mm** or similar threads on both ends for gripping in the UTM. 5. **Finish turning of gauge section:** Machine the central gauge section to the final diameter $d_0$ with a surface roughness of $Ra \leq 0.8 \text{ µm}$ to avoid premature surface crack initiation. 6. **Radiusing transitions:** Produce a smooth **fillet radius of 12 mm** between the threaded ends and the gauge section to avoid stress concentration at the shoulders. 7. **Final inspection:** Check dimensions with vernier calipers and micrometer; mark the gauge length with a scribe or layout dye. **Five essential pre-test measurements:** | # | Quantity | Instrument | |---|----------|-----------| | 1 | Gauge diameter $d_0$ (3 locations, average) | Micrometer (LC = 0.01 mm) | | 2 | Gauge length $L_0$ | Vernier caliper (LC = 0.02 mm) | | 3 | Thread diameter and pitch | Thread gauge | | 4 | Fillet radius | Radius gauge | | 5 | Specimen weight (optional, for density) | Electronic balance | > A neat **dimensional sketch** of the proportional specimen showing $L_0 = 5.65\sqrt{A_0}$ with a labelled fillet radius must accompany the answer. (1 mark reserved for sketch) --- #### Model Solution for Q-A(b) — 7 Marks **Given:** $d_0 = 15 \text{ mm}$, $L_0 = 60 \text{ mm}$, $L_f = 60.25 \text{ mm}$, $d_f = 14.90 \text{ mm}$, $P_f = P_{max} = 30 \text{ kN}$. **Step 1: Compute original area** $$A_0 = \frac{\pi d_0^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (15)^2}{4} = \frac{225 \pi}{4} = 176.71 \text{ mm}^2$$ **Step 2: Compute fracture stress = UTS** (since cast iron has no necking) $$\sigma_{UTS} = \sigma_f = \frac{P_f}{A_0} = \frac{30{,}000 \text{ N}}{176.71 \text{ mm}^2} = 169.77 \text{ MPa} \approx 170 \text{ MPa}$$ **Step 3: Compute Young's Modulus using the linear region (origin to last point)** $$\varepsilon_f = \frac{\Delta L_f}{L_0} = \frac{0.072}{60.00} = 1.20 \times 10^{-3}$$ $$E_t = \frac{\sigma_f}{\varepsilon_f} = \frac{169.77 \text{ MPa}}{1.20 \times 10^{-3}} = 141{,}475 \text{ MPa} \approx 141.5 \text{ GPa}$$ **Step 4: Percentage Elongation** $$\%EL = \frac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100 = \frac{60.25 - 60.00}{60.00} \times 100 = 0.4167\% \approx 0.42\%$$ **Final Results:** | Property | Value | |----------|-------| | Young's Modulus, $E_t$ | **141.5 GPa** | | Ultimate Tensile Strength, $\sigma_{UTS}$ | **170 MPa** | | Percentage Elongation, $\%EL$ | **0.42 %** | > These values are consistent with a **medium-grade grey cast iron** (typical $E_t = 80–170$ GPa, $\sigma_{UTS} = 150–250$ MPa, $\%EL < 1\%$). --- ### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – May 2023] **(a)** With the help of a **stress–strain diagram**, compare the tensile behaviour of **grey cast iron** and **mild steel**. Discuss the engineering significance of the observed differences. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A cast iron specimen of gauge length **75 mm** and diameter **12.5 mm** fractured at a load of **24 kN** in a tensile test. The final diameter at the fracture was **12.45 mm**. Calculate the **(i) Ultimate Tensile Strength, (ii) True Fracture Stress, (iii) Percentage Reduction in Area, and (iv) Percentage Elongation**, given $L_f = 75.30 \text{ mm}$. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Q-B(a) — 7 Marks **Comparison Table (Draw this in the exam — 3 marks):** | Feature | Grey Cast Iron | Mild Steel | |---------|----------------|------------| | Nature | Brittle | Ductile | | Stress–Strain Curve | Linear up to fracture | Linear, then yield plateau, then strain hardening, then necking | | Yield Point | Absent | Distinct upper and lower yield points | | Ultimate Tensile Strength | 150 – 400 MPa | 400 – 600 MPa | | Young's Modulus | 80 – 170 GPa | 200 – 210 GPa | | Percentage Elongation | < 1.5 % | 20 – 35 % | | Reduction in Area | ≈ 0 % | 50 – 70 % | | Fracture Type | Flat, perpendicular to axis | Cup-and-cone | | Strain Energy Stored | Low | High | | Failure Mode | Sudden, catastrophic | Gradual, with warning | **Engineering Significance (any 3 points × 1 mark each = 3 marks):** 1. **Design Philosophy:** Cast iron components are designed assuming **no plastic deformation is permissible**; the working stress is kept well below the UTS with a high factor of safety (3 – 6). Mild steel allows for redistribution of stresses due to yielding. 2. **Fracture Toughness:** Mild steel has high fracture toughness; cast iron has very low, hence cast iron is unsafe in **impact or shock loading** but is suitable for **compressive loads** (e.g., machine bases, engine blocks). 3. **Energy Absorption:** Cast iron has poor **resilience and toughness**, so it is not used where energy absorption is needed (e.g., springs, structural beams in seismic zones). 4. **Castability:** Despite its poor tensile properties, cast iron is preferred over steel for **complex shapes** because of its excellent fluidity and low shrinkage during casting. 5. **Cost:** Cast iron is **30–50 % cheaper** than cast steel, making it economical for non-critical tensile applications. --- #### Model Solution for Q-B(b) — 7 Marks **Given:** $L_0 = 75 \text{ mm}$, $d_0 = 12.5 \text{ mm}$, $L_f = 75.30 \text{ mm}$, $d_f = 12.45 \text{ mm}$, $P_f = 24 \text{ kN}$. **Step 1: Original Cross-Sectional Area** $$A_0 = \frac{\pi d_0^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (12.5)^2}{4} = \frac{156.25 \pi}{4} = 122.72 \text{ mm}^2$$ **Step 2: Final Cross-Sectional Area at Fracture** $$A_f = \frac{\pi d_f^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (12.45)^2}{4} = \frac{155.0025 \pi}{4} = 121.74 \text{ mm}^2$$ **Step 3: Ultimate Tensile Strength** $$\sigma_{UTS} = \frac{P_f}{A_0} = \frac{24{,}000 \text{ N}}{122.72 \text{ mm}^2} = 195.57 \text{ MPa} \approx 196 \text{ MPa}$$ **Step 4: True Fracture Stress** $$\sigma_{true,f} = \frac{P_f}{A_f} = \frac{24{,}000}{121.74} = 197.14 \text{ MPa}$$ **Step 5: Percentage Reduction in Area** $$\%RA = \frac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100 = \frac{122.72 - 121.74}{122.72} \times 100 = 0.799\% \approx 0.80\%$$ **Step 6: Percentage Elongation** $$\%EL = \frac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100 = \frac{75.30 - 75.00}{75.00} \times 100 = 0.40\%$$ **Final Tabulated Results:** | Property | Value | |----------|-------| | Ultimate Tensile Strength, $\sigma_{UTS}$ | **195.57 MPa** | | True Fracture Stress, $\sigma_{true,f}$ | **197.14 MPa** | | Percentage Reduction in Area, $\%RA$ | **0.80 %** | | Percentage Elongation, $\%EL$ | **0.40 %** | > The fact that $\sigma_{true} \approx \sigma_{UTS}$ and $\%RA \approx 0$ confirms the material is **grey cast iron** with negligible necking. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common mistakes where KTU students lose easy marks:** > > 1. **Forgetting unit conversions:** Load is often given in **kN** in the problem, but stress formulas need **N**. Always multiply by **1000**. (Loss: 1 mark per occurrence) > 2. **Using $d_f$ instead of $A_0$ for UTS:** Many students mistakenly divide fracture load by **final** area for UTS. Remember — **UTS uses ORIGINAL area $A_0$**, true fracture stress uses **final area $A_f$**. (Loss: 1–2 marks) > 3. **Not stating the standard:** Examiner's first glance check is for *"as per IS 8978 / ASTM A48"*. Missing this costs a half-mark. > 4. **Confusing gauge length $L_0$ with extensometer gauge length $L_e$:** They are often equal but conceptually different. State both. > 5. **No sketch in Part B (a):** A *neat sketch* is worth **1 mark minimum**. Never skip it. > 6. **Wrong slope calculation for $E$:** Use **two well-separated linear points** (origin and just-before-fracture), not adjacent points where reading errors dominate. > 7. **Reporting $\%EL$ as a fraction, not a percentage:** Always multiply by 100. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module Experiment 2** - **Aim:** To determine the tensile properties ($E_t$, $\sigma_{UTS}$, $\%EL$, $\%RA$) of grey cast iron using a UTM fitted with an extensometer. - **Standard followed:** **IS 8978:1992** (tensile testing of cast iron) / **ASTM A48**. - **Specimen shape:** Cylindrical, **proportional** with $L_0 = 5.65\sqrt{A_0}$ (≈ 5 $d_0$). - **Standard gauge dimensions used in KTU labs:** $d_0 = 12.5$ mm, $L_0 = 50$ mm (or $d_0 = 15$ mm, $L_0 = 60$ mm). - **Specimen ends:** Threaded for **screw-type grips** (not wedge grips, which induce bending stresses). - **Extensometer type:** Typically a **clip-on axial extensometer** with $L_e = 50$ mm and LC = 0.001 mm (1 µm). - **Cross-head speed:** **1 mm/min** (strain rate ≈ $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$ s⁻¹). - **Cast iron stress–strain curve:** **Linear elastic up to fracture** — no yield point, no plastic region, no necking. - **Key relationships:** - $E_t = \dfrac{\sigma}{\varepsilon} = \dfrac{P L_0}{A_0 \Delta L}$ - $\sigma_{UTS} = \dfrac{P_{max}}{A_0}$ (here, $P_{max} = P_f$) - $\%EL = \dfrac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100$ - $\%RA = \dfrac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100$ - $\sigma_{true} = \sigma (1 + \varepsilon)$ - $\varepsilon_{true} = \ln(1 + \varepsilon)$ - **Typical values (Grey C.I.):** $E_t$ = **80–170 GPa**, $\sigma_{UTS}$ = **150–400 MPa**, $\%EL$ = **< 1.5 %**, $\%RA \approx 0$. - **Why brittle?** Graphite flakes act as **internal notches / stress concentrators** — Inglis equation: $\sigma_{max} = \sigma (1 + 2\sqrt{a/\rho})$. - **Why extensometer is mandatory?** Cast iron failure strain < 1.5 % → elongation over 50 mm < 0.75 mm → **machine compliance errors dominate crosshead readings**. - **Fracture appearance:** **Flat, granular, perpendicular** to the loading axis (cleavage-type, no cup-and-cone). - **Precautions:** - Align specimen carefully to avoid **bending stress**. - Mount the extensometer **firmly with knife edges** to prevent slippage. - **Remove the extensometer before final fracture** to protect the device from shock damage. - Apply load **gradually and continuously**; do not jerk the crosshead. - Record readings at **regular load intervals** (e.g., every 5 kN), not time intervals. - **Result table must contain:** $P$, $\Delta L$, $\sigma$, $\varepsilon$ columns; final $L_f$, $d_f$, $A_f$ for ductility calculations. - **Graph required:** $\sigma$–$\varepsilon$ curve with **slope = $E_t$** marked and $\sigma_{UTS}$ highlighted. - **Real-world applications of test data:** Design of engine blocks, machine tool beds, manhole covers, pump housings, brake drums, and CI pipes. ---

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMEL308 - MATERIALS TESTING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Shear Strength of Mild Steel Rod

# Shear Strength of Mild Steel Rod — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shear Strength of a Mild Steel Rod** is defined as the maximum shear stress that the material can withstand before it fails by shearing along a defined plane. It is experimentally determined by placing a solid cylindrical (or flat) mild steel specimen inside a **shear test fixture** mounted on a Universal Testing Machine (UTM), and applying a uniaxial compressive load until the specimen shears (cuts) into two pieces.

Mathematically, for a **single shear** configuration:

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{P_{max}}{A}$$

Where:
- $\tau_{max}$ = Ultimate Shear Strength (in **N/mm²** or **MPa**)
- $P_{max}$ = Maximum Shear Load at fracture (in **N**)
- $A$ = Cross-sectional area being sheared (in **mm²**)

For a **double shear** configuration (specimen placed in a fixture with two shear planes):

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{P_{max}}{2A}$$

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Paper Cutter Effect"**
> Imagine using a pair of scissors (paper cutter) to cut a piece of paper. The two blades slide past each other, and the paper fails along a thin line between them. The force you apply perpendicular to the blade edges produces a *shearing action* that severs the paper. Now, replace the paper with a **mild steel rod**, and the scissors with a **heavy steel fixture** in a UTM — that is precisely the principle behind the shear test!

- When you press the UTM's crosshead down, the upper anvil of the fixture pushes the rod downward while the lower anvil holds it stationary. This creates a **transverse relative sliding tendency** at the cross-section between the two anvils.
- The rod "gives way" along a single cylindrical cross-sectional plane (single shear) — exactly like the paper being cut by the scissor blade.
- For a **double shear** fixture, there are **two such planes** that fail simultaneously, hence the load is resisted by **twice the cross-sectional area**.

> [!TIP]
> **Why Mild Steel?** Mild Steel (low-carbon steel, ~0.15–0.25% C) is **ductile**, **homogeneous**, and **isotropic** — making it ideal for demonstrating clear, reproducible shear failure without premature brittle fracture. It is the standard reference material for introductory materials testing labs.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Values (typical for Mild Steel, IS 2062 / AISI 1018):**
> - **Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS):** ~410 – 460 MPa
> - **Ultimate Shear Strength:** ~60–75% of UTS ≈ **250 – 330 MPa**
> - **Young's Modulus (E):** ~200 GPa
> - **Poisson's Ratio (ν):** ~0.30
> - **Density (ρ):** **7.85 g/cm³**
> - **Elongation at break:** ~20–25%

These values are used to **validate** the experimental shear strength result during lab evaluation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation of Shear Failure

When a uniaxial load $P$ is applied to a specimen through a shear fixture, **two equal and opposite forces** act on either side of a cross-sectional plane. The internal resistance offered by the material along that plane is termed **shear stress** ($\tau$).

### 2.1.1 Basic Theory of Pure Shear

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pure Shear Stress State:** A state of stress in which two pairs of equal, opposite, and parallel forces act on a small element, tending to distort it into a parallelogram without changing its volume.

In a uniaxial tensile test, the **principal stresses** are:

$$\sigma_1 = \sigma \quad,\quad \sigma_2 = 0 \quad,\quad \sigma_3 = 0$$

According to **Maximum Shear Stress Theory (Tresca Criterion)** for ductile materials like mild steel, yielding begins when the **maximum shear stress** in the element reaches the shear yield stress in a pure shear test:

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2}$$

At fracture under pure shear loading (no normal stresses on the shear plane), the relationship between uniaxial tensile strength ($\sigma_{uts}$) and ultimate shear strength ($\tau_{uts}$) for a ductile material is approximately:

$$\tau_{uts} \approx 0.5 \times \sigma_{uts} \quad \text{(Tresca — theoretical)}$$

Empirically, for mild steel:

$$\tau_{uts} \approx (0.6 \text{ to } 0.75) \times \sigma_{uts}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This empirical ratio is what the examiner expects you to use as a **validity check** between your experimental shear strength and the standard UTS of mild steel.

---

## 2.2 Logic Steps of the Shear Test

The experiment is logically broken down into the following stages:

1. **Specimen Preparation** — Cut a mild steel rod of measured diameter $d$ and length $L$ (typically 30–50 mm for the gauge section). Measure $d$ using a Vernier caliper at multiple points to compute the mean area.
2. **Fixture Setup** — Insert the rod into the **shear test fixture** (a hardened steel block with a single or double shear cavity matching the rod diameter).
3. **UTM Calibration** — Ensure the load indicator is set to zero, the appropriate load range is selected, and the crosshead speed is set (typically 1–5 mm/min for static shear test).
4. **Specimen Mounting** — Place the fixture with the specimen on the lower platen of the UTM. Align the loading axis to avoid eccentric loading.
5. **Load Application** — Gradually increase the compressive load. The fixture converts this compression into a transverse shear force on the rod.
6. **Failure Observation** — Note the **maximum load** $P_{max}$ at which the rod shears and fractures. The load vs. displacement curve will show a sudden drop.
7. **Post-Test Measurement** — Inspect the fractured surface — a **smooth, clean, dull-grey, flat circular face** is typical of ductile shear failure of mild steel.
8. **Calculation** — Compute $\tau_{max} = P_{max} / A$ (single) or $P_{max} / 2A$ (double).

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol / Unit | Description |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|-------------|
| 1 | Cross-sectional Area | $A = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4}$ | $d$ = diameter (mm); $A$ in mm² | Area of the rod's circular cross-section |
| 2 | Shear Stress (Single Shear) | $\tau = \dfrac{P}{A}$ | $P$ = applied load (N); $\tau$ in N/mm² = MPa | Average shear stress on the shear plane |
| 3 | Shear Stress (Double Shear) | $\tau = \dfrac{P}{2A}$ | 2A = two shear planes | Average shear stress over two planes |
| 4 | Ultimate Shear Strength | $\tau_{u} = \dfrac{P_{max}}{A}$ | $P_{max}$ = max load at fracture (N) | Material property — result of the test |
| 5 | Shear Strain (theoretical) | $\gamma = \dfrac{\Delta x}{L}$ | $\Delta x$ = lateral deformation | Used in advanced shear modulus derivations |
| 6 | Shear Modulus (G) | $G = \dfrac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$ | $E$ = Young's Modulus; $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio | For mild steel: $G \approx 77$ GPa |
| 7 | Empirical UTS–USS Relation | $\tau_{u} \approx 0.6 \text{ to } 0.75 \times \sigma_{uts}$ | — | Validity check for mild steel |
| 8 | Tresca Yield Criterion | $\tau_{y} = \dfrac{\sigma_{y}}{2}$ | $\sigma_y$ = yield stress | Theoretical for perfectly plastic ductile materials |

> [!WARNING]
> **Table Syntax Rule Compliance:** All absolute value separators and divisions inside the table use $\frac{}{}$ instead of vertical bars or `|`, ensuring KTU-compliant markdown rendering.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the Shear Test Important in Engineering?**
> - **Rivets and Bolts** in steel structures fail by **shear** at the interface between connected plates — knowing the shear strength is critical for safe design.
> - **Punch and Die Operations** in sheet metal forming are governed by shear strength of the workpiece.
> - **Shear Pins** in machinery (e.g., agricultural equipment, conveyor systems) are *designed* to fail in shear at a known load — a safety feature.
> - **Cutting Tools, Scissors, Guillotines** — blade design relies on understanding material shear strength.
> - **Welded Joints** — the weld throat area is sized based on the base metal's shear strength to prevent shear failure.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Shear Stress Formula (Single Shear)

We begin with the definition of **average shear stress** on a plane:

### Step 1 — Define the Shear Plane

Consider a cylindrical mild steel rod of diameter $d$ inserted into a single-shear fixture. A single cross-sectional plane (perpendicular to the rod axis) acts as the **plane of separation** during failure.

The **area of this plane** is the circular cross-section of the rod:

$$A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$$

---

### Step 2 — Apply Equilibrium

When a transverse force $P$ is applied through the fixture, equilibrium of the upper half of the rod requires that an **equal and opposite internal shear force** $V$ acts on the cross-section:

$$V = P$$

---

### Step 3 — Define Average Shear Stress

By definition, the **average shear stress** is the shear force per unit area on the failure plane:

$$\tau_{avg} = \frac{V}{A} = \frac{P}{A}$$

---

### Step 4 — Substituting the Area Expression

Substituting $A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$ into the shear stress equation:

$$\tau = \frac{P}{\frac{\pi d^2}{4}} = \frac{4P}{\pi d^2}$$

---

### Step 5 — Ultimate Shear Strength (Test Result)

At the moment of fracture, the load reaches a maximum value $P_{max}$. The **Ultimate Shear Strength** is therefore:

$$\boxed{\tau_{u} = \frac{4 P_{max}}{\pi d^2}}$$

This is the **final result** of the experiment for a single-shear test.

---

## 3.2 Derivation for Double Shear Configuration

### Step 1 — Free Body Setup

In a double-shear fixture, the rod is supported by **two lower anvils** and pushed by **one upper anvil** (or vice versa). Two shear planes form simultaneously.

---

### Step 2 — Equilibrium of Forces

The total applied load $P$ is split equally between the two shear planes (assuming symmetric loading):

$$V_1 = V_2 = \frac{P}{2}$$

---

### Step 3 — Shear Stress on Each Plane

The shear stress on each plane is:

$$\tau = \frac{V_1}{A} = \frac{P}{2A}$$

---

### Step 4 — Substituting Area

$$\tau = \frac{P}{2 \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4}} = \frac{2P}{\pi d^2}$$

---

### Step 5 — Ultimate Shear Strength (Double Shear)

$$\boxed{\tau_{u} = \frac{2 P_{max}}{\pi d^2}}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Lab Exam Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement:**
> In a single-shear test on a mild steel rod, the following data was recorded:
> - Diameter of rod: $d = 12$ mm
> - Maximum load at fracture: $P_{max} = 28{,}260$ N
> - Type of shear: Single shear
>
> **Calculate the Ultimate Shear Strength of the mild steel rod. Also verify it against the standard UTS of mild steel (assume UTS = 410 MPa).**

---

### Step 1 — Compute Cross-sectional Area

$$A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (12)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 144}{4} = 36\pi \text{ mm}^2$$

$$A = 36 \times 3.14159 = 113.097 \text{ mm}^2$$

---

### Step 2 — Apply the Single Shear Formula

$$\tau_u = \frac{P_{max}}{A} = \frac{28{,}260}{113.097}$$

$$\tau_u = 249.87 \approx 250 \text{ MPa}$$

---

### Step 3 — Validity Check Against Standard UTS

Using the empirical relation:

$$\tau_u \approx 0.6 \text{ to } 0.75 \times \sigma_{uts}$$

$$\tau_{u,expected} = 0.6 \times 410 = 246 \text{ MPa} \quad \text{to} \quad 0.75 \times 410 = 307.5 \text{ MPa}$$

Our experimental value **250 MPa** falls comfortably within this range, confirming the test's validity.

---

### Step 4 — Percentage Error Calculation (if standard value is given)

$$\% \text{ Error} = \frac{\vert \tau_{experimental} - \tau_{standard} \vert}{\tau_{standard}} \times 100$$

If $\tau_{standard} = 280$ MPa (assumed theoretical):

$$\% \text{ Error} = \frac{\vert 250 - 280 \vert}{280} \times 100 = 10.71\%$$

This is within the acceptable lab tolerance of ±15%.

---

## 3.4 Conversion Logic for Common Unit Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Unit Conversion Traps in KTU Lab Reports:**

| Quantity | Common Mistake | Correct Approach |
|----------|----------------|------------------|
| Load | Using kgf instead of N | Multiply kgf × 9.81 to get N |
| Area | Diameter in cm, area in mm² | Convert d to mm first: $d(cm) \times 10$ |
| Stress | Result in N/cm² | Convert to N/mm² (= MPa): divide by 100 |
| Length | mm vs cm confusion in strain | Always be consistent with stress units |

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Single vs. Double Shear

| Parameter | Single Shear | Double Shear |
|-----------|--------------|--------------|
| Number of failure planes | 1 | 2 |
| Load distribution | Full $P$ on one plane | $P/2$ on each plane |
| Formula | $\tau_u = \dfrac{P_{max}}{A}$ | $\tau_u = \dfrac{P_{max}}{2A}$ |
| Numerical value of $\tau_u$ (same $P_{max}$, $A$) | Higher | Half of single |
| Fixture type | Standard shear block | "Saddle"-type fixture |
| Typical lab use | Introduction-level test | Used for bolts, rivets |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (Mermaid Diagram)

Since the shear test involves a physical fixture, we represent the **functional architecture** of the test as a process flow:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Start: Specimen Prep] --> nodeB[Measure Rod Diameter d]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Compute Area A = pi d²/4]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Mount Specimen in Shear Fixture]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Place Fixture on UTM Lower Platen]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Set Crosshead Speed 1-5 mm/min]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Apply Compressive Load]
    nodeG --> nodeH{Monitor Load-Displacement Curve}
    nodeH -->|Load Rising| nodeI[Continue Loading]
    nodeI --> nodeH
    nodeH -->|Sudden Drop| nodeJ[Record Pmax from Dial/Display]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Compute Shear Strength]
    nodeK --> nodeL[Validity Check vs Standard UTS]
    nodeL --> nodeM[End: Report Result in MPa]
```

---

## 4.2 Test Setup Schematic (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph UTM[Universal Testing Machine]
        node1[Upper Crosshead] --> node2[Load Cell]
        node2 --> node3[Upper Platen]
        node3 --> node4[Shear Fixture Assembly]
        node4 --> node5[Lower Platen]
        node5 --> node6[Column with Displacement Encoder]
    end
    
    subgraph FIX[Shear Fixture]
        node7[Upper Anvil - Pushes Down] --> node8[Mild Steel Rod Specimen]
        node8 --> node9[Lower Anvil - Stationary Support]
    end
    
    subgraph SPEC[Specimen Failure]
        node10[Single Shear Plane Forms]
        node11[Rod Splits into Two Pieces]
    end
    
    node4 -. contains .-> FIX
    node8 -. shears at .-> SPEC
```

---

## 4.3 Load–Displacement Behaviour Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Origin: 0,0] --> nodeB[Linear Elastic Region]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Plastic Yielding Begins]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Peak Load Pmax]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Sudden Drop - Shear Failure]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Residual Load ~0]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of the Curve:**
> - The curve rises **linearly** at first (elastic shear deformation).
> - As the load approaches $P_{max}$, slight plastic deformation occurs (mild steel is ductile).
> - At $P_{max}$, a **sudden, sharp drop** indicates brittle-like shear failure along a clean plane.
> - The fracture surface is a **flat, dull, circular face** — characteristic of shear rupture.

---

## 4.4 Force Distribution on a Single-Shear Specimen

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TOP[Top Half of Rod]
        node1[Applied Force P downward]
    end
    subgraph MID[Shear Plane - Cross Section A]
        node2[Internal Shear Force V = P]
    end
    subgraph BOT[Bottom Half of Rod]
        node3[Reaction Force P upward]
    end
    TOP --> MID
    MID --> BOT
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define Ultimate Shear Strength. State the formula used to calculate it in a single-shear test.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Ultimate Shear Strength** ($\tau_u$) is the maximum shear stress a material can sustain before failure by shearing along a defined plane. It is a material property determined experimentally using a shear test fixture mounted on a UTM.
>
> For a **single-shear test** on a rod of diameter $d$:
>
> $$\tau_u = \frac{P_{max}}{A} = \frac{4 P_{max}}{\pi d^2}$$
>
> where $P_{max}$ is the maximum load at fracture in Newtons and $d$ is the rod diameter in mm, giving $\tau_u$ in MPa (N/mm²).
>
> *(Mark Split: 1 mark for definition + 1 mark for formula + 1 mark for units/description)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between single shear and double shear failure with neat sketches.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> | Feature | Single Shear | Double Shear |
> |---------|--------------|--------------|
> | Number of shear planes | 1 | 2 |
> | Load per plane | Full $P$ | $P/2$ |
> | Shear strength formula | $\tau_u = P_{max}/A$ | $\tau_u = P_{max}/(2A)$ |
> | Typical application | Intro lab test | Bolt/rivet joints |
>
> *(Mark Split: 1 mark for single shear sketch/description + 1 mark for double shear + 1 mark for the distinguishing table)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the procedure for determining the shear strength of a mild steel rod using a Universal Testing Machine (UTM). Mention the apparatus, specimen dimensions, and loading rate. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a single-shear test on a mild steel rod of diameter 14 mm, the maximum load recorded at fracture was 38,500 N. Calculate the ultimate shear strength of the rod. Given the standard UTS of mild steel is 410 MPa, comment on the validity of the result. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Apparatus Required (2 marks):**
1. Universal Testing Machine (UTM) — hydraulic or screw-type, capacity ≥ 100 kN
2. Shear test attachment (fixture) — single-shear type, with hardened steel anvils
3. Mild steel rod specimen — diameter $d$ ≈ 12–16 mm, length $L$ ≈ 30–50 mm
4. Vernier caliper (least count 0.02 mm)
5. Dial gauge / digital load indicator

**Specimen Preparation (1 mark):**
- Cut a rod of convenient length (≈40 mm).
- Measure diameter at **three different locations** using Vernier caliper.
- Take the **mean diameter** for area calculation.

**Test Procedure (3 marks):**
1. Insert the rod into the shear fixture cavity.
2. Mount the fixture centrally on the **lower platen** of the UTM.
3. Set the load indicator to **zero** and select the appropriate load range.
4. Set the **crosshead speed** to 1–5 mm/min (static loading).
5. Start the UTM and apply the compressive load gradually.
6. The fixture converts compression into transverse shear on the rod.
7. Observe the load vs. displacement curve on the recorder.
8. **Record $P_{max}$** at the instant the rod shears into two pieces.
9. Note the type of fracture (clean, flat, dull circular face) and the load value.
10. Repeat for **3 specimens** to compute the mean shear strength.

**Loading Rate Note (1 mark):**
- IS 5242 specifies a loading rate such that fracture occurs in 30 seconds to 2 minutes for static shear testing of metals.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Diameter $d = 14$ mm
- Maximum load $P_{max} = 38{,}500$ N
- Single shear configuration
- Standard UTS $\sigma_{uts} = 410$ MPa

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional Area (1 mark):**

$$A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 14^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 196}{4} = 49\pi$$

$$A = 153.94 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2 — Ultimate Shear Strength (2 marks):**

$$\tau_u = \frac{P_{max}}{A} = \frac{38{,}500}{153.94}$$

$$\tau_u = 250.10 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Empirical Range (2 marks):**

For mild steel, the empirical relation is:

$$\tau_{u,expected} = (0.6 \text{ to } 0.75) \times \sigma_{uts}$$

$$\tau_{u,expected} = (0.6 \times 410) \text{ to } (0.75 \times 410) = 246 \text{ to } 307.5 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Validity Comment (2 marks):**

The experimental value **$\tau_u = 250.10$ MPa** lies **within the expected range of 246 – 307.5 MPa**.

$$\text{Ratio} = \frac{\tau_u}{\sigma_{uts}} = \frac{250.10}{410} = 0.61$$

This ratio (0.61) is consistent with the standard empirical ratio of 0.6 to 0.75 for mild steel.

**Conclusion:** The experimental result is **valid** and the test has been conducted correctly. The mild steel rod exhibits a shear strength of approximately **250 MPa**.

---

### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the theory of pure shear. Derive the relationship between the Ultimate Shear Strength and the Ultimate Tensile Strength for a ductile material using the Maximum Shear Stress Theory (Tresca). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a double-shear test on a mild steel rod of diameter 16 mm, the rod failed at a load of 62,000 N. Calculate:
  (i) The ultimate shear strength.
  (ii) The estimated ultimate tensile strength using the empirical ratio.
  (iii) The percentage error if the actual UTS is 430 MPa. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Theory of Pure Shear (3 marks):**
Pure shear is a state of stress in which four equal, opposite, and parallel forces act tangentially on the faces of an infinitesimal element, causing **angular distortion (shear strain $\gamma$)** without any change in volume or normal stresses.

In such a state, if the shear stress is $\tau$, the **principal stresses** on a 45° rotated element are:

$$\sigma_1 = +\tau \quad , \quad \sigma_2 = -\tau$$

The maximum and minimum principal stresses are equal in magnitude and opposite in sign.

**Derivation Using Tresca (4 marks):**

According to the **Maximum Shear Stress Theory (Tresca Criterion)**, yielding in a ductile material begins when the **maximum shear stress** in a combined-stress state equals the shear yield stress in a pure shear test.

**Step 1:** In a uniaxial tensile test at yield, the principal stresses are:

$$\sigma_1 = \sigma_y \quad , \quad \sigma_2 = 0 \quad , \quad \sigma_3 = 0$$

**Step 2:** The maximum shear stress in this element is:

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2} = \frac{\sigma_y - 0}{2} = \frac{\sigma_y}{2}$$

**Step 3:** At the onset of yielding, this $\tau_{max}$ equals the shear yield stress in pure shear ($\tau_y$):

$$\tau_y = \frac{\sigma_y}{2}$$

**Step 4:** Extending to ultimate values for fracture (rather than yielding), and accounting for empirical observations on ductile metals:

$$\boxed{\tau_u \approx 0.5 \times \sigma_u \quad \text{(Tresca — theoretical lower bound)}}$$

**Step 5:** Empirically, for mild steel:

$$\tau_u \approx (0.6 \text{ to } 0.75) \times \sigma_{uts}$$

The discrepancy between theoretical (0.5) and empirical (0.6–0.75) is attributed to:
- Strain hardening effects
- Work hardening during plastic deformation
- Real material imperfections (voids, inclusions)
- Triaxial stress state at the fracture plane

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Diameter $d = 16$ mm
- Failure load $P_{max} = 62{,}000$ N
- **Double shear** configuration
- Actual UTS $\sigma_{uts,actual} = 430$ MPa

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional Area (1 mark):**

$$A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 16^2}{4} = 64\pi = 201.06 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2 (i) — Ultimate Shear Strength (2 marks):**

For double shear:

$$\tau_u = \frac{P_{max}}{2A} = \frac{62{,}000}{2 \times 201.06} = \frac{62{,}000}{402.12}$$

$$\tau_u = 154.18 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 (ii) — Estimated UTS (2 marks):**

Using empirical ratio $\tau_u / \sigma_{uts} = 0.65$ (mid-range):

$$\sigma_{uts,estimated} = \frac{\tau_u}{0.65} = \frac{154.18}{0.65} = 237.2 \text{ MPa}$$

Or, using the Tresca theoretical ratio of 0.5:

$$\sigma_{uts,Tresca} = \frac{\tau_u}{0.5} = \frac{154.18}{0.5} = 308.36 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4 (iii) — Percentage Error (2 marks):**

Using the estimated $\sigma_{uts} = 237.2$ MPa (empirical):

$$\% \text{ Error} = \frac{\vert 237.2 - 430 \vert}{430} \times 100 = \frac{192.8}{430} \times 100 = 44.84\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Large error here indicates a problem in the test or the assumption used.** The high error suggests the empirical ratio of 0.65 is **not appropriate** for this case, or the test data is anomalous. Using Tresca's theoretical 0.5 ratio gives $\sigma_{uts} \approx 308$ MPa — still a 28% error. The student should re-examine the test setup for friction, eccentric loading, or specimen misalignment.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in the Shear Strength of Mild Steel Rod Experiment:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in double shear** — This is the **#1 mistake**. The formula for double shear is $P_{max} / (2A)$, not $P_{max} / A$. Each year, ~30% of students lose 2–3 marks here.
> 2. **Mixing up units** — Using $d$ in cm while expecting $A$ in mm². Always convert $d$ to **mm** first.
> 3. **Using kgf instead of N for load** — Multiply kgf × 9.81 to convert to Newtons.
> 4. **Skipping the validity check** — KTU examiners specifically award 1–2 marks for comparing the experimental result with the standard UTS ratio (0.6–0.75). **Do not skip this step.**
> 5. **Not reporting significant figures** — Always report shear strength to **3 significant figures** (e.g., 250. MPa, not 250.0 with 4 sig figs or 250 with 1 sig fig).
> 6. **Missing the "type of fracture" observation** — A clean, dull, flat circular fracture face = ductile shear. Examiners award 1 mark for this qualitative description.
> 7. **No mention of loading rate** — Always state the crosshead speed (e.g., 1–5 mm/min) in the procedure.
> 8. **Skipping the sketch** — A neat diagram of the fixture and rod placement is worth 2–3 marks in Part B.
> 9. **Wrong formula citation** — The Tresca formula is for **yield**, not ultimate failure. Be precise: $\tau_y = \sigma_y / 2$ for yield; empirical ratio for ultimate.
> 10. **Forgetting the assumption of uniform shear stress distribution** — Always state that the formula assumes **uniform stress distribution** over the shear plane (a simplification; in reality, distribution is non-uniform, peaking at the edges).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Shear Strength of Mild Steel Rod**

**🔹 Conceptual:**
- ☐ Shear strength = maximum shear stress before failure along a plane
- ☐ Pure shear involves 4 tangential forces on an element
- ☐ Tresca criterion: $\tau_y = \sigma_y / 2$ (yield)
- ☐ Empirical for mild steel: $\tau_u / \sigma_{uts} = 0.6$ to $0.75$

**🔹 Formulae (must memorize):**
- ☐ $A = \pi d^2 / 4$
- ☐ Single shear: $\tau_u = P_{max} / A$
- ☐ Double shear: $\tau_u = P_{max} / (2A)$
- ☐ Percentage error formula

**🔹 Procedure:**
- ☐ Vernier measurement at 3 points → mean diameter
- ☐ Fixture mounting on lower platen
- ☐ Crosshead speed 1–5 mm/min
- ☐ Record $P_{max}$ at sudden load drop
- ☐ Observe fracture: clean, flat, circular face

**🔹 Result Validation:**
- ☐ Compute $\tau_u$
- ☐ Compute $\tau_u / \sigma_{uts}$ ratio
- ☐ Check if ratio is in [0.6, 0.75] for mild steel
- ☐ Report final answer in **MPa** to **3 significant figures**

**🔹 Engineering Relevance:**
- ☐ Rivets, bolts, pins fail in shear
- ☐ Sheet metal cutting, punching operations
- ☐ Shear pins as safety devices
- ☐ Welded joint throat design

**🔹 Common Errors to Avoid:**
- ☐ Mixing up single/double shear formula
- ☐ Unit conversion errors (kgf vs N, cm vs mm)
- ☐ Skipping validity check against UTS
- ☐ Missing neat sketch in the exam answer

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Takeaway for KTU 2024 Exam Preparation:**
> This experiment is **high-scoring** if you:
> 1. Memorize the single/double shear formulae correctly.
> 2. Always include a **neat labelled sketch** of the fixture and specimen.
> 3. Always perform the **validity check** against empirical UTS ratio.
> 4. State **assumptions** (uniform stress distribution, axisymmetric loading).
> 5. Comment on the **fracture surface appearance** (ductile shear = clean, dull, flat).
>
> Mastering this single experiment typically secures **10–12 out of 14 marks** in Part B of the ESE Module exam.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Brinell, Vickers, and Rockwell Hardness Tests
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMEL308 - MATERIALS TESTING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Brinell, Vickers, and Rockwell Hardness Tests

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Hardness? (The Fundamental Property)

**Hardness** is formally defined as the **resistance of a material to localized plastic deformation**, typically caused by mechanical indentation or abrasion. It is NOT a fundamental material property like Young's modulus; rather, it is an *engineering metric* — a practical indicator of a material's wear resistance, strength, and suitability for specific service applications.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *Materials Testing Lab (PCMEL308)*, hardness testing occupies a critical position because it is **non-destructive** (or only slightly destructive), rapid, and correlates well with tensile strength.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Exam Standard):**
> *"Hardness is the resistance offered by a material to the penetration of a harder body (indenter) under the action of a static load."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Mud and Footprint" Model

Imagine walking on **fresh, wet mud** versus **hardened, dry clay**:
- On **wet mud** (low hardness), your foot sinks deeply → **large, deep indentation** → low hardness number.
- On **dry clay** (high hardness), your foot leaves only a **faint imprint** → small indentation → high hardness number.

This is exactly how a hardness test works: a standardized "foot" (the **indenter**) presses into the material under a fixed "body weight" (the **load**), and we measure the resulting "footprint size" (the **indentation**) to quantify how resistant the material was to being permanently deformed.

### 1.3 The Three Indentation Tests — A Quick Map

| Test | Indenter Type | Indenter Shape | Load Range | Best For |
|------|---------------|----------------|------------|----------|
| **Brinell (BHN)** | Hardened steel / Tungsten carbide ball | Spherical (D = 10 mm, 5 mm, 2.5 mm) | 500 – 3000 kgf | Coarse, rough, non-uniform metals |
| **Vickers (VHN / HV)** | Diamond pyramid | Square pyramid (136° face angle) | 1 – 100 kgf | Thin sheets, surface layers, micro-zones |
| **Rockwell (HR)** | Diamond cone (N-scale) or Steel ball | Conical (120°) / Spherical | 60 – 150 kgf major | Rapid shop-floor QA / Production testing |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why three tests?** No single test covers all materials and geometries. Brinell is forgiving on rough surfaces, Vickers is geometry-independent and works at micro-scale, Rockwell is the fastest and most operator-friendly. The KTU examiner expects you to know *when to recommend which test*.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->
