<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Brinell Hardness Test (BHN) — The Original Workhorse

**Invented by Johan August Brinell in 1900** — the oldest standardized indentation hardness test still in industrial use.

#### 2.1.1 Principle
A **hardened steel ball** (or tungsten carbide ball for harder materials) of diameter **D** is pressed into a flat, ground surface of the test specimen under a known static load **P** for a specified dwell time (typically **10 – 15 seconds** for ferrous metals, **30 seconds** for soft metals).

#### 2.1.2 Procedure (Step-by-Step Logic)
1. **Surface Preparation**: The specimen surface must be flat, smooth, and free of oxides/scale. (Why? → A rough surface scatters the indentation edges, giving false readings.)
2. **Load Selection**: Per **IS 1500:2005 / ASTM E10**, the load is chosen so that:
   - Diameter of indentation $d$ lies between **0.25 D and 0.5 D** (Bregman–Bairstow condition for self-similarity of strain).
   - This range ensures consistent strain distribution independent of load magnitude.
3. **Load Application**: Load is applied **gradually** (without shock) and held for the dwell time.
4. **Indentation Measurement**: After load removal, the diameter **$d$** of the circular indentation is measured in **two perpendicular directions** using a **Brinell microscope** (graduated to 0.01 mm). The average of the two readings is used.
5. **Hardness Calculation**: Apply the BHN formula.

#### 2.1.3 The Brinell Formula (Derivation via Spherical Cap Geometry)

When a ball of diameter $D$ creates an indentation of diameter $d$ and depth $h$:

$$h = \frac{D}{2} - \sqrt{\left(\frac{D}{2}\right)^2 - \left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^2}$$

The **curved surface area** of the spherical cap (which is the actual contact area resisting indentation) is:

$$A = \pi D h$$

Substituting $h$ and simplifying yields the **classical Brinell Hardness Number (BHN)**:

$$\boxed{\text{BHN} = \frac{2P}{\pi D \left[ D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2} \right]}}$$

Where:
- $P$ = applied load (kgf)
- $D$ = ball diameter (mm)
- $d$ = measured indentation diameter (mm)
- BHN has units of **kgf/mm²** (although dimensionless in SI, it is conventionally expressed this way).

#### 2.1.4 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Foundry quality control** for cast iron and steel castings.
- **Empirical correlation** with ultimate tensile strength:
  $$\sigma_u \approx 0.35 \times \text{BHN} \quad \text{(for steels in MPa, with BHN in kgf/mm²)}$$
- Quick assessment of **annealed vs. hardened** states in heat-treatment shops.

---

### 2.2 The Vickers Hardness Test (VHN / HV) — The Precision Pyramid

**Standardized in 1925 by Smith and Sandland** at Vickers-Armstrong Ltd.

#### 2.2.1 Principle
A **square-based diamond pyramid indenter** with a face angle of **136°** between opposite faces is pressed into the specimen under a known load.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why 136°?** This angle was chosen so that Vickers hardness numbers **numerically approximate Brinell numbers** for loads above ~30 kgf, while also allowing a single indenter geometry to be used for all loads (geometric similarity principle).

#### 2.2.2 Procedure
1. Mount the specimen on the anvil of the Vickers hardness testing machine.
2. Apply the chosen load (1, 3, 5, 10, 20, 30, 50, or 100 kgf) smoothly.
3. Hold for **10 – 15 seconds** (dwell time, per **IS 1501:2002 / ASTM E92**).
4. Remove load; the indentation appears as a **square with diagonal impressions**.
5. Measure **both diagonals** ($d_1$ and $d_2$) using the measuring microscope.
6. Compute the **average diagonal** $d = \frac{d_1 + d_2}{2}$.
7. Calculate HV.

#### 2.2.3 The Vickers Formula

The contact area of a square pyramid (with face angle $\theta = 136°$, so half-angle $\alpha = 68°$) and average diagonal $d$ is:

$$A = \frac{d^2}{2 \sin(136°/2)} = \frac{d^2}{1.8544}$$

Hence:

$$\boxed{\text{HV} = \frac{1.8544 \, P}{d^2} \approx \frac{1.854 \, P}{d^2}}$$

Where:
- $P$ = applied load (kgf)
- $d$ = average diagonal of indentation (mm)
- HV = **kgf/mm²**

The constant **1.854** is universally tabulated and comes from the geometric relation $\frac{1}{2 \sin 68°}$.

---

### 2.3 The Rockwell Hardness Test (HR) — The Production Speedster

**Invented by Stanley P. Rockwell in 1919** — the most widely used hardness test worldwide due to its **speed** and **direct digital readout**.

#### 2.3.1 Principle (Two-Stage Loading — The Critical Innovation)

Unlike Brinell and Vickers, Rockwell uses a **two-stage loading sequence**:

1. **Minor Load (Preliminary Load)**: A small load of **10 kgf** is first applied. This:
   - Seats the indenter firmly on the surface.
   - Eliminates the effect of surface roughness, scale, and minor misalignment.
   - Establishes a **reference zero depth** $h_0$.

2. **Major Load (Total Load)**: The full load (60, 100, or 150 kgf depending on scale) is then applied.

3. **Minor Load Re-applied**: The major load is removed *while the minor load is still acting*. This recovers the elastic deformation, leaving only the **permanent plastic indentation depth** $h$.

4. **Measurement**: The **difference in penetration depth** $e = h - h_0$ is measured directly by a **depth-measuring dial gauge** (graduated to 0.001 mm per division).

#### 2.3.2 The Rockwell Formula

$$\boxed{\text{HR} = N - \frac{h}{0.002}}$$

Where:
- $h$ = permanent depth increase (in mm) due to major load
- $N$ = scale constant (**100** for diamond cone scales A, C, D; **130** for ball scales B, E, F, G, H, K)
- 0.002 mm = unit depth per HR number (i.e., one Rockwell point = 0.002 mm of penetration)
- HR is **dimensionless**

#### 2.3.3 The Common Rockwell Scales

| Scale | Indenter | Major Load (kgf) | Total Load (kgf) | Constant N | Typical Application |
|-------|----------|------------------|------------------|------------|---------------------|
| **A** | Diamond cone | 60 | 60 | 100 | Very hard materials (e.g., cemented carbides, thin steel) |
| **B** | 1/16" steel ball | 90 | 100 | 130 | Soft steels, brass, aluminium alloys (HRB < 100) |
| **C** | Diamond cone | 140 | 150 | 100 | Hardened steels, HRC 20 – 70 (most common in industry) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall**: A student often reports "Rockwell hardness = 60" without specifying the scale. **Always report as HRC 60, HRB 80, HRA 85**, etc. The number without a scale letter is meaningless and will lose you marks.

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula # | Equation | Where Used | Units |
|-----------|----------|-----------|-------|
| 1 | $\text{BHN} = \dfrac{2P}{\pi D \left[ D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2} \right]}$ | Brinell test | $P$ in kgf, $D, d$ in mm → BHN in kgf/mm² |
| 2 | Indentation depth: $h = \dfrac{D}{2}\left[1 - \sqrt{1 - (d/D)^2}\right]$ | Brinell geometry | mm |
| 3 | $\sigma_u \approx 0.35 \times \text{BHN}$ | UTS from BHN (steels) | MPa |
| 4 | $\text{HV} = \dfrac{1.854 \, P}{d^2}$ | Vickers test | $P$ in kgf, $d$ in mm → HV in kgf/mm² |
| 5 | Microhardness: $\text{HV} = \dfrac{0.1891 \, F}{d^2}$ | Vickers microhardness (load F in N) | $F$ in N, $d$ in μm → HV dimensionless |
| 6 | $\text{HR} = N - \dfrac{h}{0.002}$ | Rockwell test | $h$ in mm, HR dimensionless |
| 7 | Brinell load formula: $P = \text{constant} \cdot D^2$ | Load selection (e.g., $P = 30 D^2$ for steel, $P = 10 D^2$ for Al) | kgf |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Test Condition (Bregman–Bairstow Rule)** for valid Brinell measurements:
> $$0.25 \, D \le d \le 0.5 \, D$$
> If $d$ falls outside this range, the test is **invalid** and must be repeated with a different load.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Problems

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation of the Brinell Hardness Formula

**Starting from the geometry of a spherical cap indentation:**

Let a ball of diameter $D$ press into a flat surface, producing a circular impression of diameter $d$. The indentation has depth $h$, forming a spherical cap.

#### Step 1: Geometry of the Spherical Cap

The radius of the ball is $R = D/2$. The radius of the indentation circle is $r = d/2$. Using the Pythagorean theorem on the cross-section:

$$\left(\frac{D}{2}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{D}{2} - h\right)^2 + \left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^2$$

Expanding the right-hand side:

$$\frac{D^2}{4} = \frac{D^2}{4} - D h + h^2 + \frac{d^2}{4}$$

Canceling $\frac{D^2}{4}$:

$$0 = -D h + h^2 + \frac{d^2}{4}$$

Solving for $h$ (using $h \ll D$, so $h^2$ is negligible — first-order approximation):

$$h \approx \frac{d^2}{4D}$$

> For greater accuracy (no approximation), the exact relation is:
> $$h = \frac{D}{2} - \sqrt{\left(\frac{D}{2}\right)^2 - \left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^2}$$

#### Step 2: Lateral Surface Area of the Spherical Cap

The contact area resisting indentation is **not** the projected circle area ($\pi d^2 / 4$) — it is the **curved spherical surface**. For a spherical cap:

$$A = \pi D h$$

(where $D$ is the diameter of the sphere, not the radius — this is the standard cap-area formula.)

#### Step 3: Definition of Brinell Hardness

$$\text{BHN} = \frac{\text{Applied Load}}{\text{Contact Area}} = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{P}{\pi D h}$$

#### Step 4: Substitute $h$

Using the exact expression for $h$:

$$\text{BHN} = \frac{P}{\pi D \cdot \left[ \frac{D}{2} - \sqrt{\frac{D^2}{4} - \frac{d^2}{4}} \right]}$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by 2:

$$\text{BHN} = \frac{2P}{\pi D \left[ D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2} \right]}$$

This is the standard Brinell hardness equation. ✅ **Derivation complete.**

---

### 3.2 Detailed Derivation of the Vickers Hardness Constant (1.854)

A square-pyramid diamond indenter has a face angle $\theta = 136°$ between opposite faces.

The half-angle (between the indenter axis and a face) is $\theta/2 = 68°$.

If $d$ is the diagonal of the square indentation, then the half-diagonal is $d/2$.

The **slant height** (from the apex of the pyramid to a corner of the base, along a face) is:

$$\ell = \frac{d/2}{\sin(68°)}$$

The area of the **triangular face** with base $d$ and slant height $\ell$:

$$A_{\text{face}} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot d \cdot \ell = \frac{d^2}{4 \sin(68°)}$$

There are 4 such faces:

$$A_{\text{total}} = \frac{d^2}{\sin(68°)}$$

But since $\theta = 136°$ is the angle **between opposite faces** (not adjacent faces), the contact area formula uses $\sin(\theta/2) = \sin(68°)$:

$$A = \frac{d^2}{2 \sin(\theta/2)} = \frac{d^2}{2 \sin(68°)}$$

Since $\sin(68°) \approx 0.9272$:

$$A = \frac{d^2}{2 \times 0.9272} = \frac{d^2}{1.8544}$$

Therefore:

$$\text{HV} = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{1.8544 \, P}{d^2} \approx \frac{1.854 \, P}{d^2} \quad \blacksquare$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem — KTU Board Pattern (BHN)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style Question]**
> In a Brinell hardness test on a mild steel specimen, a load of **3000 kgf** is applied through a **10 mm diameter** hardened steel ball. The indentation diameter is measured as **4.5 mm**. Calculate:
> (a) The Brinell Hardness Number (BHN).
> (b) The approximate ultimate tensile strength of the steel.
> (c) Comment on whether the indentation geometry is valid.

#### Solution:

**Given:**
- $P = 3000$ kgf
- $D = 10$ mm
- $d = 4.5$ mm

**Part (a): BHN**

$$\text{BHN} = \frac{2 \times 3000}{\pi \times 10 \times \left[ 10 - \sqrt{10^2 - 4.5^2} \right]}$$

Compute the term inside the brackets:

$$10^2 - 4.5^2 = 100 - 20.25 = 79.75$$

$$\sqrt{79.75} = 8.9306 \text{ mm}$$

$$10 - 8.9306 = 1.0694 \text{ mm}$$

$$\pi \times 10 \times 1.0694 = 33.595$$

$$\text{BHN} = \frac{6000}{33.595} = 178.6 \text{ kgf/mm}^2$$

**Part (b): Ultimate Tensile Strength**

$$\sigma_u = 0.35 \times \text{BHN} = 0.35 \times 178.6 = 62.5 \text{ MPa}$$

**Part (c): Validity Check (Bregman–Bairstow)**

$$0.25 D = 0.25 \times 10 = 2.5 \text{ mm}$$

$$0.5 D = 0.5 \times 10 = 5.0 \text{ mm}$$

Since $2.5 \le 4.5 \le 5.0$, the test result is **VALID** ✅.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Problem — KTU Board Pattern (Vickers)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style Question]**
> A Vickers hardness test is performed on a polished steel specimen using a load of **30 kgf**. The diagonals of the indentation are measured as **0.495 mm** and **0.505 mm**. Calculate the Vickers Hardness Number.

#### Solution:

**Given:**
- $P = 30$ kgf
- $d_1 = 0.495$ mm, $d_2 = 0.505$ mm

**Average diagonal:**

$$d = \frac{d_1 + d_2}{2} = \frac{0.495 + 0.505}{2} = \frac{1.000}{2} = 0.500 \text{ mm}$$

**Vickers Hardness Number:**

$$\text{HV} = \frac{1.854 \times P}{d^2} = \frac{1.854 \times 30}{(0.500)^2}$$

$$= \frac{55.62}{0.25} = 222.5 \text{ kgf/mm}^2$$

$$\boxed{\text{HV} \approx 223 \text{ kgf/mm}^2}$$

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Problem — KTU Board Pattern (Rockwell)

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Style Question]**
> In a Rockwell hardness test on the **C scale** (diamond cone, 150 kgf total load), the permanent penetration depth after load removal is found to be **0.120 mm**. Calculate the Rockwell C hardness number.

#### Solution:

**Given:**
- Scale: C (diamond cone, $N = 100$)
- Permanent depth $h = 0.120$ mm

**Applying the Rockwell formula:**

$$\text{HRC} = N - \frac{h}{0.002}$$

$$\text{HRC} = 100 - \frac{0.120}{0.002}$$

$$= 100 - 60$$

$$\boxed{\text{HRC} = 40}$$

**Interpretation:** This material has a hardness equivalent to a medium-hardened steel suitable for gears, axles, and structural components.

---

### 3.6 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Brinell vs. Vickers vs. Rockwell

| Parameter | Brinell (BHN) | Vickers (HV) | Rockwell (HR) |
|-----------|---------------|--------------|---------------|
| Indenter | Steel / WC ball | Diamond pyramid | Diamond cone or steel ball |
| Geometry | Spherical, $D$ varies | Square pyramid, fixed | Cone 120° / Ball |
| Load | High (500–3000 kgf) | Any (1–100 kgf) | Medium (60–150 kgf) |
| Measured Quantity | Indentation diameter $d$ | Diagonal length $d$ | Penetration depth $h$ |
| Calculated Quantity | $\text{BHN} = f(P, D, d)$ | $\text{HV} = f(P, d)$ | $\text{HR} = N - h/0.002$ |
| Specimen Finish | Coarse OK | Polished required | Moderate (roughness matters less due to pre-load) |
| Test Time | ~30 s | ~20 s | ~10 s |
| Suitable For | Castings, forgings, rough stock | Thin sheets, coatings, micro-zones | Production QA, hardened parts |
| Bregman Validity | $0.25D \le d \le 0.5D$ | Always valid (geometric similarity) | Always valid (depth-based) |
| Operator Skill | Moderate | High (microscope) | Low (direct dial) |
| Indentation Visibility | Large, easy | Small, requires scope | Not directly visible |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Brinell Test — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Mount Specimen] --> B[Apply Minor Seating Force]
    B --> C[Select Ball Indenter D = 10mm]
    C --> D[Apply Major Load P via Hydraulic Lever]
    D --> E[Hold for Dwell Time 10-30 sec]
    E --> F[Release Load Slowly]
    F --> G[Remove Specimen]
    G --> H[Measure Indentation Diameter d1]
    G --> I[Measure Indentation Diameter d2]
    H --> J[Compute Average d]
    I --> J
    J --> K{Is 0.25D less than d less than 0.5D?}
    K -->|Yes| L[Compute BHN using formula]
    K -->|No| M[Test Invalid - Repeat with New Load]
    L --> N[Report BHN kgf per mm squared]
    M --> D
```

### 4.2 Vickers Test — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Polish Specimen Surface] --> B[Place on Anvil]
    B --> C[Select Load P 1 to 100 kgf]
    C --> D[Apply Load Smoothly via Lever]
    D --> E[Hold Dwell Time 10-15 sec]
    E --> F[Remove Load]
    F --> G[Indentation Square Pyramid Mark Visible]
    G --> H[Measure Diagonal d1 with Microscope]
    G --> I[Measure Diagonal d2 with Microscope]
    H --> J[Average Diagonal d]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Apply HV formula 1.854 P over d squared]
    K --> L[Report HV value]
    L --> M{Is Specimen Thin or Coated?}
    M -->|Yes| N[Use Microhardness Mode Low Load]
    M -->|No| O[Test Complete]
    N --> C
```

### 4.3 Rockwell Test — Two-Stage Loading Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Place Specimen on Anvil] --> B[Apply Minor Load 10 kgf]
    B --> C[Set Dial Gauge to Zero Reference]
    C --> D{Which Scale?}
    D -->|Hard Steels| E[Use C Scale: Diamond Cone, 150 kgf]
    D -->|Soft Metals| F[Use B Scale: Steel Ball, 100 kgf]
    E --> G[Apply Major Load 140 kgf]
    F --> H[Apply Major Load 90 kgf]
    G --> I[Hold for Dwell Time]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Remove Major Load - Keep Minor Load]
    J --> K[Read Permanent Depth h from Dial]
    K --> L[Compute HR using N minus h over 0.002]
    L --> M[Report HRC or HRB value]
```

### 4.4 Indenter Geometry Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Brinell_Indenter
        B1[Hardened Steel Ball] --> B2[Diameter D 10mm typical]
    end
    subgraph Vickers_Indenter
        V1[Diamond Pyramid] --> V2[Face Angle 136 degrees]
    end
    subgraph Rockwell_Indenter
        R1[Diamond Cone 120 degrees] --> R2[Or Steel Ball 1/16 inch]
    end
```

### 4.5 Specimen Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[Material to be Tested?] --> R{What is the Hardness Range?}
    R -->|Soft BHN less than 200| S[Use Brinell Test]
    R -->|Hard HRC 20 to 70| T[Use Rockwell C Scale]
    R -->|Thin Section or Coating| U[Use Vickers or Microhardness]
    R -->|Rapid QA needed| V[Use Rockwell for Speed]
    Q2[Surface Condition?] --> S2{Rough or Machined?}
    S2 -->|Rough Cast Surface| S3[Prefer Brinell - Forgiving]
    S2 -->|Polished Lab Sample| S4[Vickers is Ideal]
    S2 -->|Production Part| S5[Rockwell - No Special Prep]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks Total)

---

#### Question A1 (3 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
> *"Differentiate between Brinell and Vickers hardness testing methods in terms of indenter, load, and typical applications."*

**Model Answer (3 key points expected by KTU examiner):**

| Feature | Brinell | Vickers |
|---------|---------|---------|
| **Indenter** | Hardened steel / WC ball (spherical) | Diamond pyramid (square-based, 136° face angle) |
| **Load** | 500–3000 kgf | 1–100 kgf |
| **Measured Quantity** | Diameter $d$ of indentation | Average diagonal $d$ of indentation |
| **Formula** | $\text{BHN} = \dfrac{2P}{\pi D[D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2}]}$ | $\text{HV} = \dfrac{1.854 \, P}{d^2}$ |
| **Application** | Coarse, rough surfaces, castings | Thin sections, microhardness, surface coatings |
| **Validity Rule** | $0.25D \le d \le 0.5D$ | Geometrically self-similar at all loads |

(Any 3 valid differences = 3 marks; full table = bonus credit for analytical depth.)

---

#### Question A2 (3 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
> *"Why is a minor (preliminary) load applied in the Rockwell hardness test, but not in the Brinell or Vickers tests?"*

**Model Answer:**

The minor load of **10 kgf** in the Rockwell test serves three critical functions that Brinell/Vickers do not require:

1. **Surface Penetration**: It breaks through any oxide scale, oil film, or surface roughness, ensuring the indenter contacts the true base metal.
2. **Establishes a Reference Zero**: It sets a stable baseline depth, against which the additional penetration caused by the major load is measured. This eliminates errors from surface irregularities and minor elastic deflection of the testing machine frame.
3. **Eliminates Elastic Recovery Effects**: When the major load is removed, elastic recovery is automatically compensated, and only permanent plastic depth is recorded.

In contrast, Brinell and Vickers measure the **indentation size directly** after full load removal — they do not need a baseline reference because the geometry of the indentation itself encodes the hardness information.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions — Module Internal Choice (2 × 14 = 28 Marks)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - Sample Paper 2024, Module 2 Choice]**
> **(a)** Derive the Brinell hardness formula in detail, clearly stating the geometry of the spherical cap and the Bregman–Bairstow validity condition. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** In a Brinell test on a cast iron specimen, a load of **3000 kgf** was applied through a **10 mm ball**. The indentation diameter was **5.2 mm**. Calculate the BHN and the approximate ultimate tensile strength. Also verify the test validity. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks):**

[Refer to the full derivation in **Section 3.1** above. Student should write out Steps 1–4 with a clean diagram of the spherical cap showing $D$, $d$, $h$, and $R$.]

Key marks distribution:
- Geometry setup + Pythagoras (2 marks)
- Exact $h$ expression (2 marks)
- Lateral surface area $A = \pi D h$ (1 mark)
- Final BHN formula + validity condition (2 marks)

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- $P = 3000$ kgf, $D = 10$ mm, $d = 5.2$ mm

**Step 1:** Compute the bracket term:

$$D^2 - d^2 = 100 - 27.04 = 72.96$$

$$\sqrt{72.96} = 8.542 \text{ mm}$$

$$D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2} = 10 - 8.542 = 1.458 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2:** Compute BHN:

$$\text{BHN} = \frac{2 \times 3000}{\pi \times 10 \times 1.458} = \frac{6000}{45.79} = 131.0 \text{ kgf/mm}^2$$

**Step 3:** Ultimate tensile strength:

$$\sigma_u \approx 0.35 \times 131.0 = 45.85 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4:** Validity check:

$$0.25D = 2.5 \text{ mm}, \quad 0.5D = 5.0 \text{ mm}$$

Since $d = 5.2 \text{ mm} > 5.0 \text{ mm}$, **the test is INVALID** ❌.

**Conclusion:** The load is too high (or the material too soft). The test should be repeated with a lower load, e.g., 1000 kgf (using the $P = 30D^2$ rule for harder materials doesn't apply to cast iron).

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam - Sample Paper 2024, Module 2 Choice]**
> **(a)** With a neat functional block diagram, explain the working principle of the Rockwell hardness testing machine. Discuss the significance of the minor load and list any four Rockwell scales with their indenters and major loads. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** A Vickers hardness test on a hardened steel specimen uses a load of **50 kgf**. The measured diagonals are **0.385 mm** and **0.395 mm**. Compute the Vickers hardness number and comment on whether this material could be tested on the Rockwell C scale instead. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Rockwell Working Principle (7 Marks):**

[Refer to the flow diagram in **Section 4.3**. Student should write:]

1. **Minor load (10 kgf) applied** → establishes reference zero (2 marks).
2. **Major load applied** depending on scale (60/100/150 kgf) (1 mark).
3. **Major load removed** → elastic recovery; depth $h$ measured (2 marks).
4. **Hardness = $N - h/0.002$** directly read from dial (1 mark).
5. List four scales with indenter + major load (1 mark):

| Scale | Indenter | Major Load |
|-------|----------|------------|
| HRA | Diamond cone | 60 kgf |
| HRB | 1/16" steel ball | 90 kgf |
| HRC | Diamond cone | 140 kgf |
| HRD | Diamond cone | 90 kgf |

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- $P = 50$ kgf, $d_1 = 0.385$ mm, $d_2 = 0.395$ mm

**Step 1:** Average diagonal:

$$d = \frac{0.385 + 0.395}{2} = 0.390 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2:** Vickers hardness:

$$\text{HV} = \frac{1.854 \times 50}{(0.390)^2} = \frac{92.70}{0.1521} = 609.5 \text{ kgf/mm}^2$$

**Step 3:** Convert HV to approximate HRC using empirical chart (or rule of thumb: HV ≈ 0.95 × HRC × 10 for steels above 400 HV):

Approximate HRC ≈ **60** (typical of tool steel / high-carbon hardened steel).

**Conclusion:** Yes, this material can be tested on the Rockwell C scale because:
- HRC range covers **20 – 70**, and our material falls in this range.
- Diamond cone indenter is required for hard materials (HRC > 100 HRB equivalent).
- Vickers and HRC give **comparable** values for hardened steels above HRC 40.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Typically Lose Marks (Module 2 — Hardness Tests):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the validity range check** (0.25D ≤ d ≤ 0.5D) — Examiners award **2 bonus marks** for this check; missing it loses 2 marks.
> 2. **Not specifying the Rockwell scale letter** (HRC vs. HRB vs. HRA) — Writing just "Rockwell = 60" is **incomplete** and will be penalized.
> 3. **Unit confusion in BHN/HV**: Always report in **kgf/mm²** (NOT N/mm², NOT MPa, unless explicitly converted).
> 4. **Mixing up indenters**: A student who writes "diamond ball" or "steel pyramid" immediately signals a fundamental misunderstanding — **0 marks** for that sub-part.
> 5. **Skipping the Bregman condition in numericals**: This is a free 2-mark check that 80% of students forget.
> 6. **Approximation error in BHN formula**: Using $h \approx d^2/(4D)$ instead of the exact expression gives slightly different BHN. Examiners expect the **exact** formula unless otherwise stated.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 2 — Hardness Testing: KTU 2024 Rapid-Revision Checklist**
>
> ✅ Hardness = resistance to **localized plastic deformation** under a standardized indenter.
>
> ✅ **Brinell (BHN)** — Steel/WC ball, $D = 10$ mm, load 500–3000 kgf, formula $\dfrac{2P}{\pi D[D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2}]}$, validity $0.25D \le d \le 0.5D$, UTS $\approx 0.35 \times$ BHN.
>
> ✅ **Vickers (HV)** — Diamond pyramid, 136° face angle, constant 1.854, formula $\dfrac{1.854 P}{d^2}$, valid for **all** loads (geometric similarity).
>
> ✅ **Rockwell (HR)** — Two-stage loading (10 kgf minor + 60/100/150 kgf major), formula $N - h/0.002$, **must report scale letter** (HRC, HRB, HRA, etc.).
>
> ✅ HRC = 100 for diamond cone scales; HRB = 130 for ball scales (memorize the constants).
>
> ✅ Bregman–Bairstow validity is **exclusive** to Brinell; for Vickers, always valid; for Rockwell, always valid.
>
> ✅ Brinell = rough surfaces; Vickers = thin/micro; Rockwell = speed/production.
>
> ✅ Approximate conversions (for quick reference):
>    - $\text{HRC} \approx 0.1 \times \text{HV}$ (rough)
>    - $\sigma_u \approx 3.4 \times \text{HRC}$ (MPa, rough)
>
> ✅ In lab records, always draw the **indentation geometry diagram** showing $D$, $d$, and $h$ — examiners award marks for a clean labelled sketch.
>
> ✅ Numerical problem-solving sequence: **(1)** List given values, **(2)** State the appropriate formula, **(3)** Substitute with units, **(4)** Validate the result, **(5)** Conclude with engineering interpretation.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Torsional Rigidity of Mild Steel, Copper, and Brass Rods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMEL308 - MATERIALS TESTING LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Torsional Rigidity of Mild Steel, Copper, and Brass Rods ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Torsional Rigidity** is defined as the product of the **Modulus of Rigidity (G)** and the **Polar Moment of Inertia (J)** of a cross-section, i.e., **GJ**. It quantifies a shaft's resistance to twisting deformation (angular distortion) when subjected to a torque. For a circular shaft subjected to a pure torque **T** at one end while the other end is fixed, the fundamental **torsion equation** (also called the **Bredt-Bach equation**) is: $$\frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau}{r} = \frac{G \theta}{L}$$ In the laboratory experiment, the goal is to experimentally determine the **Modulus of Rigidity (G)** — and hence the **Torsional Rigidity (GJ)** — of three engineering materials: **Mild Steel (MS)**, **Copper (Cu)**, and **Brass**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCMEL308 - Module 2):** > The experiment is *non-destructive* in nature (within elastic limit) and tests the pure shear behaviour of ductile metals under torsion. The values of **G** obtained experimentally are compared with standard textbook values (MS ≈ 79–83 GPa, Copper ≈ 42–48 GPa, Brass ≈ 36–41 GPa). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are wringing out a wet towel by twisting both ends in opposite directions. The towel resists your twisting — that resistance is **torsional rigidity**. Now consider three rods of the same diameter but different materials, all gripped the same way: | Material | Behaviour Under Twist | Intuitive Reason | |----------|----------------------|------------------| | Mild Steel | Twists only slightly; high resistance | Strong atomic metallic bonds; high stiffness | | Copper | Twists noticeably more; moderate resistance | Softer metal, more elastic deformation | | Brass | Twists between MS and Cu | Alloy of Cu + Zn; intermediate stiffness | The **angle of twist θ** (in radians) you observe per unit applied torque **T** is inversely proportional to **GJ**. A smaller θ → stiffer shaft → higher **GJ**. > [!NOTE] > **Physical Constants & Units (Bold for emphasis):** > - **T (Torque) → N·m** > - **G (Modulus of Rigidity) → N/m² or Pa (commonly GPa)** > - **J (Polar Moment of Inertia) → m⁴ (commonly mm⁴)** > - **θ (Angle of Twist) → radians (rad)** > - **L (Gauge Length) → m** > - **d (Diameter of Rod) → m** > - **Torsional Rigidity (GJ) → N·m²** --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Theoretical Background — The Theory of Pure Torsion When a cylindrical shaft of circular cross-section is subjected to a torque about its longitudinal axis, the following assumptions are made (valid within the **elastic limit**): 1. The material is **homogeneous** and **isotropic**. 2. Cross-sections, originally plane and perpendicular to the axis, **remain plane and undistorted** after twisting (this is **Saint-Venant's principle**). 3. The **radius** of the cross-section remains **straight and unchanged** in length. 4. The deformation is **pure shear** — no bending, no axial force. 5. The material obeys **Hooke's Law in shear**: τ = G·γ, where γ is the shear strain. > [!NOTE] > **Why are only circular cross-sections considered here?** > Because non-circular sections **warp** (do not remain planar) under torsion, requiring more complex analysis (e.g., membrane analogy, Prandtl's stress function). For KTU lab purposes, circular rods only. ### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Torsion Test - **Step 1: Apply a known torque** *T* via a pulley-and-weight arrangement: *T = W × R*, where *W* is the applied dead weight and *R* is the pulley radius. - **Step 2: Measure the resulting angle of twist** *θ* (in radians) over a known gauge length *L*. This is read from a graduated disc (or pointer + dial gauge) attached to the free end of the specimen. - **Step 3: Determine the polar moment of inertia** of the rod: *J = πd⁴/32*. - **Step 4: Calculate Modulus of Rigidity** *G* from: $$G = \frac{T \cdot L}{J \cdot \theta}$$ - **Step 5: Calculate Torsional Rigidity** = *G × J*. - **Step 6: Repeat** for each material (MS, Cu, Brass) and tabulate. ### 2.3 Why This Test Matters in Engineering Torsional rigidity is a critical design parameter for: - **Drive shafts** in automobiles and machinery - **Propeller shafts** in ships and aircraft - **Drill bits and tool shafts** in manufacturing - **Helical springs** (though governed by a different formula) - **Turbine rotors** in power plants > [!TIP] > **KTU Board Examiner's Note:** A common theoretical question asks *"Why is torsion testing preferred over tensile testing for shafts?"* — Answer: Torsion simulates the actual in-service loading of shafts, gives information about shear modulus G (which cannot be obtained from tensile testing alone), and detects surface defects more sensitively. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity / Relation | Formula | Unit | Description | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Applied Torque | $T = W \times R$ | N·m | W = weight (N), R = pulley/lever arm radius (m) | | 2 | Polar Moment of Inertia (solid circular shaft) | $J = \dfrac{\pi d^{4}}{32}$ | m⁴ | d = diameter of rod | | 3 | Polar Moment of Inertia (hollow circular shaft) | $J = \dfrac{\pi (D^{4} - d^{4})}{32}$ | m⁴ | D = outer, d = inner diameter | | 4 | Torsion Equation (Bredt-Bach) | $\dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{\tau}{r} = \dfrac{G \theta}{L}$ | — | Master relation | | 5 | Shear Stress (maximum at surface) | $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{16 T}{\pi d^{3}}$ | N/m² or Pa | r = d/2 substituted | | 6 | Modulus of Rigidity | $G = \dfrac{T L}{J \theta}$ | N/m² or Pa | Rearranged torsion equation | | 7 | Angle of Twist (in degrees → radians) | $\theta_{rad} = \theta_{deg} \times \dfrac{\pi}{180}$ | rad | Unit conversion | | 8 | Torsional Rigidity | $GJ$ | N·m² | Resistance to twisting | | 9 | Shear Strain at radius r | $\gamma = \dfrac{r \theta}{L}$ | rad | From geometry of twist | | 10 | Strain Energy in Torsion | $U = \dfrac{T^{2} L}{2 G J}$ | J (joules) | Elastic strain energy stored | > [!WARNING] > **Unit Consistency Pitfall (Common Board Mistake):** > If diameter is measured in **mm** and length in **mm**, then J will be in **mm⁴**, and to get G in standard **N/m² (Pa)**, you must convert using 1 mm⁴ = 10⁻¹² m⁴, OR keep all units consistent (mm, N) and accept G in **N/mm² (= MPa)**. Mixing units (mm for length, m for torque arm) is the #1 source of numerical errors. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Full Derivation of the Torsion Equation We start from the kinematic (geometric) picture of twisting. **Geometric Setup:** A cylindrical rod of radius *r* and length *L* is fixed at one end. The free end rotates by an angle *θ* (radians) relative to the fixed end. Consider a point *P* on the outer surface at radius *r = d/2*. The arc length swept by *P* is *s = r·θ*. Since the rod is twisted, this arc represents shear deformation over the length *L*. The **shear strain γ** is therefore: $$\gamma = \frac{\text{arc length}}{\text{length}} = \frac{r \theta}{L}$$ This is the **geometric equation of torsion** — it relates strain to geometry. **Constitutive Law (Hooke's Law in Shear):** Within the elastic limit, shear stress and shear strain are linearly related by the **Modulus of Rigidity G**: $$\tau = G \cdot \gamma$$ Substituting γ: $$\tau = G \cdot \frac{r \theta}{L}$$ Rearranging: $$\frac{\tau}{r} = \frac{G \theta}{L}$$ **Equilibrium (Static) Equation — Torque and Stress:** For a circular cross-section, the shear stress at radius *r* acts tangentially. The elemental torque contributed by an infinitesimal area *dA* at radius *r* is: $$dT = \tau \cdot r \cdot dA$$ Substituting τ from the relation above: $$dT = \frac{G \theta}{L} \cdot r^{2} \, dA$$ Integrating over the entire cross-section: $$T = \frac{G \theta}{L} \int_{A} r^{2} \, dA$$ By definition, $\int_{A} r^{2} \, dA = J$ (the polar moment of inertia of the cross-section). Therefore: $$T = \frac{G \theta \cdot J}{L}$$ Or, in the canonical form: $$\frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau}{r} = \frac{G \theta}{L}$$ This is the complete **torsion equation** for circular shafts. ### 3.2 Derivation of Polar Moment of Inertia J for a Solid Circular Section For a solid circle of radius *R*, we use polar coordinates. An annular ring of radius *r* and thickness *dr* has area *dA = 2πr·dr*. Therefore: $$J = \int_{0}^{R} r^{2} \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = 2\pi \int_{0}^{R} r^{3} \, dr$$ Evaluating the integral: $$J = 2\pi \left[ \frac{r^{4}}{4} \right]_{0}^{R} = \frac{2\pi R^{4}}{4} = \frac{\pi R^{4}}{2}$$ Since $R = d/2$, we have $R^{4} = d^{4}/16$. Substituting: $$J = \frac{\pi d^{4}}{32}$$ This is the standard formula for the polar moment of inertia of a solid circular shaft. ### 3.3 Derivation of Maximum Shear Stress in Torsion At the **outer surface** of the shaft, *r = R = d/2*. Substituting into τ = (T·r)/J: $$\tau_{max} = \frac{T \cdot (d/2)}{\pi d^{4}/32} = \frac{16 T}{\pi d^{3}}$$ This is the **maximum shear stress** acting at the surface of the shaft under torsion. It is a critical design value. ### 3.4 Analytical Solution: Computing G from the Lab Experiment Given the experimental readings: | Quantity | Symbol | Typical Value | |----------|--------|---------------| | Diameter of rod | d | 10 mm | | Gauge length | L | 500 mm | | Pulley/lever arm radius | R | 50 mm | | Applied weight | W | 5 kg → 5 × 9.81 = 49.05 N | | Angle of twist (degrees) | θ° | 4.5° | | Angle of twist (radians) | θ | 4.5 × π/180 ≈ 0.0785 rad | **Step 1: Compute the applied torque.** $$T = W \times R = 49.05 \, \text{N} \times 0.050 \, \text{m} = 2.4525 \, \text{N·m}$$ **Step 2: Compute J (using mm units to keep numbers manageable).** $$J = \frac{\pi d^{4}}{32} = \frac{\pi (10)^{4}}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 10000}{32} \approx 981.75 \, \text{mm}^{4}$$ Converting to m⁴: $J = 981.75 \times 10^{-12} \, \text{m}^{4} = 9.8175 \times 10^{-10} \, \text{m}^{4}$ **Step 3: Compute θ in radians.** $$\theta_{rad} = 4.5 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 0.07854 \, \text{rad}$$ **Step 4: Compute G.** $$G = \frac{T \cdot L}{J \cdot \theta}$$ Substituting (keeping units consistent in mm and N → G in N/mm² = MPa): $$G = \frac{2.4525 \, \text{N·m} \times 0.500 \, \text{m}}{9.8175 \times 10^{-10} \, \text{m}^{4} \times 0.07854 \, \text{rad}}$$ $$G = \frac{1.22625}{7.7117 \times 10^{-11}} \, \text{Pa}$$ $$G \approx 1.590 \times 10^{10} \, \text{Pa} = 15.90 \, \text{GPa}$$ This is suspiciously **low** for Mild Steel. This indicates the assumed experimental values are illustrative — actual lab values depend on real readings. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Realistic Expected Ranges (for KTU Viva):** > - **Mild Steel:** G ≈ 79–83 GPa > - **Copper:** G ≈ 42–48 GPa > - **Brass:** G ≈ 36–41 GPa > - **Relative ranking:** G_MS > G_Cu > G_Brass (for the same diameter, MS will twist the least) ### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Parameter | Mild Steel (MS) | Copper (Cu) | Brass | |---|---|---|---| | Composition | Fe + ≤ 0.25% C | Pure Cu (≥ 99%) | Cu (~70%) + Zn (~30%) | | Density (kg/m³) | ~7850 | ~8960 | ~8500–8700 | | Expected G (GPa) | 79–83 | 42–48 | 36–41 | | Expected Torsional Rigidity GJ (N·m²) for d = 10 mm, L = 500 mm | High | Medium | Medium-Low | | Angle of twist (per same torque) | Smallest | Largest | Intermediate | | Failure mode | Ductile shear | Highly ductile | Moderately ductile | | Application | Drive shafts, axles | Electrical + plumbing | Decorative, instruments | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Torsion Testing Apparatus The following Mermaid diagram shows the functional layout of a standard KTU-lab torsion testing rig, depicting how components interact to produce and measure the torque-twist response. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Dead Weight
W in Newtons] --> B[Pulley or Lever Arm
Radius R metres] B --> C[Torque Applied
T equals W times R] C --> D[Specimen Rod
Mild Steel Copper or Brass] D --> E[Fixed Chuck
Left End Anchored] D --> F[Rotating Chuck
Right End Free] F --> G[Graduated Disc
or Pointer] G --> H[Dial Gauge
or Vernier Scale] H --> I[Angle of Twist Theta
Read in Degrees] I --> J[Computation Unit
Apply T equals GJ theta over L] J --> K[Modulus of Rigidity G
in GPa] J --> L[Torsional Rigidity GJ
in N m squared] ``` **Reading the diagram:** - **Dead Weight (A)** produces a downward force. - **Pulley (B)** converts linear force into **rotational torque (C)**. - **Specimen Rod (D)** is gripped between a **fixed chuck (E)** and a **rotating chuck (F)**. - The rotating chuck turns a **graduated disc (G)**, which is read via a **dial gauge or vernier (H)** to yield the **angle of twist (I)**. - The computation unit (J) processes T, θ, L, J to yield **G (K)** and **GJ (L)**. ### 4.2 Block Diagram of the Experimental Procedure (Sequential Flow) ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[Start: Prepare Specimen] --> P2[Measure Diameter d using Vernier] P2 --> P3[Measure Gauge Length L] P3 --> P4[Fix Rod in Torsion Rig Chucks] P4 --> P5[Set Pulley Radius R] P5 --> P6[Apply Incremental Dead Weights W] P6 --> P7[Record Angle of Twist theta for each W] P7 --> P8{Elastic Limit Reached?} P8 -- No --> P6 P8 -- Yes --> P9[Tabulate T and theta Values] P9 --> P10[Plot T versus theta Graph] P10 --> P11[Find Slope of Linear Region] P11 --> P12[Compute G equals Slope times L divided by J] P12 --> P13[Compute Torsional Rigidity GJ] P13 --> P14[Repeat for All Three Materials] P14 --> P15[Compare with Standard Values and Tabulate] P15 --> P16[End of Experiment] ``` ### 4.3 Block Diagram — Load-Deflection Behaviour in Torsion ```mermaid flowchart LR R1[Linear Region
Hookean Elastic] --> R2[Yield Point
Proportional Limit] R2 --> R3[Plastic Region
Permanent Twist] R3 --> R4[Ultimate Shear Stress] R4 --> R5[Fracture Surface
Flat for Brittle] R4 --> R6[Fracture Surface
Rough for Ductile] R5 --> R7[Failure Mode Type A] R6 --> R7[Failure Mode Type B] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Explanation of Diagram:** > In the **linear region (R1)**, the slope of the *T vs θ* graph equals *GJ/L*. As long as we operate in this region, **Hooke's law in shear** holds, and our computed *G* is valid. Once we cross the yield point (R2), the relationship becomes non-linear, and our simplified torsion equation no longer applies. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** *Define Torsional Rigidity. State its SI unit.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** Torsional Rigidity is defined as the product of the **Modulus of Rigidity (G)** and the **Polar Moment of Inertia (J)** of a cross-section. It represents the resistance offered by a shaft to twisting deformation under an applied torque. Mathematically: $$\text{Torsional Rigidity} = G \times J$$ **SI unit:** N·m² (Newton-metre squared). A higher GJ means the shaft resists twisting more for a given applied torque. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** *State the torsion equation for a circular shaft and explain the meaning of each term.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The torsion equation (Bredt-Bach equation) is: $$\frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau}{r} = \frac{G \theta}{L}$$ where: - **T** = Applied external torque (N·m) - **J** = Polar moment of inertia of the cross-section (m⁴) = πd⁴/32 for a solid shaft - **τ** = Shear stress at radius *r* (Pa) - **r** = Radial distance from the centre of the shaft (m) - **G** = Modulus of rigidity of the material (Pa) - **θ** = Angle of twist over the gauge length (rad) - **L** = Gauge length of the shaft (m) This equation is valid within the **elastic limit** and for **circular cross-sections** only. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) #### **Question A — Choice 1 (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Past Year Pattern]** **(a)** *With a neat sketch, explain the apparatus used to determine the torsional rigidity of a given rod. List the observations and measurements taken during the experiment.* **(7 marks)** **(b)** *For a mild steel specimen of diameter 12 mm and gauge length 600 mm tested in torsion, the following readings were obtained:* | Dead Weight W (N) | 9.81 | 19.62 | 29.43 | 39.24 | 49.05 | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Angle of Twist θ (°) | 0.50 | 1.05 | 1.55 | 2.10 | 2.65 | *The pulley radius is 50 mm. Determine:* *(i) The Modulus of Rigidity of the material.* **(4 marks)** *(ii) The maximum shear stress in the rod.* **(3 marks)** --- ##### Model Solution for Question A: **(a) Apparatus Description (7 marks):** The standard torsion test rig consists of: 1. **Two Chucks (fixed and movable):** One end of the specimen is held in a fixed chuck (wall-mounted), the other in a movable chuck. 2. **Pulley or Lever Arm:** Attached to the movable chuck. Dead weights hung from the pulley apply a known torque T = W × R. 3. **Graduated Disc / Scale:** Coaxial with the specimen, marked in degrees. It rotates with the free end. 4. **Pointer / Index:** Fixed, indicates the angular rotation on the graduated disc. 5. **Specimen Rod:** The MS / Cu / Brass rod under test, with known d and L. 6. **Spirit Level:** To ensure the apparatus is horizontal, eliminating bending effects. **Observations recorded:** - Diameter *d* of the rod (using Vernier caliper, taken at 3 locations) - Gauge length *L* (between chucks) - Pulley/lever radius *R* - For each increment of weight *W*, the corresponding angle of twist θ is read. - Load is applied in **small increments** and removed to check for elastic recovery (verifying operation within elastic limit). **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** *Step 1: Compute Torque for each weight.* Torque: $T = W \times R$ Using R = 50 mm = 0.050 m: | W (N) | T (N·m) | θ° | θ (rad) = θ° × π/180 | |---|---|---|---| | 9.81 | 0.4905 | 0.50 | 0.00873 | | 19.62 | 0.9810 | 1.05 | 0.01833 | | 29.43 | 1.4715 | 1.55 | 0.02705 | | 39.24 | 1.9620 | 2.10 | 0.03665 | | 49.05 | 2.4525 | 2.65 | 0.04625 | *Step 2: Compute J for d = 12 mm.* $$J = \frac{\pi (12)^{4}}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 20736}{32} \approx 2035.75 \, \text{mm}^{4} = 2.03575 \times 10^{-9} \, \text{m}^{4}$$ *Step 3: Compute G for each reading using $G = \dfrac{T L}{J \theta}$* Using L = 600 mm = 0.600 m: For W = 9.81 N: $G = \dfrac{0.4905 \times 0.600}{2.03575 \times 10^{-9} \times 0.00873} = \dfrac{0.2943}{1.7772 \times 10^{-11}} \approx 1.656 \times 10^{10}$ Pa = **16.56 GPa** Repeating for all: | W (N) | G (GPa) | |---|---| | 9.81 | 16.56 | | 19.62 | 15.79 | | 29.43 | 16.04 | | 39.24 | 15.79 | | 49.05 | 15.64 | **Average G ≈ 15.96 GPa** > [!NOTE] > The values are on the lower side due to illustrative data; typical lab values for MS are **79–83 GPa**. In an actual exam, students would use the given data and present the average. *(i) Modulus of Rigidity G ≈ 15.96 GPa* (4 marks) *Step 4: Maximum shear stress using $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{16 T}{\pi d^{3}}$* For the maximum W = 49.05 N → T = 2.4525 N·m: $$\tau_{max} = \frac{16 \times 2.4525}{\pi \times (0.012)^{3}} = \frac{39.24}{\pi \times 1.728 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{39.24}{5.429 \times 10^{-6}}$$ $$\tau_{max} \approx 7.227 \times 10^{6} \, \text{Pa} = 7.23 \, \text{MPa}$$ *(ii) Maximum shear stress ≈ 7.23 MPa* (3 marks) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Unit mismatch**: Forgetting to convert mm⁴ → m⁴ while computing G in Pa. This gives errors of 10¹² magnitude. > 2. **Radian-degree confusion**: Using θ in degrees directly in the formula gives G values ~57.3 times smaller than the actual value. > 3. **Forgetting π/32 factor**: Some students write J = πd⁴ (incorrect — the factor is 1/32, not 1). > 4. **T vs W confusion**: T = W × R, not just W. Forgetting R is a very common mistake. --- #### **Question B — Choice 2 (14 Marks) [ALTERNATIVE]** **[KTU University Exam – Past Year Pattern]** **(a)** *Derive the torsion equation $\dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{\tau}{r} = \dfrac{G\theta}{L}$ for a solid circular shaft, clearly stating the assumptions involved.* **(7 marks)** **(b)** *Compare the torsional behaviour of Mild Steel, Copper, and Brass rods of identical dimensions. What conclusions can you draw about their relative shear moduli from a torsion test?* **(7 marks)** --- ##### Model Solution for Question B: **(a) Derivation (7 marks):** *Assumptions (1 mark):* 1. Material is homogeneous, isotropic, and obeys Hooke's law in shear. 2. Shaft has a uniform circular cross-section. 3. Cross-sections remain plane and undistorted after twisting (Saint-Venant's principle). 4. Deformation is within the elastic limit. *Geometric Relation (2 marks):* Consider a shaft of length *L* fixed at one end, twisted by angle *θ* at the free end. A point *P* on the surface (radius *r*) moves through an arc *s = r·θ*. The shear strain γ is: $$\gamma = \frac{s}{L} = \frac{r\theta}{L}$$ *Constitutive Relation (1 mark):* By Hooke's law in shear: $\tau = G\gamma$ Substituting γ: $$\tau = G \cdot \frac{r\theta}{L} \implies \frac{\tau}{r} = \frac{G\theta}{L}$$ *Equilibrium Relation (2 marks):* The torque T equals the integral of elemental tangential forces over the cross-section: $$T = \int_A \tau \cdot r \, dA = \int_A \frac{G\theta}{L} \cdot r^2 \, dA = \frac{G\theta}{L} \int_A r^2 \, dA$$ Since $\int_A r^2 \, dA = J$ (polar moment of inertia): $$T = \frac{G\theta J}{L} \implies \frac{T}{J} = \frac{G\theta}{L}$$ Combining all three: $$\boxed{\frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau}{r} = \frac{G\theta}{L}}$$ (1 mark for final boxed result) **(b) Comparative Analysis (7 marks):** For **identical diameter and gauge length**, *J* and *L* are constants. From $G = \dfrac{TL}{J\theta}$, we have $G \propto \dfrac{1}{\theta}$ for a fixed torque *T*. **Expected Behaviour:** | Property | Mild Steel | Copper | Brass | |---|---|---|---| | Composition | Fe + C | Pure Cu | Cu + Zn alloy | | Expected G (GPa) | 79–83 | 42–48 | 36–41 | | Angle of Twist θ (per unit T) | Smallest | Largest | Intermediate | | Atomic bonding | Strong metallic | Metallic + softer | Metallic + intermetallic | **Conclusions:** 1. **Mild Steel** has the **highest shear modulus** (~80 GPa), making it most resistant to twisting. This makes it the preferred material for **drive shafts, axles, and high-stress torque transmission components**. 2. **Copper** has a **moderate shear modulus** (~45 GPa) — it twists more than steel but offers excellent **ductility and corrosion resistance**, suitable for **plumbing, electrical, and low-stress applications**. 3. **Brass** has the **lowest shear modulus** (~38 GPa) of the three. Its twist is **between** MS and Cu. Brass offers **good machinability, decorative appeal, and acoustic properties**, making it ideal for **instruments, fittings, and low-load gears**. 4. **Relative ranking of G:** $G_{MS} > G_{Cu} > G_{Brass}$. 5. For the **same torque**, the angle of twist follows the **reverse ranking**: $\theta_{Brass} > \theta_{Cu} > \theta_{MS}$. (1 mark for overall conclusion synthesis) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Do not confuse** torsional rigidity *GJ* with modulus of rigidity *G*. Torsional rigidity also depends on geometry (J). > 2. **Do not extrapolate** the linear T–θ relationship beyond the elastic limit. Doing so gives a false (higher) G. > 3. **Do not ignore** the friction in the pulley / bearings. In a precise setup, friction correction is applied by **extrapolating the T–θ line back to zero twist**. > 4. **Always compare** your experimental G with **standard values** and compute the **percentage error**: $\%\text{Error} = \dfrac{\vert G_{exp} - G_{std} \vert}{G_{std}} \times 100$. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ✅ **Torsion Equation:** $\dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{\tau}{r} = \dfrac{G\theta}{L}$ — valid **only for circular shafts** and **within the elastic limit**. - ✅ **Polar Moment of Inertia (solid):** $J = \dfrac{\pi d^{4}}{32}$. **Common error:** omitting the 32 in denominator. - ✅ **Torsional Rigidity:** $GJ$ — product of material stiffness and geometric property. - ✅ **Modulus of Rigidity:** $G = \dfrac{TL}{J\theta}$ — primary unknown in this experiment. - ✅ **Applied Torque:** $T = W \times R$, where *W* is dead weight and *R* is pulley radius. - ✅ **Angle Conversion:** Always convert θ from **degrees to radians**: $\theta_{rad} = \theta_{deg} \times \dfrac{\pi}{180}$. - ✅ **Maximum Shear Stress:** $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{16T}{\pi d^{3}}$ — occurs at the outer surface of the shaft. - ✅ **Material Ranking (G):** Mild Steel > Copper > Brass. Therefore, **angle of twist ranking is reversed**. - ✅ **Expected Ranges:** - Mild Steel → 79–83 GPa - Copper → 42–48 GPa - Brass → 36–41 GPa - ✅ **Procedure Essentials:** Apply small **incremental loads**, take **multiple readings**, plot **T vs θ graph** to identify the **linear (elastic) region**, compute slope = *GJ/L*, then extract *G*. - ✅ **Assumptions:** Material homogeneous, isotropic, obeys Hooke's law in shear, cross-section remains plane and undistorted. - ✅ **Unit Vigilance:** Mixing mm and m is the #1 cause of wrong answers. Convert ALL quantities to a single unit system before computation. - ✅ **Applications of Torsional Rigidity Knowledge:** Drive shafts, propeller shafts, drill bits, turbine rotors, helical spring design. - ✅ **Hooke's Law in Shear:** $\tau = G\gamma$ — the foundation of elastic torsion analysis. - ✅ **Strain Energy Stored in Torsion:** $U = \dfrac{T^{2} L}{2GJ}$ — useful for impact/shock loading problems. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU Board Tip:** Always present experimental results in a **standard table format** (d, L, R, W, θ, T, G) and conclude with a **percentage error analysis** vs. standard textbook values. This demonstrates analytical maturity and is rewarded in KTU valuations. ---

topics.title: Flexural Stiffness of Mild Steel, Copper, and Brass Specimens Using Three-Point Bend Test on UTM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMEL308 - MATERIALS TESTING LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Flexural Stiffness of Mild Steel, Copper, and Brass Specimens Using Three-Point Bend Test on UTM # MODULE EXPERIMENT 2 — FLEXURAL STIFFNESS BY THREE-POINT BEND TEST ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Three-Point Bend Test (Flexural Test):** A standardized mechanical test in which a specimen of rectangular or circular cross-section is supported on two lower anvils (supports) and loaded by a single central loading nose, producing a state of **pure bending** between the supports. The test is governed by **ASTM E290** (for metallic flat products) and yields the **Flexural Modulus (E)**, **Flexural Yield Strength**, and the derived property **Flexural Stiffness (S = EI)** of the material. > [!NOTE] > **Flexural Stiffness (S):** The resistance offered by a structural member to bending deformation under an applied transverse load. Mathematically expressed as the product of the **Young's Modulus (E)** of the material and the **Second Moment of Area (I)** of the cross-section about the neutral axis: > $$ S = E \cdot I $$ The Universal Testing Machine (UTM) is used to perform this test in the laboratory because it provides controlled, monotonic application of load and accurate measurement of the corresponding mid-span deflection through a high-precision crosshead displacement encoder or a dial gauge. ## 1.2 Intuitive Overview & Real-World Analogy > [!TIP] > **The Diving Board Analogy:** Imagine standing at the free end of a diving board. A wooden board (low stiffness) flexes dramatically, while a steel-reinforced concrete diving board (high stiffness) barely bends. Both boards may be of identical dimensions, but the **material's resistance to bending** differs vastly. This resistance is exactly what we measure in a three-point bend test. The "stiffness" is NOT the material's strength (how much load it can take before breaking) — it is how much it **resists deforming** under load. **Plain English Explanation:** - When a load is applied at the **center** of a beam supported at its two ends, the beam **bends downward**. - The **stiffness** tells us how much load is required to produce a unit deflection at the center. - A **stiffer** material will deflect **less** for the same applied load. - In this experiment, we will test three metallic materials — **Mild Steel**, **Copper**, and **Brass** — and determine which one has the highest flexural stiffness. ## 1.3 Materials Under Test — Standard Property Ranges > [!NOTE] > The following values are **approximate reference ranges** (as per standard material handbooks like *ASM Metals Handbook*) used only for **comparison with experimentally obtained values**: | Material | Young's Modulus (E) | Density | Nature of Stress-Strain Response | |:---------|:-------------------:|:-------:|:---------------------------------| | **Mild Steel** | **195–210 GPa** | 7850 kg/m³ | Linear-elastic then yielding | | **Copper (Pure)** | **110–130 GPa** | 8960 kg/m³ | Linear-elastic, ductile | | **Brass (Cartridge 70/30)** | **100–120 GPa** | 8530 kg/m³ | Linear-elastic, ductile | These reference ranges are essential for **validation** of the experimental data during the lab record evaluation. # 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS ## 2.1 Underlying Beam Bending Theory (Euler–Bernoulli Assumptions) The three-point bend test is derived from the **Euler–Bernoulli beam theory**, which assumes: 1. The beam is initially **straight** and has a **uniform cross-section**. 2. The material is **homogeneous** and **isotropic** (uniform properties in all directions). 3. The **plane sections remain plane** and **perpendicular to the neutral axis** after bending (no shear deformation). 4. **Strain** varies **linearly** with the distance from the neutral axis. 5. **Deflections** are **small** compared to the span length. 6. The beam is long and slender (span-to-depth ratio ≥ 16:1 as per **ASTM D790** for flexural testing of similar materials). > [!IMPORTANT] > **For a valid flexural test (per ASTM E290 / IS 1599):** The span-to-depth ratio **L/d** must be maintained at **16:1 or greater** to ensure that the failure mode is **flexural** and not dominated by **shear** or **indentation effects** at the loading nose. ## 2.2 Mechanics of the Three-Point Bend Test Consider a simply supported beam of rectangular cross-section: - **Length** (L) = distance between the two support anvils - **Breadth (Width)** = b - **Thickness (Depth)** = d - **Load (F)** applied at the **mid-span** via the central loading nose Under this loading: - The **maximum bending moment** occurs at the mid-span and is given by: $$ M_{max} = \frac{F \cdot L}{4} $$ - The **maximum shear force** in the beam is **F/2** on each side of the loading point. - The **maximum flexural (bending) stress** is developed at the **top and bottom surfaces** of the beam (farthest from the neutral axis): $$ \sigma_f = \frac{M_{max} \cdot y}{I} = \frac{3 F L}{2 b d^2} $$ - The **maximum flexural strain** at the outer surface is: $$ \varepsilon_f = \frac{6 \cdot \delta \cdot d}{L^2} $$ - The **maximum deflection (δ)** at the mid-span for an elastic beam under central point load: $$ \delta = \frac{F L^3}{48 E I} $$ ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!TIP] > **Master these equations — they appear in 90% of KTU viva and record evaluation questions for this experiment.** | # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes | |:-:|:----------|:--------|:------|:------| | 1 | Second Moment of Area (Rectangle) | $I = \dfrac{b d^3}{12}$ | mm⁴ | About the neutral axis | | 2 | Maximum Bending Moment | $M = \dfrac{F L}{4}$ | N·mm | At mid-span | | 3 | Maximum Flexural Stress | $\sigma_f = \dfrac{3 F L}{2 b d^2}$ | N/mm² (MPa) | At outer surface | | 4 | Maximum Flexural Strain | $\varepsilon_f = \dfrac{6 \delta d}{L^2}$ | dimensionless | At outer surface | | 5 | Maximum Mid-Span Deflection | $\delta = \dfrac{F L^3}{48 E I}$ | mm | Linear-elastic region | | 6 | Flexural Modulus | $E = \dfrac{F L^3}{4 b d^3 \delta} = \dfrac{m L^3}{4 b d^3}$ | N/mm² (MPa) | m = slope of F vs δ curve | | 7 | Flexural Stiffness | $S = E \cdot I = \dfrac{F L^3}{48 \delta}$ | N·mm | Direct from load-deflection data | | 8 | Span-to-Depth Ratio | $\dfrac{L}{d}$ | dimensionless | Must be ≥ 16 | > [!WARNING] > **Common Unit Confusion in KTU Records:** Always convert dimensions to **mm** and loads to **N** before substitution. If load is in kgf, multiply by **g = 9.81 m/s²** to get Newtons. Never mix cm and mm in the same equation. ## 2.4 Why This Test Matters in Engineering | Industry | Practical Application | |:---------|:---------------------| | **Structural Engineering** | Selection of beams, girders, and floor joists | | **Automotive** | Design of chassis, leaf springs, anti-roll bars | | **Aerospace** | Wing spar materials, control surface stiffness | | **Civil Infrastructure** | Concrete reinforcement, bridge deck design | | **Manufacturing** | Quality control of rolled metals, castings, and extrusions | | **Failure Analysis** | Determining if a component failed due to insufficient bending resistance | > [!NOTE] > For **ductile metals** like Mild Steel, Copper, and Brass, the three-point bend test typically does **not cause fracture** within the elastic range. The test in this experiment is therefore conducted in the **linear-elastic region** only, and we extract the **slope (m)** of the initial straight-line portion of the load–deflection curve to compute **E** and **S**. # 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation of Maximum Flexural Stress (σ_f) We start from the fundamental **flexure formula** (derived from Euler–Bernoulli theory): $$ \frac{M}{I} = \frac{\sigma}{y} = \frac{E}{R} $$ where **R** is the radius of curvature of the neutral axis. For a rectangular cross-section, the section modulus is: $$ Z = \frac{I}{y_{max}} = \frac{b d^2}{6} $$ Maximum bending stress occurs at the outermost fiber (y = d/2): $$ \sigma_{max} = \frac{M_{max}}{Z} $$ Substituting $M_{max} = \dfrac{F L}{4}$ and $Z = \dfrac{b d^2}{6}$: $$ \sigma_{max} = \frac{F L / 4}{b d^2 / 6} $$ $$ \sigma_{max} = \frac{F L}{4} \cdot \frac{6}{b d^2} $$ $$ \boxed{\sigma_{max} = \frac{3 F L}{2 b d^2}} $$ ## 3.2 Derivation of Maximum Flexural Strain (ε_f) From the geometric relation for a deflected beam (assuming small deflections and circular curvature): $$ \frac{1}{R} = \frac{M}{E I} = \frac{2 \delta}{L^2} \quad \text{(at mid-span)} $$ Strain at distance **y** from the neutral axis is: $$ \varepsilon = \frac{y}{R} $$ Maximum strain at y = d/2: $$ \varepsilon_{max} = \frac{d/2}{R} = \frac{d}{2R} $$ Substituting $R = \dfrac{L^2}{2 \delta}$: $$ \varepsilon_{max} = \frac{d}{2} \cdot \frac{2 \delta}{L^2} $$ $$ \boxed{\varepsilon_{max} = \frac{6 \delta d}{L^2}} $$ ## 3.3 Derivation of Mid-Span Deflection (δ) by Double Integration Method **Step 1:** The bending moment at a distance **x** from the left support (for 0 ≤ x ≤ L/2) is: $$ M(x) = \frac{F}{2} x $$ **Step 2:** The Euler–Bernoulli differential equation is: $$ E I \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2} = M(x) = \frac{F x}{2} $$ **Step 3:** Integrate once (slope equation): $$ E I \frac{d y}{d x} = \frac{F x^2}{4} + C_1 $$ By symmetry, the slope at x = L/2 is zero (for the half-beam method) — but using full beam with appropriate boundary conditions: $$ E I \frac{d y}{d x} = \frac{F x^2}{4} - \frac{F L x}{4} + C_1 $$ **Step 4:** Integrate again (deflection equation): $$ E I y = \frac{F x^3}{12} - \frac{F L x^2}{8} + C_1 x + C_2 $$ **Step 5:** Apply boundary conditions: - At x = 0, y = 0 → C₂ = 0 - At x = L, y = 0 → $C_1 = \dfrac{F L^2}{16}$ **Step 6:** Maximum deflection at mid-span (x = L/2): $$ E I y_{max} = \frac{F (L/2)^3}{12} - \frac{F L (L/2)^2}{8} + \frac{F L^2}{16} \cdot \frac{L}{2} $$ $$ E I y_{max} = \frac{F L^3}{96} - \frac{F L^3}{32} + \frac{F L^3}{32} $$ $$ E I y_{max} = \frac{F L^3}{96} $$ $$ \boxed{\delta_{max} = \frac{F L^3}{48 E I}} $$ ## 3.4 Derivation of Flexural Modulus from Load-Deflection Data Rearranging the deflection equation: $$ \delta = \frac{F L^3}{48 E I} = \frac{F L^3 \cdot 12}{48 E \cdot b d^3} = \frac{F L^3}{4 E b d^3} $$ Solving for E: $$ \boxed{E = \frac{F L^3}{4 b d^3 \delta}} $$ In practice, the load-deflection curve is **not perfectly linear** due to seating effects. We take the **slope (m)** of the **initial linear portion** of the F vs δ plot: $$ m = \frac{\Delta F}{\Delta \delta} \quad \text{(slope in N/mm)} $$ Substituting F = m·δ: $$ \boxed{E = \frac{m L^3}{4 b d^3}} \quad \text{and} \quad \boxed{S = E \cdot I = \frac{m L^3}{48}} $$ ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Record Problem) **Given Data (from a typical experiment):** | Parameter | Mild Steel | Copper | Brass | |:----------|:----------:|:------:|:-----:| | Span (L) | 100 mm | 100 mm | 100 mm | | Width (b) | 20 mm | 20 mm | 20 mm | | Thickness (d) | 6 mm | 6 mm | 6 mm | | Slope (m) from F–δ plot | 1850 N/mm | 1120 N/mm | 1040 N/mm | **Step A: Calculate I (same for all, since geometry is identical)** $$ I = \frac{b d^3}{12} = \frac{20 \times 6^3}{12} = \frac{20 \times 216}{12} = 360 \; \text{mm}^4 $$ **Step B: Calculate Flexural Modulus (E) for Mild Steel** $$ E_{MS} = \frac{m L^3}{4 b d^3} = \frac{1850 \times (100)^3}{4 \times 20 \times (6)^3} $$ $$ E_{MS} = \frac{1850 \times 1{,}000{,}000}{4 \times 20 \times 216} = \frac{1.85 \times 10^9}{17280} $$ $$ \boxed{E_{MS} \approx 1.07 \times 10^5 \; \text{N/mm}^2 = 107 \; \text{GPa (apparent)}} $$ *Note: In practice, with proper specimen preparation, expected E_MS ≈ 200 GPa. The reduced value may indicate compliance in the loading fixture, which students should comment upon in the record.* **Step C: Calculate Flexural Stiffness (S = EI) for Mild Steel** $$ S_{MS} = E_{MS} \times I = 1.07 \times 10^5 \times 360 $$ $$ \boxed{S_{MS} \approx 3.86 \times 10^7 \; \text{N·mm}^2 = 38.6 \; \text{kN·m}^2} $$ **Step D: Repeat for Copper and Brass** | Calculation | Mild Steel | Copper | Brass | |:------------|:----------:|:------:|:-----:| | $E$ (GPa) | **107** | **64.8** | **60.2** | | $S = E \cdot I$ (kN·m²) | **38.6** | **23.3** | **21.7** | > [!NOTE] > Even though the geometric stiffness contribution (I) is identical, the **overall flexural stiffness S** differs because of the material's **E**. Mild Steel > Copper > Brass in terms of flexural stiffness (per this experimental sample). ## 3.6 Conversion Reminders for KTU Records $$ 1 \; \text{GPa} = 10^3 \; \text{MPa} = 10^3 \; \text{N/mm}^2 $$ $$ 1 \; \text{kN} = 1000 \; \text{N}, \quad 1 \; \text{kN·m}^2 = 10^7 \; \text{N·mm}^2 $$ $$ 1 \; \text{kgf} \approx 9.81 \; \text{N} $$ # 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Three-Point Bend Test Setup Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Specimen
Rectangular Bar] --> B1[Support Anvils
Lower Fixture] B1 --> C1[Loading Nose
Central Point Load] C1 --> D1[UTM Crosshead
Force Application] D1 --> E1[Load Cell
Force Measurement] D1 --> F1[Displacement Encoder
Deflection Recording] E1 --> G1[Data Acquisition System
X-Y Recorder or Software] F1 --> G1 G1 --> H1[Load vs Deflection
Curve Plot] H1 --> I1[Slope Calculation
Linear Region] I1 --> J1[Flexural Modulus E
and Stiffness S] ``` ## 4.2 Process Flow — Experimental Procedure ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Select Specimen] --> B[Measure b, d, L
using Vernier and Micrometer] B --> C[Set Span L on UTM
Lower Fixture] C --> D[Place Specimen
Centrally on Supports] D --> E[Position Loading Nose
at Mid-Span] E --> F[Zero the Load
and Deflection Readings] F --> G[Apply Load Monotonically
at Controlled Rate] G --> H[Record F and Delta
at Regular Intervals] H --> I{Continue Until
Yielding or Limit?} I -->|Yes| J[Plot F vs Delta Curve] I -->|No| G J --> K[Identify Linear Region
Calculate Slope m] K --> L[Compute E and S
for All Three Materials] L --> M[Compare with Standard Values
Discuss Sources of Error] M --> N[End: Record Submission] ``` ## 4.3 Functional Block Diagram — Calculation Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Input["Input Data"] P1[L Span mm] P2[b Width mm] P3[d Thickness mm] P4[m Slope N per mm] end subgraph Computation["Calculation Engine"] Q1[I equals b times d cubed over 12] Q2[E equals m times L cubed over 4 b d cubed] Q3[S equals E times I] end subgraph Output["Result Output"] R1[Flexural Modulus E in GPa] R2[Flexural Stiffness S in kN m squared] end P1 --> Q1 P2 --> Q1 P3 --> Q1 P4 --> Q2 P1 --> Q2 P2 --> Q2 P3 --> Q2 Q1 --> Q3 Q2 --> Q3 Q2 --> R1 Q3 --> R2 ``` ## 4.4 Schematic — Free Body and Bending Moment Diagram (Conceptual) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph BeamSetup["3 Point Bending Schematic"] A1[Reaction R1 equals F over 2] --- S1[Support 1] S1 --- M1[Beam Length L] M1 --- S2[Support 2] S2 --- A2[Reaction R2 equals F over 2] M1 -.->|F Applied Here| LN[Central Loading Nose] end subgraph BendingMoment["Bending Moment Diagram Shape"] BMD1[Zero at x=0] --> BMD2[Linear Rise to F L over 4 at x equals L over 2] BMD2 --> BMD3[Linear Drop to Zero at x equals L] end ``` ## 4.5 Typical Load vs Deflection Curve (ASCII Reproduction) ``` Load F (N) ^ | . E | .: | .: : | .: : <- Yield point begins | .: : (for Mild Steel) | .: : | .: : Elastic Region | .: : (used for E calc) | .: : | .: : | .: : | .: : | .: : |.:_______________________: +---------------------------------> Deflection δ (mm) 0 0.5 1.0 1.5 2.0 2.5 ^-------^ Slope m = ΔF/Δδ → E = mL³/(4bd³) ``` # 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]** **Q1. Define flexural stiffness. State its SI unit.** > **Model Answer:** > Flexural stiffness is the resistance of a structural member to bending deformation under an applied transverse load. Mathematically, it is the product of the material's Young's Modulus (E) and the second moment of area (I) of the cross-section about the neutral axis. > $$ S = E \times I $$ > **SI Unit:** N·m² (also commonly expressed as N·mm² in laboratory records for convenience). > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]** **Q2. What is the significance of the span-to-depth ratio (L/d) in a three-point bend test?** > **Model Answer:** > The span-to-depth ratio (L/d) is critical for ensuring **pure bending** behavior. As per **ASTM E290 / IS 1599**, the ratio must be **≥ 16:1** to: > - Minimize **shear deformation** effects. > - Avoid **localized indentation** at the loading nose. > - Ensure the test measures **true flexural properties** of the material rather than combined bending and shear. > A low L/d ratio (< 10:1) would result in **erroneously high** flexural modulus values. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam — ESE Model Question, May 2024]** ### Question A (14 Marks) **(a)** Derive the expression for the **maximum flexural stress** developed in a rectangular specimen subjected to a three-point bend test. (7 marks) **(b)** In a three-point bend test on a brass specimen (b = 25 mm, d = 8 mm), the load at the proportional limit was found to be **1200 N** with a corresponding deflection of **0.65 mm**. The span was maintained at **120 mm**. Calculate the **flexural modulus** and the **flexural stiffness** of the specimen. (7 marks) ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **(a)** Explain the **Euler–Bernoulli beam theory assumptions** and discuss why a **span-to-depth ratio of at least 16:1** is mandated in a three-point bend test as per **ASTM E290**. (7 marks) **(b)** A copper specimen (width = 20 mm, thickness = 5 mm, span = 90 mm) is tested in three-point bending on a UTM. The load–deflection data recorded in the linear elastic region are given below. Determine the **flexural modulus (E)** and **flexural stiffness (S)** of copper. (7 marks) | Load F (N) | Deflection δ (mm) | |:----------:|:-----------------:| | 100 | 0.10 | | 200 | 0.21 | | 300 | 0.32 | | 400 | 0.42 | | 500 | 0.53 | --- ## 5.3 COMPLETE MODEL SOLUTIONS ### Solution to Question A #### Part (a) — Derivation of Maximum Flexural Stress **Step 1:** State the flexure formula (bending equation): $$ \frac{M}{I} = \frac{\sigma}{y} = \frac{E}{R} $$ **Step 2:** For a simply supported beam with a central point load, the maximum bending moment at mid-span is: $$ M_{max} = \frac{F \cdot L}{4} $$ **Step 3:** For a rectangular cross-section, the second moment of area is: $$ I = \frac{b d^3}{12} $$ **Step 4:** The maximum distance from the neutral axis is: $$ y_{max} = \frac{d}{2} $$ **Step 5:** Substituting into the flexure formula: $$ \sigma_{max} = \frac{M_{max} \cdot y_{max}}{I} = \frac{(F L / 4) \cdot (d/2)}{b d^3 / 12} $$ **Step 6:** Simplification: $$ \sigma_{max} = \frac{F L d}{8} \times \frac{12}{b d^3} = \frac{3 F L}{2 b d^2} $$ $$ \boxed{\sigma_f = \frac{3 F L}{2 b d^2} \; \text{N/mm}^2} $$ **Step 7:** Mention physical significance: This stress occurs at the **topmost (compression)** and **bottommost (tension)** fibers of the beam at the mid-span section. #### Part (b) — Numerical Problem **Given:** - b = 25 mm, d = 8 mm, L = 120 mm - F = 1200 N, δ = 0.65 mm **Step 1: Calculate I** $$ I = \frac{b d^3}{12} = \frac{25 \times 8^3}{12} = \frac{25 \times 512}{12} = 1066.67 \; \text{mm}^4 $$ **Step 2: Calculate Flexural Modulus E** $$ E = \frac{F L^3}{4 b d^3 \delta} = \frac{1200 \times (120)^3}{4 \times 25 \times (8)^3 \times 0.65} $$ Numerator: $1200 \times 1{,}728{,}000 = 2.0736 \times 10^9$ Denominator: $4 \times 25 \times 512 \times 0.65 = 33{,}280$ $$ E = \frac{2.0736 \times 10^9}{33{,}280} \approx 62{,}308 \; \text{N/mm}^2 $$ $$ \boxed{E \approx 62.31 \; \text{GPa}} $$ **Step 3: Calculate Flexural Stiffness S** $$ S = E \times I = 62{,}308 \times 1066.67 $$ $$ \boxed{S \approx 6.65 \times 10^7 \; \text{N·mm}^2 = 66.5 \; \text{kN·m}^2} $$ **Step 4: Comment:** The standard value of E for brass is 100–120 GPa. The lower experimental value (62 GPa) indicates possible sources of error: specimen non-flatness, fixture compliance, or measurement inaccuracy. --- ### Solution to Question B #### Part (a) — Euler–Bernoulli Assumptions & Span-to-Depth Ratio **The Six Euler–Bernoulli Assumptions:** 1. The beam is **initially straight** and has a **uniform cross-section** along its length. 2. The material is **homogeneous** and **isotropic** (same properties in all directions). 3. **Plane sections remain plane** and **perpendicular to the neutral axis** even after bending. 4. The **strain** varies **linearly** with distance from the neutral axis. 5. **Deflections are small** compared to the span length (small deformation theory). 6. The beam is long and slender, so **shear deformation is negligible** compared to bending deformation. **Why Span-to-Depth Ratio ≥ 16:1 is Mandated (ASTM E290):** - If L/d is too small, the beam behaves as a **deep beam**, where **shear deformation** becomes significant and **plane sections do not remain plane**. - The **shear stress** contribution to total deflection becomes comparable to bending deflection, leading to **erroneously high** E values. - **Localized indentation** at the loading nose and supports becomes significant, causing **overestimation of stiffness**. - The **Saint-Venant's principle** requires the span to be sufficiently long so that local stress concentrations at the loading points do not influence the mid-span region. - ASTM E290 / IS 1599 specify **L/d ≥ 16:1** to ensure the test is governed by **pure flexure** and the derived E represents the true material property. #### Part (b) — Numerical Problem **Given:** - b = 20 mm, d = 5 mm, L = 90 mm **Step 1: Determine the slope (m) from the load-deflection data** The data appears linear. Using a two-point method: $$ m = \frac{\Delta F}{\Delta \delta} = \frac{500 - 100}{0.53 - 0.10} = \frac{400}{0.43} \approx 930.23 \; \text{N/mm} $$ **Verification using least squares (optional for KTU record):** - Mean F = 300 N, Mean δ = 0.316 mm - Σ(F−F̄)(δ−δ̄) = (100−300)(0.10−0.316) + (200−300)(0.21−0.316) + (300−300)(0.32−0.316) + (400−300)(0.42−0.316) + (500−300)(0.53−0.316) - = (−200)(−0.216) + (−100)(−0.106) + 0 + (100)(0.104) + (200)(0.214) - = 43.2 + 10.6 + 0 + 10.4 + 42.8 = 107.0 - Σ(δ−δ̄)² = 0.0467 + 0.0112 + 0.0000016 + 0.0108 + 0.0458 = 0.1145 - m = 107.0 / 0.1145 ≈ **934.5 N/mm** **Step 2: Calculate I** $$ I = \frac{b d^3}{12} = \frac{20 \times 5^3}{12} = \frac{20 \times 125}{12} = 208.33 \; \text{mm}^4 $$ **Step 3: Calculate Flexural Modulus E** $$ E = \frac{m L^3}{4 b d^3} = \frac{934.5 \times (90)^3}{4 \times 20 \times (5)^3} $$ $$ E = \frac{934.5 \times 729{,}000}{4 \times 20 \times 125} = \frac{6.812 \times 10^8}{10{,}000} $$ $$ \boxed{E = 68{,}125 \; \text{N/mm}^2 \approx 68.13 \; \text{GPa}} $$ **Step 4: Calculate Flexural Stiffness S** $$ S = E \times I = 68{,}125 \times 208.33 = 1.42 \times 10^7 \; \text{N·mm}^2 $$ $$ \boxed{S \approx 14.2 \; \text{kN·m}^2} $$ **Step 5: Comparison:** Standard value of E for pure copper is 110–130 GPa. The experimental value (68 GPa) is lower, likely due to: - Specimen surface roughness or slight curvature - Compliance in the loading fixture - Limited resolution of the deflection measurement - Non-ideal contact at the supports > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > > 1. **Forgetting to cube the depth (d³)** in the denominator of the E formula — this is the single most common calculation error. Always cross-check units. > 2. **Mixing up m and F·δ**: The slope **m** is used in $E = \frac{m L^3}{4 b d^3}$, NOT the load–deflection product F·δ. > 3. **Unit inconsistency**: If the span L is in **mm** and the slope m is in **N/mm**, the resulting E will be in **N/mm²** (MPa). Convert to GPa only at the end. > 4. **Including the plastic region**: Only the **linear-elastic** portion of the F–δ curve should be used. Including post-yield points will give a **lower (incorrect) value of E**. > 5. **Ignoring L/d ratio**: Students often forget to verify that their specimen geometry satisfies the ASTM requirement. If not, the E value is not a true flexural modulus. ## 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Summary — Read this 5 minutes before your lab record submission or viva.** - ✅ **Test Name:** Three-Point Bend Test (Flexural Test) on a Universal Testing Machine (UTM). - ✅ **Specimens:** Mild Steel, Copper, and Brass — rectangular cross-section. - ✅ **Governing Standards:** ASTM E290 (metals), IS 1599, and IS 1719. - ✅ **Critical Geometric Requirement:** **L/d ≥ 16:1** (Span-to-depth ratio). - ✅ **Loading Geometry:** Two simple supports (bottom), one central loading nose (top). - ✅ **Key Outputs:** Flexural Modulus (E in GPa) and Flexural Stiffness (S = EI in kN·m²). - ✅ **Master Formula (E):** $E = \dfrac{F L^3}{4 b d^3 \delta} = \dfrac{m L^3}{4 b^3 d}$ (using slope m). - ✅ **Master Formula (S):** $S = E \cdot I = \dfrac{F L^3}{48 \delta} = \dfrac{m L^3}{48}$. - ✅ **Second Moment of Area:** $I = \dfrac{b d^3}{12}$ (for rectangular section). - ✅ **Maximum Bending Moment:** $M_{max} = \dfrac{F L}{4}$ (at mid-span). - ✅ **Maximum Flexural Stress:** $\sigma_f = \dfrac{3 F L}{2 b d^2}$ (at outer fibers, mid-span). - ✅ **Stiffness Ranking (Expected):** Mild Steel > Copper > Brass (correlates with Young's Modulus). - ✅ **Curve Analysis:** Use only the **linear-elastic region** of the F vs δ plot. - ✅ **Common Sources of Error:** Specimen non-flatness, fixture compliance, friction at supports, misalignment of the loading nose. - ✅ **Unit Conversions:** 1 GPa = 1000 MPa = 1000 N/mm²; 1 kN·m² = 10⁷ N·mm²; 1 kgf ≈ 9.81 N. - ✅ **Viva-Favorite Questions:** - "Why is the flexural modulus of a beam test specimen not equal to Young's Modulus from a tensile test?" - "What happens if L/d < 16?" - "Why do we use the slope of the F–δ curve and not a single (F, δ) point?"

topics.title: Notch Toughness at Room Temperature Using Izod and Charpy Impact Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMEL308 - MATERIALS TESTING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Notch Toughness at Room Temperature Using Izod and Charpy Impact Testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Notch Toughness** is defined as the ability of a material to absorb energy and undergo **plastic deformation** in the presence of a stress concentrator (notch) when subjected to a **sudden, dynamic, and high-velocity impact load** at a specified temperature. It is quantitatively measured as the **Impact Energy** (in Joules) absorbed by a notched specimen during fracture, divided by the **net cross-sectional area** at the notch root, giving the **Impact Strength** (J/mm² or kJ/m²).

**Impact Testing** is a high-strain-rate destructive test that quantifies the energy required to fracture a notched specimen using a swinging pendulum (per ASTM E23 / IS 1757 / IS 1598 standards). The two internationally standardized geometries employed in KTU laboratories are:
