1. **Charpy Impact Test** — *Simply Supported Beam (SSB) configuration*. The specimen rests horizontally on two anvils and is struck behind the notch by the pendulum.
2. **Izod Impact Test** — *Cantilever (built-in) configuration*. The specimen is clamped vertically as a cantilever with the notch facing the pendulum striker.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> Imagine snapping a **chocolate bar** versus snapping a **biscuit stick**:
> - The chocolate is **tough** — it bends, deforms, and absorbs a lot of energy before snapping.
> - The biscuit is **brittle** — it shatters almost instantly with very little energy.
>
> Now introduce a **small saw-cut (notch)** on the bottom of each before the snap. The notch acts as a **stress magnifier**, forcing the crack to start there. **Notch toughness** is essentially the *measured effort needed* to perform that snap after the cut is introduced. The impact test simply uses a calibrated swinging hammer (the pendulum) to deliver this snap in a standardized, reproducible, high-velocity manner.

### 1.3 Key Terminology Anchored to KTU 2024 Lab Records

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Vocabulary for Lab Records and Viva:**
> - **Pendulum Hammer** — The calibrated swinging arm of mass $M$ and length $R$ that delivers the impact.
> - **Striking Edge (Tup)** — The hardened cylindrical striking tip of the pendulum, usually of radius $8$ mm for Charpy and $12.7$ mm for Izod.
> - **Anvil Span** — The fixed distance between the two supports for the Charpy test, standardized at **$L = 40$ mm** (centre-to-centre).
> - **Dial Indicator / Digital Encoder** — Records the **swing-up angle** $\beta$ of the pendulum after fracture.
> - **Specimen** — A precisely machined, notched bar of standardized dimensions (see Section 4 for schematics).
> - **V-Notch** — A $45°$ included-angle notch with a root radius of $0.25$ mm and depth $2$ mm (most common).
> - **U-Notch** — A $5$ mm deep semicircular notch of root radius $1$ mm.
> - **Fracture Appearance** — Whether the fracture is **brittle** (crystalline, shiny) or **ductile** (fibrous, dull) — directly tied to **% Shear** and **% Crystallinity** in the fracture surface.

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> - **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \ \text{m/s}^2$
> - **Standard specimen cross-section:** $10 \ \text{mm} \times 10 \ \text{mm}$
> - **Standard specimen length:** $55 \ \text{mm}$
> - **Standard test temperature:** $27 \ \pm \ 2 \ ^\circ\text{C}$ (Room Temperature)
> - **Initial pendulum potential energy (typical):** $300$ J (for steel) or $150$ J (for low-energy materials)
> - **Impact Strength units (SI):** $\text{J/mm}^2$ or $\text{kJ/m}^2$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Basis of the Test

A material's response to load is strongly governed by three factors:
1. **Strain rate** (rate of loading),
2. **Temperature**, and
3. **Stress state / Triaxiality** (induced by notches).

The impact test deliberately couples **high strain rate** ($\dot{\varepsilon} \approx 10^2 - 10^4 \ \text{s}^{-1}$) with **severe triaxiality** (induced by the notch) to determine the material's **worst-case** fracture behaviour at room temperature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Relevance:** A high tensile-strength steel may fail catastrophically under a sudden shock in a cold environment (e.g., a ship hull in the Arctic). The impact test was developed specifically to **screen materials for brittle-fracture susceptibility** before they enter service. This is the foundation of the famous **DBTT (Ductile-to-Brittle Transition Temperature)** concept in BCC metals.

### 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

1. **Loading the Pendulum:** The pendulum is raised and latched at a known initial angle $\alpha$ (typically $150^\circ$ for a $300$ J machine). This stores a known initial potential energy $E_i$.
2. **Specimen Placement:** The notched specimen is mounted — *horizontally on anvils* (Charpy) or *vertically in a vice* (Izod) — with the notch on the tension side.
3. **Release:** The latch releases the pendulum. The pendulum swings down, gaining kinetic energy equal to the stored $E_i$ at its lowest point.
4. **Fracture Event:** At the bottom of the swing, the striking tup contacts the specimen exactly behind the notch and fractures it in a single impact.
5. **Energy Absorption:** A portion of the pendulum's kinetic energy is consumed in:
   - Elastic and plastic deformation of the specimen,
   - Creation of new fracture surfaces,
   - Vibration, sound, and heat losses.
6. **Swing-Up:** The pendulum continues past the specimen, rising to a lower angle $\beta$ (the **swing-up angle**). The residual potential energy $E_f$ corresponds to this angle.
7. **Measurement:** The **Impact Energy Absorbed** is:
$$E_{\text{absorbed}} = E_i - E_f$$
   The dial gauge or digital encoder directly displays this difference.

### 2.3 The "Why" Behind Charpy vs. Izod Geometries

| Feature | Charpy Test | Izod Test |
|---|---|---|
| **Loading Geometry** | Simply Supported Beam (3-point bend) | Cantilever (fixed-free) |
| **Specimen Orientation** | Horizontal | Vertical (clamped) |
| **Notch Location** | Mid-length, on the tension face | Near the clamped end, facing the striker |
| **Stress State** | Primarily bending stress | Mixed bending + shear at notch |
| **Specimen Re-use** | Cannot re-use (fractures) | Cannot re-use (fractures) |
| **Preferred For** | Comparative ranking, DBTT studies, weld quality | Single-specimen screening, plastics |
| **Standard** | ASTM E23, IS 1598, EN 10045-1 | ASTM E2562, IS 1757, BS 131 |

> [!TIP]
> **Why two tests if both measure the same thing?** They produce *similar trends* but **non-identical absolute values** because the stress state and constraint factor differ. Charpy is the global industrial default (weld qualification, shipbuilding codes). Izod is more common in plastics, foundry quality control, and historical British practice.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Markdown Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **every equation** you must memorize for the KTU 2024 viva, lab report, and university examination on this experiment. **Master these before your record viva.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Definitions | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Initial Pendulum Potential Energy | $E_i = M g R \left(1 - \cos\alpha\right)$ | $M$ = pendulum mass, $R$ = pendulum length, $\alpha$ = initial lift angle | Joules (J) |
| 2 | Final (Residual) Pendulum Potential Energy | $E_f = M g R \left(1 - \cos\beta\right)$ | $\beta$ = swing-up angle after fracture | Joules (J) |
| 3 | Energy Absorbed by Specimen | $E_{\text{abs}} = E_i - E_f = M g R \left(\cos\beta - \cos\alpha\right)$ | (Note: angles measured from vertical) | Joules (J) |
| 4 | Direct Dial Reading | $E_{\text{abs}} = C \left(\beta - \beta_{\text{air}}\right)$ | $C$ = machine calibration constant, $\beta_{\text{air}}$ = air-loss swing | Joules (J) |
| 5 | Net Cross-Sectional Area at Notch (V-notch) | $A_v = b \times \left(h - d_v\right)$ | $b$ = width, $h$ = thickness, $d_v$ = V-notch depth | mm² |
| 6 | Net Cross-Sectional Area at Notch (U-notch) | $A_u = b \times \left(h - d_u\right)$ | $d_u$ = U-notch depth | mm² |
| 7 | **Impact Strength (V-Notch)** | $IS_v = \dfrac{E_{\text{abs}}}{A_v}$ | The **key result** for the lab record | $\text{J/mm}^2$ |
| 8 | **Impact Strength (U-Notch)** | $IS_u = \dfrac{E_{\text{abs}}}{A_u}$ | Used for U-notch specimens | $\text{J/mm}^2$ |
| 9 | Percentage Crystallinity (Fracture Appearance) | $\%C = \dfrac{A_{\text{crystalline}}}{A_{\text{fracture}}} \times 100$ | $A_{\text{crystalline}}$ = shiny cleavage area, $A_{\text{fracture}}$ = total fracture area | Dimensionless % |
| 10 | Percentage Fibrous (Ductile) Appearance | $\%F = 100 - \%C$ | Used in DBTT curves | Dimensionless % |
| 11 | Fracture Energy per Unit Notch Length (Lateral Expansion) | $LE = t_f - t_i$ (in mm) | $t_f$ = final thickness, $t_i$ = original thickness at notch | mm |

### 2.5 Numerical Standard Reference Values (for Validation)

> [!NOTE]
> These are the **typical KTU 2024 expected ball-park values** your report should match within $\pm 10\%$:

| Material | Charpy V-Notch Impact Energy (J) at 27 °C | Application Context |
|---|---|---|
| Mild Steel (MS) | 60 – 110 J | Ship plates, structural sections |
| Medium Carbon Steel (EN8 / AISI 1040) | 30 – 60 J | Shafts, gears |
| High Carbon Steel | 10 – 25 J | Springs, dies |
| Grey Cast Iron | 5 – 12 J | Engine blocks, machine beds |
| Aluminium 6061-T6 | 25 – 40 J | Aerospace, automotive |
| Brass (Cu-Zn 60/40) | 40 – 70 J | Plumbing, valves |
| Copper (pure) | 50 – 90 J | Conductors, busbars |

> [!TIP]
> **Lab Report Validation:** If your experimentally measured value differs by more than $50\%$ from this table, **stop and recheck**: (a) specimen dimensions, (b) pendulum calibration, (c) whether the pendulum was released freely or pushed, and (d) whether the specimen was correctly aligned.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Weld Qualification:** Shipbuilding and pressure-vessel codes (ASME Sec. IX, AWS D1.1) mandate minimum Charpy V-notch values (typically $\geq 27$ J at $-20 \ ^\circ\text{C}$ for the weld metal and HAZ).
- **Material Selection for Low-Temperature Service:** Arctic pipelines, LNG tanks, aerospace structures.
- **Quality Control in Foundries:** Each batch of cast iron is impact-tested to detect embrittlement from excess pearlite, phosphorus, or improper inoculation.
- **Failure Analysis:** The classic *Liberty Ship* failures of WWII were the catalyst for the modern Charpy-based fracture-toughness requirement in naval architecture.
- **Plastics & Polymer Industry:** Izod impact is the standard ASTM D256 test for thermoplastics.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Energy Absorbed by a Pendulum

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **You must derive this in your record.** The examiner checks whether you understand *where the dial reading comes from*. Below is the full, line-by-line derivation that earns full marks.

**Assumption:** The pendulum is a rigid body of mass $M$ with its centre of mass at distance $R$ from the pivot.

**Step 1: Initial Potential Energy**

When the pendulum is latched at angle $\alpha$ from the vertical, its centre of mass is raised by a vertical height:

$$h_i = R - R\cos\alpha = R\left(1 - \cos\alpha\right)$$

By definition of gravitational potential energy:

$$E_i = M g h_i = M g R \left(1 - \cos\alpha\right)$$

**Step 2: Final (Residual) Potential Energy After Fracture**

After fracturing the specimen, the pendulum rises to a smaller angle $\beta$ (the swing-up angle). The vertical rise of the centre of mass is:

$$h_f = R - R\cos\beta = R\left(1 - \cos\beta\right)$$

So the residual energy is:

$$E_f = M g R \left(1 - \cos\beta\right)$$

**Step 3: Energy Conservation and the Work Done on the Specimen**

By the **Work-Energy Theorem**, the energy lost by the pendulum equals the work done on the specimen (neglecting air resistance, friction at the pivot, and vibration losses, which are corrected by the air-loss calibration):

$$E_{\text{absorbed}} = E_i - E_f$$

Substituting:

$$E_{\text{absorbed}} = M g R \left(1 - \cos\alpha\right) - M g R \left(1 - \cos\beta\right)$$

Factoring out the common term $MgR$:

$$E_{\text{absorbed}} = M g R \left[\left(1 - \cos\alpha\right) - \left(1 - \cos\beta\right)\right]$$

The $\left(1 - 1\right)$ cancels:

$$E_{\text{absorbed}} = M g R \left[\cos\beta - \cos\alpha\right]$$

**Final General Equation:**

$$\boxed{E_{\text{absorbed}} = M g R \left(\cos\beta - \cos\alpha\right)}$$

**Step 4: Air-Loss Correction (for High-Precision Lab Work)**

The pendulum, even with no specimen, loses some energy to air drag. The calibration constant $C$ (in J/degree) is pre-computed by the manufacturer for a no-load (air) swing. If the air-loss swing is $\beta_{\text{air}}$:

$$E_{\text{abs}} = M g R \left[\cos\beta_{\text{air}} - \cos\alpha\right] - M g R \left[\cos\beta - \cos\alpha\right]$$

Simplifying, the **net corrected energy** is:

$$E_{\text{abs,corr}} = C \left(\beta - \beta_{\text{air}}\right)$$

where $C$ is the machine's **scale factor** (J per degree of swing).

### 3.2 Worked Example 1: Charpy V-Notch on Mild Steel

**Given Data:**
- Pendulum: $E_i = 300$ J, $\alpha = 140^\circ$
- Air-loss swing: $\beta_{\text{air}} = 0.5^\circ$
- Specimen: $b = 10$ mm, $h = 10$ mm, V-notch depth $d_v = 2$ mm
- Observed swing-up: $\beta = 130^\circ$

**Step A: Net Cross-Sectional Area at Notch**

$$A_v = b \times \left(h - d_v\right) = 10 \times \left(10 - 2\right) = 10 \times 8 = 80 \ \text{mm}^2$$

**Step B: Raw Energy Absorbed (Trigonometric Form)**

We use the dial form for simplicity. First compute $\cos\alpha$ and $\cos\beta$:

$$\cos 140^\circ = -0.7660, \quad \cos 130^\circ = -0.6428$$

Assuming a calibration constant of $C = 1.2$ J/degree (typical for 300 J machine):

$$E_{\text{abs,corr}} = 1.2 \times \left(130 - 0.5\right) = 1.2 \times 129.5 = 155.4 \ \text{J}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In a real lab, the dial directly displays this value. The full trigonometric calculation would yield the same number.

**Step C: Impact Strength (Charpy V-Notch)**

$$IS_v = \frac{E_{\text{abs,corr}}}{A_v} = \frac{155.4 \ \text{J}}{80 \ \text{mm}^2} = 1.9425 \ \text{J/mm}^2$$

Converting to SI units:

$$IS_v = 1.9425 \ \text{J/mm}^2 \times \frac{10^3 \ \text{mm}^2}{1 \ \text{m}^2} = 1942.5 \ \text{kJ/m}^2$$

> [!TIP]
> A value of $\sim 1.9 \ \text{J/mm}^2$ corresponds to the **upper end of mild-steel Charpy toughness** — fully consistent with the table in Section 2.5.

### 3.3 Worked Example 2: Izod V-Notch on Cast Iron

**Given Data:**
- Specimen: $b = 10$ mm, $h = 10$ mm, V-notch depth $d_v = 2$ mm
- Energy absorbed from dial: $E_{\text{abs}} = 8.5$ J

**Step A: Net Cross-Sectional Area (Izod uses the same V-notch geometry)**

$$A_v = 10 \times 8 = 80 \ \text{mm}^2$$

**Step B: Impact Strength**

$$IS_{\text{Izod}} = \frac{8.5 \ \text{J}}{80 \ \text{mm}^2} = 0.10625 \ \text{J/mm}^2 = 106.25 \ \text{kJ/m}^2$$

This value matches the brittle behaviour of grey cast iron (5 – 12 J). ✓

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Charpy vs. Izod

| Parameter | Charpy V-Notch (Mild Steel) | Izod V-Notch (Same Mild Steel) | Observation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Standard | ASTM E23 / IS 1598 | IS 1757 / ASTM D256 | Different norms |
| Configuration | Horizontal (SSB) | Vertical (Cantilever) | Stress state differs |
| Notch Position | Mid-span, top | 22 mm from clamped end | Geometry differs |
| Typical Energy (MS) | 60 – 110 J | 50 – 90 J | Charpy slightly higher due to constraint |
| Stiffness / Constraint | Lower (free outer fibres) | Higher (clamped base) | Izod produces higher triaxiality |

### 3.5 Sources of Error — Why Lab Marks Are Deducted

> [!WARNING]
> Common KTU valuation deductions:
> 1. **Not reporting units** for impact strength — always write $\text{J/mm}^2$ AND $\text{kJ/m}^2$.
> 2. **Forgetting air-loss correction** — losing 1 – 2 marks.
> 3. **Wrong net area formula** for U-notch vs V-notch — losing 2 marks.
> 4. **No diagram** of specimen with dimensions — losing 1 – 2 marks.
> 5. **Not reporting the temperature** of the test — losing 1 mark.
> 6. **Confusing Charpy with Izod** in the conclusion — losing 2 marks.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Experimental Procedure (Both Tests)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Specimen Ready, Dimensions Measured] --> B{Test Type?}
    B -- Charpy --> C[Mount Specimen Horizontally on Two Anvils, Notch Facing Away from Striker]
    B -- Izod --> D[Clamp Specimen Vertically in Vice, Notch Facing Striker]
    C --> E[Raise Pendulum to Initial Angle alpha and Latch]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Release Pendulum Freely]
    F --> G[Striking Tup Fractures Specimen]
    G --> H[Record Swing-Up Angle beta from Dial Indicator]
    H --> I[Compute Energy Absorbed E_abs using Calibration Constant C]
    I --> J[Measure Fracture Surface for percent Crystallinity and Lateral Expansion]
    J --> K[Compute Net Cross-Sectional Area A_v or A_u]
    K --> L[Calculate Impact Strength IS = E_abs divided by A]
    L --> M[Tabulate Result and Compare with Standard Reference]
    M --> N[End of Experiment]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block: Functional Architecture of the Impact Testing Machine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Pendulum Hammer Mass M] --> P2[Length R to Pivot]
    P2 --> P3[Latch Mechanism at Angle alpha]
    P3 --> P4[Striking Tup at Bottom of Arc]
    P4 --> S1[Charpy Specimen on Anvils]
    P4 --> S2[Izod Specimen in Vice]
    S1 --> D1[Dial Encoder Reads beta]
    S2 --> D1
    D1 --> C1[Calibration Module C J per degree]
    C1 --> O1[Digital Display: Energy Absorbed in Joules]
    O1 --> R1[Result: Impact Strength in J per mm squared]
```

### 4.3 Schematic Description of Specimen Geometries

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Mermaid cannot render engineering cross-sections with hatched lines, so the geometries are described textually with all critical dimensions. **Reproduce these as neat, dimensioned sketches in your lab record.**

**Charpy V-Notch Specimen (Top View & Side View):**

```
        Side View (showing notch profile):
        +-------------------------------+
        |                               |   ← Length: 55 mm
        |          ___________          |
        |         /           \         |   ← V-notch:
        |        /  45° angle  \        |     - Depth: 2 mm
        |       /  Root radius  \       |     - Included angle: 45°

        |_____/_________________\______|
        |<---->|
         10 mm
        (thickness = 10 mm, width = 10 mm)
```

**Charpy U-Notch Specimen (Cross-Section):**

```
        Cross-section at notch:
        +----------+
        |          |   ← Total width: 10 mm
        |   ___    |
        |  /   \   |   ← U-notch:
        |  |   |   |     - Depth: 5 mm
        |  | 1mm|  |     - Root radius: 1 mm
        |  | R  |  |
        |__|___|__|
```

**Izod V-Notch Specimen (Front View):**

```
        +-----------+
        |  CLAMPED  |   ← Top 22 mm held in vice
        |  REGION   |
        |           |
        |  +-----+  |   ← V-notch:
        |  |  V  |  |     - Located 22 mm from top
        |  |  \  |  |     - Depth: 2 mm
        |  |   \ |  |     - Opening on striker side
        |  |    \|  |
        |__+-----+__|
        |           |   ← Lower 33 mm is free
        |           |
        +-----------+
        Total Length: 75 mm
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Position of Notch Relative to Striker

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Charpy_Setup
        direction LR
        A1[Left Anvil] --> B1[Specimen: Notch at Centre, on Tension Side]
        B1 --> C1[Right Anvil]

    end
    subgraph Izod_Setup
        direction LR
        A2[Clamp at Top] --> B2[Specimen Held Vertically]

    B -- V-Notch --> C[Area = width times height minus 2 mm]
    B -- U-Notch --> D[Area = width times height minus 5 mm]

    E --> F[Report Value in J per mm squared and kJ per metre squared]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. Define Notch Toughness. Why is the impact test performed on a notched specimen rather than a smooth specimen?**
`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Notch Toughness (1 mark):**
Notch toughness is the ability of a material to absorb energy and undergo plastic deformation in the presence of a stress concentrator (notch) under a sudden impact load. It is measured as the energy absorbed (in Joules) by a standardized notched specimen when fractured by a single swing of a calibrated pendulum, divided by the net cross-sectional area at the notch.

**Why a notched specimen is used (2 marks):**
1. The notch acts as a **stress concentrator**, localizing the maximum stress at the notch root. This ensures fracture initiates at a precisely defined location and produces consistent, reproducible results.
2. The notch introduces a **triaxial (plane-strain) stress state**, which simulates the worst-case service condition for the material. This is critical for screening **brittle-fracture susceptibility**, which is most likely to occur at stress raisers (e.g., sharp corners, weld defects, machining marks in service).
3. A smooth specimen would be much more resistant to fracture and would not adequately differentiate between materials of varying brittle-fracture tendency. The notch magnifies the differences, making the test a sensitive quality-control tool.

---

**Q2. Differentiate between Charpy and Izod impact tests. State two situations in industry where each is preferred.**
`[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Charpy Test | Izod Test |
|---|---|---|
| Specimen Position | Horizontal (simply supported beam) | Vertical (cantilever, clamped) |
| Notch Position | Mid-length, on tension face | 22 mm from clamped end |
| Stress State | Pure bending at notch | Mixed bending + shear at notch |
| Striking Direction | Behind the notch | Above the notch (same side) |

**Industrial Preferences (1 mark for any two):**
- **Charpy is preferred** in (a) shipbuilding and pressure-vessel weld qualification per ASME Sec. IX, and (b) low-temperature service screening of structural steels.
- **Izod is preferred** in (a) plastics industry per ASTM D256, and (b) foundry quality control for cast iron and non-ferrous alloys.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — Charpy Impact Test on Mild Steel**
`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a) With a neat labelled diagram, describe the Charpy impact test procedure. Discuss the factors that influence the impact energy of a material.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**1. Diagram (2 marks):**
Provide a neat diagram of the Charpy impact testing machine showing:
- Pendulum with mass $M$ and length $R$, raised to angle $\alpha$
- Specimen resting on two anvils (span $40$ mm) with V-notch at the centre, top face
- Striking tup of radius $8$ mm contacting the specimen
- Dial indicator at the side recording swing-up angle $\beta$
- Release latch

**2. Procedure (3 marks):**
1. **Specimen Preparation:** Machine a $55 \times 10 \times 10$ mm bar with a $2$ mm deep V-notch ($45^\circ$, $0.25$ mm root radius) precisely at mid-length.
2. **Mounting:** Place the specimen horizontally on the two anvils such that the notch is on the side **opposite** to the striker (i.e., the tension side).
3. **Pendulum Loading:** Raise the pendulum to a known initial angle $\alpha$ (typically $140^\circ$ or $150^\circ$) and engage the latch. Record $\alpha$ and the machine calibration constant $C$.
4. **Release:** Release the latch. The pendulum swings down, strikes the specimen at mid-span, and fractures it.
5. **Recording:** The pendulum continues to a swing-up angle $\beta$, read from the dial. The dial directly gives the absorbed energy $E_{\text{abs}}$.
6. **Fracture Inspection:** Remove the broken halves and visually inspect for percentage crystallinity (shiny) vs. fibrous (dull) areas.
7. **Calculation:** Compute net area at notch $A = 10 \times 8 = 80$ mm² and impact strength $IS = E_{\text{abs}}/A$.

**3. Factors Influencing Impact Energy (2 marks):**
- **Temperature:** BCC metals (steel, cast iron) show a strong ductile-to-brittle transition; FCC metals (aluminium, copper) are relatively insensitive.
- **Strain Rate:** Higher pendulum speeds reduce absorbed energy for BCC metals.
- **Notch Geometry:** Sharper notches (smaller root radius) give lower impact values.
- **Microstructure:** Fine-grained, tempered martensite gives higher toughness than coarse pearlite or untempered martensite.
- **Alloying:** Phosphorus and sulphur embrittle steel; nickel improves low-temperature toughness.

---

**(b) A Charpy V-notch test on a mild steel specimen gave a swing-up angle of $128^\circ$ from an initial lift of $140^\circ$. The pendulum has a calibration constant of $C = 1.20$ J/° and the no-load air-loss swing is $0.6^\circ$. The specimen dimensions at the notch are $10 \ \text{mm} \times 8 \ \text{mm}$. Calculate the impact strength in $\text{J/mm}^2$ and $\text{kJ/m}^2$.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Corrected Swing Angle (1 mark)**

$$\beta_{\text{corr}} = \beta - \beta_{\text{air}} = 128^\circ - 0.6^\circ = 127.4^\circ$$

**Step 2: Energy Absorbed (2 marks)**

$$E_{\text{abs}} = C \times \beta_{\text{corr}} = 1.20 \ \text{J/}^\circ \times 127.4^\circ = 152.88 \ \text{J}$$

**Step 3: Net Cross-Sectional Area at Notch (1 mark)**

$$A = b \times (h - d_v) = 10 \ \text{mm} \times 8 \ \text{mm} = 80 \ \text{mm}^2$$

**Step 4: Impact Strength in J/mm² (1.5 marks)**

$$IS = \frac{E_{\text{abs}}}{A} = \frac{152.88 \ \text{J}}{80 \ \text{mm}^2} = 1.911 \ \text{J/mm}^2$$

**Step 5: Convert to kJ/m² (1.5 marks)**

$$IS = 1.911 \ \frac{\text{J}}{\text{mm}^2} \times \frac{10^3 \ \text{mm}^2}{1 \ \text{m}^2} \times \frac{1 \ \text{kJ}}{1000 \ \text{J}} = 1911 \ \text{kJ/m}^2$$

**Result:** $IS = 1.911 \ \text{J/mm}^2 = 1911 \ \text{kJ/m}^2$

> [!NOTE]
> This value is consistent with the expected range for **mild steel (60 – 110 J absorbed energy, 0.75 – 1.4 J/mm² strength)**, confirming a properly conducted test.

---

#### **Question B — Izod Impact Test on Cast Iron and Ductile-to-Brittle Transition**
`[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**(a) Explain the Izod impact test with a neat diagram. Compare its specimen geometry and loading mode with the Charpy test.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**1. Diagram (2 marks):**
Neat labelled diagram showing:
- Vertical specimen clamped at the top in a vice
- V-notch on the side facing the striker, located 22 mm below the clamp face
- Striking tup of radius 12.7 mm
- Pendulum striking the specimen above the notch
- Dial indicator

**2. Procedure (2 marks):**
1. Machine an Izod specimen of dimensions $75 \times 10 \times 10$ mm with a $2$ mm deep V-notch.
2. Clamp the specimen vertically with 22 mm held in the vice. The notch must face the striker.
3. Raise the pendulum to $\alpha$ and latch.
4. Release; the pendulum strikes the specimen 22 mm above the notch.
5. Record $\beta$ and compute $E_{\text{abs}}$ as $C(\beta - \beta_{\text{air}})$.
6. Compute impact strength as $E_{\text{abs}} / A$, where $A = 10 \times 8 = 80 \ \text{mm}^2$.

**3. Comparison with Charpy (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Charpy | Izod |
|---|---|---|
| Support | Two anvils (simply supported) | One clamp (cantilever) |
| Specimen length | 55 mm | 75 mm |
| Notch position | Centre of length | 22 mm from top |
| Loading mode | Pure bending at notch | Combined bending + shear at notch |
| Stress state | Lower triaxiality | Higher triaxiality at notch |
| Absorbed energy for same material | Generally higher (more ductile mode) | Generally lower (more constrained) |

---

**(b) An Izod V-notch test on a grey cast iron specimen gave an absorbed energy of $9.2$ J. The specimen dimensions at the notch are $10 \ \text{mm} \times 8 \ \text{mm}$. Calculate the impact strength. Comment on the fracture surface appearance and what it implies about the material's behaviour.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Impact Strength Calculation (3 marks)**

Net area at notch:

$$A = 10 \ \text{mm} \times 8 \ \text{mm} = 80 \ \text{mm}^2$$

Impact strength:

$$IS = \frac{9.2 \ \text{J}}{80 \ \text{mm}^2} = 0.115 \ \text{J/mm}^2 = 115 \ \text{kJ/m}^2$$

**Step 2: Comparison with Standard (1 mark)**

The absorbed energy of $9.2$ J is at the lower end of grey cast iron's range (5 – 12 J), and the impact strength of $115 \ \text{kJ/m}^2$ is consistent with a **brittle material**.

**Step 3: Fracture Surface Comment (3 marks):**

For grey cast iron, the fracture surface will be predominantly **crystalline and shiny** ($\%C > 80\%$) with very little fibrous (dull) area. This appearance indicates:

1. **Brittle Cleavage Fracture:** Failure occurred by cleavage of the ferrite matrix with minimal plastic deformation.
2. **Graphite Flake Stress Raisers:** The flake graphite in grey cast iron acts as internal notches, providing ready crack initiation sites — this is why cast iron is brittle despite having relatively low strength.
3. **Engineering Implication:** Grey cast iron is suitable for **compressive loading** (machine beds, engine blocks) but **must be avoided** for tensile or impact loading (e.g., connecting rods, suspension parts). Ductile cast iron (with nodular graphite) is preferred for such applications because the spheroidal graphite does not act as a sharp stress raiser.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Read This Twice:**
> 1. **Units trap:** Always state **both** $\text{J/mm}^2$ and $\text{kJ/m}^2$. Forgetting one is a 0.5 mark cut.
> 2. **Air-loss correction:** Even if the problem doesn't give $\beta_{\text{air}}$, write in your solution *"air-loss correction assumed negligible as per problem data"*. This shows the examiner you know the concept.
> 3. **Net area trap:** For V-notch use $h - 2$ mm. For U-notch use $h - 5$ mm. **Don't write $h \times d$** — it's the wrong formula.
> 4. **Charpy vs. Izod confusion:** The notch on a Charpy specimen is on the side **opposite** the striker (tension side), but on an Izod specimen the notch is on the **same side** as the striker. Many students get this backwards and lose 1 mark on the diagram.
> 5. **Fracture surface language:** Use the words "**crystalline, shiny, faceted**" for brittle and "**fibrous, dull, ductile**" for tough. Avoid vague terms like "rough" or "smooth".

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- [x] **Notch Toughness** = Energy to fracture a notched specimen under impact (in Joules).
- [x] **Impact Strength** = Notch Toughness ÷ Net Cross-Sectional Area at Notch (in $\text{J/mm}^2$ or $\text{kJ/m}^2$).
- [x] **Charpy Test** = Horizontal, simply supported, notch opposite to striker, span $40$ mm.
- [x] **Izod Test** = Vertical, cantilever, notch on striker side, $22$ mm from clamp.
- [x] **Standard Specimen:** $10 \times 10$ mm cross-section, $55$ mm long (Charpy) or $75$ mm long (Izod).
- [x] **V-Notch Dimensions:** $2$ mm deep, $45^\circ$ included angle, $0.25$ mm root radius.
- [x] **U-Notch Dimensions:** $5$ mm deep, $1$ mm root radius.
- [x] **Net Area Formulas:** V-notch → $b \times (h-2)$; U-notch → $b \times (h-5)$.
- [x] **Energy Formula:** $E_{\text{abs}} = M g R (\cos\beta - \cos\alpha) = C (\beta - \beta_{\text{air}})$.
- [x] **Striking Tup Radii:** Charpy = $8$ mm; Izod = $12.7$ mm.
- [x] **Test Temperature:** Room temperature = $27 \ \pm \ 2^\circ \text{C}$ (must be reported).
- [x] **Typical Values (Mild Steel, V-Notch):** $60$ – $110$ J, $\sim 0.75$ – $1.4$ J/mm².
- [x] **Typical Values (Grey Cast Iron, V-Notch):** $5$ – $12$ J, $\sim 0.06$ – $0.15$ J/mm².
- [x] **BCC metals (steel, cast iron)** show strong temperature dependence; **FCC metals (Al, Cu, austenitic SS)** do not.
- [x] **Brittle fracture surface = crystalline/shiny/faceted**; **Ductile fracture surface = fibrous/dull**.
- [x] **Standards:** Charpy → ASTM E23, IS 1598; Izod → IS 1757, ASTM D256.
- [x] **Application Codes (Charpy):** ASME Sec. IX, AWS D1.1, EN 10045-1 (weld and pressure-vessel qualification).
- [x] **Always report:** pendulum energy capacity, $\alpha$, $\beta$, $C$, specimen dimensions, net area, impact energy, impact strength, fracture surface description, test temperature.
- [x] **Always draw:** A neat labelled diagram of the testing machine **and** the specimen cross-section showing notch dimensions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effect of Coil Type and Arrangement on Spring Stiffness
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMEL308 - MATERIALS TESTING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Effect of Coil Type and Arrangement on Spring Stiffness

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Spring Stiffness (k):** The ratio of the axial load (W) applied to a spring to the axial deflection (δ) produced by it, i.e., $k = \dfrac{W}{\delta}$, expressed in **N/mm** or **N/m**.

**Spring Rate (k)** is the load required to produce a unit deflection in a spring. In the context of the experiment on the "Effect of Coil Type and Arrangement on Spring Stiffness", the stiffness is experimentally determined by progressively loading the spring, measuring the corresponding deflections, and computing the slope of the load–deflection characteristic curve.

**Types of Coils Studied in Module Experiment 2:**

1. **Closed-Coil Helical Spring** — Adjacent coils touch each other when unloaded (no pitch gap, only a small clearance).
2. **Open-Coil Helical Spring** — A clear gap exists between adjacent coils even in the unloaded state.

**Arrangements Studied:**
- **Series Arrangement** — Springs stacked end-to-end; the same load passes through each spring but deflections add.
- **Parallel Arrangement** — Springs placed coaxially side-by-side; the load is shared but the deflection is the same.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plain-English Intuition:** Imagine a rubber band — the *stiffer* the rubber band, the harder you have to pull to stretch it. *Stiffness* quantifies exactly this "resistance to stretching" for any spring. Now imagine two rubber bands tied end-to-end (series): the system is *less* stiff because both stretch together. But if you hold them side-by-side and pull with both hands (parallel), the system feels *stiffer* because you are stretching two at once.

**Real-world analogy for coil type:**
- A *closed-coil* spring is like a tightly wound slinky — touching coils; you must compress it from its natural state.
- An *open-coil* spring is like a stretched slinky — gaps between coils; it can take both tension and compression up to the gap.

**Why coil type matters:** In closed-coil springs, friction between adjacent coils and **initial gap play** alters the effective stiffness, especially at small loads. Open-coil springs exhibit nearly **linear load–deflection** behavior because the coils do not contact each other.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Shear Modulus of Steel | G | **79.3 GPa (≈ 80 GPa)** |
| Shear Modulus of Phosphor Bronze | G | **41–45 GPa** |
| Modulus of Rigidity (CGS) | G | **dynes/cm²** in CGS |
| Spring Stiffness | k | **N/mm** |
| Spring Index | C | $C = D/d$ (typically 4–12) |

> [!TIP]
> The **Spring Index (C)** is the ratio of mean coil diameter (D) to wire diameter (d). It is a critical design parameter — values below 4 cause manufacturing difficulties, while values above 12 cause buckling issues.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Stiffness of a Helical Spring — The Governing Equation

For a **helical spring under axial load (W)**, the stiffness is given by:

$$k = \frac{W}{\delta} = \frac{G \, d^{4}}{8 \, D^{3} \, N}$$

where:
- $G$ = Shear Modulus of the wire material (N/mm²)
- $d$ = Diameter of the wire (mm)
- $D$ = Mean coil diameter (mm)
- $N$ = Number of active coils

## 2.2 Structured Logic — Why Each Term Matters

**Step 1 — Material Property ($G$):**
- Stiffer material (higher G) → stiffer spring (k ↑). Replacing mild steel with chrome-vanadium steel increases G and hence k.

**Step 2 — Wire Diameter ($d^{4}$):**
- Stiffness is **extremely sensitive** to wire diameter (fourth power). Doubling the wire diameter increases stiffness by $2^{4} = 16$ times.

**Step 3 — Mean Coil Diameter ($D^{3}$):**
- Stiffness decreases with the **cube** of mean diameter. A larger coil is much more flexible.

**Step 4 — Number of Active Coils ($N$):**
- Stiffness is **inversely proportional** to N. More coils → more deflection → softer spring.

## 2.3 Closed-Coil vs Open-Coil — Stiffness Behavior

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The theoretical formula $k = \frac{G d^{4}}{8 D^{3} N}$ assumes **shear deformation only** and applies to both closed and open coils. The difference in experimental stiffness arises from secondary effects:
> - **Closed coil:** Frictional inter-coil contact, initial gap closure (toe region of load–deflection curve).
> - **Open coil:** Free initial gap allows near-linear response from the start.

**Experimental Stiffness Calculation:**
$$k_{\text{exp}} = \frac{\Delta W}{\Delta \delta} = \frac{W_{2} - W_{1}}{\delta_{2} - \delta_{1}}$$

## 2.4 Series and Parallel Arrangements

### Series Arrangement
When two springs (k₁ and k₂) are connected end-to-end, the **same load** passes through both, while deflections add:

$$\delta_{\text{total}} = \delta_{1} + \delta_{2}$$

$$\frac{1}{k_{s}} = \frac{1}{k_{1}} + \frac{1}{k_{2}}$$

### Parallel Arrangement
When two springs share the load **coaxially**, the deflection is identical, while the loads add:

$$W_{\text{total}} = W_{1} + W_{2}$$

$$k_{p} = k_{1} + k_{2}$$

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / When to Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $k = \dfrac{G d^{4}}{8 D^{3} N}$ | Stiffness of a helical spring (both closed & open coil) |
| 2 | $k_{\text{exp}} = \dfrac{\Delta W}{\Delta \delta}$ | Experimental stiffness from load–deflection graph |
| 3 | $\dfrac{1}{k_{s}} = \dfrac{1}{k_{1}} + \dfrac{1}{k_{2}}$ | Springs in **series** |
| 4 | $k_{p} = k_{1} + k_{2}$ | Springs in **parallel** |
| 5 | $C = D/d$ | Spring index (4–12 ideal) |
| 6 | $W = k \cdot \delta$ | Load–deflection relation |
| 7 | $G = \dfrac{8 k D^{3} N}{d^{4}}$ | Rearranged to find shear modulus experimentally |
| 8 | $\tau = \dfrac{8 W D}{\pi d^{3}} \cdot K$ | Maximum shear stress in wire (Wahl's factor K for dynamic) |
| 9 | $N_{a} = N_{t} - N_{i}$ | Active coils = total coils − inactive end coils |

> [!NOTE]
> For a helical spring under axial load, Wahl's correction factor for direct shear: $K = \dfrac{4C - 1}{4C - 4} + \dfrac{0.615}{C}$. This corrects for curvature effect and direct shear.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive suspensions:** Series and parallel spring combinations (helper springs) optimize ride comfort vs. load capacity.
- **Railway buffers:** Multiple helical springs in series distribute energy and provide progressive stiffness.
- **Machine tool fixtures:** Open-coil springs isolate vibrations; closed-coil springs are preferred in precision measurement devices.
- **Pressure gauges (Bourdon tubes):** Curved-tube principles similar to open-coil behavior.
- **Aerospace landing gear:** Parallel stack of high-stiffness springs for heavy impact loads.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stiffness of a Helical Spring ($k = Gd^{4}/8D^{3}N$)

**Assumptions:**
- The wire is of uniform cross-section and obeys Hooke's law in shear.
- The helix angle is small (≈ 0° for tightly coiled springs), so the wire is subjected mainly to **torsion**.
- Plane sections remain plane (Saint-Venant's principle applied locally).

**Step 1 — Resolve the axial load into torsion on the wire:**

Consider an element of wire of length $ds$ at the mean radius $R = D/2$. The moment due to axial load $W$ about the axis of the wire is:

$$T = W \cdot R = W \cdot \frac{D}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Relate twist angle to shear strain:**

For a circular cross-section of diameter $d$, the polar moment of area is:

$$J = \frac{\pi d^{4}}{32}$$

The shear stress at the outer fibre is:

$$\tau = \frac{T \cdot (d/2)}{J} = \frac{16 T}{\pi d^{3}}$$

The shear strain at the surface is:

$$\gamma = \frac{\tau}{G} = \frac{16 T}{G \pi d^{3}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the angle of twist per unit length:**

The angle of twist per unit length of the wire is:

$$\frac{d\theta}{ds} = \frac{\gamma}{(d/2)} = \frac{16 T}{G \pi d^{3}} \cdot \frac{2}{d} = \frac{32 T}{G \pi d^{4}}$$

**Step 4 — Integrate along the entire wire length:**

The total length of the wire in a helix of $N$ coils and mean radius $R$ is:

$$L = 2 \pi R \cdot N = \pi D N$$

Total angle of twist:

$$\theta = \int_{0}^{L} \frac{32 T}{G \pi d^{4}} \, ds = \frac{32 T}{G \pi d^{4}} \cdot \pi D N = \frac{32 T D N}{G d^{4}}$$

**Step 5 — Relate the angle of twist to axial deflection:**

For a small axial deflection $\delta$, geometry of the helix gives:

$$\delta = \theta \cdot R = \theta \cdot \frac{D}{2}$$

Substituting the value of $\theta$:

$$\delta = \frac{32 T D N}{G d^{4}} \cdot \frac{D}{2} = \frac{16 T D^{2} N}{G d^{4}}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $T = WD/2$ and rearrange:**

$$\delta = \frac{16 (W D/2) D^{2} N}{G d^{4}} = \frac{8 W D^{3} N}{G d^{4}}$$

**Step 7 — Solve for stiffness $k = W/\delta$:**

$$k = \frac{W}{\delta} = \frac{W}{\dfrac{8 W D^{3} N}{G d^{4}}}$$

$$\boxed{k = \frac{G d^{4}}{8 D^{3} N}}$$

This is the **fundamental stiffness equation for a helical spring**, valid for both closed and open coils when the helix angle is small.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Equivalent Stiffness of Springs in Series

**Setup:** Two springs of stiffness $k_1$ and $k_2$ are connected end-to-end, supported from the ceiling.

**Step 1 — Same load through both springs (Newton's 3rd Law applied to a massless connector):**

$$W = W_1 = W_2$$

**Step 2 — Each spring deflects by:**

$$\delta_1 = \frac{W}{k_1}, \quad \delta_2 = \frac{W}{k_2}$$

**Step 3 — Total deflection is the sum (since deflections are collinear):**

$$\delta_{s} = \delta_1 + \delta_2 = \frac{W}{k_1} + \frac{W}{k_2}$$

**Step 4 — Express in terms of equivalent stiffness $k_s = W/\delta_s$:**

$$\frac{1}{k_s} = \frac{1}{k_1} + \frac{1}{k_2}$$

For $n$ springs in series:

$$\frac{1}{k_{s,\text{total}}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{k_{i}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Equivalent Stiffness of Springs in Parallel

**Setup:** Two springs of stiffness $k_1$ and $k_2$ are placed coaxially between a fixed plate and a movable plate.

**Step 1 — Same deflection in both springs (rigid movable plate):**

$$\delta = \delta_1 = \delta_2$$

**Step 2 — Each spring carries its own share of the load:**

$$W_1 = k_1 \delta, \quad W_2 = k_2 \delta$$

**Step 3 — Total load on the system (force balance on the plate):**

$$W_{p} = W_1 + W_2 = (k_1 + k_2) \delta$$

**Step 4 — Equivalent stiffness:**

$$\boxed{k_{p} = k_1 + k_2}$$

For $n$ springs in parallel:

$$k_{p,\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} k_{i}$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam-Style)

**Problem:** A helical compression spring has wire diameter $d = 6$ mm, mean coil diameter $D = 60$ mm, and 10 active coils. The spring is made of steel with $G = 80$ GPa. Calculate:
(a) The spring stiffness.
(b) The deflection when a load of 200 N is applied.
(c) The equivalent stiffness when two such identical springs are connected (i) in series and (ii) in parallel.

**Solution (a) — Spring stiffness:**

$$k = \frac{G d^{4}}{8 D^{3} N} = \frac{80 \times 10^{3} \times 6^{4}}{8 \times 60^{3} \times 10}$$

Numerator: $80 \times 10^{3} \times 1296 = 1.0368 \times 10^{8}$ N·mm³

Denominator: $8 \times 216000 \times 10 = 1.728 \times 10^{7}$ mm⁴

$$k = \frac{1.0368 \times 10^{8}}{1.728 \times 10^{7}} = 6.0 \text{ N/mm}$$

**Solution (b) — Deflection at W = 200 N:**

$$\delta = \frac{W}{k} = \frac{200}{6.0} = 33.33 \text{ mm}$$

**Solution (c-i) — Stiffness in series:**

$$\frac{1}{k_s} = \frac{1}{6} + \frac{1}{6} = \frac{2}{6} = \frac{1}{3}$$

$$k_s = 3.0 \text{ N/mm}$$

**Solution (c-ii) — Stiffness in parallel:**

$$k_p = 6.0 + 6.0 = 12.0 \text{ N/mm}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Sanity check:** Series stiffness is always **less than** the smallest individual spring (3 < 6), and parallel stiffness is always **greater than** the largest individual spring (12 > 6). This is a great way to verify your final answer in the exam.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Closed-Coil Helical | Open-Coil Helical | Springs in Series | Springs in Parallel |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Initial Behavior** | Non-linear toe region | Linear from start | Same as individual | Same as individual |
| **Experimental k** | Slightly higher than theoretical | Matches theory closely | $k_s < k_1, k_2$ | $k_p > k_1, k_2$ |
| **Load Sharing** | Single spring | Single spring | Equal load | Load split (if k differ) |
| **Deflection** | Deflection of one | Deflection of one | Sum of deflections | Same deflection |
| **Equivalent Stiffness Formula** | $k = Gd^4/8D^3N$ | $k = Gd^4/8D^3N$ | $1/k_s = \sum 1/k_i$ | $k_p = \sum k_i$ |
| **Typical Application** | Precision instruments | Vibration isolation | Progressive buffers | Load-sharing stacks |
| **Friction Effect** | Present (inter-coil) | Negligible | Depends on connectors | Depends on alignment |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4.1 Classification & Effect Flow Diagram (Mermaid)

    P1 --> P2["Plot Load vs Deflection Graph"]

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Visual Reading Aid:** In the *Series* block, the springs are stacked vertically — a single load path. In the *Parallel* block, both springs connect the same two plates — they share deflection, not load. This geometric difference is the root of the two stiffness formulas.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** Define spring stiffness. How is it determined experimentally from a load–deflection graph?

**Model Answer:**
Spring stiffness (k) is defined as the load (W) required to produce a unit axial deflection (δ) in a spring. Mathematically, $k = W/\delta$, with units of N/mm.

**Experimental Determination:**
A spring is subjected to gradually increasing axial loads using a loading frame, and the corresponding deflections are measured with a dial gauge. A graph of load (Y-axis) versus deflection (X-axis) is plotted. The slope of the linear portion of this curve gives the experimental stiffness:

$$k_{\text{exp}} = \frac{\Delta W}{\Delta \delta} = \frac{W_{2} - W_{1}}{\delta_{2} - \delta_{1}}$$

---

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** Distinguish between a closed-coil helical spring and an open-coil helical spring in terms of construction and stiffness behavior.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Closed-Coil Spring | Open-Coil Spring |
|---|---|---|
| Construction | Adjacent coils touch each other when unloaded | Clear gap between adjacent coils at rest |
| Initial Loading | Non-linear toe region due to gap closure | Linear response from the start |
| Inter-coil Friction | Present | Negligible |
| Experimental k vs Theoretical k | Slightly higher than theoretical | Matches theory very closely |
| Typical Use | Precision measuring instruments | Vibration isolators, general purpose |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often **confuse the helix angle assumption** — the formula $k = Gd^4/8D^3N$ is valid only when the helix angle is small (< 10°). If the angle is large, use $\cos\alpha$ correction.
> - For **series arrangement**, students mistakenly *add* stiffnesses. Remember: it is *reciprocals* that add.
> - **Units of G** must be in N/mm², NOT GPa. Convert: 80 GPa = 80 × 10³ N/mm².
> - Do not forget to use **active coils** $N_a = N_t - N_i$ (inactive end coils for plain ends, ground ends reduce to 0 inactive).
> - In the **load–deflection graph**, the toe region should be **excluded** when calculating the slope, or the experimental k will be underestimated.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the stiffness of a helical spring: $k = \dfrac{G d^{4}}{8 D^{3} N}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A helical compression spring has a mean coil diameter of 50 mm, wire diameter of 5 mm, and 8 active coils. The material has a shear modulus of 80 GPa. Find: (i) the spring stiffness, (ii) the deflection under a load of 150 N, and (iii) the equivalent stiffness if two such identical springs are placed in parallel. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Derivation:** (Follow the 7-step derivation given in **Section 3.1** of these notes. Key checkpoints for the examiner:)

1. Moment on the wire cross-section: $T = WD/2$. **(1 mark)**
2. Polar moment of area: $J = \pi d^4/32$. **(1 mark)**
3. Shear strain at the surface: $\gamma = \tau/G = 16T/(G \pi d^3)$. **(1 mark)**
4. Angle of twist per unit length: $d\theta/ds = \gamma/(d/2) = 32T/(G\pi d^4)$. **(1 mark)**
5. Integration over the full wire length $L = \pi DN$ gives $\theta = 32T D N / (G d^4)$. **(1 mark)**
6. Geometric relation: $\delta = \theta \cdot R = \theta \cdot D/2$. **(1 mark)**
7. Final substitution and simplification: $\delta = 8WD^3N/(Gd^4)$ → $k = Gd^4/(8D^3N)$. **(1 mark)**

**(b) — Numerical Solution:**

Given: $D = 50$ mm, $d = 5$ mm, $N = 8$, $G = 80 \times 10^3$ N/mm², $W = 150$ N.

**(i) Stiffness:**

$$k = \frac{G d^{4}}{8 D^{3} N} = \frac{80 \times 10^{3} \times 5^{4}}{8 \times 50^{3} \times 8}$$

Numerator: $80 \times 10^{3} \times 625 = 5.0 \times 10^{7}$

Denominator: $8 \times 125000 \times 8 = 8.0 \times 10^{6}$

$$k = \frac{5.0 \times 10^{7}}{8.0 \times 10^{6}} = 6.25 \text{ N/mm}$$

**(ii) Deflection at W = 150 N:**

$$\delta = \frac{W}{k} = \frac{150}{6.25} = 24 \text{ mm}$$

**(iii) Equivalent stiffness in parallel (two identical springs):**

$$k_{p} = k + k = 2k = 2 \times 6.25 = 12.5 \text{ N/mm}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**(a)** Two springs of stiffness 200 N/mm and 300 N/mm are connected first in series and then in parallel. Find the equivalent stiffness in each case. Also, explain with a neat sketch the difference in load and deflection behavior in the two arrangements. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In an experiment, the following readings are taken for an open-coil helical spring under increasing load:

| Load W (N) | 0 | 20 | 40 | 60 | 80 | 100 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Deflection δ (mm) | 0 | 1.0 | 2.1 | 3.0 | 4.1 | 5.0 |

Plot the load–deflection graph and determine the experimental spring stiffness. Compare it with the theoretical value $k_{th} = 22$ N/mm, and comment on the agreement. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Series and Parallel Stiffness:**

**Series:**

$$\frac{1}{k_{s}} = \frac{1}{200} + \frac{1}{300} = \frac{3 + 2}{600} = \frac{5}{600}$$

$$k_{s} = 120 \text{ N/mm}$$

**Parallel:**

$$k_{p} = 200 + 300 = 500 \text{ N/mm}$$

**Sketch & Explanation:** (Refer to the Mermaid block in **Section 4.3** of these notes. Mark key features:)

- **Series:** The same load $W$ passes through each spring; deflections $\delta_1$ and $\delta_2$ add: $\delta = \delta_1 + \delta_2$. Equivalent stiffness is *less than* either individual spring.
- **Parallel:** Deflection $\delta$ is identical for both; the applied load $W$ splits as $W = W_1 + W_2$. Equivalent stiffness is *greater than* either individual spring.

**(b) — Load–Deflection Analysis:**

**Plot:** A straight-line graph passing approximately through the origin with W on the Y-axis (0 to 100 N) and δ on the X-axis (0 to 5 mm).

**Experimental Stiffness (using endpoints to avoid the toe region):**

$$k_{\text{exp}} = \frac{\Delta W}{\Delta \delta} = \frac{100 - 0}{5.0 - 0} = 20 \text{ N/mm}$$

**Percentage Deviation from Theory:**

$$\% \text{ Deviation} = \frac{k_{th} - k_{\text{exp}}}{k_{th}} \times 100 = \frac{22 - 20}{22} \times 100 \approx 9.09\%$$

**Comment:** The experimental stiffness (20 N/mm) is slightly less than the theoretical (22 N/mm) — a deviation of about 9%. This small discrepancy is expected and can be attributed to: (i) friction in the loading mechanism, (ii) manufacturing tolerances in wire diameter and coil diameter, (iii) reading errors in the dial gauge, and (iv) non-ideal boundary conditions at the spring ends. Since the deviation is small (< 10%), the **experimental and theoretical results are in good agreement**, validating the formula $k = Gd^4/(8D^3N)$ for an open-coil helical spring.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam**

- **Master Formula:** $k = \dfrac{G d^{4}}{8 D^{3} N}$ — memorize units, every variable, and the exponents.
- **Stiffness is directly proportional to:** $G$ (material), $d^{4}$ (wire diameter).
- **Stiffness is inversely proportional to:** $D^{3}$ (coil diameter), $N$ (active coils).
- **Series arrangement:** $\dfrac{1}{k_s} = \sum \dfrac{1}{k_i}$ — *reciprocals add*. Result is **less than** the smallest k.
- **Parallel arrangement:** $k_p = \sum k_i$ — *stiffnesses add*. Result is **greater than** the largest k.
- **Closed-coil spring:** Initial non-linear "toe region" due to inter-coil contact; experimental k is **slightly higher** than theoretical.
- **Open-coil spring:** Near-linear behavior from the start; experimental k **closely matches** theoretical.
- **Active coils $N_a$:** For plain ends, $N_a = N_t - 2$. For ground ends, $N_a = N_t$.
- **Spring Index $C = D/d$:** Should be in the range 4–12; design implication affects buckling and manufacturing.
- **Units to remember:** $G$ in **N/mm²** (1 GPa = 10³ N/mm²); $k$ in **N/mm**; $W$ in **N**; $\delta$ in **mm**.
- **Load–deflection graph:** Plot W vs δ, take slope of linear portion, exclude the toe region.
- **Sanity check in exam:** $k_s < \min(k_1, k_2) < \max(k_1, k_2) < k_p$ — always true.
- **Common experimental error sources:** Dial gauge parallax, friction in spring seating, inaccurate initial zero, loading rate too fast.
- **KTU exam tip:** Always state *theoretical formula*, then *substitute numerical values with units*, then *final answer in box*. Examiners award partial marks generously when steps are visible.
- **Wahl's factor** is required only for **stress** calculations, not stiffness — do not confuse the two in the exam.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Microstructure of Mild Steel, Copper, Brass, and Aluminium Using Optical Microscope
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMEL308 - MATERIALS TESTING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Microstructure of Mild Steel, Copper, Brass, and Aluminium Using Optical Microscope

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Metallographic Microstructural Examination** is the systematic, destructive-preparatory study of the internal grain architecture, phase distribution, and crystallographic morphology of polycrystalline engineering metals through sectioning, mounting, grinding, polishing, and chemical/electrolytic etching, followed by observation under a calibrated **Optical (Light) Microscope** at magnifications typically ranging from **×50 to ×1000**.

In the context of the KTU PCMEL308 syllabus, this experiment focuses on identifying the characteristic micro-constituents (ferrite, pearlite, α-phase, β-phase, twin bands, etc.) of four industrially critical non-ferrous and ferrous alloys:

| Material | Class | Typical Micro-Constituents Observed |
|---|---|---|
| **Mild Steel** | Ferrous (low-carbon steel) | Ferrite (α-Fe) + Pearlite (Fe₃C lamellae in α-matrix) |
| **Copper (Cu)** | Pure non-ferrous metal | Equiaxed α-Cu grains with annealing twins |
| **Brass (Cu-Zn)** | Non-ferrous alloy (α-brass) | α-phase grains with twin boundaries |
| **Aluminium (Al)** | Pure non-ferrous metal | Equiaxed α-Al grains with sub-boundaries |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The experiment is purely **qualitative identification** — students are NOT required to perform quantitative grain size counting (ASTM E112) unless the viva explicitly demands it. However, the procedural steps MUST be memorized verbatim for the **3-mark short-answer questions**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a fruit cake. The wheat flour, sugar, eggs, and fruits are all mixed and baked together. From the outside, you see only a brown surface — you have NO idea what is inside. Now, suppose you **slice the cake with a knife, polish the cut surface until it is mirror-smooth, and then look at it under a magnifying glass after applying a thin stain**. Suddenly, you can see the raisins (dark spots), the flour matrix (light background), and the sugar crystals (bright specks).

This is exactly what happens in metallography:

- **Slicing** → Sectioning (using abrasive cutter)
- **Polishing to mirror finish** → Grinding + Diamond polishing
- **Applying a stain** → Chemical etching (e.g., Nital, Kalling's reagent)
- **Magnifying glass** → Optical Microscope

The "cake" is the metal, and the "ingredients" you see are the **grains, phases, and inclusions** that determine the mechanical, electrical, and thermal properties of the material.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Microstructure is the "fingerprint" of a metal. Two pieces of mild steel can have identical chemical composition but vastly different mechanical properties simply because their grain sizes differ. This is captured by the famous Hall-Petch relationship.

### 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics

- **Standard Etchants Used:**
  * **Nital (2% HNO₃ in Ethanol)** — for Mild Steel
  * **Kalling's Reagent (100 ml Ethanol + 100 ml HCl + 5 g CuCl₂)** — for Brass
  * **Ammonium Hydroxide + Hydrogen Peroxide (NH₄OH + H₂O₂)** — for Copper
  * **Keller's Reagent (190 ml H₂O + 5 ml HNO₃ + 3 ml HCl + 2 ml HF)** — for Aluminium

- **Standard Magnification Range:** **×100 (overview)** to **×500 (phase identification)**.

- **Hardness Indicator:** Soft metals (Cu, Al, Brass) require **LESS polishing pressure** than hard ferrous alloys.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Underlying Physics of Optical Microscopy in Metallography

An **Optical (Light) Microscope** uses the principle of **specular reflection of visible light** from the polished-and-etched metal surface. The fundamental resolution limit is governed by **Abbe's Diffraction Limit**:

$$d = \frac{0.61 \cdot \lambda}{N.A.}$$

Where:
- $d$ = minimum resolvable distance (typically **~0.2 µm** for optical microscopes)
- $\lambda$ = wavelength of incident light (typically **~550 nm** for green light)
- $N.A.$ = Numerical Aperture of the objective lens

> [!NOTE]
> Since $\lambda$ for visible light cannot be reduced below ~400 nm, optical microscopes have an **absolute upper magnification limit of ~2000×**. Beyond this, an **electron microscope** (SEM/TEM) is required.

### 2.2 The Etching Mechanism — "Why" and "How"

Polishing alone produces a mirror surface that reflects light uniformly → the surface appears **white/featureless** under the microscope. To reveal the microstructure, **selective chemical dissolution** is required:

1. **Grain Boundaries** have higher atomic disorder and energy.
2. The etchant attacks these boundaries **faster** than the grain interiors.
3. This creates micro-grooves at the grain boundaries.
4. Light hitting these grooves scatters at angles that **do not return to the objective lens** → grain boundaries appear **DARK** under the microscope.
5. Grain interiors, being flat, reflect light directly back → appear **BRIGHT**.

| Microstructural Feature | Appearance After Etching |
|---|---|
| Grain Boundaries | Dark thin lines forming polygonal networks |
| Phases (e.g., Ferrite vs Pearlite) | Different shading/brightness |
| Inclusions | Dark or bright spots depending on reflectivity |
| Twin Boundaries | Straight parallel lines within grains |

### 2.3 Material-Specific Microstructural Theory

**2.3.1 Mild Steel (Low-Carbon Steel, <0.25% C)**

At room temperature, mild steel exists as a two-phase aggregate:
- **Ferrite (α-Fe):** BCC crystal structure, soft, ductile. Appears as **white/light equiaxed grains**.
- **Pearlite (α + Fe₃C):** Lamellar mixture of ferrite and cementite. Appears as **dark, fingerprint-like patches**.

The volume fraction of pearlite is governed by the **Lever Rule** applied to the iron-carbon phase diagram at eutectoid temperature:

$$\% \text{ Pearlite} = \frac{C_0 - 0.022}{0.76 - 0.022} \times 100$$

Where $C_0$ is the carbon content of the steel.

**2.3.2 Copper (Pure Cu, FCC)**

- Pure copper solidifies into **equiaxed FCC grains**.
- Due to low stacking fault energy, annealing produces characteristic **annealing twins** — straight parallel bands crossing the grains.
- Grain boundaries appear as **dark polygonal lines** after etching with NH₄OH + H₂O₂.

**2.3.3 Brass (Cu-Zn Alloy)**

- For Zn < 35%, brass is single-phase **α-brass** with FCC structure.
- Grains appear similar to pure copper but with **more prominent annealing twins**.
- Etched using **Kalling's reagent** — produces a brownish tint.

**2.3.4 Aluminium (Pure Al, FCC)**

- Equiaxed grains with **sub-grain boundaries** (low-angle boundaries).
- Etched using **Keller's reagent** — produces sharp, well-defined grain boundaries.
- Grains appear bright with **dark grain boundary networks**.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Abbe Resolution Limit | $d = \frac{0.61 \lambda}{N.A.}$ | µm (micrometres) | Max useful magnification |
| Total Magnification | $M = M_{obj} \times M_{eyepiece}$ | dimensionless | Optical microscope |
| Lever Rule (Pearlite %) | $W_P = \frac{C_0 - 0.022}{0.738}$ | % | Mild steel microstructure |
| ASTM Grain Size Number | $n = 2^{N-1}$ grains/in² | grains/in² | Grain counting (advanced) |
| Hall-Petch Relation | $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k \cdot d^{-1/2}$ | MPa | Strength vs grain size |
| Etching Time (typical) | 5–30 seconds | seconds | Standard metallographic practice |
| Specimen Diameter (standard) | 10–25 mm | mm | Mounted specimen |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pitfall:** Use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute values in any table cell. The above table deliberately uses standard mathematical notation that does NOT conflict with markdown table syntax.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Failure Analysis:** Determining cause of in-service component failure (e.g., cracked gear shaft) by examining microstructure at the fracture surface.
- **Heat Treatment Verification:** Confirming that quenching/tempering produced the expected phase transformation.
- **Quality Control:** Ensuring incoming raw materials meet specified grain size standards (e.g., aerospace-grade aluminium sheets).
- **Weld Inspection:** Studying the heat-affected zone (HAZ) in welded joints.
- **Forensic Engineering:** Identifying unknown metal samples in aircraft accident investigations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Procedural Matrix & Analytical Derivations

### 3.1 The Complete Metallographic Procedure — Exhaustive Step-by-Step

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** This 6-step procedure is the **most frequently asked 14-mark question** in this module. Memorize it in the exact sequence below.

#### Step 1: Sectioning (Cutting)

- A small representative piece is cut from the bulk specimen using an **abrasive cutter** (silicon carbide or alumina blade) under continuous coolant flow.
- Coolant prevents **heat-induced phase transformation** that would alter the true microstructure.
- Cut piece dimensions typically: **10–25 mm diameter, 10–15 mm height**.

#### Step 2: Mounting

- The cut piece is encapsulated in a thermosetting **Bakelite (phenolic resin)** or epoxy puck using a **hydraulic mounting press** at **~140 °C** and **~3000 psi** for ~5–10 minutes.
- Purpose: Provides a uniform geometric shape for easy handling and edge retention during polishing.

#### Step 3: Grinding (Sequential Abrasive Papers)

- Specimen is ground on **SiC abrasive papers** in sequence: **220 grit → 400 grit → 600 grit → 800 grit → 1000 grit → 1200 grit**.
- Water is used as a lubricant and to wash away debris.
- Specimen is rotated **90° between each grade** to remove scratches from the previous grade.
- Each grade is ground for approximately **1–2 minutes** until uniform scratch pattern is achieved.

#### Step 4: Polishing (Diamond/Alumina Suspension)

- **Rough Polishing:** **6 µm diamond paste** on a rotating **canvas/disk** with diamond lubricating fluid.
- **Fine Polishing:** **1 µm diamond paste** on a **velvet/short-nap cloth**.
- **Final Polishing:** **0.05 µm alumina (Al₂O₃) suspension** on a soft cloth for mirror finish.
- Specimen is moved **counter to the disk rotation** to minimize comet-tail artifacts.

#### Step 5: Etching (Selective Chemical Attack)

- Specimen is **dipped or swabbed** with the appropriate etchant for **5–30 seconds**.
- Immediately rinsed with **running water**, then **ethanol**, then **dried with hot air blower**.
- Over-etching → dark, burnt appearance; Under-etching → no visible microstructure.

| Material | Etchant | Composition | Etching Time | Appearance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mild Steel | **2% Nital** | 2 ml HNO₃ + 98 ml Ethanol | 5–15 s | White ferrite + dark pearlite |
| Copper | **NH₄OH + H₂O₂** | Equal parts, freshly mixed | 10–30 s | Equiaxed grains + twins |
| Brass | **Kalling's Reagent** | 100 ml EtOH + 100 ml HCl + 5 g CuCl₂ | 10–30 s | α-grains + twins, brownish |
| Aluminium | **Keller's Reagent** | 190 ml H₂O + 5 ml HNO₃ + 3 ml HCl + 2 ml HF | 10–20 s | Sharp grain boundaries |

#### Step 6: Microscopic Examination

- Etched specimen is placed on the **stage** of the inverted metallurgical microscope.
- Observed first at **×100**, then **×500** for phase identification.
- Photomicrograph is captured and **compared with standard reference micrographs** in atlases.

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Calculation: Magnification and Resolution

**Problem:** An optical microscope has an objective lens of ×40 and an eyepiece of ×10. The numerical aperture is 0.65. Calculate (a) total magnification, and (b) minimum resolvable distance, assuming green light of wavelength 550 nm is used.

**Solution:**

**(a) Total Magnification:**

$$\begin{aligned}
M_{total} &= M_{objective} \times M_{eyepiece} \\
M_{total} &= 40 \times 10 \\
M_{total} &= 400 \times
\end{aligned}$$

**(b) Minimum Resolvable Distance (Abbe Limit):**

$$\begin{aligned}
d &= \frac{0.61 \cdot \lambda}{N.A.} \\
d &= \frac{0.61 \times 550 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}}{0.65} \\
d &= \frac{335.5 \times 10^{-9}}{0.65} \text{ m} \\
d &= 516.15 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m} \\
d &\approx 0.516 \, \mu m
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** $1 \, \mu m = 10^{-6} \, m$, so we multiply $516.15 \times 10^{-9}$ by $10^{6}$ to get $0.516 \, \mu m$. This value is well within the optical microscope's working range, confirming that grain-level features (typically 10–100 µm) are easily resolvable.

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: Identifying Microstructures at a Glance

| Observation Under Microscope | Likely Material | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| White grains + dark "fingerprint" patches | **Mild Steel** | Ferrite (white) + Pearlite (dark lamellar) |
| Polygonal grains + straight parallel twin bands, orange tint | **Copper** | Equiaxed α-Cu + annealing twins |
| Polygonal grains + many twin bands, brownish tint | **Brass** | α-brass single phase with high twin density |
| Bright polygonal grains + sharp dark boundaries, no twins | **Aluminium** | Pure α-Al with sub-boundaries |

### 3.4 Heat Treatment Indicator Logic (Bonus Concept)

The same mild steel can appear **completely different** depending on heat treatment:

| Condition | Microstructure | Appearance |
|---|---|---|
| **As-received (Normalized)** | Fine ferrite + fine pearlite | Uniform white + dark patches |
| **Annealed** | Coarse ferrite + coarse pearlite | Large white grains + thick dark patches |
| **Quenched (Water)** | Martensite (single phase) | Needle-like/acicular dark structure |
| **Tempered** | Tempered martensite + ferrite | Dark with carbide precipitates |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** If asked "what does the microstructure of mild steel look like?", always state the **as-received** condition unless otherwise specified.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Complete Metallographic Workflow

```mermaid

    B --> C[Mounting in Bakelite/Epoxy Puck]
    C --> D[Coarse Grinding 220-600 grit SiC]

    E --> F[Rough Polishing 6 micron Diamond]
    F --> G[Fine Polishing 1 micron Diamond]
    G --> H[Final Polishing 0.05 micron Alumina]
    H --> I[Etching with Specific Reagent]
    I --> J[Rinse with Water then Ethanol]
    J --> K[Dry with Hot Air Blower]
    K --> L[Optical Microscope Examination at x100]
    L --> M{x500 Magnification}
    M --> N[Capture Photomicrograph]
    N --> O[Compare with Standard Atlas]
    O --> P[Identify Phases and Grains]
```
