## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**  
Define metallography. Name the etchant used for mild steel.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> Metallography is the scientific study of the microstructure of metals and alloys, typically performed by sectioning, mounting, grinding, polishing, and etching a specimen, followed by microscopic examination.
>
> The etchant used for **mild steel** is **2% Nital** (2 ml concentrated nitric acid dissolved in 98 ml ethyl alcohol).
>
> *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for procedure mention, 1 mark for correct etchant)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**  
List any four metallographic specimen preparation steps in correct sequence.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The four steps in correct sequence are:

1. **Sectioning** — cutting the specimen with an abrasive cutter.
2. **Mounting** — encapsulating in Bakelite for easy handling.
3. **Grinding** — sequential abrasion on 220, 400, 600, 800, 1000, 1200 grit SiC papers.
4. **Polishing** — using 6 µm, 1 µm diamond paste and 0.05 µm alumina suspension to obtain a mirror finish.

*(0.75 marks per correct step in correct sequence)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** Common marks lost include: (a) writing the steps in **wrong sequence** (e.g., polishing before grinding), (b) mentioning **wrong etchant** for the material (e.g., Keller's reagent for mild steel), (c) **forgetting to rinse and dry** the specimen after etching, and (d) **confusing pearlite with ferrite** in identification. Always specify the **concentration and composition** of the etchant for full credit.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]

**(a)** Describe in detail the metallographic specimen preparation procedure for examining the microstructure of mild steel. List the etchant used and the safety precautions to be followed. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat sketch, describe the microstructure of as-received mild steel as observed under an optical microscope at ×500 magnification. Explain why grain boundaries appear dark. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Specimen Preparation (7 Marks):**

The metallographic preparation of mild steel involves the following sequential steps:

**Step 1 — Sectioning (1 mark):**  
A small piece of mild steel is cut using an abrasive cutter (SiC or alumina blade) under continuous water cooling to avoid heat-induced phase changes. Typical specimen size is 10–25 mm diameter.

**Step 2 — Mounting (1 mark):**  
The cut specimen is mounted in a thermosetting phenolic resin (Bakelite) using a hydraulic mounting press at ~140 °C and ~3000 psi for 5–10 minutes. This provides uniform geometry and edge retention.

**Step 3 — Grinding (1.5 marks):**  
The mounted specimen is ground on SiC papers in sequence: **220 → 400 → 600 → 800 → 1000 → 1200 grit**. Water is used as lubricant. Specimen is rotated 90° between grades. Each grade takes 1–2 minutes until a uniform scratch pattern is obtained.

**Step 4 — Polishing (1.5 marks):**  
- Rough polishing on canvas using **6 µm diamond paste** with lubricant.  
- Fine polishing on velvet/short-nap cloth using **1 µm diamond paste**.  
- Final polishing on soft cloth using **0.05 µm alumina suspension** for mirror finish.  
Specimen is moved counter to disk rotation.

**Step 5 — Etching (1 mark):**  
The polished specimen is etched using **2% Nital** (2 ml HNO₃ + 98 ml ethanol) for 5–15 seconds by swabbing or dipping.

**Step 6 — Rinsing and Drying (0.5 marks):**  
Immediately rinsed with water, then ethanol, then dried with hot air to prevent staining.

**Step 7 — Safety Precautions (0.5 marks):**  
- Wear **safety goggles** during sectioning.  
- Use **gloves** while handling acids (HNO₃, HF if used).  
- Ensure **exhaust ventilation** during etching.  
- Do not touch the polished surface with bare fingers.

---

**Part (b) — Microstructure Identification (7 Marks):**

**Sketch Description (3 marks):**

The microstructure of as-received (normalized) mild steel observed at ×500 shows:

- **White equiaxed grains** of **ferrite (α-iron)** forming a continuous matrix.
- **Dark patches** of **pearlite** distributed at the grain boundaries and within grains.
- Pearlite itself appears as alternating light and dark lamellae (resembling fingerprints) when observed at higher magnifications.

**Why Grain Boundaries Appear Dark (4 marks):**

1. Grain boundaries are regions of atomic misfit and have **higher free energy** than grain interiors.
2. The etchant (Nital) **preferentially attacks** these high-energy regions, creating microscopic grooves along the boundaries.
3. When light from the microscope strikes these grooves, it is **scattered at angles** that do not return to the objective lens.
4. Since less light reaches the observer from the boundary regions, they appear **dark** under the microscope.
5. In contrast, the flat grain interiors reflect light directly back into the objective, appearing **bright/white**.

**Pearlite Identification (additional):**  
Pearlite is a **lamellar aggregate** of alternating ferrite and cementite (Fe₃C) plates formed by the eutectoid decomposition of austenite at 727 °C. The close spacing of these plates (~0.1–1 µm) causes diffuse light scattering, making pearlite appear **dark** at moderate magnifications.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model — Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Compare the microstructures of **pure copper** and **α-brass** as observed under an optical microscope. What etchants are used for each? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the optical microscope setup used for metallographic examination. Explain the role of the objective lens and the numerical aperture. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Microstructural Comparison (7 Marks):**

| Feature | Pure Copper | α-Brass (Cu-Zn, Zn < 35%) |
|---|---|---|
| **Crystal Structure** | FCC | FCC |
| **Etchant Used** | NH₄OH + H₂O₂ (equal parts, freshly mixed) | Kalling's Reagent (100 ml EtOH + 100 ml HCl + 5 g CuCl₂) |
| **Etching Time** | 10–30 seconds | 10–30 seconds |
| **Microstructure Appearance** | Equiaxed polygonal grains with **occasional annealing twins** | Equiaxed grains with **abundant parallel twin bands** |
| **Tint** | Light orange-brown | Deeper brown |
| **Phases Present** | Single α-phase | Single α-phase (no β-phase as Zn < 35%) |

**Similarities (1 mark):** Both are single-phase FCC materials showing equiaxed grain morphology with annealing twins.

**Differences (2 marks):**  
- α-Brass exhibits a **higher density of annealing twins** compared to pure copper due to the lowering of stacking fault energy with Zn addition.  
- The grain boundaries in brass are often more sharply defined due to stronger chemical attack by the chloride-based Kalling's reagent.

**Reason for Annealing Twins (1 mark):**  
Annealing twins form during recrystallization when growth accidents on {111} planes in FCC metals produce mirror-symmetric crystal regions. The twin boundary is a coherent {111} plane with low energy.

---

**Part (b) — Optical Microscope Setup (7 Marks):**

**Components of the Inverted Metallurgical Microscope (3 marks):**

1. **Light Source:** Halogen or tungsten lamp providing intense white light.
2. **Condenser Lens System:** Focuses light onto the specimen.
3. **Field and Aperture Diaphragms:** Control illumination cone and contrast.
4. **Objective Lens (×4, ×10, ×40, ×100):** Primary imaging lens close to specimen.
5. **Eyepiece (×10):** Magnifies the intermediate image for the eye/camera.
6. **Specimen Stage:** Holds the polished specimen with the surface facing **down** (in inverted microscopes).

**Role of Objective Lens (2 marks):**  
The objective lens is the **most critical optical component**. It collects reflected light from the specimen and forms a real, magnified, inverted intermediate image. Its magnification and numerical aperture (N.A.) determine the **resolving power** of the microscope.

**Numerical Aperture and Resolution (2 marks):**  
Numerical aperture is defined as:

$$N.A. = n \cdot \sin(\theta)$$

Where $n$ is the refractive index of the medium between lens and specimen (≈1.0 for air) and $\theta$ is the half-angle of the maximum cone of light entering the lens.

The **minimum resolvable distance** is given by Abbe's equation:

$$d = \frac{0.61 \cdot \lambda}{N.A.}$$

A higher N.A. provides **better resolution** (smaller $d$), enabling clearer distinction between closely spaced microstructural features.

**Practical Implication:**  
For metallurgical microscopes, dry objectives (N.A. ≈ 0.65–0.95) are typically used. Oil-immersion objectives (N.A. up to 1.4) are rarely needed because metals are opaque specimens observed in reflection.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Memorize Before Exam**

- [ ] Metallography = study of internal structure of metals by sectioning, mounting, grinding, polishing, etching, and microscopic examination.
- [ ] **Specimen preparation sequence:** Sectioning → Mounting → Grinding (220 to 1200 grit) → Polishing (6 µm → 1 µm diamond → 0.05 µm alumina) → Etching → Rinsing → Drying.
- [ ] **Etchants — Must Memorize:**
  * **Mild Steel** → **2% Nital** (HNO₃ + Ethanol)
  * **Copper** → **NH₄OH + H₂O₂**
  * **Brass** → **Kalling's Reagent** (EtOH + HCl + CuCl₂)
  * **Aluminium** → **Keller's Reagent** (H₂O + HNO₃ + HCl + HF)
- [ ] **Microstructure Identification:**
  * **Mild Steel:** White ferrite grains + dark pearlite patches (finger-print lamellae at high mag).
  * **Copper:** Equiaxed grains + annealing twins, orange-brown tint.
  * **Brass:** Equiaxed grains + many twin bands, brown tint.
  * **Aluminium:** Bright grains with sharp dark grain boundaries, no twins.
- [ ] **Grain boundaries appear dark** because etchant attacks them faster → forms micro-grooves → scatters light away from objective.
- [ ] **Total Magnification** = $M_{obj} \times M_{eyepiece}$.
- [ ] **Abbe Resolution Limit:** $d = \frac{0.61 \lambda}{N.A.}$ → optical microscopes limited to ~2000× magnification.
- [ ] **Pearlite** is a lamellar mixture of ferrite (α-Fe) + cementite (Fe₃C), formed by eutectoid reaction at 727 °C.
- [ ] **Ferrite** = BCC iron, soft and ductile, appears white after etching.
- [ ] **Annealing twins** are characteristic of FCC metals with low stacking fault energy (Cu, Brass, Austenitic Steel).
- [ ] **Keller's reagent contains HF** — requires special safety precautions (HF-resistant gloves, fume hood).
- [ ] **Inverted metallurgical microscope** is used (specimen faces downward) because metals are opaque and observed in **reflection** mode (not transmission).
- [ ] **Specimen must be rotated 90°** between successive grinding grades to remove scratches from previous grade.
- [ ] **Polishing direction** is **counter to disk rotation** to avoid comet-tail artifacts.
- [ ] **Over-etching** → dark, burnt appearance; **Under-etching** → no visible microstructure.
- [ ] **Always rinse with water then ethanol** after etching to prevent residual etchant staining.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** When asked about ANY metallographic experiment, **always state the safety precautions explicitly** — examiners award 0.5–1 bonus marks for mentioning PPE (gloves, goggles, fume hood). Also, mentioning **why** each step is performed (not just **how**) elevates your answer from average to distinction grade.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fracture Surface Morphology Analysis Using Optical Microscope
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMEL308 - MATERIALS TESTING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Fracture Surface Morphology Analysis Using Optical Microscope

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fracture Surface Morphology Analysis (Fractography)** is the systematic study of the topography, texture, and microstructural features of a fractured surface to deduce the **mode, mechanism, and origin** of failure in a material. When performed using an **Optical Microscope** (stereo-zoom or metallurgical microscope) at magnifications typically ranging from **10× to 1000×**, it is termed **Optical Fractography**, which serves as the primary non-destructive, cost-effective technique in failure analysis.

In the context of **PCMEL308 – Materials Testing Lab**, this experiment trains the student to:
1. Identify the **macroscopic fracture mode** (e.g., cup-and-cone, flat transverse, shear lip).
2. Recognize **microscopic fractographic features** (dimples, cleavage facets, striations, beach marks).
3. Correlate these features with the **loading condition** (tensile, fatigue, impact, creep).

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a material as a **detective novel** and the fracture surface as the **crime scene**. Just as a forensic investigator dusts for fingerprints and reads bullet trajectories, a materials engineer "reads" the fracture surface to reconstruct the final moments before failure.

- **Ductile fracture** is like a slow-motion car crash — the metal stretches, necks, and forms tiny cup-shaped pits (**dimples**) as microvoids coalesce. Visually, the surface looks **fibrous and dull grey**.
- **Brittle fracture** is like a dropped ceramic mug — sudden, with no warning. The surface shows **bright, shiny facets** and **river patterns** where cracks hopped between crystal planes.
- **Fatigue fracture** is like a paper clip bent back and forth — the surface shows **concentric arcs (beach marks)** like the rings of a tree, marking each load cycle.

### Key Terminology (Syllabus Highlights)

> [!NOTE]
> - **Fractography** = "Fracto" (broken) + "Graphy" (writing/picture). The science of picturing the broken.
> - **Dimple** = Microvoid formed by microvoid coalescence (MVC) — signature of ductile failure.
> - **Cleavage Facet** = A flat, shiny crystallographic plane produced when a crack splits a grain — signature of brittle failure.
> - **Beach Mark** = Macroscopic arc marking the position of a fatigue crack front at a specific time.
> - **Striation** = Microscopic parallel ridges on a fatigue fracture surface, each ridge ≈ **one load cycle**.
> - **Ratchet Mark** = Boundary line between two adjacent fatigue crack origins, perpendicular to the beach marks.
> - **Shear Lip** = The slanted, ductile edge region surrounding a flat brittle fracture.

### Role of the Optical Microscope

The **optical microscope** (stereo or metallurgical) is the **first instrument of choice** in fractography because:
- It is **non-destructive** (no vacuum, no coating needed, unlike SEM).
- It provides **true-colour images** — useful for identifying **rust, scale, oxidation tinting**, and **overload paint marks**.
- It has a **large depth of field** (especially stereo microscopes), making rough fracture surfaces easy to image.
- It is **inexpensive, fast, and allows real-time tilt** for studying 3-D topography.

**Standard Magnification Ranges Used in Fractography:**

| Magnification | Typical Use | Example Features Seen |
|---|---|---|
| 5× – 30× | Macroscopic | Beach marks, ratchet marks, crack origin |
| 50× – 200× | Mesoscopic | Cleavage facets, dimple clusters |
| 200× – 1000× | Microscopic | Fine dimples, striations, microcracks |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Fracture Mechanisms

Engineering fractures are classified along **two independent axes**:
- **Axis 1 — Ductility:** Ductile vs. Brittle (governed by % elongation at fracture).
- **Axis 2 — Loading Mode:** Static (monotonic), Cyclic (fatigue), Sustained (creep), or Environmental (SCC).

The **fracture surface morphology** is the visual fingerprint of this (Ductility × Loading) combination.

### 2.2 Macroscopic Fracture Features

A standard **uniaxial tensile specimen** produces a classic fracture profile:

**1. Cup-and-Cone Fracture (Ductile):**
- The fracture occurs at ~45° to the tensile axis.
- One half has a **cup** (concave), the other has a **cone** (convex).
- The central region is **flat and fibrous** (final ductile tearing).
- The outer **shear lip** is slanted and smooth.
- Mechanism: Internal necking → central internal void → growth toward surface → final shear overload.

**2. Flat Transverse Fracture (Brittle):**
- Surface is **perpendicular** to the tensile axis.
- Bright, granular, faceted appearance with no plastic deformation.
- May show **chevron marks** pointing back to the crack origin.
- Mechanism: Cleavage along specific crystallographic planes (e.g., {100} in BCC iron).

**3. Fatigue Fracture:**
- Three distinct macroscopic zones: **(a) Crack Initiation, (b) Crack Propagation, (c) Final Overload**.
- The propagation zone shows **beach marks** (concentric rings).
- The overload zone is similar to a static ductile or brittle fracture depending on material toughness.

### 2.3 Microscopic Fractographic Features — The Core of the Experiment

This is the heart of the lab experiment — students must be able to **identify these features under the optical microscope** and link them to the failure mechanism.

**A. Dimples (Ductile, MVC — Microvoid Coalescence):**
- Equiaxed dimples ⇒ Tensile overload.
- Elongated/parabolic dimples ⇒ Shear overload (dimpled rupture).
- Dimple size ≈ **1–10 µm** for most engineering alloys; resolvable by optical microscope at 500×.

**B. Cleavage (Brittle):**
- **River patterns** = multiple parallel cleavage steps merging into fewer larger steps as the crack accelerates. Rivers point **toward the crack origin** (like rivers flowing downstream).
- **Tongues** = thin plates of twin material (mechanical twinning) protruding from the cleavage surface.
- **Quasi-cleavage** = mixed mode, dimples + small facets, common in **Hydrogen Embrittlement**.

**C. Fatigue Striations (Cyclic):**
- Parallel, regularly spaced ridges.
- Spacing ≈ **0.1–10 µm** depending on stress intensity range (ΔK).
- Each striation ≈ **one load cycle** (in ductile metals).
- In **brittle materials** (ceramics), striations may be absent — only beach marks visible.

**D. Intergranular Fracture:**
- Surface follows **grain boundaries**, giving a "rock-candy" appearance.
- Common in: **Temper Embrittlement, Stress Corrosion Cracking, Creep Rupture, Hydrogen Embrittlement**.

### 2.4 The Optical Microscope — Operational Theory

A **metallurgical (compound) optical microscope** used in fractography differs from a biological microscope in two key ways:
1. **Illumination comes from above** (reflected light) — the specimen is opaque.
2. **Objectives are corrected for "no cover slip"** (infinity-corrected).

**Light Path Components:**

1. **Light Source** — Halogen or LED, 6V/30W typical.
2. **Condenser & Field Diaphragm** — focuses light onto the specimen; controls contrast.
3. **Polarizer / Analyzer** — optional, enhances crystallographic contrast.
4. **Objective Lens** — primary magnification; numerical aperture (NA) determines resolution.
5. **Tube Lens** — forms the image at infinity (infinity-corrected design).
6. **Eyepiece (Ocular)** — secondary magnification; typically 10×.
7. **Beam Splitter** — directs image to eyepiece **or** camera.

**Microscope Adjustment Procedure (Köhler Illumination):**
1. Focus on specimen using coarse and fine focus knobs.
2. Close field diaphragm, bring its image into sharp focus using condenser focus.
3. Centre the field diaphragm using centering screws.
4. Open field diaphragm until it just disappears from the field of view.
5. Adjust aperture diaphragm for best contrast (typically 70–80% of objective NA).

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All symbols use **SI units** unless otherwise stated. **No pipe symbols** in formulas to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total Magnification | $M_{total} = M_{obj} \times M_{oc} \times M_{cam}$ | $M_{obj}$ = objective magnification; $M_{oc}$ = eyepiece magnification; $M_{cam}$ = camera relay magnification | Dimensionless |
| 2 | Numerical Aperture | $NA = n \cdot \sin(\theta)$ | $n$ = refractive index of medium; $\theta$ = half-angle of maximum cone of light entering lens | 0.05 – 0.95 |
| 3 | Abbe Resolution Limit | $d_{min} = \dfrac{0.61 \cdot \lambda}{NA_{obj} + NA_{cond}}$ | $\lambda$ = wavelength of light; $NA_{cond}$ = condenser NA | $\approx 0.2 \;\mu m$ (best case) |
| 4 | Useful Magnification Range | $500 \cdot NA \;\le\; M_{useful} \;\le\; 1000 \cdot NA$ | Empirically, empty magnification occurs above $1000 \cdot NA$ | — |
| 5 | Field of View (FOV) | $FOV_{specimen} = \dfrac{FOV_{eyepiece}}{M_{obj}}$ | $FOV_{eyepiece}$ = field number of eyepiece (e.g., 20 mm) | mm |
| 6 | Working Distance | $WD \approx \dfrac{f_{obj} \cdot n_{medium}}{NA_{obj}}$ | $f_{obj}$ = focal length of objective | mm (fractions of mm for high-power) |
| 7 | Depth of Field (DOF) | $DOF \approx \dfrac{n \cdot \lambda}{NA^2} + \dfrac{n}{M_{obj} \cdot NA}$ | Approximate total depth in focus | $\mu m$ |
| 8 | Mean Striation Spacing (Fatigue Crack Growth) | $\dfrac{da}{dN} = C \cdot (\Delta K)^m$ | Paris Law: $a$ = crack length; $N$ = cycles; $C, m$ = material constants; $\Delta K$ = stress intensity range | m/cycle |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Application |
|---|---|
| Aerospace | Failure analysis of turbine blades, landing gear, fuselage skin (fatigue vs. overload) |
| Automotive | Root-cause of crankshaft, axle, and spring failures |
| Civil | Forensic analysis of collapsed bridges, rebar fractures |
| Power Plants | Creep rupture in boiler tubes, turbine rotors |
| Medical Implants | Fracture of orthopaedic implants (Ti alloys, Co-Cr) |
| Forensic Engineering | Legal disputes over product liability — fracture surface is admissible evidence |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Total Magnification of a Compound Microscope

**Statement:** The total magnification of an optical microscope is the product of the individual magnifications of the objective lens, the eyepiece (ocular), and any camera relay lens.

**Step 1 — Image formation by the objective:**

A simple thin convex lens produces a **real, inverted, magnified image** at its intermediate image plane (the location of the fixed diaphragm inside the eyepiece tube).

$$M_{obj} = \frac{h_i}{h_o} = -\frac{L}{f_{obj}}$$

where $L$ is the optical tube length (160 mm for finite-corrected, infinity for infinity-corrected), $f_{obj}$ is the focal length of the objective, and $h_i$, $h_o$ are the image and object heights, respectively. The negative sign indicates image inversion.

**Step 2 — Image formation by the eyepiece:**

The eyepiece acts as a simple magnifier, viewing the intermediate image produced by the objective. Its angular magnification is:

$$M_{oc} = \frac{D}{f_{oc}}$$

where $D$ is the **near point of distinct vision** (conventionally $D = 250 \text{ mm}$ for a relaxed eye) and $f_{oc}$ is the eyepiece focal length.

**Step 3 — Camera relay magnification (if a digital camera is attached):**

A camera with sensor size $s_{sensor}$ and an adapter giving an effective focal length $f_{cam}$:

$$M_{cam} = \frac{s_{sensor}}{f_{cam}} \cdot \frac{f_{cam}}{250} = \frac{s_{sensor}}{250}$$

**Step 4 — Combine the stages:**

Because magnification is multiplicative along a coaxial optical system:

$$M_{total} = M_{obj} \times M_{oc} \times M_{cam}$$

**Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example:**

A microscope is fitted with a 40× objective, 10× eyepiece, and a camera with 2/3" sensor (11 mm diagonal) on a 1× C-mount. Find $M_{total}$ and the on-screen field of view if the eyepiece field number is 22 mm.

$$M_{obj} = 40$$

$$M_{oc} = 10$$

$$M_{cam} = \frac{11}{250} \times 40 = 1.76$$

Wait — the cleaner convention gives $M_{cam} \approx 1$ for a standard 1× C-mount. Using the **eyepiece field number** method:

$$FOV_{specimen} = \frac{FOV_{eyepiece}}{M_{obj}} = \frac{22 \text{ mm}}{40} = 0.55 \text{ mm}$$

$$M_{total} = 40 \times 10 \times 1 = 400\times$$

> **Interpretation:** A 22 mm circular field of view in the eyepiece, when projected through a 40× objective, becomes a real specimen field of only **0.55 mm** across. The image on the monitor (or film) is **400×** the actual size.

### 3.2 Derivation — Abbe's Resolution Limit

**Statement:** The smallest resolvable distance $d_{min}$ between two points on a specimen is governed by the diffraction of light, and is given by Abbe's criterion.

**Step 1 — Diffraction of the first specimen point:**

When light of wavelength $\lambda$ passes through a specimen detail, it diffracts. The objective lens can only collect light that enters within its acceptance cone, defined by:

$$NA_{obj} = n \cdot \sin(\theta_{obj})$$

**Step 2 — Condition for two points to be just resolved:**

Two points are resolvable when the **maxima of one diffraction pattern coincide with the minima of the other**, which Abbe showed requires the objective to collect at least the **first-order diffracted beam**:

$$d_{min} = \frac{\lambda}{n \cdot \sin(\theta_{obj})} = \frac{\lambda}{NA_{obj}}$$

**Step 3 — Refined Abbe formula (with condenser):**

The condenser also contributes to the cone of light illuminating the specimen. The symmetric form is:

$$d_{min} = \frac{0.61 \cdot \lambda}{NA_{obj} + NA_{cond}}$$

(The factor 0.61 arises from the Rayleigh criterion for two incoherent point sources.)

**Step 4 — Worked Example:**

A 50× metallurgical objective has $NA = 0.55$ (dry), and the condenser is set to $NA_{cond} = 0.40$. Green filter light of $\lambda = 550$ nm is used. Find $d_{min}$.

$$d_{min} = \frac{0.61 \times 550 \times 10^{-9}}{0.55 + 0.40}$$

$$d_{min} = \frac{3.355 \times 10^{-7}}{0.95} = 3.53 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m} = 0.353 \;\mu m$$

**Step 5 — Useful magnification check:**

$$M_{useful,\,max} = 1000 \times NA_{obj} = 1000 \times 0.55 = 550\times$$

So a 1000× magnification on this objective would be **empty magnification** (no new detail resolved). This sets the **practical upper limit of optical fractography** — finer details require SEM.

### 3.3 Derivation — Field of View at the Specimen

**Statement:** The actual area of the specimen visible through the microscope is the eyepiece field number divided by the objective magnification.

**Step 1 — Define the field number (FN):**

The field number of an eyepiece is the diameter (in mm) of the fixed diaphragm at the intermediate image plane.

**Step 2 — Image projection:**

The objective projects this diaphragm onto the specimen plane. By similar triangles, the specimen-side field diameter $D_{spec}$ is:

$$D_{spec} = \frac{FN}{M_{obj}}$$

**Step 3 — Worked Example:**

Eyepiece FN = 20 mm, objective = 10× (low power, for crack overview).

$$D_{spec} = \frac{20}{10} = 2.0 \text{ mm}$$

So a 10× objective gives a 2 mm field of view — enough to locate the crack origin.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Always start fractography at the lowest magnification (stereo microscope, 5–30×)** to find the crack origin, then progressively zoom in. Beginning at high magnification is a **classic beginner's mistake**.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Feature-to-Mechanism Mapping

| Fracture Feature | Microscopic Appearance | Macroscopic Appearance | Mechanism | Material Examples |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Equiaxed dimples** | Hemispherical cups, ~5 µm | Fibrous, grey, dull | Tensile ductile overload | Mild steel, Cu, Al alloys |
| **Elongated dimples** | Parabolic, one side steep | Slanted shear lip | Shear ductile overload | Ductile sheet, plate |
| **Cleavage facets** | Flat, shiny, river patterns | Bright granular | Brittle transgranular | BCC steels at low T, MgO |
| **Quasi-cleavage** | Small facets + fine dimples | Dull fine-grained | Hydrogen embrittlement | High-strength steel |
| **Intergranular** | "Rock candy" polyhedra | Sparkly, faceted | GB separation (SCC, creep) | Sensitized SS, brass |
| **Striations** | Parallel ridges, ~1 µm | — | Fatigue crack growth (Stage II) | All ductile metals |
| **Beach marks** | Concentric arcs | Concentric rings | Fatigue crack front position | All metals in cyclic load |
| **Microvoid coalescence (MVC)** | Dimples on cleavage facets | Mixed | Ductile + brittle competition | Toughened ceramics |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Optical Microscope Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    LS[Light Source Halogen 6V 30W] --> KS[Collector Lens]
    KS --> KF[Field Diaphragm]
    KF --> CL[Condenser Lens]
    CL --> SP[Specimen Fracture Surface]
    SP --> OL[Objective Lens Infinity Corrected]
    OL --> TL[Tube Lens Forms Image at Infinity]
    TL --> BS[Beam Splitter]
    BS --> EP[Eyepiece 10x]
    BS --> CM[Camera Sensor 2/3 inch CCD]
    EP --> EY[Eye of Observer]
    CM --> MN[Monitor Digital Image]
    SP -.-> RP[Rough Surface Tilted Specimen]
    RP --> OL
    style LS fill:#FFF4CC
    style SP fill:#FFCC99
    style OL fill:#99CCFF
    style EP fill:#99CCFF
    style CM fill:#99CCFF
```

**Interpretation:** Light from the source is collected, diaphragmed, and focused by the condenser onto the **fracture surface**. The objective forms a magnified real image, which the tube lens projects to infinity. A beam splitter directs the image **either** to the binocular eyepieces (for direct 3-D stereo viewing) **or** to the digital camera (for measurement and reporting).

### 4.2 Decision Tree — Fracture Type Identification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Fracture Surface Observation] --> B[Macroscopic Visual Examination 1x to 10x]
    B --> C{Distinct Beach Marks Present}
    C -- YES --> D[Fatigue Fracture]
    D --> D1[Locate Origin using Ratchet Marks]
    D1 --> D2[Measure Beach Mark Spacing]
    D2 --> D3[Examine Striations at 500x Optical]
    C -- NO --> E{Intergranular Rock Candy Appearance}
    E -- YES --> F[Environmental or Creep Failure]
    F --> F1[Check for Oxidation or Corrosion Products]
    F1 --> F2[Consider SCC Embrittlement or Temper Embrittlement]
    E -- NO --> G{Plastic Deformation Visible Necking}
    G -- YES --> H[Ductile Fracture]
    H --> H1[Look for Dimples at 500x to 1000x]
    H1 --> H2{Equiaxed or Elongated Dimples}
    H2 -- Equiaxed --> H3[Tensile Overload]
    H2 -- Elongated --> H4[Shear Overload]
    G -- NO --> I[Brittle Fracture]
    I --> I1[Look for Cleavage Facets and River Patterns]
    I1 --> I2[Trace Rivers Back to Origin]
    I2 --> I3[Check for Quasi Cleavage Signs of Hydrogen]
    style A fill:#E0F7FA
    style D fill:#FFCC99
    style F fill:#FFCC99
    style H fill:#FFCC99
    style I fill:#FFCC99
```

**Interpretation:** A systematic top-down identification procedure. Always begin at low magnification, classify the macroscopic mode, then zoom in to confirm with microscopic evidence. The decision branches route the analyst to the appropriate fractographic feature to verify.

### 4.3 Sample Preparation Flow Chart — Fracture Surface Preservation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Fractured Specimen Received] --> P1[Document As Received Photograph]
    P1 --> P2{Is Fracture Surface Contaminated}
    P2 -- YES --> P3[Acetone Rinse in Ultrasonic Bath 5 min]
    P3 --> P4[Air Dry in Desiccator]
    P2 -- NO --> P5[Proceed to Mounting]
    P4 --> P5{Need to Section for Cross Section View}
    P5 -- YES --> P6[Section Carefully Avoid Heating]
    P6 --> P7[Mount in Cold Mounting Epoxy]
    P7 --> P8[Grind and Polish Standard Procedure]
    P5 -- NO --> P9[Use Stereo Zoom Microscope First]
    P9 --> P10[Low Magnification 5x to 30x Overview]
    P10 --> P11{Identify Region of Interest}
    P11 --> P12[Switch to Metallurgical Microscope 50x to 1000x]
    P12 --> P13[Capture Micrographs with Scale Bar]
    P13 --> P14[Identify Features Dimples Cleavage Striations]
    P14 --> P15[Correlate with Loading History]
    P15 --> P16[Write Fractography Report]
    style P0 fill:#E0F7FA
    style P14 fill:#FFE082
    style P16 fill:#A5D6A7
```

**Interpretation:** The flowchart emphasises the **golden rule of fractography — never touch, never contaminate, and always document the as-received condition first**. Cleaning is minimal (ultrasonic acetone) — aggressive chemical cleaning destroys oxide layers that hold vital failure-history information.

### 4.4 Köhler Illumination Setup Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    K1[Place Specimen on Stage] --> K2[Focus on Specimen Using 10x Objective]
    K2 --> K3[Close Field Diaphragm Fully]
    K3 --> K4[Use Condenser Focus Knob to Sharpen Diaphragm Image]
    K4 --> K5[Use Centering Screws to Center Diaphragm in Field of View]
    K5 --> K6[Open Field Diaphragm until Just Disappears from View]
    K6 --> K7[Remove One Eyepiece Look at Back Focal Plane]
    K7 --> K8[Close Aperture Diaphragm to 70 to 80 percent of Objective NA]
    K8 --> K9[Reinstall Eyepiece]
    K9 --> K10[Optimal Contrast and Resolution Achieved]
    style K10 fill:#A5D6A7
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] Define fractography. List any four microscopic features used to identify the mode of failure.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - **Definition (1 mark):** Fractography is the study of fracture surfaces to determine the cause and mechanism of material failure.
> - **Four microscopic features (2 marks):**
>   1. **Dimples** — indicate ductile failure by microvoid coalescence (MVC).
>   2. **Cleavage facets / River patterns** — indicate brittle transgranular failure.
>   3. **Fatigue striations** — parallel ridges, each ≈ one load cycle; indicate fatigue crack propagation.
>   4. **Intergranular facets (rock-candy appearance)** — indicate grain-boundary failure (SCC, creep, temper embrittlement).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] Differentiate between beach marks and striations in fatigue fracture.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> | Aspect | Beach Marks | Striations |
> |---|---|---|
> | Scale | Macroscopic (visible to naked eye or stereo microscope at 5–30×) | Microscopic (visible at 200–1000×) |
> | Spacing | Wide, irregular (µm to mm), reflects load-history variations (start/stop, overload) | Fine, regular (~0.1–10 µm), one per load cycle |
> | Cause | Corrosion, oxidation, or roughness at the crack front between operating periods | Localised plastic blunting at the crack tip each cycle |
> | Use | Locate crack origin and growth direction | Estimate crack growth rate (da/dN) |
> | Visibility | Stereo zoom or low-power optical microscope | High-power optical or SEM |

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

**Q1 (A). [KTU University Exam — June 2024] With neat sketches, describe the various microscopic features observed on the fracture surfaces of (a) ductile, (b) brittle, and (c) fatigue fractures. How are these features used in failure analysis? (14 marks)**

> **Model Answer:**
>
> **(a) Ductile Fracture — 4 marks**
> - The fracture surface is **fibrous, dull grey**, with no metallic lustre.
> - The microscopic signature is the **dimple** — a cup-shaped depression of size 1–10 µm formed when a microvoid nucleates (at an inclusion or second-phase particle), grows, and coalesces with neighbouring voids.
> - **Equiaxed dimples** form under tensile (Mode I) loading; **elongated / parabolic dimples** form under shear (Mode II/III) loading, with the dimple tails pointing in the direction of shear.
> - Dimpled rupture is the most energy-absorbing mode and is the desired failure mode in tough engineering materials.
>
> **(b) Brittle Fracture — 4 marks**
> - The surface is **bright, granular, and faceted**, with no prior plastic deformation.
> - The dominant microscopic feature is the **cleavage facet** — a flat plane corresponding to low-index crystallographic planes (e.g., {100} in BCC iron).
> - **River patterns** form when a cleavage crack crosses a high-angle grain boundary; multiple parallel steps merge into fewer larger steps, with rivers pointing **back to the crack origin**.
> - **Tongues** are thin twin plates seen in BCC materials fractured at low temperatures.
> - **Quasi-cleavage** (mixed-mode) appears in hydrogen-embrittled high-strength steels.
>
> **(c) Fatigue Fracture — 4 marks**
> - The surface has three macroscopic zones — **initiation, propagation, final overload**.
> - **Beach marks** are macroscopic concentric arcs caused by changes in the operating environment (corrosion, load variations).
> - **Ratchet marks** are linear boundaries between multiple initiation sites, perpendicular to beach marks.
> - **Striations** are microscopic parallel ridges (one per load cycle) used to estimate the **Paris-law crack growth rate da/dN**.
> - **Microvoid coalescence dimples** may appear in the final overload zone, indicating the residual toughness of the material.
>
> **Application in Failure Analysis — 2 marks**
> 1. **Identify the failure mode** — overload, fatigue, SCC, creep, or embrittlement.
> 2. **Locate the crack origin** — by tracing rivers / striations / ratchet marks back to the start.
> 3. **Estimate the crack growth rate** — using striation spacing and the Paris law.
> 4. **Recommend preventive action** — e.g., shot peening to introduce compressive residual stress against fatigue, or change of material if SCC is identified.

---

**Q1 (B). [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] Explain the principle, construction, and working of a metallurgical optical microscope used for fracture surface analysis. Discuss Köhler illumination and the resolution limit. (14 marks)**

> **Model Answer:**
>
> **Principle (3 marks):**
> The metallurgical microscope uses **reflected light** (epi-illumination) to form a magnified image of an opaque, polished (or fracture) surface. Two-stage magnification is used: a primary objective forms a real, inverted, magnified image at a fixed tube length, and a secondary eyepiece (ocular) acts as a simple magnifier to view this image. The **total magnification** is the product of the individual magnifications.
>
> **Construction (4 marks):**
> - **Illumination system:** Light source (halogen/LED) → collector lens → field diaphragm → aperture diaphragm → condenser → polarizer (optional).
> - **Observation system:** Specimen on stage → objective lens (infinity-corrected, no cover slip) → tube lens → beam splitter → eyepiece / camera.
> - **Mechanical system:** Coarse and fine focus knobs, XY stage controls, Köhler centring screws, objective turret (nose piece) with 4–5 objectives.
> - **Imaging system:** Monocular, binocular, or trinocular head; optional camera with C-mount adapter.
>
> **Working (3 marks):**
> Light from the source is focused by the condenser onto the specimen. Light reflected from the specimen enters the objective, which forms the primary magnified image. The eyepiece magnifies this further for the eye or camera. The microscope should be set up for **Köhler illumination** for optimal contrast and uniform brightness.
>
> **Köhler Illumination (2 marks):**
> A two-step process: (1) the field diaphragm is imaged on the specimen plane by adjusting the condenser, then centred; (2) the aperture diaphragm is adjusted to ≈ 70–80 % of the objective NA for best compromise between contrast and resolution. This provides uniform illumination and minimises glare.
>
> **Resolution Limit (2 marks):**
> Governed by **Abbe's diffraction limit**:
> $$d_{min} = \frac{0.61 \cdot \lambda}{NA_{obj} + NA_{cond}}$$
> For green light ($\lambda = 550$ nm) and a 50× dry objective ($NA = 0.55$): $d_{min} \approx 0.35 \;\mu m$. Features finer than this (e.g., very fine striations, sub-micron dimples) require SEM.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students lose marks in fractography viva & written exams:**
> 1. **Mixing up scale:** Confusing macroscopic beach marks (visible at 10×) with microscopic striations (visible at 500×). They are *not* the same feature.
> 2. **Wrong direction of rivers:** River patterns point **toward the crack origin** (rivers flow *into* the source), not away. This is a classic trap.
> 3. **Forgetting ratchet marks:** Many students describe fatigue but ignore ratchet marks — they are the *only* way to identify **multiple initiation sites** (a sign of high-cycle fatigue).
> 4. **Confusing dimples with corrosion pits:** Dimples have a **cup-within-cup** micro-structure (the Mannesmann effect) and are interconnected; corrosion pits are isolated hemispherical cavities with chemical etching around them.
> 5. **Ignoring oxidation colour:** Rust or blue-tempered regions on a fatigue fracture give the *time* of exposure — examiners love to ask "how do you date the crack?"
> 6. **Quoting empty magnification:** Anything above $1000 \cdot NA$ is "empty magnification" — say so if asked about useful magnification limits.
> 7. **Not protecting the fracture surface:** A 1-mark question often asks about sample preparation — mention "acetone ultrasonic cleaning, no touching, document first".

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Quick-Recall Bullet Checklist:**
> - [ ] **Fractography** = study of fracture surface; **Fracto** (broken) + **Graphy** (picture).
> - [ ] Optical microscope gives magnifications from **5× to 1000×**; useful upper limit is $1000 \cdot NA$ (≈ 200–500× typically).
> - [ ] **Ductile fracture** ⇒ Cup-and-cone macro; **dimples (MVC)** micro.
> - [ ] **Brittle fracture** ⇒ Flat transverse macro; **cleavage + river patterns** micro.
> - [ ] **Fatigue fracture** ⇒ Three zones (initiation, propagation, overload); **beach marks (macro) + striations (micro)**.
> - [ ] **Intergranular fracture** ⇒ Rock-candy appearance; caused by **SCC, temper embrittlement, creep, hydrogen**.
> - [ ] **River patterns point toward the crack origin** — remember the mnemonic "rivers flow to the source".
> - [ ] **Ratchet marks** separate multiple fatigue origins — perpendicular to beach marks.
> - [ ] **Köhler illumination** = the standard setup procedure; field + aperture diaphragms must be adjusted.
> - [ ] **Abbe resolution limit** $d_{min} = 0.61\lambda / (NA_{obj} + NA_{cond})$ — sets the lower limit of optical fractography.
> - [ ] **Useful magnification** lies in $500 \cdot NA \le M \le 1000 \cdot NA$; above this, it's empty magnification.
> - [ ] **Total magnification** $M_{total} = M_{obj} \times M_{oc} \times M_{cam}$.
> - [ ] **Field of view** at specimen $D_{spec} = FN / M_{obj}$ — always start at low $M_{obj}$ for crack overview.
> - [ ] **Never touch or contaminate the fracture surface** — document as-received, then acetone ultrasonic clean if needed.
> - [ ] **Paris Law** for fatigue crack growth: $da/dN = C \cdot (\Delta K)^m$.
> - [ ] SEM is needed when features are **below ~0.3 µm** (finer striations, sub-micron dimples, fine intergranular facets).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fracture Toughness of Materials Using UTM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMEL308 - MATERIALS TESTING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Fracture Toughness of Materials Using UTM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fracture Toughness (K_Ic)** is a quantitative measure of a material's resistance to brittle fracture when a sharp crack of a defined geometry is present under monotonically increasing Mode I (tensile opening) loading. It is defined as the **critical value of the Stress Intensity Factor (K_I)** at which a pre-existing crack of a critical size begins to propagate catastrophically.

In the context of the **Universal Testing Machine (UTM)** experiment, the fracture toughness **K_Ic** is experimentally determined by subjecting a **pre-cracked standard specimen** (typically a **Single Edge Notched Bend (SENB)** or **Compact Tension (CT)** specimen, as per **ASTM E399**) to a controlled three-point bend or tensile load, recording the crack-initiation load, and substituting it into a geometry-dependent calibration formula.

## 1.2 Units and Standard Notation

| Parameter | Symbol | Unit | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| Fracture Toughness | **K_Ic** | **MPa·√m** or **N/mm^(3/2)** | Critical Stress Intensity Factor |
| Applied Load | P | N (Newton) | Load at crack initiation |
| Specimen Thickness | B | mm | Thickness of the specimen |
| Specimen Width | W | mm | Width (depth) of the specimen |
| Crack Length | a | mm | Pre-crack from notch tip |
| Span Length | S | mm | Support span in 3-point bend |
| Yield Strength | σ_y | MPa | Material's 0.2% offset yield strength |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mode I (Opening Mode)** is universally accepted in KTU examinations as the reference mode, denoted by the subscript "I" in K_Ic. The Roman numeral "I" specifically indicates tensile crack opening — NOT the number 1.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Cracked Glass Plate**
> Imagine two glass plates of identical size. The **first plate** has no visible crack. The **second plate** has a tiny, almost invisible scratch on its edge. Both plates are made of the *same material* and have the *same thickness*. When you press your thumbs in the middle of each plate, the first plate requires a large force to snap, while the second plate shatters instantly at a much smaller force, right from the tip of the scratch.
>
> This is because the *tiny scratch* acts as a **stress concentrator**. The **Fracture Toughness (K_Ic)** is essentially the material's "intrinsic tolerance" to such pre-existing flaws. A material with **high K_Ic** (like ductile steel) can "absorb" a flaw and still carry a heavy load. A material with **low K_Ic** (like ceramic or glass) catastrophically fails even when the flaw is microscopically small.

**Engineering Insight:** K_Ic is *not* the same as **toughness** in everyday language. In materials science, "toughness" refers to the area under the stress-strain curve. **K_Ic is a *fracture mechanics property* that quantifies flaw tolerance** — it is the property used by aerospace, pressure-vessel, and pipeline designers to determine safe-operating stresses and inspection intervals.

## 1.4 Why UTM is Used for Fracture Toughness Testing

The **Universal Testing Machine** is the standard platform because it provides:
1. **Controlled, monotonically increasing load** (static loading) — required by ASTM E399.
2. **Accurate load measurement** via a calibrated load cell (typically ±1% accuracy).
3. **Stable crosshead displacement** for displacement-controlled testing (essential for valid K_Ic per ASTM E399 §8.1.1).
4. **Rigid loading fixtures** to ensure pure Mode I (tensile) opening at the crack tip.
5. Compliance with the **standard test fixture geometries** (3-point bend fixture for SENB, clevis grips for CT).

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Linear Elastic Fracture Mechanics (LEFM) — The Underlying Theory

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU students **must** understand that K_Ic is only valid under **Linear Elastic Fracture Mechanics (LEFM)** conditions, which requires that **plastic deformation at the crack tip remains small** compared to the crack length and specimen dimensions.

### 2.1.1 The Stress Field at a Crack Tip (Williams' Solution)

For a sharp crack of length **2a** in an infinite plate under remote uniform tensile stress **σ**, the stress field near the crack tip in polar coordinates (r, θ) is:

$$
\sigma_{ij} = \frac{K_I}{\sqrt{2\pi r}} \cdot f_{ij}(\theta) + \text{higher order terms}
$$

**Why this matters:** The Stress Intensity Factor **K_I** captures the *intensity* of the singular stress field at the crack tip. When **K_I** reaches a critical value **K_Ic**, the crack propagates. This **single parameter (K_Ic)** governs fracture for brittle and quasi-brittle materials.

### 2.1.2 The Generalized Definition of K_I

For a **finite body** (specimen) with a crack, the Stress Intensity Factor is computed as:

$$
K_I = \sigma \cdot \sqrt{\pi a} \cdot Y\left(\frac{a}{W}\right)
$$

Where:
- **σ** = applied remote (gross) stress
- **a** = crack length
- **Y(a/W)** = dimensionless **geometry factor** (function of crack length-to-width ratio)
- **W** = characteristic specimen dimension (width or depth)

## 2.2 The SENB (Three-Point Bend) Specimen — Most Common in KTU Labs

The **Single Edge Notched Bend (SENB)** specimen, also called the **Three-Point Bend (TPB)** specimen, is the most commonly tested geometry in KTU-affiliated Materials Testing Labs because of its simplicity.

### 2.2.1 Standard Geometry Constraints (ASTM E399)

$$
\frac{S}{W} = 4 \quad (\text{Standard Span-to-Width ratio})
$$

$$
0.45 \leq \frac{a}{W} \leq 0.55 \quad (\text{Pre-crack length must be ~half the width})
$$

### 2.2.2 The SENB Calibration Equation (ASTM E399 §9.1.1)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **single most important formula** for the KTU lab exam. Memorize the structure (not the full polynomial — it is given in the question paper).

$$
K_{Ic} = \frac{P \cdot S}{B \cdot W^{3/2}} \cdot f\!\left(\frac{a}{W}\right)
$$

Where the **geometry function** $f(a/W)$ is given by:

$$
f\!\left(\frac{a}{W}\right) = \frac{3\sqrt{\frac{a}{W}} \cdot \left[1.99 - \frac{a}{W}\left(1 - \frac{a}{W}\right)\left(2.15 - 3.93\frac{a}{W} + 2.7\left(\frac{a}{W}\right)^{2}\right)\right]}{2\left(1 + 2\frac{a}{W}\right)\left(1 - \frac{a}{W}\right)^{3/2}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU practical exams, you will almost never have to compute $f(a/W)$ by hand. The examiner provides a precomputed value or a look-up table. Focus on correctly substituting **P, S, B, W** and the **provided f(a/W)**.

## 2.3 The Compact Tension (CT) Specimen — For Reference

The **CT specimen** is used when material is limited. The K_Ic equation is:

$$
K_{Ic} = \frac{P}{B\sqrt{W}} \cdot f_{CT}\!\left(\frac{a}{W}\right)
$$

$$
f_{CT}\!\left(\frac{a}{W}\right) = \frac{\left(2 + \frac{a}{W}\right)\left[0.886 + 4.64\frac{a}{W} - 13.32\left(\frac{a}{W}\right)^{2} + 14.72\left(\frac{a}{W}\right)^{3} - 5.6\left(\frac{a}{W}\right)^{4}\right]}{\left(1 - \frac{a}{W}\right)^{3/2}}
$$

## 2.4 Validity Criteria for a Valid K_Ic Test (ASTM E399 §8.1)

> [!CAUTION]
> The numerical value obtained from the formula is **NOT automatically a valid K_Ic**. The following three size requirements **MUST** be satisfied for the result to be called a "valid" plane-strain fracture toughness.

$$
B \geq 2.5\left(\frac{K_{Ic}}{\sigma_{y}}\right)^{2}
$$

$$
a \geq 2.5\left(\frac{K_{Ic}}{\sigma_{y}}\right)^{2}
$$

$$
(W - a) \geq 2.5\left(\frac{K_{Ic}}{\sigma_{y}}\right)^{2}
$$

**Physical Meaning:** The **plastic zone size** at the crack tip must be small compared to the crack length and the specimen dimensions. The factor **2.5** is empirical, derived from the requirement that the plastic zone occupies less than ~2% of the relevant dimension.

**Engineering Utility:** For a high-strength steel with $\sigma_y$ = 1500 MPa and K_Ic = 50 MPa·√m, the required thickness is:
$$
B \geq 2.5 \times \left(\frac{50}{1500}\right)^{2} = 2.78 \text{ mm} \approx 3 \text{ mm minimum}
$$
This is why small laboratory specimens often fail the validity test, and engineers must use **larger specimens** for tough, high-yield materials.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Use Case | Typical KTU Marks |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $K_I = \sigma \sqrt{\pi a} \cdot Y\!\left(\frac{a}{W}\right)$ | General K_I definition for any geometry | 2–3 |
| 2 | $K_{Ic} = \dfrac{P \cdot S}{B \cdot W^{3/2}} \cdot f\!\left(\dfrac{a}{W}\right)$ | SENB specimen — **primary exam formula** | 5–7 |
| 3 | $K_{Ic} = \dfrac{P}{B\sqrt{W}} \cdot f_{CT}\!\left(\dfrac{a}{W}\right)$ | CT specimen | 3–4 |
| 4 | $B \geq 2.5\left(\dfrac{K_{Ic}}{\sigma_y}\right)^{2}$ | Plane-strain validity (thickness) | 2–3 |
| 5 | $a \geq 2.5\left(\dfrac{K_{Ic}}{\sigma_y}\right)^{2}$ | Plane-strain validity (crack length) | 2 |
| 6 | $(W-a) \geq 2.5\left(\dfrac{K_{Ic}}{\sigma_y}\right)^{2}$ | Plane-strain validity (ligament) | 2 |
| 7 | $K_Q = \dfrac{P_Q \cdot S}{B \cdot W^{3/2}} \cdot f\!\left(\dfrac{a}{W}\right)$ | Conditional / provisional K value | 1 |
| 8 | $P_{max} \leq 1.10 \, P_Q$ | Validity check — peak load criterion | 1 |

> [!NOTE]
> In the KTU formula sheet, $P_Q$ is the **provisional load** obtained by the **5% secant offset method** on the load-displacement curve. If the validity checks pass, $K_Q = K_{Ic}$.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: SENB Fracture Toughness Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> The following is a **complete KTU-style numerical problem** with every single step written out explicitly. This is the type of 14-mark problem that appears in the ESE (End-Semester Exam).

### 3.1.1 Given Data (Typical KTU Exam Problem)

A Single Edge Notched Bend (SENB) specimen of an aluminum alloy is tested on a UTM. The following data are recorded:

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Specimen width, **W** | 25.4 mm |
| Specimen thickness, **B** | 12.7 mm |
| Span length, **S** | 4W = 101.6 mm |
| Pre-crack length, **a** | 12.7 mm (a/W = 0.50) |
| Load at crack initiation, **P_Q** | 35,500 N |
| Yield strength, **σ_y** | 360 MPa |
| Geometry factor (given), f(a/W) | 2.66 (at a/W = 0.50) |

**Required:** Calculate the conditional fracture toughness **K_Q** and verify if it qualifies as a **valid plane-strain fracture toughness K_Ic**.

---

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Solution

#### Step 1: Compute the conditional Stress Intensity Factor $K_Q$

The governing equation for the SENB specimen is:

$$
K_{Ic} = \frac{P \cdot S}{B \cdot W^{3/2}} \cdot f\!\left(\frac{a}{W}\right)
$$

Substituting the values, where $P = P_Q = 35{,}500$ N:

$$
K_Q = \frac{35{,}500 \times 101.6}{12.7 \times (25.4)^{3/2}} \times 2.66
$$

**Step 1a — Compute $W^{3/2}$:**

$$
W^{3/2} = (25.4)^{3/2} = (25.4)^{1} \times \sqrt{25.4} = 25.4 \times 5.0397
$$

$$
W^{3/2} = 128.01 \text{ mm}^{3/2}
$$

**Step 1b — Compute the denominator $B \cdot W^{3/2}$:**

$$
B \cdot W^{3/2} = 12.7 \times 128.01 = 1625.7 \text{ mm}^{5/2}
$$

**Step 1c — Compute the numerator $P \cdot S$:**

$$
P \cdot S = 35{,}500 \times 101.6 = 3{,}606{,}800 \text{ N·mm}
$$

**Step 1d — Compute the load-moment term $\frac{P \cdot S}{B \cdot W^{3/2}}$:**

$$
\frac{P \cdot S}{B \cdot W^{3/2}} = \frac{3{,}606{,}800}{1625.7} = 2218.4 \text{ N/mm}^{3/2}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The unit N/mm^(3/2) is the **derived unit** of Stress Intensity Factor in the SI-mm convention. The conversion to the standard MPa·√m is shown in Step 1f.

**Step 1e — Multiply by the geometry factor $f(a/W) = 2.66$:**

$$
K_Q = 2218.4 \times 2.66 = 5900.9 \text{ N/mm}^{3/2}
$$

**Step 1f — Convert to the standard SI unit MPa·√m:**

Using the conversion: $1 \text{ N/mm}^{3/2} = \frac{1 \text{ N}}{(\text{mm})^{3/2}} = \frac{10^{-3} \text{ MPa} \cdot (10^3)^{1/2}}{1} = 1 \cdot \sqrt{10^3} \cdot 10^{-3} \text{ MPa}\sqrt{\text{m}}$

Let me do this carefully:

$$
1 \text{ N/mm}^{3/2} = 1 \frac{\text{N}}{\text{mm}^{3/2}} = 1 \frac{10^{-6} \text{ MN}}{10^{-9/2} \text{ m}^{3/2}} = 1 \times 10^{-6 + 9/2} \frac{\text{MN}}{\text{m}^{3/2}} = 1 \times 10^{-1.5} \text{ MPa·m}^{1/2} = 0.03162 \text{ MPa·}\sqrt{\text{m}}
$$

Therefore:

$$
K_Q = 5900.9 \times 0.03162 = 186.6 \text{ MPa·}\sqrt{\text{m}}
$$

$$
\boxed{K_Q \approx 186.6 \text{ MPa}\sqrt{\text{m}}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU exams, you may be allowed to **report the answer in N/mm^(3/2) directly** (especially if the question is silent on units). The examiner will accept both. Always show **unit conversion explicitly** in the workings.

---

#### Step 2: Check Validity Criterion 1 — Thickness Condition

The required minimum thickness for plane-strain conditions is:

$$
B_{min} = 2.5\left(\frac{K_Q}{\sigma_y}\right)^{2}
$$

Substituting $K_Q = 186.6$ MPa·√m and $\sigma_y = 360$ MPa:

$$
B_{min} = 2.5 \times \left(\frac{186.6}{360}\right)^{2}
$$

**Step 2a — Compute the ratio $K_Q / \sigma_y$:**

$$
\frac{K_Q}{\sigma_y} = \frac{186.6}{360} = 0.5183 \text{ m}^{1/2}
$$

**Step 2b — Square the ratio:**

$$
\left(\frac{K_Q}{\sigma_y}\right)^{2} = (0.5183)^{2} = 0.2686 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2c — Convert to mm (since B is in mm):**

$$
\left(\frac{K_Q}{\sigma_y}\right)^{2} = 0.2686 \text{ m} = 268.6 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2d — Multiply by 2.5:**

$$
B_{min} = 2.5 \times 268.6 = 671.5 \text{ mm}
$$

**Comparison:**

$$
B_{actual} = 12.7 \text{ mm} \ll B_{min} = 671.5 \text{ mm}
$$

**Result:** ❌ **Validity FAILED.** The specimen is far too thin relative to the required minimum thickness. The result is **NOT a valid K_Ic**.

> [!CAUTION]
> In KTU examinations, when validity fails, you **must** explicitly state: "Since the validity criteria are not satisfied, the computed $K_Q = 186.6$ MPa·√m **cannot be reported as a valid plane-strain fracture toughness K_Ic**. The result represents an apparent or upper-bound toughness under plane-stress conditions. A larger specimen (with $B \geq 671.5$ mm) would be required for a valid K_Ic test."

---

#### Step 3: Check Validity Criterion 2 — Crack Length Condition

$$
a_{min} = 2.5\left(\frac{K_Q}{\sigma_y}\right)^{2} = 671.5 \text{ mm}
$$

**Comparison:** $a_{actual} = 12.7$ mm $\ll$ $a_{min} = 671.5$ mm → ❌ FAILED.

---

#### Step 4: Check Validity Criterion 3 — Ligament Condition

$$
(W - a)_{min} = 2.5\left(\frac{K_Q}{\sigma_y}\right)^{2} = 671.5 \text{ mm}
$$

**Comparison:** $(W - a)_{actual} = 12.7$ mm $\ll$ $(W - a)_{min} = 671.5$ mm → ❌ FAILED.

---

### 3.1.3 Conclusion

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result:** The provisional fracture toughness $K_Q = 186.6$ MPa·√m fails **all three plane-strain validity checks**. Therefore, it must be reported as a **conditional toughness value (K_Q)** and NOT as K_Ic. To obtain a valid K_Ic, the specimen thickness, crack length, and unbroken ligament must each be at least **671.5 mm** — a practical impossibility for this material. This indicates the material is **excessively tough relative to its yield strength**, and the actual K_Ic would need to be measured on a much larger specimen, or alternatively, the elastic-plastic fracture toughness **J_Ic** should be measured using the J-integral method.

## 3.2 Design-Problem Analytical Matrix (KTU 14-Mark Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is a **KTU examiner's checklist** for typical Part B sub-questions. Use it to ensure full marks.

| Sub-part | Typical Task | Key Formula(s) | Common Pitfall |
|---|---|---|---|
| (a) | Compute $K_Q$ from given P, S, B, W, a, f | SENB or CT equation | Forgetting to raise W to 3/2 correctly |
| (a) | Unit conversion: N/mm^(3/2) ↔ MPa·√m | $1$ N/mm^(3/2) $= 0.03162$ MPa·√m | Reporting in wrong units |
| (b) | Verify all three validity conditions | $B, a, (W-a) \geq 2.5(K/\sigma_y)^{2}$ | Checking only one of the three conditions |
| (b) | Conclude whether the result is a valid K_Ic | Validity OR failure | Not stating the conclusion explicitly |
| (b) | Suggest material/design improvements | $B \uparrow$ or use J-integral | Vague answers like "use a stronger material" |

## 3.3 Key Unit Conversions (Memorize)

$$
1 \text{ MPa}\sqrt{\text{m}} = 1 \frac{\text{MN}}{\text{m}^{3/2}} = \frac{10^{6} \text{ N}}{(10^3 \text{ mm})^{3/2}} = \frac{10^{6}}{10^{4.5}} \text{ N/mm}^{3/2} = 10^{1.5} \text{ N/mm}^{3/2} \approx 31.62 \text{ N/mm}^{3/2}
$$

$$
1 \text{ ksi}\sqrt{\text{in}} = 1.0988 \text{ MPa}\sqrt{\text{m}}
$$

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The UTM-Based Fracture Toughness Test Workflow

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagram below maps the **complete sequence of operations** involved in determining K_Ic using a UTM. This is the **standard KTU lab viva question**: "Describe the step-by-step procedure for fracture toughness testing."

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Start: KTU Lab Experiment 2]) --> prep1[Step 1: Specimen Preparation]
    prep1 --> prep1a[Cut SENB Specimen per ASTM E399]
    prep1a --> prep1b[Machine Notch of Depth approx 0.45W to 0.50W]
    prep1b --> prep1c[Pre-crack by Fatigue Cycling: a to W minus 0.05W]
    prep1c --> prep2[Step 2: Measure Specimen Dimensions]
    prep2 --> prep2a[Record B, W, a using Vernier Caliper or Micrometer]
    prep2a --> prep2b[Record S = 4W for 3-Point Bend]
    prep2b --> prep3[Step 3: UTM Setup and Calibration]
    prep3 --> prep3a[Install 3-Point Bend Fixture on UTM]
    prep3a --> prep3b[Zero Load Cell and Displacement Gauge]
    prep3b --> prep3c[Place Specimen with Crack Facing Downward]
    prep3c --> test[Step 4: Conduct the Test]
    test --> testa[Apply Displacement-Controlled Loading]
    testa --> testb[Record Load versus Displacement Curve]
    testb --> testc[Identify Provisional Load P_Q via 5% Secant Method]
    testc --> testd[Identify Maximum Load P_max]
    testd --> calc[Step 5: Computation of K_Q]
    calc --> calca[Compute f of a over W from Standard Table]
    calca --> calcb[Apply SENB Formula: K_Q = P_Q times S divided by B times W to the power 3 over 2 times f of a over W]
    calcb --> calcc[Convert Units to MPa square root meter]
    calcc --> valid[Step 6: Validity Verification]
    valid --> valida[Check B greater than or equal to 2.5 times K_Q over sigma_y squared]
    valida --> validb[Check a greater than or equal to 2.5 times K_Q over sigma_y squared]
    validb --> validc[Check W minus a greater than or equal to 2.5 times K_Q over sigma_y squared]
    validc --> validd[Check P_max less than or equal to 1.10 times P_Q]
    validd --> decision{All Validity Checks Passed?}
    decision -->|Yes| report[Report K_Q as Valid K_Ic]
    decision -->|No| note[Report K_Q as Conditional Apparent Toughness]
    note --> suggest[Recommend Larger Specimen or J-Integral Test]
    report --> end1([End: Result Documented])
    suggest --> end1
```

## 4.2 Schematic: SENB Specimen Geometry and Loading Configuration

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    spec[SENB Specimen Cross-Section] --> dimW[Total Width W]
    spec --> dimB[Total Thickness B]
    spec --> dimA[Pre-crack Length a]
    spec --> dimL[Unbroken Ligament W minus a]
    load[Loading Configuration] --> roller1[Support Roller 1]
    load --> roller2[Support Roller 2]
    load --> anvil[Central Loading Nose]
    roller1 --> span[Span Length S = 4W]
    roller2 --> span
    anvil --> align[Crack Aligned with Central Loading Point]
    spec -.aligned with.-> load
```

## 4.3 Schematic: Load-Displacement Curve and the 5% Secant Method

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **textual schematic** showing the conceptual identification of P_Q. The 5% secant method is a **standard KTU viva question**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    curve[Load versus Displacement Curve from UTM] --> region1[Region 1: Linear Elastic Region - Compliance Slope C_i]
    curve --> region2[Region 2: Slight Deviation due to Crack Tip Plasticity]
    curve --> region3[Region 3: Maximum Load P_max]
    region1 --> secant[Draw Secant Line through Origin with Slope = 0.95 times C_i]
    secant --> intersect[Intersection of Secant with Curve = P_Q]
    region2 --> intersect
    intersect --> check{Maximum Load Check}
    check -->|P_max less than or equal to 1.10 P_Q| valid_curve[P_Q is Valid - Proceed to K_Ic Calculation]
    check -->|P_max greater than 1.10 P_Q| invalid_curve[Test Invalid - Excessive Plasticity]
```

## 4.4 Comparative Block Diagram: SENB vs CT Specimens

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    parent{Fracture Toughness Specimen Selection} --> opt1[Option 1: SENB - Single Edge Notched Bend]
    parent --> opt2[Option 2: CT - Compact Tension]
    opt1 --> pros1[Advantages: Simple to Machine, Easy to Fixture, Low Material Usage]
    opt1 --> cons1[Disadvantages: Requires High-Stiffness UTM, Span Wastage]
    opt2 --> pros2[Advantages: Less Material, Used for Cylindrical Bar Stock]
    opt2 --> cons2[Disadvantages: Complex Clevis Grips Required, More Pin Load Issues]
    opt1 --> load1[Loading: 3-Point Bending on UTM]
    opt2 --> load2[Loading: Tensile Pull through Clevis on UTM]
    load1 --> eq1[Equation: K = P times S divided by B times W to the power 3 over 2 times f of a over W]
    load2 --> eq2[Equation: K = P divided by B times square root of W times f_CT of a over W]
```
