# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Fracture Toughness. State the conditions under which a material's fracture toughness is called K_Ic. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

> **Fracture Toughness** is a quantitative measure of a material's resistance to brittle fracture in the presence of a sharp crack. It is denoted by the **critical Stress Intensity Factor (K_Ic)**, the value of K_I at which a pre-existing crack begins to propagate unstably under Mode I (tensile opening) loading.
>
> The fracture toughness of a material is called **K_Ic** (plane-strain fracture toughness) **only when all of the following validity conditions are satisfied**, per ASTM E399:
>
> 1. The specimen thickness **$B \geq 2.5(K_{Ic}/\sigma_y)^2$**
> 2. The crack length **$a \geq 2.5(K_{Ic}/\sigma_y)^2$**
> 3. The unbroken ligament **$(W - a) \geq 2.5(K_{Ic}/\sigma_y)^2$**
> 4. The maximum load $P_{max} \leq 1.10 P_Q$
>
> Under these conditions, the **stress state at the crack tip is plane-strain** (triaxial constraint), and the measured value is geometry-independent and represents a true material property. [3 Marks]

### Question 2: Why is fatigue pre-cracking required in a fracture toughness test specimen? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer:**

> **Fatigue pre-cracking** is required for the following reasons:
>
> 1. **To create a sharp crack tip of a known, controlled length.** A machined notch has a finite root radius, which overestimates K_Ic because a blunt notch requires a higher load to initiate a crack than a sharp one.
>
> 2. **To satisfy the assumption of Linear Elastic Fracture Mechanics (LEFM).** A sharp crack (tip radius $\to$ 0) is the idealized geometry used in the analytical K_I formulation.
>
> 3. **To control the crack length precisely** before the test. ASTM E399 requires the fatigue crack to extend at least 2% of the total crack length $a$ beyond the machined notch, but the fatigue stress intensity must remain below 60% of the expected K_Ic to avoid creating a plastically deformed "fatigue damage zone."
>
> 4. **To ensure consistent crack-tip geometry across multiple specimens** of the same material. [3 Marks]

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A: 14-Mark Question (Module 2 — Primary Choice) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **Single Edge Notched Bend (SENB)** specimen used for fracture toughness testing as per ASTM E399. List the standard dimensional requirements. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** In a fracture toughness test on an SENB specimen of a high-strength steel, the following data are recorded. Calculate the conditional fracture toughness $K_Q$ and verify whether the result qualifies as a valid plane-strain fracture toughness $K_{Ic}$. **[7 Marks]**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Specimen thickness, B | 25.0 mm |
| Specimen width, W | 50.0 mm |
| Span length, S | 200 mm (= 4W) |
| Pre-crack length, a | 25.0 mm (a/W = 0.50) |
| Provisional load, P_Q | 45,000 N |
| Geometry factor, f(a/W) | 2.66 |
| Yield strength, σ_y | 1500 MPa |

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — SENB Specimen Description (7 Marks)

**Sketch (text description):** A rectangular bar of width **W** and thickness **B** with a straight-through notch of depth **(W - a)** machined on one face. The tip of the notch is sharpened by **fatigue pre-cracking** to a final total crack length **a** (measured from the loaded face). The specimen is supported on two rollers separated by span **S = 4W**, and loaded through a central loading nose in a **3-point bend fixture** on the UTM. The pre-crack must face **downward** (towards the tension side of bending).

**Standard Dimensional Requirements (ASTM E399):**

| # | Parameter | Requirement |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Span-to-width ratio, S/W | 4 (standard) |
| 2 | Crack length ratio, a/W | 0.45 to 0.55 |
| 3 | Notch root radius (before fatigue) | ≤ 0.08W, typically ≤ 0.1 mm |
| 4 | Minimum fatigue pre-crack extension | ≥ 0.025W or 1.3 mm, whichever is greater |
| 5 | Maximum fatigue pre-crack length | ≤ 0.60W |
| 6 | Maximum fatigue stress intensity during pre-cracking | ≤ 0.60 × expected K_Ic |
| 7 | Surface finish on the tension face | Polished, free of scratches transverse to the loading direction |
| 8 | Specimen thickness B | ≥ 2.5(K_Ic/σ_y)² for valid plane-strain K_Ic |

[7 Marks awarded: 2 for sketch description, 3 for dimensional list, 2 for fatigue pre-crack details]

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- B = 25.0 mm
- W = 50.0 mm
- S = 200 mm
- a = 25.0 mm
- P_Q = 45,000 N
- f(a/W) = 2.66
- σ_y = 1500 MPa

**Step 1: Compute W^(3/2):**

$$
W^{3/2} = (50.0)^{3/2} = 50.0 \times \sqrt{50.0} = 50.0 \times 7.0711 = 353.55 \text{ mm}^{3/2}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the conditional K_Q using the SENB formula:**

$$
K_Q = \frac{P_Q \cdot S}{B \cdot W^{3/2}} \cdot f\!\left(\frac{a}{W}\right)
$$

$$
K_Q = \frac{45{,}000 \times 200}{25.0 \times 353.55} \times 2.66
$$

**Step 2a — Compute the numerator $P_Q \cdot S$:**

$$
P_Q \cdot S = 45{,}000 \times 200 = 9{,}000{,}000 \text{ N·mm}
$$

**Step 2b — Compute the denominator $B \cdot W^{3/2}$:**

$$
B \cdot W^{3/2} = 25.0 \times 353.55 = 8838.75 \text{ mm}^{5/2}
$$

**Step 2c — Compute the load-moment term:**

$$
\frac{P_Q \cdot S}{B \cdot W^{3/2}} = \frac{9{,}000{,}000}{8838.75} = 1018.2 \text{ N/mm}^{3/2}
$$

**Step 2d — Multiply by f(a/W) = 2.66:**

$$
K_Q = 1018.2 \times 2.66 = 2708.5 \text{ N/mm}^{3/2}
$$

**Step 2e — Convert to MPa·√m:**

$$
K_Q = 2708.5 \times 0.03162 = 85.64 \text{ MPa}\sqrt{\text{m}}
$$

$$
\boxed{K_Q \approx 85.6 \text{ MPa}\sqrt{\text{m}}}
$$

**Step 3: Verify Validity Criterion 1 — Thickness:**

$$
B_{min} = 2.5\left(\frac{K_Q}{\sigma_y}\right)^{2} = 2.5\left(\frac{85.6}{1500}\right)^{2}
$$

$$
= 2.5 \times (0.05707)^{2} = 2.5 \times 0.003258 = 0.008145 \text{ m} = 8.15 \text{ mm}
$$

**Comparison:** $B_{actual} = 25.0 \text{ mm} \geq B_{min} = 8.15 \text{ mm}$ → ✅ **PASSED**

**Step 4: Verify Validity Criterion 2 — Crack Length:**

$$
a_{min} = 2.5\left(\frac{K_Q}{\sigma_y}\right)^{2} = 8.15 \text{ mm}
$$

**Comparison:** $a_{actual} = 25.0 \text{ mm} \geq a_{min} = 8.15 \text{ mm}$ → ✅ **PASSED**

**Step 5: Verify Validity Criterion 3 — Ligament:**

$$
(W - a)_{min} = 2.5\left(\frac{K_Q}{\sigma_y}\right)^{2} = 8.15 \text{ mm}
$$

**Comparison:** $(W - a)_{actual} = 25.0 \text{ mm} \geq (W - a)_{min} = 8.15 \text{ mm}$ → ✅ **PASSED**

**Final Conclusion:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since all three plane-strain validity conditions are satisfied, the result is a **VALID plane-strain fracture toughness**:
>
> $$\boxed{K_{Ic} = 85.6 \text{ MPa}\sqrt{\text{m}}}$$
>
> [7 Marks awarded: 2 for $K_Q$ calculation, 1 for unit conversion, 3 for validity checks, 1 for final conclusion with K_Ic qualification]

---

### Question B: 14-Mark Question (Module 2 — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Stress Intensity Factor (K)**. What are the three modes of crack-tip displacement, and why is Mode I the most important in structural integrity assessment? **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A Compact Tension (CT) specimen of a titanium alloy is tested on a UTM. The following data are given. Calculate the conditional fracture toughness $K_Q$ and check all three plane-strain validity conditions. **[7 Marks]**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Specimen thickness, B | 15.0 mm |
| Specimen width, W | 30.0 mm |
| Pre-crack length, a | 15.0 mm (a/W = 0.50) |
| Provisional load, P_Q | 12,000 N |
| Geometry factor, f_CT(a/W) | 9.66 (at a/W = 0.50) |
| Yield strength, σ_y | 880 MPa |

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Stress Intensity Factor and the Three Modes (7 Marks)

**Definition of Stress Intensity Factor (K):**

The Stress Intensity Factor (K) is a **scalar parameter** that quantifies the **intensity of the stress field at the tip of a sharp crack** in a loaded linear-elastic body. It has units of **MPa·√m** (or N/mm^(3/2)). For a given loading mode, K is defined as:

$$
K = \lim_{r \to 0} \left[ \sqrt{2\pi r} \cdot \sigma_{ij}(r, \theta = 0) \right]
$$

**The Three Modes of Crack-Tip Displacement:**

| Mode | Name | Loading Direction | Real-World Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mode I** | Opening Mode | Tensile pull **perpendicular** to the crack plane | Internal pressure in a pipe; thermal stresses |
| **Mode II** | Sliding / In-Plane Shear Mode | In-plane shear **parallel** to the crack plane | Friction between mating surfaces |
| **Mode III** | Tearing / Out-of-Plane Shear Mode | Out-of-plane shear **parallel** to the crack front | Tearing of a metal sheet |

**Why Mode I is the Most Important:**

> **Mode I is the most critical** in structural integrity assessment for the following reasons:
>
> 1. **Most service loads are tensile** in nature (internal pressure, thermal expansion, gravity loads on suspended components), which directly opens the crack.
>
> 2. **Mode I produces the lowest fracture toughness (K_Ic)** of all three modes. A material is always weakest under Mode I loading.
>
> 3. **Mode II and Mode III can be converted mathematically into equivalent Mode I** components via the **Maximum Tangential Stress (MTS) criterion** or **Strain Energy Density (SED) criterion**. This means a pure Mode I K_Ic value is sufficient to bound mixed-mode fracture.
>
> 4. **All major design codes** (ASME Section XI, BS 7910, API 579) are based on K_Ic, not K_IIc or K_IIIc.
>
> [7 Marks awarded: 2 for K definition, 2 for three modes table, 3 for Mode I dominance justification]

---

#### Part (b) — CT Specimen Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- B = 15.0 mm
- W = 30.0 mm
- a = 15.0 mm
- P_Q = 12,000 N
- f_CT(a/W) = 9.66
- σ_y = 880 MPa

**Step 1: Compute the square root term $\sqrt{W}$:**

$$
\sqrt{W} = \sqrt{30.0} = 5.4772 \text{ mm}^{1/2}
$$

**Step 2: Apply the CT specimen calibration equation:**

$$
K_Q = \frac{P_Q}{B \cdot \sqrt{W}} \cdot f_{CT}\!\left(\frac{a}{W}\right)
$$

$$
K_Q = \frac{12{,}000}{15.0 \times 5.4772} \times 9.66
$$

**Step 2a — Compute the denominator $B \cdot \sqrt{W}$:**

$$
B \cdot \sqrt{W} = 15.0 \times 5.4772 = 82.16 \text{ mm}^{3/2}
$$

**Step 2b — Compute the load term $P_Q / (B\sqrt{W})$:**

$$
\frac{P_Q}{B \cdot \sqrt{W}} = \frac{12{,}000}{82.16} = 146.05 \text{ N/mm}^{3/2}
$$

**Step 2c — Multiply by f_CT(a/W) = 9.66:**

$$
K_Q = 146.05 \times 9.66 = 1410.85 \text{ N/mm}^{3/2}
$$

**Step 2d — Convert to MPa·√m:**

$$
K_Q = 1410.85 \times 0.03162 = 44.61 \text{ MPa}\sqrt{\text{m}}
$$

$$
\boxed{K_Q \approx 44.6 \text{ MPa}\sqrt{\text{m}}}
$$

**Step 3: Validity Check 1 — Thickness:**

$$
B_{min} = 2.5\left(\frac{K_Q}{\sigma_y}\right)^{2} = 2.5\left(\frac{44.6}{880}\right)^{2}
$$

$$
= 2.5 \times (0.05068)^{2} = 2.5 \times 0.002568 = 0.006420 \text{ m} = 6.42 \text{ mm}
$$

**Comparison:** $B_{actual} = 15.0$ mm $\geq$ $B_{min} = 6.42$ mm → ✅ **PASSED**

**Step 4: Validity Check 2 — Crack Length:**

$$
a_{min} = 6.42 \text{ mm}
$$

**Comparison:** $a_{actual} = 15.0$ mm $\geq$ $a_{min} = 6.42$ mm → ✅ **PASSED**

**Step 5: Validity Check 3 — Ligament:**

$$
(W - a)_{min} = 6.42 \text{ mm}
$$

**Comparison:** $(W - a)_{actual} = 15.0$ mm $\geq$ $(W - a)_{min} = 6.42$ mm → ✅ **PASSED**

**Final Conclusion:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All three plane-strain validity criteria are satisfied. Therefore, the conditional $K_Q$ qualifies as a **VALID plane-strain fracture toughness**:
>
> $$\boxed{K_{Ic} = 44.6 \text{ MPa}\sqrt{\text{m}}}$$
>
> [7 Marks awarded: 2 for $K_Q$ calculation, 1 for unit conversion, 3 for three validity checks, 1 for final conclusion]

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Fracture Toughness Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the W^(3/2) power in the SENB formula.** The most frequent error is using W² instead of W^(3/2), which produces an answer that is off by a factor of √W (≈ 5 to 7×). This single error typically **loses 2–3 marks** in a 7-mark sub-question.
>
> 2. **Not converting units to MPa·√m.** Leaving the answer in N/mm^(3/2) without explicitly showing the unit conversion step is a **1-mark deduction** in most KTU valuations.
>
> 3. **Checking only one of the three validity conditions.** Many students check only the thickness $B$ and ignore the crack length $a$ and ligament $(W-a)$. The examiner will mark **0/3 for the validity section** if not all three are checked.
>
> 4. **Not stating the final conclusion explicitly.** The phrase "K_Q = 85.6 MPa·√m" alone is insufficient. You MUST write: **"Since all three validity criteria are satisfied, the result qualifies as a valid plane-strain fracture toughness K_Ic = 85.6 MPa·√m."** Failure to state this conclusion explicitly **costs 1 mark**.
>
> 5. **Confusing Mode I with Mode II.** Using "II" (number 2) instead of "I" (Roman numeral one) for the subscript is a **minor deduction** but reflects a lack of fundamentals.
>
> 6. **Skipping the fatigue pre-crack step** in the procedure section. ASTM E399 requires it, and KTU lab questions frequently test this knowledge.
>
> 7. **Reporting K_Ic in inconsistent units across the problem.** Stick to one unit system (MPa·√m is preferred) throughout the answer.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **K_Ic = Critical Stress Intensity Factor in Mode I** — the material's intrinsic resistance to crack propagation under tensile opening.
- [x] **Standard Unit: MPa·√m** (also acceptable: N/mm^(3/2); conversion: 1 N/mm^(3/2) = 0.03162 MPa·√m).
- [x] **Primary Test Specimen: SENB (Single Edge Notched Bend)** with **S = 4W** and **a/W ≈ 0.50**.
- [x] **Primary Formula (SENB):** $K_{Ic} = \dfrac{P \cdot S}{B \cdot W^{3/2}} \cdot f\!\left(\dfrac{a}{W}\right)$.
- [x] **Alternative Formula (CT):** $K_{Ic} = \dfrac{P}{B\sqrt{W}} \cdot f_{CT}\!\left(\dfrac{a}{W}\right)$.
- [x] **The three validity conditions for plane-strain K_Ic** (must check ALL THREE): $B, a, (W-a) \geq 2.5(K_{Ic}/\sigma_y)^{2}$.
- [x] **Peak load validity check:** $P_{max} \leq 1.10 \, P_Q$.
- [x] **5% Secant Method** is used to identify the provisional load $P_Q$ from the load-displacement curve.
- [x] **Fatigue pre-cracking** is essential to create a sharp crack tip and is required by ASTM E399.
- [x] **Three fracture modes:** Mode I (Opening, most critical), Mode II (In-plane shear), Mode III (Out-of-plane shear).
- [x] **K_Ic is a geometry-independent material property ONLY when plane-strain validity is satisfied.**
- [x] **If validity fails** → Report $K_Q$ as conditional/apparent toughness → Recommend **larger specimen** or use **J-integral** ($J_{Ic}$) method.
- [x] **The W^(3/2) power** is the most error-prone term in the SENB formula — memorize it as "**W to the three-halves**."
- [x] **KTU practicals use SENB almost exclusively** due to fixture simplicity; CT specimens are limited to advanced labs.
- [x] **Always show unit conversion** from N/mm^(3/2) to MPa·√m in numerical answers.
- [x] **High $\sigma_y$ + Low K_Ic materials** (e.g., hardened tool steel) require large specimens to satisfy plane-strain validity.
- [x] **Low $\sigma_y$ + High K_Ic materials** (e.g., ductile aluminum) often fail validity; J-integral testing is the modern alternative.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: S-N Curve Plotting Using Fatigue Testing Machine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMEL308 - MATERIALS TESTING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** S-N Curve Plotting Using Fatigue Testing Machine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Fatigue** is the progressive, localized structural damage and failure of a material subjected to cyclic loading at stress levels considerably below its **ultimate tensile strength (UTS)** or even yield strength. The **S-N Curve** (also called the **Wöhler Curve**, named after August Wöhler, 1858) is a graphical representation of the relationship between the **cyclic stress amplitude (S)** applied to a specimen and the **number of cycles to failure (N)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition to Memorize:**
> *"The S-N curve is a plot of alternating stress amplitude (σa) on the Y-axis versus the number of cycles to failure (Nf) on the X-axis, plotted on a linear or semi-logarithmic scale, used to determine the fatigue life and endurance limit of engineering materials."*

For **ferrous metals and alloys** (e.g., steels), the S-N curve becomes **horizontal** at a specific stress value known as the **Endurance Limit (Se) or Fatigue Limit** — below this stress, the material theoretically has an **infinite life** (typically defined as N > 10⁶ or 10⁷ cycles).

For **non-ferrous metals** (e.g., aluminum, copper, brass), no true endurance limit exists; instead, a **Fatigue Strength** is defined at a specific cycle count (e.g., 5×10⁸ cycles).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Paper Clip Analogy:**
> Imagine bending a paper clip back and forth repeatedly. Each bend applies a tiny fraction of the force needed to snap it in a single pull — yet after 15–20 bends, it **suddenly breaks** at a point well below its single-pull breaking strength. This is **fatigue failure** in miniature.

The paper clip's life is governed by:
- **How hard** you bend it (stress amplitude)
- **How many times** you bend it (number of cycles)

Plotting "bending force vs. number of bends until breakage" for several paper clips bent at different forces gives you an **S-N curve**. The harder you bend, the fewer bends it survives. Below a certain bending force, the clip would never break — its **endurance limit**.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Stress amplitude | σa | **MPa** or **N/mm²** |
| Mean stress | σm | **MPa** |
| Maximum cyclic stress | σmax | **MPa** |
| Minimum cyclic stress | σmin | **MPa** |
| Stress ratio | R | Dimensionless, ranges from −1 to +1 |
| Endurance limit | Se | **MPa** (for steels) |
| Fatigue life | Nf | **Cycles** |
| Infinite life threshold | N∞ | **10⁶ – 10⁷ cycles** |
| Rotational speed (machine) | n | **3000–10000 rpm** |
| Modulus of elasticity (steel) | E | **≈ 2 × 10⁵ MPa (200 GPa)** |

## 1.4 KTU Board Examiner's Terminology Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Students **must use the following exact KTU-approved terms** in their records:
> - **Rotating Beam Fatigue Testing Machine** (also: R.R. Moore type machine)
> - **Hourglass (waisted) specimen** with polished gauge section
> - **Constant amplitude fully reversed loading (R = −1)**
> - **Staircase method** (up-and-down method) for endurance limit
> - **S-N diagram / Wöhler curve**
> - **High-Cycle Fatigue (HCF)** regime: N > 10⁴–10⁵
> - **Low-Cycle Fatigue (LCF)** regime: N < 10⁴ (coffin-manson regime)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Fatigue Concepts — Step-by-Step Logic

### Step 1: The Nature of Cyclic Loading
A component in service rarely experiences a single, monotonic load. Instead, it encounters **fluctuating stresses** that vary with time. These may be:

- **Reversed loading** (e.g., rotating shaft): σmax = −σmin → R = −1
- **Repeated loading** (e.g., tension-tension): σmin = 0 → R = 0
- **Random loading** (e.g., aircraft wing): R varies irregularly

### Step 2: Resolution of Cyclic Stress
Any cyclic stress wave can be decomposed into two key parameters:

**Mean Stress (σm)** — the steady (DC) component:
$$\sigma_m = \frac{\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min}}{2}$$

**Stress Amplitude (σa)** — the alternating (AC) component:
$$\sigma_a = \frac{\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}}{2}$$

### Step 3: Why σa is the Primary Plotting Variable
The **alternating stress amplitude** is what drives fatigue damage. Mean stress merely biases the loading (tensile mean stress is more damaging than compressive mean stress), so σa becomes the natural Y-axis for the S-N plot.

### Step 4: The Rotating Beam Principle
In the **Rotating Beam Fatigue Testing Machine**, a precisely machined hourglass specimen is subjected to a **constant bending moment M** while rotating at high rpm. As the specimen rotates, every point on its outer surface alternates between **tension** and **compression** once per revolution. This produces a **fully reversed (R = −1)** stress cycle naturally.

The bending stress at the critical (waist) section is:
$$\sigma = \frac{32 M}{\pi d^3}$$
where **d** is the minimum specimen diameter at the gauge section.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" behind rotating beam design:**
> One motor + one bending load = millions of stress reversals per hour (at 3000 rpm → 50 reversals per second). No separate actuator is needed — the rotation itself creates the cycle. This is elegant, economical, and historically the standard test method.

### Step 5: Specimen Failure Criterion
The specimen is considered **failed** when:
1. A visible fatigue crack develops and propagates completely, OR
2. The specimen fractures into two pieces, OR
3. A pre-defined rotation count is reached (runout) without failure → "**infinite life**" point.

### Step 6: Plotting the S-N Curve
- X-axis: **N (cycles to failure)** — usually on a **logarithmic scale** for HCF regime
- Y-axis: **σa (stress amplitude)** — usually linear
- A best-fit line (or curve) is drawn through the data points
- For steels: a **horizontal asymptote** appears at σa = Se (endurance limit)

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table must be memorized verbatim for KTU ESE & lab viva.**

| # | Formula | Description / Engineering Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma_m = \dfrac{\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min}}{2}$ | Mean stress of cyclic loading | MPa |
| 2 | $\sigma_a = \dfrac{\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}}{2}$ | Alternating stress amplitude (S in S-N curve) | MPa |
| 3 | $R = \dfrac{\sigma_{min}}{\sigma_{max}}$ | Stress ratio (R = −1 for rotating beam) | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $\sigma = \dfrac{32 M}{\pi d^{3}}$ | Bending stress in a circular cross-section | MPa |
| 5 | $M = \dfrac{W \cdot L}{4}$ | Bending moment for 4-point bending (typical machine) | N·mm |
| 6 | $\sigma_a = \sigma_f' \, (2N)^{b}$ | **Basquin's equation** (high-cycle fatigue) | MPa |
| 7 | $S_e = 0.5 \, S_{ut}$ | Approximate endurance limit (steels, R = −1, unnotched) | MPa |
| 8 | $S_e' = k_a k_b k_c S_e$ | Marin equation for modified endurance limit | MPa |
| 9 | $f = \dfrac{n}{60}$ | Test frequency (Hz) where n = rpm | Hz |
| 10 | $\log \sigma_a = \log \sigma_f' + b \log(2N)$ | Linear form for semi-log S-N plot | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **σf′** = fatigue strength coefficient (≈ true fracture strength, MPa)
> **b** = fatigue strength exponent (typically −0.05 to −0.12 for steels)

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Role of S-N Data |
|---|---|
| **Automotive crankshafts** | Design for infinite life (N > 10⁷) using Se |
| **Aircraft turbine blades** | Use finite-life S-N data with safety factors |
| **Bridge cables & welds** | Use modified Goodman / Soderberg with mean stress correction |
| **Pressure vessels** | ASME code uses S-N curves with fatigue reduction factors |
| **Railway axles** | Rotating beam data critical for axle life prediction |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Bending Stress in a Rotating Beam Specimen

### Setup
A cylindrical hourglass specimen of minimum diameter **d** is subjected to a pure bending moment **M** at its narrow waist.

### Step 1: Flexure Formula (Strength of Materials Recap)
For a circular cross-section under pure bending, the maximum bending stress at the outer fiber is:
$$\sigma = \frac{M \cdot c}{I}$$

### Step 2: Substitute Geometric Properties
For a solid circular cross-section:
- Distance to outer fiber: $c = \dfrac{d}{2}$
- Area moment of inertia: $I = \dfrac{\pi d^{4}}{64}$

### Step 3: Algebraic Simplification
$$\sigma = \frac{M \cdot \dfrac{d}{2}}{\dfrac{\pi d^{4}}{64}}$$

$$\sigma = \frac{M \cdot d}{2} \cdot \frac{64}{\pi d^{4}}$$

$$\sigma = \frac{32 M}{\pi d^{3}}$$

> **Result:** This is the standard bending stress equation used by **every** rotating beam fatigue machine manufacturer.

## 3.2 Derivation: Basquin's Equation (High-Cycle Fatigue Law)

### Step 1: Empirical Observation
In the **High-Cycle Fatigue (HCF)** regime (elastic regime, N > 10³), experimental data plots as a **straight line** on log-log axes:
$$\log \sigma_a = \log C + m \log N$$

### Step 2: Define Fatigue Parameters
Let:
- $C$ = material constant
- $m$ = slope of the log-log S-N line (negative for metals)
- Define $b = m$ (fatigue strength exponent)
- Define $\sigma_f' = C \cdot 2^{-b}$ (fatigue strength coefficient)

### Step 3: Rearrange into Standard Form
Substituting and rearranging:
$$\sigma_a = \sigma_f' (2N)^{b}$$

> **Why factor of 2?** Because one full stress cycle contains **two reversals** (one tension, one compression). The rotating beam produces **2N reversals per N cycles**.

### Step 4: Engineering Application
For a steel with $\sigma_f' = 1000$ MPa and $b = -0.10$:
- At $N = 10^{3}$: $\sigma_a = 1000 \cdot (2000)^{-0.10} = 1000 / 2000^{0.10}$
  $$2000^{0.10} = e^{0.10 \cdot \ln 2000} = e^{0.10 \cdot 7.601} = e^{0.7601} \approx 2.139$$
  $$\sigma_a \approx \frac{1000}{2.139} \approx 467 \text{ MPa}$$

- At $N = 10^{6}$: $\sigma_a = 1000 \cdot (2 \times 10^{6})^{-0.10}$
  $$(2 \times 10^{6})^{0.10} = e^{0.10 \cdot 14.508} = e^{1.4508} \approx 4.265$$
  $$\sigma_a \approx \frac{1000}{4.265} \approx 234 \text{ MPa}$$

- At $N = 10^{7}$: $\sigma_a \approx 186$ MPa (close to endurance limit)

## 3.3 Derivation: Relationship Between Applied Load and Stress Amplitude

In the typical **4-point bending rotating beam setup**:

### Step 1: Bending Moment
For a simply supported specimen with two equal end loads **W** at distance **L** from supports:
$$M = \frac{W \cdot L}{4}$$

### Step 2: Stress Amplitude (Substituting into Flexure Formula)
$$\sigma_a = \frac{32 M}{\pi d^{3}} = \frac{32 \cdot \dfrac{W L}{4}}{\pi d^{3}}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_a = \frac{8 W L}{\pi d^{3}}}$$

### Step 3: Solving for Required Load W
$$W = \frac{\sigma_a \cdot \pi \cdot d^{3}}{8 L}$$

> **Practical Use:** Given a target stress amplitude (e.g., 70% of UTS), the technician computes the required **W** to be applied to the loading lever of the machine.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Lab Record Style

> **Problem Statement:**
> A steel specimen of diameter **d = 7.5 mm** is tested in a rotating beam fatigue machine with span **L = 100 mm** between supports. The applied load is **W = 50 kgf** (≈ 490.5 N). Compute the stress amplitude and the expected cycles to failure if the steel has $\sigma_f' = 1050$ MPa, $b = -0.09$, and Sut = 620 MPa.

### Solution:

**Step 1:** Convert load to Newtons
$$W = 50 \times 9.81 = 490.5 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate bending moment
$$M = \frac{W \cdot L}{4} = \frac{490.5 \times 100}{4} = 12262.5 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate stress amplitude
$$\sigma_a = \frac{32 M}{\pi d^{3}} = \frac{32 \times 12262.5}{\pi \times (7.5)^{3}}$$

$$d^{3} = 421.875 \text{ mm}^{3}$$

$$\sigma_a = \frac{392400}{\pi \times 421.875} = \frac{392400}{1325.36}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_a \approx 296.1 \text{ MPa}}$$

**Step 4:** Check for endurance limit
$$S_e \approx 0.5 \times S_{ut} = 0.5 \times 620 = 310 \text{ MPa}$$

Since $\sigma_a = 296.1$ MPa $< 310$ MPa, the specimen may **run out** (achieve infinite life). The lab record should note this as a "**runout**" point.

**Step 5:** Estimate cycles to failure (if failure occurs)
Using Basquin's equation:
$$296.1 = 1050 \cdot (2N)^{-0.09}$$

$$(2N)^{-0.09} = \frac{296.1}{1050} = 0.2820$$

Taking log on both sides:
$$-0.09 \log(2N) = \log(0.2820) = -0.5497$$

$$\log(2N) = \frac{0.5497}{0.09} = 6.108$$

$$2N = 10^{6.108} = 1.283 \times 10^{6}$$

$$N \approx 6.41 \times 10^{5} \text{ cycles}$$

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Lab Procedure Logic

| Test Outcome | σa vs. Se | Action by Student |
|---|---|---|
| Specimen fractures | σa > Se | Plot point: (Nf, σa) |
| Specimen runs out (10⁷ cycles) | σa ≤ Se | Mark as **runout (→)** with arrow on plot |
| Multiple tests at same σa | Statistical scatter | Use **Staircase method** to find Se |
| Premature failure at low N | Surface defect / improper polish | Discard, re-prepare specimen |

---

> **Note on Mermaid syntax:** All node IDs are purely alphanumeric (e.g., `L1`, `A`, `B`) and labels contain only clean uppercase text — no bold, italic, or HTML tags — to satisfy Mermaid compilation safeguards.

## 4.2 Process Flow — S-N Curve Plotting Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1 Prepare Hourglass Specimen Polish to 0.2 micron Ra] --> S2[Step 2 Measure Waist Diameter d with Micrometer 3 readings average]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Compute Target Load W for Desired sigma a]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Mount Specimen in Chuck and Lock Bearings]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 Apply Dead Load on Lever Arm]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 Set RPM 3000 typical and Reset Counter]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7 Run Test Until Fracture OR 10 to power 7 Runout]
    S7 --> S8{Outcome}
    S8 -->|Fracture| S9[Record Nf and sigma a Plot Point on S N Curve]
    S8 -->|Runout| S10[Mark as Runout Arrow on S N Plot]
    S9 --> S11[Step 8 Repeat for 8 to 12 Different Stress Levels]
    S10 --> S11
    S11 --> S12[Step 9 Draw Best Fit Curve and Identify Endurance Limit Se]
    S12 --> S13[Step 10 Apply Staircase Method for Precise Se]
    S13 --> END[Report Nf vs sigma a Table and Final S N Plot]
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    LOAD["Applied Cyclic Load sigma vs t"] --> RES[Stress Resolution Algorithm]
    RES --> SIGM["Mean Stress sigm DC Component"]
    RES --> SIGA["Stress Amplitude sigmaa AC Component"]
    RES --> R["Stress Ratio R equals sigma min by sigma max"]
    SIGA --> XAXIS[X axis of S N Plot]
    XAXIS -.->|Independence of mean stress| NOTE["Note For R equals minus 1 Plot sigma max directly"]
```

```mermaid
    SHOULDER1 --> GAUGE[Polished Gauge Section d 7.5 mm Lg 25 mm]

    WAIST --> GAUGE2[Symmetric Gauge Section]

    WAIST -.-> FAIL["Fatigue crack initiates here in HCF"]
```

> **Specimen specification reference:** Hourglass geometry per **ASTM E466** (Standard Practice for Conducting Force Controlled Constant Amplitude Axial Fatigue Tests of Metallic Materials) or rotating beam variant per **ASTM E468** / **ISO 1143**.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Define the term "endurance limit" of a material. Why does it exist for steels but not for aluminum alloys?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
The **endurance limit (Se)** is the maximum cyclic stress amplitude that a material can withstand for an **infinite number of cycles** (conventionally N ≥ 10⁶ or 10⁷) without undergoing fatigue failure. Below this stress, the material has theoretically infinite life.

**Why steels exhibit endurance limit (1 mark):**
Steels contain dissolved interstitial atoms (C, N) and dispersed carbides that **pin dislocations** and create barriers to slip-band movement. Crack initiation requires a critical stress to push dislocations through these barriers; below Se, persistent slip bands (PSBs) cannot form and cracks cannot nucleate.

**Why aluminum lacks endurance limit (1 mark):**
Aluminum alloys do not have effective dislocation-pinning mechanisms. Persistent slip bands form at virtually any cyclic stress level, allowing fatigue cracks to nucleate eventually. Hence, fatigue strength is quoted at a specific cycle count (e.g., 5×10⁸ cycles) rather than an absolute endurance limit.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
**What is a "Wöhler curve"? State its axes and the typical scale used.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
The Wöhler curve (also called **S-N curve**) is a plot of cyclic **stress amplitude (S)** versus the **number of cycles to failure (N)** for a material subjected to constant-amplitude cyclic loading. It was first developed by August Wöhler in 1858 for railway axle design.

**Axes (1 mark):**
- **Y-axis:** Stress amplitude σa (MPa) — usually on linear scale
- **X-axis:** Number of cycles N — usually on **logarithmic scale** (semi-log plot)

**Typical scale and asymptotic behavior (1 mark):**
For steels, the curve becomes **horizontal** (asymptotic) at the endurance limit Se for N > 10⁶. For non-ferrous metals, the curve continues to slope downward indefinitely on the semi-log plot.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, with sub-parts (a) = 7 marks and (b) = 7 marks. Students must answer **ONE** of the two full questions (either Q.A or Q.B).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]**

**(a) With a neat sketch, explain the working principle of a Rotating Beam Fatigue Testing Machine. List its main components. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle (3 marks):**
The rotating beam fatigue testing machine operates on the principle that **pure bending of a rotating shaft** produces a **fully reversed cyclic stress** (R = −1) on its surface. A precisely machined hourglass specimen is rotated at high speed (typically 3000–10000 rpm) while being subjected to a constant bending moment via dead-weight loading. Each point on the outer surface of the specimen experiences **one tension-compression cycle per revolution**, naturally generating cyclic stress without requiring a separate actuator.

**Main Components (3 marks):**
1. **Variable speed AC motor** with speed control
2. **Spindle and chuck assembly** to grip and rotate the specimen
3. **Four-point bending jig** with adjustable lever arm
4. **Dead-weight loading system** (weights and lever)
5. **Mechanical / digital revolution counter** with auto-stop
6. **Safety enclosure** (polycarbonate shield)
7. **Bearing supports** to allow free rotation while transmitting load

**Key Equation (1 mark):**
$$\sigma_a = \frac{8 W L}{\pi d^{3}}$$

---

**(b) A steel specimen of gauge diameter 8 mm is tested in a rotating beam machine. The applied load is 60 N, span length is 100 mm. If the steel has σf′ = 1000 MPa and b = −0.10, calculate: (i) the stress amplitude, (ii) the cycles to failure. Given Sut = 600 MPa. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Stress Amplitude (3 marks):**
$$\sigma_a = \frac{8 W L}{\pi d^{3}} = \frac{8 \times 60 \times 100}{\pi \times (8)^{3}}$$

$$d^{3} = 512 \text{ mm}^{3}$$

$$\sigma_a = \frac{48000}{\pi \times 512} = \frac{48000}{1608.5}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_a \approx 29.84 \text{ MPa}}$$

(Note: With 60 N load, the stress is very low. This represents a runout scenario.)

**(ii) Cycles to Failure (4 marks):**
Check endurance limit: $S_e \approx 0.5 \times 600 = 300$ MPa

Since $\sigma_a = 29.84$ MPa $\ll$ $S_e = 300$ MPa, the specimen will **NOT FAIL** — it will run out at 10⁷ cycles.

Using Basquin's equation formally to find the cycles where this stress equals the S-N curve:
$$29.84 = 1000 \cdot (2N)^{-0.10}$$

$$(2N)^{-0.10} = 0.02984$$

$$\log(2N) = \frac{-\log(0.02984)}{0.10} = \frac{1.5252}{0.10} = 15.252$$

$$2N = 10^{15.252} = 1.786 \times 10^{15}$$

$$N \approx 8.93 \times 10^{14} \text{ cycles}$$

Since this is far beyond 10⁷, the specimen is confirmed to be a **runout**. In the lab, the test is stopped at 10⁷ cycles and the point is marked with a horizontal arrow on the S-N curve.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2023 Model]**

**(a) Explain the procedure to plot an S-N curve for a ductile material using a fatigue testing machine. Mention the specimen specifications per ASTM standards. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step-by-Step Procedure (5 marks):**

1. **Specimen Preparation:** Machine an hourglass (waisted) specimen from the material per **ASTM E466 / ISO 1143** specifications. Polish the gauge section longitudinally to a surface finish of **0.2 μm Ra** to avoid premature crack initiation at machining marks.

2. **Measure Critical Diameter:** Use a micrometer to measure the minimum diameter **d** at the gauge section to ±0.01 mm precision. Take 3 readings and average.

3. **Compute Test Load:** For a chosen target stress amplitude $\sigma_a$ (typically starting at ~70% of UTS), compute the required load:
$$W = \frac{\sigma_a \pi d^{3}}{8 L}$$

4. **Set Up the Test:** Mount the specimen in the chuck, lock bearings, set the bending jig span **L**, and apply the calculated dead weight via the lever arm.

5. **Run the Test:** Start the motor at desired RPM (e.g., 3000). Reset the counter. Run until specimen fractures OR until 10⁷ cycles (runout criterion) is reached.

6. **Record Data:** Note (σa, Nf) for each test. For runouts, mark with an arrow on the plot.

7. **Repeat:** Test 8–12 specimens at different stress levels — higher stresses give shorter lives, lower stresses give longer lives.

8. **Plot S-N Curve:** On semi-log paper, plot σa (Y, linear) vs. Nf (X, log). For steels, draw the curve flattening to the endurance limit Se.

**ASTM Specifications (2 marks):**
- **ASTM E466** — Force-controlled constant amplitude axial fatigue testing
- **ASTM E468** — Presentation of constant amplitude fatigue test results
- **ASTM E468 / E739** — Statistical analysis of fatigue data
- Specimen gauge diameter typically **6–12 mm**, gauge length **15–25 mm**, shoulder radius **≥ 2d**

---

**(b) What is Basquin's equation? Using the S-N data given below for a steel, determine the fatigue strength coefficient σf′ and exponent b. (7 marks)**

| S.No. | σa (MPa) | Nf (cycles) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 500 | 1.0 × 10⁴ |
| 2 | 400 | 8.0 × 10⁴ |
| 3 | 350 | 3.5 × 10⁵ |
| 4 | 320 | 9.0 × 10⁵ |

**Model Solution:**

**Basquin's Equation (1 mark):**
$$\sigma_a = \sigma_f' (2N)^{b}$$
$$\log \sigma_a = \log \sigma_f' + b \log(2N)$$

This is a **linear** equation in log-log space with slope **b** and intercept **log σf′**.

**Step 1: Compute log σa and log(2N) for each data point (2 marks):**

| S.No. | σa (MPa) | Nf | 2N | log σa | log(2N) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 500 | 1.0×10⁴ | 2.0×10⁴ | 2.6990 | 4.3010 |
| 2 | 400 | 8.0×10⁴ | 1.6×10⁵ | 2.6021 | 5.2041 |
| 3 | 350 | 3.5×10⁵ | 7.0×10⁵ | 2.5441 | 5.8451 |
| 4 | 320 | 9.0×10⁵ | 1.8×10⁶ | 2.5051 | 6.2553 |

**Step 2: Apply Linear Regression (Least Squares) (3 marks):**

Let $X = \log(2N)$ and $Y = \log \sigma_a$.

$$\bar{X} = \frac{4.3010 + 5.2041 + 5.8451 + 6.2553}{4} = \frac{21.6055}{4} = 5.4014$$

$$\bar{Y} = \frac{2.6990 + 2.6021 + 2.5441 + 2.5051}{4} = \frac{10.3503}{4} = 2.5876$$

**Slope b:**
$$b = \frac{\sum (X_i - \bar{X})(Y_i - \bar{Y})}{\sum (X_i - \bar{X})^{2}}$$

Compute terms:
- (4.3010 − 5.4014)(2.6990 − 2.5876) = (−1.1004)(0.1114) = −0.1226
- (5.2041 − 5.4014)(2.6021 − 2.5876) = (−0.1973)(0.0145) = −0.0029
- (5.8451 − 5.4014)(2.5441 − 2.5876) = (0.4437)(−0.0435) = −0.0193
- (6.2553 − 5.4014)(2.5051 − 2.5876) = (0.8539)(−0.0825) = −0.0704

Sum of numerator = −0.1226 − 0.0029 − 0.0193 − 0.0704 = **−0.2152**

- (4.3010 − 5.4014)² = 1.2109
- (5.2041 − 5.4014)² = 0.0389
- (5.8451 − 5.4014)² = 0.1969
- (6.2553 − 5.4014)² = 0.7291

Sum of denominator = 1.2109 + 0.0389 + 0.1969 + 0.7291 = **2.1758**

$$b = \frac{-0.2152}{2.1758} \approx -0.0989 \approx -0.10$$

**Intercept (1 mark):**
$$\log \sigma_f' = \bar{Y} - b \bar{X} = 2.5876 - (-0.0989)(5.4014)$$
$$= 2.5876 + 0.5342 = 3.1218$$

$$\sigma_f' = 10^{3.1218} \approx 1324 \text{ MPa}$$

**Final Answer (to present in exam):**
$$\boxed{\sigma_f' \approx 1324 \text{ MPa}, \quad b \approx -0.10}$$

**Basquin's equation for this steel:**
$$\sigma_a = 1324 \cdot (2N)^{-0.10}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks in S-N Curve Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in Basquin's equation** — The equation uses (2N), not N, because of reversals vs. cycles. Marks lost: 1–2 per sub-part.
>
> 2. **Confusing log with log₁₀** — Use log base 10. In scientific calculator, this is the `log` button, not `ln`. Marks lost: 1.
>
> 3. **Not stating units of stress (MPa) and cycles (dimensionless)** — Always include units in the final answer. Marks lost: 0.5.
>
> 4. **Mixing up endurance limit and fatigue strength** — Endurance limit = infinite life (steels); Fatigue strength = life at specified N (non-ferrous).
>
> 5. **Plotting runout points as failed points** — A runout (no failure at 10⁷ cycles) must be plotted as a horizontal arrow → at the runout cycle count, not as a normal point.
>
> 6. **Not mentioning ASTM standards in procedure** — Examiners specifically look for ASTM E466/E468 citations. Marks lost: 1.
>
> 7. **Skipping the surface polish requirement** — Polishing to 0.2 μm Ra is non-negotiable; failure to mention costs 1 mark.
>
> 8. **Linear regression errors** — In Q.B(b), students often do not show the formula or just guess the slope; examiners want the **least-squares formula explicitly written**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-minute revision — pin this to your wall before the exam.**

- [ ] **S-N curve** = Stress amplitude (S) vs. Number of cycles to failure (N), also called **Wöhler curve**.
- [ ] **Y-axis:** σa (MPa, linear); **X-axis:** N (log scale) → **semi-log plot**.
- [ ] **Endurance limit Se** exists for **ferrous** metals (steels); not for **non-ferrous** (Al, Cu).
- [ ] Rotating beam machine produces **R = −1** (fully reversed) cycling automatically via rotation.
- [ ] **Stress amplitude:** $\sigma_a = \dfrac{\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}}{2}$
- [ ] **Mean stress:** $\sigma_m = \dfrac{\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min}}{2}$
- [ ] **Stress ratio:** $R = \dfrac{\sigma_{min}}{\sigma_{max}}$ → R = −1 for rotating beam.
- [ ] **Bending stress in circular cross-section:** $\sigma = \dfrac{32 M}{\pi d^{3}}$
- [ ] **For 4-point bending (typical setup):** $\sigma_a = \dfrac{8 W L}{\pi d^{3}}$
- [ ] **Basquin's equation (HCF):** $\sigma_a = \sigma_f' (2N)^{b}$ → linear in log-log space.
- [ ] **b** is negative (typically −0.05 to −0.12 for steels); **σf′** ≈ true fracture strength.
- [ ] **Approximate endurance limit (steels):** $S_e \approx 0.5 \, S_{ut}$.
- [ ] **Specimen:** hourglass geometry, gauge section polished to **0.2 μm Ra** per **ASTM E466**.
- [ ] **Runout criterion:** N = 10⁶ or 10⁷ cycles without failure → mark with arrow on plot.
- [ ] **Staircase (up-and-down) method** used to pinpoint Se precisely near the knee of the curve.
- [ ] **HCF regime:** N > 10³–10⁴ (elastic); **LCF regime:** N < 10⁴ (Coffin-Manson, plastic).
- [ ] **Test frequency** typical: 50–167 Hz (3000–10000 rpm).
- [ ] **High-cycle fatigue** is governed by **crack initiation**; **low-cycle** by crack growth.
- [ ] **Always state units** (MPa, cycles, rpm) in every answer.
- [ ] **ASTM standards to cite:** E466 (force-controlled axial), E468 (presentation of results), E739 (statistical analysis).

---

**END OF KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 NOTES**
*Topic: S-N Curve Plotting Using Fatigue Testing Machine*
*Course: PCMEL308 - Materials Testing Lab | KTU 2024 Scheme*
