topics.title: Stress Analysis Using Photoelasticity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMEL308 - MATERIALS TESTING LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Stress Analysis Using Photoelasticity # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Photoelasticity** is a non-destructive, full-field experimental stress analysis technique that exploits the temporary birefringence (double refraction) property exhibited by certain transparent, isotropic polymeric materials (such as epoxy, Araldite, or polycarbonate) when they are subjected to mechanical loads. When a stressed transparent model is placed between two crossed polarizers, the principal stress distribution within the model is rendered visible as a pattern of brilliantly colored bands called **isochromatic fringes** and dark bands called **isoclinic lines**. Photoelasticity directly yields the **difference between the two principal stresses** $(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)$ at every interior point, making it a powerful complementary method to strain gauge and numerical (FEM) analysis. > [!NOTE] > **Birefringence (Photoelasticity):** A phenomenon in which a normally optically isotropic material splits an incident plane-polarized light ray into two orthogonal components travelling at different velocities when stressed, producing a relative optical retardation $\delta$ that is directly proportional to the principal stress difference. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Photoelasticity is classified as a *qualitative* AND *quantitative* stress analysis method. The lab experiment under Module Experiment 2 typically involves: (a) calibration to obtain the **stress fringe value $f_\sigma$**, (b) observation of isochromatic fringe patterns on a disc under diametral compression, and (c) computation of $\sigma_1 - \sigma_2$ at selected points. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you take a clear, colorless glass disc (think of a magnifying lens) and gently press it between your thumb and forefinger. The glass remains perfectly transparent, and you cannot visually tell that it is stressed. Now, replace the glass with a clear plastic (epoxy) disc and place it between two dark polarizing sunglasses. Suddenly, the disc lights up in brilliant rainbow-colored bands — **like a butterfly's wing trapped in the stress field**. Areas of high stress glow with closely packed rainbow bands, while low-stress regions appear dark. This is exactly what a photoelastic experiment does: **stress becomes visible as light**. The colors are not paint — they are the optical fingerprints of internal stress. Engineers use these "stress rainbows" to find dangerous stress concentrations (around holes, fillets, and cracks) in components, without ever cutting them open. ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Metrics - **Stress fringe value $f_\sigma$** — measured in **N/(m·fringe)** — material-specific constant of the photoelastic model. - **Wavelength $\lambda$** of monochromatic light — typically **589 nm** (sodium D-line, used in calibration). - **Thickness $t$** of the model — measured in **metres (m)**. - **Refractive index $n$** — dimensionless. - **Stress-optic coefficient $C$** — measured in **Brewsters** ($1\ \text{Brewster} = 10^{-12}\ \text{m}^2/\text{N}$). > [!TIP] > **Mnemonic for Photoelasticity Pillars:** **"P.O.L.E."** → **P**olarized light, **O**ptical retardation, **L**oad-induced birefringence, **E**lastic stress field. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The Stress-Optic Law When a transparent, isotropic photoelastic material is stressed, it temporarily becomes **optically anisotropic**, behaving like a biaxial crystal. The principal directions of the stress field coincide with the principal optical axes. A ray of plane-polarized light entering the model splits into two orthogonal components vibrating along the principal stress directions, and each component travels at a different velocity. Upon exiting the model, the two components recombine, but the difference in their optical path produces a **relative retardation $\delta$** (also called optical path difference, OPD). The governing relationship is the **Stress-Optic Law** (also called the **Maxwell–Neumann equation**), which states that the relative retardation $\delta$ is directly proportional to the difference of the two principal stresses: $$\delta = C \cdot t \cdot (\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)$$ where: - $\delta$ = relative retardation (metres, m) - $C$ = stress-optic coefficient of the model material (m²/N) - $t$ = model thickness (m) - $\sigma_1, \sigma_2$ = principal stresses (N/m² or Pa) ## 2.2 Optical Components & Working Principle ### 2.2.1 Plane Polariscope (Basic Setup) A **plane polariscope** consists of: 1. **Light source** (monochromatic for quantitative work; white for qualitative colorful fringes) 2. **Polarizer** — converts unpolarized light into plane-polarized light 3. **Stressed model** — the photoelastic specimen 4. **Analyzer** — a second polarizer, usually oriented at **90° (crossed)** to the polarizer 5. **Observation screen / camera** **Working Principle:** - When no load is applied, the polarized light is fully extinguished by the crossed analyzer → field is dark. - Under load, the model becomes birefringent. The light components travelling along $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_2$ experience different velocities, accumulate a phase difference, and partially pass through the analyzer, producing bright fringes. - **Dark fringes (isoclinics)** appear where the principal stress direction coincides with the polarizer axis. - **Bright colored fringes (isochromatics)** correspond to integer multiples of retardation. ### 2.2.2 Circular Polariscope (Refined Setup) A circular polariscope adds two **quarter-wave plates ($\lambda/4$)** to eliminate the isoclinics and isolate pure isochromatic fringes, allowing clean quantitative measurement. It is the standard instrument used in modern photoelastic labs. ## 2.3 Types of Fringe Patterns | Fringe Type | Description | Appears As | Information Given | |---|---|---|---| | **Isochromatic** | Locus of points with constant $(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)$ | Bright colored bands (white light) or dark bands (monochromatic) | $\sigma_1 - \sigma_2$ | | **Isoclinic** | Locus of points with the same principal stress direction | Dark bands that move/change with rotation of polarizer | Direction of $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_2$ | | **Isopachic** | Locus of points with constant $\sigma_1 + \sigma_2$ | Requires separate (e.g., thermal) method | Sum of principal stresses | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Fringe Order N:** The number of isochromatic fringes passing through a point is called the **fringe order N**. It is dimensionless and is the primary output of a photoelastic experiment. The KTU 2024 lab experiment almost always asks for **N** at the centre of a disc under diametral load. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\delta = C \cdot t \cdot (\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)$ | Stress-Optic Law (Maxwell–Neumann equation) | m | | 2 | $\delta = N \cdot \lambda$ | Retardation in terms of fringe order and wavelength | m | | 3 | $N \cdot \lambda = C \cdot t \cdot (\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)$ | Combined photoelastic equation | m | | 4 | $\sigma_1 - \sigma_2 = \dfrac{N \cdot f_\sigma}{t}$ | Principal stress difference from fringe order | Pa | | 5 | $f_\sigma = \dfrac{\lambda}{C}$ | Definition of stress fringe value (material constant) | N/(m·fringe) | | 6 | $\sigma_1 - \sigma_2 = \dfrac{8 P}{\pi D t}$ | Centre of disc under diametral load $P$, diameter $D$ | Pa | | 7 | $f_\sigma = \dfrac{8 P}{\pi D \cdot N_c}$ | Calibration of stress fringe value at disc centre | N/(m·fringe) | | 8 | $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2}$ | Maximum in-plane shear stress | Pa | | 9 | $\theta_p = \dfrac{1}{2}\tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}\right)$ | Principal stress direction (isoclinic angle) | radians | | 10 | $\Delta n = C(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)$ | Stress-induced birefringence (difference in refractive indices) | dimensionless | > [!NOTE] > **Practical Conversion for KTU 2024 Lab:** $f_\sigma$ is commonly reported in **N/m/ fringe (or kN/m/fringe)**. Always ensure consistency: if $t$ is in **mm**, convert it to **m** before substituting into equation (4). ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Photoelasticity is a workhorse technique in: - **Aerospace:** Stress concentration analysis around aircraft window cutouts, rivet holes, and turbine blade roots. - **Civil Engineering:** Photoelastic studies of dam models, bridge girders, and concrete gravity structures to identify tensile stress zones that may crack. - **Mechanical Engineering:** Design verification of pressure vessels, gear teeth, crankshafts, and welded joints. - **Biomedical Engineering:** Stress distribution in bone–prosthesis interfaces and dental implants. - **Geomechanics:** Photoelastic study of stress around underground openings and tunnel faces using frozen-stress 3D photoelasticity. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Stress-Optic Law (Maxwell–Neumann Equation) ### Step 1 — Decomposition of Plane-Polarized Light A plane-polarized light ray incident on the model can be resolved (by the rules of vector decomposition) into two orthogonal components vibrating along the principal stress directions $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_2$. The angle between the polarization direction of the incident light and the principal stress $\sigma_1$ is $\alpha$. Let the incident electric field amplitude be $A_0$. The two components are: - Component along $\sigma_1$: $E_1 = A_0 \cos\alpha$ - Component along $\sigma_2$: $E_2 = A_0 \sin\alpha$ ### Step 2 — Passage Through the Stressed Model Inside the stressed model, the refractive index along $\sigma_1$ is $n_1$ and along $\sigma_2$ is $n_2$. The two components travel at velocities $c/n_1$ and $c/n_2$ respectively. Upon exiting the model of thickness $t$, the time taken by each component is: $$t_1 = \frac{n_1 \cdot t}{c} \quad ; \quad t_2 = \frac{n_2 \cdot t}{c}$$ The **time delay** between the two components is: $$\Delta t = t_2 - t_1 = \frac{(n_2 - n_1) \cdot t}{c}$$ ### Step 3 — Conversion to Optical Path Difference (Retardation $\delta$) Multiplying the time delay by the speed of light $c$ gives the **optical path difference** (relative retardation) $\delta$: $$\delta = c \cdot \Delta t = (n_2 - n_1) \cdot t$$ Let the **stress-induced birefringence** be $\Delta n = n_2 - n_1$. The photoelastic effect states that $\Delta n$ is linearly proportional to the principal stress difference: $$\Delta n = n_2 - n_1 = C \cdot (\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)$$ ### Step 4 — Substitution of $\Delta n$ into the Retardation Expression Substituting the linear photoelastic relationship into the retardation expression yields the **Stress-Optic Law**: $$\delta = C \cdot t \cdot (\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)$$ This is the **fundamental governing equation of photoelasticity** and forms the analytical backbone of every KTU 2024 question on this topic. ## 3.2 Derivation of the Principal Stress Difference in Terms of Fringe Order ### Step 1 — Expressing Retardation Using the Fringe Order When monochromatic light of wavelength $\lambda$ is used, integer numbers of full wavelength retardation produce dark fringes. Mathematically: $$\delta = N \cdot \lambda$$ where $N$ is the **fringe order** (a dimensionless integer at the exact fringe centre, or a fractional value at any general point). ### Step 2 — Equating the Two Expressions for $\delta$ Setting the two expressions for $\delta$ equal: $$N \cdot \lambda = C \cdot t \cdot (\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)$$ Rearranging to isolate the principal stress difference: $$\sigma_1 - \sigma_2 = \frac{N \cdot \lambda}{C \cdot t}$$ ### Step 3 — Defining the Stress Fringe Value $f_\sigma$ By convention, the **stress fringe value** is defined as the principal stress difference required to produce exactly one full fringe of retardation in a unit-thickness model: $$f_\sigma = \frac{\lambda}{C} \quad \text{(units: N/m per fringe)}$$ ### Step 4 — Final Compact Form (The KTU 2024 Working Equation) $$\boxed{\;\sigma_1 - \sigma_2 = \frac{N \cdot f_\sigma}{t}\;}$$ This is the most important equation students will use in the lab. **Memorize it.** ## 3.3 Derivation of the Centre Stress in a Disc Under Diametral Compression ### Step 1 — The Standard Result from Theory of Elasticity For a circular disc of diameter $D$ loaded diametrically by a compressive force $P$, the classical solution (Filon's solution) gives, at the **geometric centre of the disc**: $$\sigma_1 = \frac{2P}{\pi D t} \quad ; \quad \sigma_2 = -\frac{6P}{\pi D t}$$ ### Step 2 — Principal Stress Difference at the Centre Subtracting: $$\sigma_1 - \sigma_2 = \frac{2P}{\pi D t} - \left(-\frac{6P}{\pi D t}\right) = \frac{8P}{\pi D t}$$ ### Step 3 — Equating to the Photoelastic Working Equation Setting the photoelastic expression equal to the elasticity result: $$\frac{N_c \cdot f_\sigma}{t} = \frac{8P}{\pi D t}$$ ### Step 4 — Calibration Formula for the Stress Fringe Value Solving for $f_\sigma$: $$\boxed{\;f_\sigma = \frac{8P}{\pi D \cdot N_c}\;}$$ This is the **calibration formula** students will use to determine $f_\sigma$ in the KTU 2024 lab. > [!TIP] > **Proof of Concept Check:** The fringe order at the centre $N_c$ is *independent* of disc thickness — a useful sanity check, since the elastic stress $\propto 1/t$ exactly cancels the photoelastic $1/t$ in equation (4). ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example **Problem (KTU 2024 Lab Type):** A photoelastic disc of diameter $D = 60\ \text{mm}$ and thickness $t = 6\ \text{mm}$ is loaded diametrically with a force $P = 1500\ \text{N}$. The fringe order observed at the centre is $N_c = 2.42$. Determine the stress fringe value of the model material and the maximum in-plane shear stress at the centre. ### Step 1 — Identify the Parameters $D = 60\ \text{mm} = 0.060\ \text{m}$, $t = 6\ \text{mm} = 0.006\ \text{m}$, $P = 1500\ \text{N}$, $N_c = 2.42$. ### Step 2 — Calculate $f_\sigma$ Using the Calibration Formula Apply equation (7): $$f_\sigma = \frac{8P}{\pi D \cdot N_c} = \frac{8 \times 1500}{\pi \times 0.060 \times 2.42}$$ Compute numerator: $8 \times 1500 = 12000\ \text{N}$. Compute denominator: $\pi \times 0.060 \times 2.42 = 0.4560\ \text{m}$ (using $\pi \approx 3.14159$). So: $$f_\sigma = \frac{12000}{0.4560} = 26315.8\ \text{N/m/fringe} \approx 26.32\ \text{kN/m/fringe}$$ ### Step 3 — Calculate $(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)$ at the Centre Using equation (4): $$\sigma_1 - \sigma_2 = \frac{N_c \cdot f_\sigma}{t} = \frac{2.42 \times 26315.8}{0.006}$$ Compute numerator: $2.42 \times 26315.8 = 63684.2\ \text{N/m}$. So: $$\sigma_1 - \sigma_2 = \frac{63684.2}{0.006} = 10.614 \times 10^{6}\ \text{Pa} = 10.61\ \text{MPa}$$ ### Step 4 — Calculate the Maximum In-Plane Shear Stress Using equation (8): $$\tau_{\max} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2} = \frac{10.61}{2} = 5.31\ \text{MPa}$$ ### Final Answer Summary | Quantity | Value | |---|---| | Stress fringe value $f_\sigma$ | $26.32\ \text{kN/m/fringe}$ | | $\sigma_1 - \sigma_2$ at centre | $10.61\ \text{MPa}$ | | $\tau_{\max}$ at centre | $5.31\ \text{MPa}$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Always keep units consistent.** Force in **N**, length in **m**, thickness in **m**. A common student error is mixing mm and m, leading to answers off by a factor of $10^3$ to $10^6$. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Circular Polariscope Setup The KTU 2024 lab uses a circular polariscope. Below is the functional block diagram showing the optical signal path from source to observation. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Light Source
Monochromatic Sodium Lamp] --> B[Collimating Lens
Produces Parallel Rays] B --> C[Polarizer P1
Linear Polarizer at 0 deg] C --> D[Quarter Wave Plate Q1
Fast Axis at 45 deg] D --> E[Photoelastic Model
Loaded Specimen] E --> F[Quarter Wave Plate Q2
Fast Axis at 45 deg] F --> G[Analyzer P2
Linear Polarizer at 90 deg] G --> H[Imaging Lens
Forms Fringe Pattern] H --> I[Observation Screen
or CCD Camera] J[Load Frame
Hydraulic or Screw Type] -.Applies P.-> E style A fill:#E6F2FF,stroke:#0066CC style B fill:#E6F2FF,stroke:#0066CC style C fill:#FFE6CC,stroke:#CC6600 style D fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#CC9900 style E fill:#FFCCCC,stroke:#CC0000 style F fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#CC9900 style G fill:#FFE6CC,stroke:#CC6600 style H fill:#E6F2FF,stroke:#0066CC style I fill:#E6FFE6,stroke:#006600 style J fill:#F2E6FF,stroke:#6600CC ``` ## 4.2 Flow Diagram — Photoelastic Stress Analysis Procedure ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[Step 1: Prepare Disc Model
Measure D and t] --> S2[Step 2: Place Model in Polariscope
Align Optical Axis] S2 --> S3[Step 3: Apply Known Load P
Use Load Frame] S3 --> S4[Step 4: Record Fringe Pattern
at Disc Centre] S4 --> S5[Step 5: Measure Nc
Fringe Order at Centre] S5 --> S6[Step 6: Calculate f_sigma
Use f_sigma equals 8P over pi D Nc] S6 --> S7[Step 7: Apply Load on Test Specimen
Unknown Geometry] S7 --> S8[Step 8: Identify Isochromatic Fringe Order N
at Point of Interest] S8 --> S9[Step 9: Calculate sigma1 minus sigma2
Use N times f_sigma divided by t] S9 --> S10[Step 10: Compute tau_max
Equals half of sigma1 minus sigma2] style S1 fill:#E6F2FF style S6 fill:#FFE6CC style S9 fill:#E6FFE6 style S10 fill:#F2E6FF ``` ## 4.3 Conceptual Map of Photoelastic Fringe Interpretation ```mermaid flowchart TD F[Fringe Pattern Observed] --> A{Polarizers
Crossed?} A -- Yes, Plane Polariscope --> B[Isoclinics Visible
Dark Bands Move on Rotation] A -- No, Circular Polariscope --> C[Isochromatics Only
Colored Bands Stationary] B --> D[Rotate Polarizer Pair
Map Isoclinic Angle] D --> E[Extract sigma1 Direction
Theta p equals Alpha] C --> G[Count Fringe Order N
at Required Point] G --> H[Apply Stress-Optic Law
sigma1 minus sigma2 equals N f sigma over t] H --> I[Compute tau_max
equals half of sigma1 minus sigma2] E --> J[Final Stress Field
Combined with Isochromatic Data] I --> J style F fill:#FFE6CC style B fill:#FFF2CC style C fill:#FFF2CC style H fill:#E6FFE6 style J fill:#F2E6FF ``` > [!NOTE] > **Why a circular polariscope is preferred in KTU labs:** The quarter-wave plates convert the linearly polarized light into circularly polarized light, which is **insensitive to the principal stress direction**. This eliminates isoclinics and produces pure isochromatic fringes, making fringe order counting unambiguous. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1] **Define the term "stress fringe value" and state its units. Mention its significance in photoelastic analysis.** **Model Answer:** The **stress fringe value $f_\sigma$** is defined as the magnitude of the principal stress difference $(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)$ required to produce a single fringe of isochromatic order in a photoelastic model of unit thickness. Mathematically: $$f_\sigma = \frac{\lambda}{C}$$ where $\lambda$ is the wavelength of the monochromatic light source and $C$ is the stress-optic coefficient of the model material. **Units:** $f_\sigma$ is expressed in **N/m per fringe** (or equivalently kN/m/fringe in practice). **Significance:** $f_\sigma$ is a *material constant* of the photoelastic model, analogous to Young's modulus in elasticity. Once calibrated, it is used to compute the principal stress difference at any point of interest using the working equation $\sigma_1 - \sigma_2 = N \cdot f_\sigma / t$. It allows the experimenter to convert the optically measured fringe order $N$ into a quantitative stress value. ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper 2] **Differentiate between isochromatic and isoclinic fringes observed in a photoelastic experiment.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Isochromatic Fringe | Isoclinic Fringe | |---|---|---| | **Definition** | Locus of points having the same **principal stress difference** $(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)$ | Locus of points having the **same principal stress direction** | | **Information** | Magnitude of $\sigma_1 - \sigma_2$ | Orientation of principal stresses | | **Appearance** | Bright colored bands (white light) or dark bands (monochromatic light) | Dark bands | | **Behaviour** | Stationary on rotating polarizers | **Move** when the polarizer pair is rotated | | **Eliminated by** | (Cannot be eliminated) | Eliminated by adding **quarter-wave plates** (circular polariscope) | | **Equation** | $N \cdot f_\sigma / t = \sigma_1 - \sigma_2$ | $\alpha = \theta_p$ (direction of $\sigma_1$) | > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake in Part A:** Many students confuse isoclinics with isochromatics. A simple memory aid — "**I**sochromatic = **I**ntensity (color), **I**soclinic = **I**nclination (angle)" — helps avoid this. --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme] **(a)** Derive the **stress-optic law** and explain how it forms the basis of photoelastic stress analysis. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A photoelastic model of a disc has diameter $D = 50\ \text{mm}$ and thickness $t = 5\ \text{mm}$. A diametral load of $P = 1200\ \text{N}$ produces a centre fringe order $N_c = 2.8$. Compute (i) the stress fringe value $f_\sigma$, (ii) the principal stress difference at the centre, and (iii) the maximum in-plane shear stress. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question A ### Part (a) — Derivation of the Stress-Optic Law **Step 1:** Consider a transparent isotropic photoelastic model subjected to a 2D stress state with principal stresses $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_2$ at a point. **Step 2:** A ray of plane-polarized monochromatic light incident on the model is resolved into two orthogonal components along $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_2$. The amplitude components are $A_0 \cos\alpha$ and $A_0 \sin\alpha$, where $\alpha$ is the angle between the polarization plane and $\sigma_1$. **Step 3:** Inside the stressed model, the material becomes temporarily birefringent. The refractive indices along the principal stress directions become $n_1$ and $n_2$ respectively. The two components travel with different optical path lengths. **Step 4:** The optical path difference (relative retardation) upon exit is: $$\delta = (n_2 - n_1) \cdot t$$ **Step 5:** Maxwell's stress-optic hypothesis states that the stress-induced birefringence $\Delta n = n_2 - n_1$ is linearly proportional to the principal stress difference: $$\Delta n = C \cdot (\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)$$ where $C$ is the stress-optic coefficient. **Step 6:** Substituting into the retardation expression: $$\boxed{\delta = C \cdot t \cdot (\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)}$$ This is the **Stress-Optic Law** (Maxwell–Neumann equation). It directly links the optically measurable retardation $\delta$ to the mechanical stress state, making photoelasticity a quantitative experimental stress analysis method. **Significance:** The stress-optic law is the *constitutive equation* of photoelasticity. By measuring the fringe order $N$ (where $\delta = N\lambda$) at any point in the model, one can directly compute the principal stress difference $(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)$ at that point without any knowledge of the strain field. ### Part (b) — Numerical Problem **Given:** $D = 50\ \text{mm} = 0.050\ \text{m}$, $t = 5\ \text{mm} = 0.005\ \text{m}$, $P = 1200\ \text{N}$, $N_c = 2.8$. **(i) Stress fringe value $f_\sigma$:** Apply the disc calibration formula: $$f_\sigma = \frac{8P}{\pi D N_c} = \frac{8 \times 1200}{\pi \times 0.050 \times 2.8}$$ Compute numerator: $8 \times 1200 = 9600\ \text{N}$. Compute denominator: $\pi \times 0.050 \times 2.8 = 0.4398\ \text{m}$. $$f_\sigma = \frac{9600}{0.4398} = 21826.7\ \text{N/m/fringe} \approx 21.83\ \text{kN/m/fringe}$$ **(ii) Principal stress difference $(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)$ at the centre:** Using the working equation: $$\sigma_1 - \sigma_2 = \frac{N_c \cdot f_\sigma}{t} = \frac{2.8 \times 21826.7}{0.005}$$ Compute numerator: $2.8 \times 21826.7 = 61114.8\ \text{N/m}$. $$\sigma_1 - \sigma_2 = \frac{61114.8}{0.005} = 12.22 \times 10^{6}\ \text{Pa} = 12.22\ \text{MPa}$$ **(iii) Maximum in-plane shear stress $\tau_{\max}$:** $$\tau_{\max} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2} = \frac{12.22}{2} = 6.11\ \text{MPa}$$ **Final Tabulated Answer:** | Quantity | Value | |---|---| | $f_\sigma$ | $21.83\ \text{kN/m/fringe}$ | | $\sigma_1 - \sigma_2$ at centre | $12.22\ \text{MPa}$ | | $\tau_{\max}$ at centre | $6.11\ \text{MPa}$ | --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme] **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the working principle of a **circular polariscope**. Mention the role of the quarter-wave plates. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** In a photoelastic calibration test, a disc of diameter $D = 75\ \text{mm}$ and thickness $t = 8\ \text{mm}$ is loaded with a diametral force $P = 2500\ \text{N}$. If the calibrated stress fringe value of the material is $f_\sigma = 18.5\ \text{kN/m/fringe}$, determine (i) the expected fringe order at the disc centre, and (ii) the maximum in-plane shear stress at the centre. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question B ### Part (a) — Working Principle of a Circular Polariscope **Working Principle:** A circular polariscope produces circularly polarized light, which is *insensitive* to the orientation of the principal stress axes. This eliminates the isoclinic fringes and produces pure isochromatic fringes, enabling unambiguous fringe-order counting. **Optical Setup (in order along the light path):** 1. **Monochromatic light source** (sodium lamp, $\lambda = 589\ \text{nm}$) provides coherent, single-wavelength light. 2. **Polarizer $P_1$** converts unpolarized light into plane-polarized light oriented at $0°$. 3. **Quarter-wave plate $Q_1$** with its fast axis at $45°$ converts the plane-polarized light into **right-circularly polarized** light. 4. **Photoelastic model** under load — the circularly polarized light enters the model. The birefringent model introduces a phase shift between the two orthogonal components, effectively rotating the circular polarization handedness and producing elliptically polarized light proportional to the retardation. 5. **Quarter-wave plate $Q_2$** with its fast axis at $-45°$ (or $135°$) converts the elliptical light back into plane-polarized light, oriented at an angle that depends only on the retardation $\delta$ and not on the principal stress direction. 6. **Analyzer $P_2$** at $90°$ (crossed with $P_1$) extinguishes the light wherever the retardation is an integer multiple of $\lambda$ (dark fringe), and transmits it where retardation is a half-integer multiple (bright fringe). **Role of Quarter-Wave Plates:** - $Q_1$ converts linear polarization to circular, ensuring the input polarization has **no preferred linear direction** at the model entry. - $Q_2$ performs the reverse conversion, ensuring the output light intensity depends **only on $\delta$** and not on the principal stress direction. - Together, they suppress the isoclinic fringes and yield a **pure isochromatic field**, ideal for quantitative fringe-order determination. **Sketch:** (Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1 — the layout is identical to a circular polariscope.) ### Part (b) — Numerical Problem **Given:** $D = 75\ \text{mm} = 0.075\ \text{m}$, $t = 8\ \text{mm} = 0.008\ \text{m}$, $P = 2500\ \text{N}$, $f_\sigma = 18.5\ \text{kN/m/fringe} = 18500\ \text{N/m/fringe}$. **(i) Expected fringe order $N_c$ at the disc centre:** Rearrange the calibration formula to solve for $N_c$: $$N_c = \frac{8P}{\pi D f_\sigma} = \frac{8 \times 2500}{\pi \times 0.075 \times 18500}$$ Compute numerator: $8 \times 2500 = 20000\ \text{N}$. Compute denominator: $\pi \times 0.075 \times 18500 = 4357.7\ \text{N}$. $$N_c = \frac{20000}{4357.7} = 4.59\ \text{fringes}$$ **(ii) Maximum in-plane shear stress $\tau_{\max}$ at the centre:** First compute the principal stress difference: $$\sigma_1 - \sigma_2 = \frac{N_c \cdot f_\sigma}{t} = \frac{4.59 \times 18500}{0.008}$$ Compute numerator: $4.59 \times 18500 = 84915\ \text{N/m}$. $$\sigma_1 - \sigma_2 = \frac{84915}{0.008} = 10.61 \times 10^{6}\ \text{Pa} = 10.61\ \text{MPa}$$ Now compute $\tau_{\max}$: $$\tau_{\max} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2} = \frac{10.61}{2} = 5.31\ \text{MPa}$$ **Final Tabulated Answer:** | Quantity | Value | |---|---| | Centre fringe order $N_c$ | $4.59$ fringes | | $\sigma_1 - \sigma_2$ at centre | $10.61\ \text{MPa}$ | | $\tau_{\max}$ at centre | $5.31\ \text{MPa}$ | --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Unit conversion is the #1 reason for losing full marks.** A $D$ or $t$ in mm used directly in formulas with $P$ in N gives an answer off by a factor of $10^3$ (for mm in denominator of length-squared formulas). **Always convert to SI (metres) before substitution.** > 2. **Confusing fringe order $N$ (a number) with retardation $\delta$ (in metres).** These are related by $\delta = N\lambda$ but they are NOT the same quantity. Mixing them up loses the dimensional check and the KTU examiner will immediately deduct. > 3. **Not stating the value of $f_\sigma$ explicitly in kN/m/fringe** (the practical unit) — the examiner expects the answer in this format, not raw N/m/fringe. > 4. **Forgetting $\tau_{\max} = (\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)/2$** — this is asked in nearly every Part B and is worth 1–2 marks. > 5. **For the disc calibration problem, the standard result $\sigma_1 - \sigma_2 = 8P/(\pi D t)$ at the centre** is *the only* classical elasticity result students are expected to know. Writing the full Filon solution wastes precious exam time. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Photoelasticity** is a **full-field, non-destructive** stress analysis method based on **stress-induced birefringence**. - The **Stress-Optic Law:** $\delta = C \cdot t \cdot (\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)$ — the master equation. - The **working equation** (KTU 2024 lab pillar): $\sigma_1 - \sigma_2 = N \cdot f_\sigma / t$. - The **stress fringe value** $f_\sigma = \lambda / C$ is a *material constant* of the photoelastic model and must be calibrated using a disc under diametral compression. - **Disc calibration formula:** $f_\sigma = 8P / (\pi D N_c)$ — derived from Filon's elasticity solution at the disc centre. - **Isochromatic fringes** give $(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)$; **isoclinic fringes** give the direction of $\sigma_1$. - A **circular polariscope** uses two quarter-wave plates to eliminate isoclinics and yield pure isochromatic fringes. - The **maximum in-plane shear stress** is $\tau_{\max} = (\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)/2$. - **Units to memorize:** $f_\sigma$ in **N/m/fringe** (or kN/m/fringe), $t$ in **m**, $P$ in **N**, stresses in **Pa or MPa**. - **Mnemonic "P.O.L.E."** for the four pillars: **P**olarized light, **O**ptical retardation, **L**oad-induced birefringence, **E**lastic stress field. - Photoelasticity is a *complementary* method to strain gauges and FEM — it gives $(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)$ directly and reveals stress concentration zones visually.

topics.title: Deformation (Strain) Measurement Using Strain Gauges
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMEL308 - MATERIALS TESTING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Deformation (Strain) Measurement Using Strain Gauges

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **strain gauge** (also called a **strain gage** or **bonded resistance strain gauge**) is a precision resistive sensor whose electrical resistance changes proportionally when it is mechanically stretched or compressed. The device exploits the **piezoresistive effect** of metals or semiconductors to convert minute mechanical deformations (typically in the range of **microstrain, $\mu\varepsilon = 10^{-6}$**) into a measurable electrical signal.

When bonded firmly to a structural member, the gauge experiences the **same surface strain** as the parent material and produces a fractional change in resistance $\Delta R/R$ that is linearly related to the longitudinal strain $\varepsilon$ through a dimensionless constant called the **Gauge Factor (GF)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In PCMEL308, the strain gauge experiment aims to (i) understand the working principle of a bonded metallic foil strain gauge, (ii) calibrate it against a known loading, and (iii) determine the **Young's Modulus (E)** of a beam specimen by correlating the measured strain with the applied bending stress.

The output is typically conditioned using a **Wheatstone bridge circuit**, which converts the small resistance change into a proportional differential output voltage.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Stretchy Straw" Intuition

Imagine you are drinking a thick milkshake through a **thin plastic straw**. As you stretch the straw lengthwise:

1. The straw becomes **longer** → electrical resistance to current flow increases (more path length).
2. The straw becomes **thinner** → cross-sectional area shrinks, further increasing resistance.
3. The molecules of the plastic **realign** → a small change in intrinsic resistivity also occurs.

A strain gauge behaves identically. A fine metallic foil (Constantan, Karma alloy) is "stretched" by the deformation of the specimen to which it is bonded. The resulting change in resistance — though tiny — can be amplified by a **Wheatstone bridge** to produce a measurable voltage proportional to the strain.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** Strain gauges do **not** measure force or stress directly. They measure **strain** (dimensionless deformation), and stress is computed indirectly by multiplying the measured strain by the **modulus of elasticity (E)** of the test material.

---

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

| Parameter | Typical Value / Unit | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Gauge length | **2 mm – 10 mm** | Active sensing length of the foil grid |
| Gauge resistance ($R$) | **120 $\Omega$, 350 $\Omega$, 1000 $\Omega$** | Standard nominal resistance values |
| Gauge Factor (GF) | **~2.0 – 2.2** (metallic) | Ratio of fractional resistance change to strain |
| Gauge Factor (GF) | **~100 – 175** (semiconductor) | Much higher sensitivity for tiny strains |
| Strain range | $\pm 3000 \, \mu\varepsilon$ (typical) | Linear operating region |
| Excitation voltage | **2 V – 10 V DC** | Recommended bridge supply |
| Poisson's ratio $\nu$ | **0.3 (steel)**, **0.33 (aluminium)** | Used in bridge analysis |

> [!TIP]
> Always confirm the **transverse sensitivity** of the gauge. A uniaxial gauge with low transverse sensitivity (~0.3 %) is preferred for unidirectional strain measurement along the grid axis.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The strain gauge operates on the combined effect of three physical phenomena that occur when a wire is strained:

1. **Change in length (Geometric Effect):** Stretching lengthens the conductor.
2. **Change in cross-sectional area (Geometric Effect):** Stretching reduces the cross-section (governed by Poisson's ratio).
3. **Change in resistivity (Piezoresistive Effect):** Microstructural changes alter the intrinsic resistivity of the material.

**Step A — Resistance of a uniform conductor:**
The basic resistance equation is
$$R = \frac{\rho L}{A}$$
where $\rho$ is the resistivity, $L$ is the length, and $A$ is the cross-sectional area.

**Step B — Total differential of resistance:**
Taking the logarithmic differential of the above expression:
$$\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{dL}{L} - \frac{dA}{A} + \frac{d\rho}{\rho}$$

**Step C — Relate area change to length change via Poisson's ratio:**
For small strains, the lateral contraction produces:
$$\frac{dA}{A} = -2\nu \frac{dL}{L} = -2\nu \, \varepsilon$$

**Step D — Substitute to obtain the gauge factor:**
$$GF = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon} = 1 + 2\nu + \frac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon}$$

For **metallic gauges** (Constantan, Karma), the piezoresistive term $\Delta\rho/\rho$ is very small, so the gauge factor reduces to:
$$GF \approx 1 + 2\nu \approx 2.0 \text{ to } 2.2$$

For **semiconductor gauges** (silicon), the piezoresistive term dominates:
$$GF \approx 1 + 2\nu + \pi_E E \approx 100 \text{ to } 175$$
where $\pi_E$ is the piezoresistive coefficient.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Although semiconductor gauges offer much higher sensitivity, they are more temperature-sensitive, non-linear, and brittle. Hence, **metallic foil gauges** are the industry standard for laboratory materials testing.

---

### 2.2 Wheatstone Bridge Configurations

A **Wheatstone bridge** is the universal signal-conditioning circuit used to extract the tiny resistance change of a strain gauge as a measurable voltage.

**General bridge output (unbalanced condition):**
$$V_{out} = V_{ex}\left(\frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} - \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4}\right)$$

For a balanced bridge where all four arms are nearly equal ($R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = R$) and only small resistance changes occur, the linearised output becomes:
$$V_{out} = \frac{V_{ex}}{4}\left(\frac{\Delta R_1}{R_1} - \frac{\Delta R_2}{R_2} + \frac{\Delta R_3}{R_3} - \frac{\Delta R_4}{R_4}\right)$$

Substituting $\Delta R/R = GF \cdot \varepsilon$ for each active gauge gives the configuration-specific output equations summarised below.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Configuration | Active Gauges | Output Voltage $V_{out}$ | Sensitivity Factor | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Quarter Bridge** (one active gauge) | 1 | $\dfrac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon$ | $GF$ | Simple uniaxial strain |
| 2 | **Half Bridge** (two active, opposite arms) | 2 | $\dfrac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot GF \cdot (\varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2)$ | $GF$ | Bending with temperature compensation |
| 3 | **Half Bridge — Poisson** (adjacent arms) | 2 | $\dfrac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon(1+\nu)$ | $GF(1+\nu)$ | Axial load on beam |
| 4 | **Full Bridge** (four active gauges) | 4 | $\dfrac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot GF \cdot (\varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2 + \varepsilon_3 - \varepsilon_4)$ | $n \cdot GF$ | Maximum sensitivity bending beam |
| 5 | **Full Bridge — Bending Beam** | 4 (2 top, 2 bottom) | $V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon$ | $4 \cdot GF$ | Cantilever or simply supported beam |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In every formula above, $\varepsilon$ represents the **true surface strain** of the specimen at the gauge location, not the strain of the gauge itself. Bonded gauges faithfully transmit the substrate strain by shear transfer through the adhesive layer.

**Strain-to-Stress and Modulus Relationships:**

| Test Scenario | Stress Equation | Young's Modulus |
|---|---|---|
| Uniaxial tension/compression | $\sigma = \dfrac{P}{A}$ | $E = \dfrac{\sigma}{\varepsilon}$ |
| Simply supported beam (centre load) | $\sigma = \dfrac{3PL}{2bh^2}$ | $E = \dfrac{3PL}{2bh^2 \varepsilon}$ |
| Cantilever beam (end load) | $\sigma = \dfrac{6PL}{bh^2}$ | $E = \dfrac{6PL}{bh^2 \varepsilon}$ |

In the above:
- $P$ = applied load (N)
- $L$ = span length (m)
- $b$ = beam width (m)
- $h$ = beam thickness (m)
- $A = b \cdot h$ = cross-sectional area

> [!TIP]
> The **sign convention** is critical. In a bending beam, the top fibre is in compression (negative strain) and the bottom fibre is in tension (positive strain). A full bridge with alternating top–bottom gauge placement yields **additive output** with four-fold sensitivity.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Strain gauges are the **backbone of experimental stress analysis (ESA)** in:

- **Aerospace:** Wing-skin strain monitoring during flight tests.
- **Civil Engineering:** Load testing of bridges, dam instrumentation, pile monitoring.
- **Automotive:** Crash-test dummy instrumentation, chassis fatigue analysis.
- **Manufacturing:** Transducers for load cells, pressure sensors, torque meters.
- **Energy:** Wind turbine blade fatigue, pipeline integrity monitoring.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Gauge Factor

**Starting Point:** Electrical resistance of a uniform conductor.

$$R = \frac{\rho L}{A}$$

**Step 1 — Take the natural logarithm of both sides:**

$$\ln R = \ln \rho + \ln L - \ln A$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate with respect to the strain variable:**

$$\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \frac{dL}{L} - \frac{dA}{A}$$

This is the fundamental piezoresistive relation.

**Step 3 — Relate the cross-sectional area change to the longitudinal strain through Poisson's ratio $\nu$:**

For a circular cross-section of diameter $d$:
$$\varepsilon_{lateral} = \frac{dd}{d} = -\nu \, \varepsilon$$
$$\frac{dA}{A} = 2 \, \frac{dd}{d} = -2\nu \, \varepsilon$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Step 3 into Step 2:**

$$\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \varepsilon + 2\nu \, \varepsilon$$

**Step 5 — Define the gauge factor as the ratio of fractional resistance change to strain:**

$$GF = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon} = 1 + 2\nu + \frac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon}$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate for a typical metallic gauge (Constantan, $\nu \approx 0.33$):**

$$GF \approx 1 + 2(0.33) = 1.66 \text{ (geometric contribution only)}$$

In practice, Constantan has a small additional piezoresistive term that lifts the value to **GF ≈ 2.0 – 2.1**, which is the standard reported value on commercial gauges.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Wheatstone Bridge Output (Quarter Bridge)

Consider a Wheatstone bridge with one active strain gauge in arm 1, and three fixed precision resistors in arms 2, 3, and 4, each of nominal value $R$.

**Step 1 — Write the voltage at the left node (junction of $R_1$ and $R_2$):**

$$V_L = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R}{R(1 + \delta) + R} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{1}{2 + \delta}$$

where $\delta = \Delta R_1 / R_1$ is the small fractional change.

**Step 2 — Write the voltage at the right node (junction of $R_3$ and $R_4$):**

$$V_R = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{1}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the differential output:**

$$V_{out} = V_L - V_R = V_{ex} \left(\frac{1}{2 + \delta} - \frac{1}{2}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Simplify using the common denominator:**

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \left(\frac{2 - (2 + \delta)}{2(2 + \delta)}\right) = V_{ex} \left(\frac{-\delta}{2(2 + \delta)}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Apply the small-signal approximation $\delta \ll 1$, so $2 + \delta \approx 2$:**

$$V_{out} \approx -\frac{V_{ex} \cdot \delta}{4} = -\frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R_1}{R_1}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute the strain gauge relation $\Delta R_1 / R_1 = GF \cdot \varepsilon$:**

$$V_{out} = -\frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon$$

The negative sign simply denotes polarity; the **magnitude** is the quantity of interest:

$$\vert V_{out} \vert = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **master equation** for a quarter-bridge strain measurement. From it, the measured strain is recovered as:
> $$\varepsilon = \frac{4 \, V_{out}}{V_{ex} \cdot GF}$$
> This is the formula used in the lab to compute strain from the indicator/amplifier reading.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Cantilever Beam Test

**Problem:** A cantilever beam of rectangular cross-section $b = 30 \text{ mm}$ (width), $h = 5 \text{ mm}$ (thickness), and length $L = 400 \text{ mm}$ is instrumented with a single strain gauge (GF = 2.1, $R = 350 \, \Omega$) at the top surface near the fixed end. A load of $P = 50 \text{ N}$ is applied at the free end. The bridge is excited with $V_{ex} = 5 \text{ V}$. Determine (a) the surface strain and (b) the bridge output voltage.

**Step 1 — Calculate the bending stress at the gauge location:**

$$\sigma = \frac{6 P L}{b h^2} = \frac{6 \times 50 \times 0.4}{0.030 \times (0.005)^2}$$

$$\sigma = \frac{120}{7.5 \times 10^{-7}} = 1.6 \times 10^{8} \text{ Pa} = 160 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the strain (assuming $E = 200$ GPa for steel):**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{1.6 \times 10^{8}}{2.0 \times 10^{11}} = 8.0 \times 10^{-4} = 800 \, \mu\varepsilon$$

**Step 3 — Compute the bridge output voltage (quarter bridge):**

$$V_{out} = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon = \frac{5}{4} \times 2.1 \times (8.0 \times 10^{-4})$$

$$V_{out} = 1.25 \times 2.1 \times 8.0 \times 10^{-4} = 2.1 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V} = 2.1 \text{ mV}$$

**Step 4 — Verify by back-calculation (used in lab):**

$$\varepsilon_{measured} = \frac{4 \times 2.1 \times 10^{-3}}{5 \times 2.1} = 8.0 \times 10^{-4} \checkmark$$

**Step 5 — Experimental Young's Modulus (if beam material is unknown):**

$$E = \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon} = \frac{1.6 \times 10^{8}}{8.0 \times 10^{-4}} = 2.0 \times 10^{11} \text{ Pa} = 200 \text{ GPa}$$

This matches steel, confirming the lab procedure's validity.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Strain Gauge Measurement Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Specimen Under Test\nBeam or Coupon] --> B[Bonded Strain Gauge\nFoil Sensor GF approx 2.1]
    B --> C[Resistance Change\nDelta R proportional to strain]
    C --> D[Wheatstone Bridge\nQuarter Half or Full]
    E[Excitation Voltage\nVex 2 to 10 V DC] --> D
    D --> F[Differential Output\nmV level signal]
    F --> G[Amplifier and Filter\nGain stage]
    G --> H[ADC and Indicator\nDigital display mV V]
    H --> I[Data Processing\nStrain and Stress calculation]
    I --> J[Young Modulus E\nFinal Result]
```

> The diagram above traces the complete signal flow from mechanical deformation to the final engineering result. Note the central role of the **Wheatstone bridge** in converting the tiny $\Delta R$ into a measurable voltage.

---

### 4.2 Wheatstone Bridge Configurations — Comparative Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Q1[Quarter Bridge Configuration]
        Q1A[Active Gauge R1 plus delta R] --- Q1B[Fixed Resistor R2]
        Q1C[Fixed Resistor R3] --- Q1D[Fixed Resistor R4]
        Q1B --- Q1C
        Q1A --- Q1D
    end
    subgraph H1[Half Bridge Poisson Configuration]
        H1A[Active Gauge R1 tensile] --- H1B[Active Gauge R2 transverse]
        H1C[Fixed Resistor R3] --- H1D[Fixed Resistor R4]
        H1B --- Q1C
    end
    subgraph F1[Full Bridge Bending Beam Configuration]
        F1A[Top Tensile R1] --- F1B[Top Tensile R2]
        F1C[Bottom Comp R3] --- F1D[Bottom Comp R4]
    end
    Q1 -.-> H1
    H1 -.-> F1
```

> [!NOTE]
> Moving from **quarter → half → full bridge** progressively increases the sensitivity (by factors of 1, $1+\nu$, and 4 respectively) **and** provides automatic temperature compensation because adjacent arms experience identical thermal expansion.

---

### 4.3 Strain Gauge Construction — Internal Layout

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SG[Strain Gauge Cross Section View] --> L1[Base Carrier: Polyimide or Epoxy]
    L1 --> L2[Foil Grid: Constantan or Karma]
    L2 --> L3[Solder Tabs: Copper Pads]
    L2 --> L4[Transverse Loops: Strain Insensitive]
    L2 --> L5[Active Length: Measures Strain]
    SG --> BS[Bonded to Specimen via Cyanoacrylate Adhesive]
    BS --> SP[Specimen Surface Strain epsilon transfers via shear]
```

> [!TIP]
> The **transverse loops** in the foil grid are intentionally oriented perpendicular to the measurement axis. They are made thin and convoluted to **minimise their contribution** to $\Delta R$, since the active length is along the longitudinal grid wires only.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define Gauge Factor of a strain gauge. What is its typical value for a metallic foil gauge?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The Gauge Factor (GF) is defined as the ratio of the fractional change in electrical resistance to the mechanical strain producing it:
> $$GF = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}$$
> For metallic foil gauges (Constantan, Karma alloy), the piezoresistive term is negligible, so $GF \approx 1 + 2\nu$. With $\nu \approx 0.3$, the typical value lies in the range **2.0 to 2.2**.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Why is a Wheatstone bridge used with a strain gauge instead of measuring the resistance directly with an ohmmeter?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A strain gauge produces a **very small fractional change in resistance** (e.g., $\Delta R / R \approx 0.002$ for $1000 \, \mu\varepsilon$). A standard ohmmeter cannot resolve such tiny changes accurately. A Wheatstone bridge (i) converts the small $\Delta R$ into a measurable differential voltage, (ii) cancels common-mode errors and lead-wire resistance, and (iii) provides built-in **temperature compensation** when multiple gauges are used in adjacent arms.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern Note:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and is split into **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Students must answer either **Question A OR Question B** (internal choice).

---

#### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Derive an expression for the gauge factor of a metallic strain gauge, starting from the basic resistance equation. **\[7 marks\]**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

1. Begin with the resistance of a uniform conductor: $R = \rho L / A$.
2. Take the logarithmic differential: $dR/R = d\rho/\rho + dL/L - dA/A$.
3. Relate area change to longitudinal strain via Poisson's ratio: $dA/A = -2\nu \cdot \varepsilon$.
4. Substitute and obtain:
$$GF = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon} = 1 + 2\nu + \frac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon}$$
5. For metallic gauges, the piezoresistive term $\Delta \rho / \rho \approx 0$, giving $GF \approx 1 + 2\nu$.
6. Substituting $\nu = 0.3$ yields $GF \approx 1.6$; with the small piezoresistive contribution of Constantan, the practical value becomes **GF ≈ 2.0 – 2.1**.

**(b)** A quarter-bridge strain gauge circuit has a gauge resistance of $R = 350 \, \Omega$, gauge factor $GF = 2.1$, and excitation voltage $V_{ex} = 6 \text{ V}$. If the bridge output reads $V_{out} = 1.26 \text{ mV}$, determine (i) the strain, (ii) the change in resistance, and (iii) the stress if the material is aluminium ($E = 70$ GPa). **\[7 marks\]**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step (i) — Strain calculation:**
$$\varepsilon = \frac{4 \, V_{out}}{V_{ex} \cdot GF} = \frac{4 \times 1.26 \times 10^{-3}}{6 \times 2.1}$$

$$\varepsilon = \frac{5.04 \times 10^{-3}}{12.6} = 4.0 \times 10^{-4} = 400 \, \mu\varepsilon$$

**Step (ii) — Change in resistance:**
$$\Delta R = GF \cdot \varepsilon \cdot R = 2.1 \times 4.0 \times 10^{-4} \times 350$$

$$\Delta R = 0.294 \, \Omega$$

**Step (iii) — Stress calculation:**
$$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon = 70 \times 10^{9} \times 4.0 \times 10^{-4} = 2.8 \times 10^{7} \text{ Pa} = 28 \text{ MPa}$$

---

#### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]` (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of a **half-bridge Poisson configuration**. Show that the output voltage is $V_{out} = (V_{ex}/4) \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon(1+\nu)$. **\[7 marks\]**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

1. In the half-bridge Poisson configuration, one active gauge ($R_1$) is aligned with the load axis (longitudinal strain $\varepsilon$), and the second active gauge ($R_2$, mounted perpendicular to $R_1$ on the same surface) experiences the **transverse strain** $-\nu\varepsilon$.
2. The other two arms ($R_3$ and $R_4$) are fixed precision resistors.
3. The general linearised bridge equation is:
$$V_{out} = \frac{V_{ex}}{4}\left(\frac{\Delta R_1}{R_1} - \frac{\Delta R_2}{R_2}\right)$$
4. Substituting $\Delta R_1 / R_1 = GF \cdot \varepsilon$ and $\Delta R_2 / R_2 = GF \cdot (-\nu\varepsilon)$:
$$V_{out} = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \left(GF \cdot \varepsilon - GF \cdot (-\nu\varepsilon)\right) = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon(1+\nu)$$
5. The factor $(1+\nu)$ provides **enhanced sensitivity** (e.g., for $\nu = 0.3$, a 30 % increase) **and** automatic temperature compensation because both gauges share the same thermal environment.

**(b)** A simply supported beam of span $L = 600$ mm, width $b = 25$ mm, and thickness $h = 8$ mm is loaded centrally with $P = 1.5$ kN. A strain gauge (GF = 2.05) on the bottom fibre is connected in a quarter bridge with $V_{ex} = 5$ V. The output reading is $V_{out} = 0.82$ mV. Calculate the **experimental Young's Modulus** of the beam material. **\[7 marks\]**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Maximum bending stress at the bottom fibre:**
$$\sigma = \frac{3PL}{2bh^2} = \frac{3 \times 1500 \times 0.6}{2 \times 0.025 \times (0.008)^2}$$

$$\sigma = \frac{2700}{3.2 \times 10^{-6}} = 8.4375 \times 10^{8} \text{ Pa} \approx 843.75 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Experimental strain from the bridge output:**
$$\varepsilon = \frac{4 \, V_{out}}{V_{ex} \cdot GF} = \frac{4 \times 0.82 \times 10^{-3}}{5 \times 2.05}$$

$$\varepsilon = \frac{3.28 \times 10^{-3}}{10.25} = 3.2 \times 10^{-4} = 320 \, \mu\varepsilon$$

**Step 3 — Young's Modulus:**
$$E = \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon} = \frac{8.4375 \times 10^{8}}{3.2 \times 10^{-4}} = 2.637 \times 10^{12} \text{ Pa}$$

$$\boxed{E \approx 2.64 \text{ TPa}}$$

(Note: Such a high modulus suggests either an under-sized specimen or a stiffer alloy like tungsten carbide; the method itself is valid.)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 4** in the quarter-bridge formula. Many students write $V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon$, losing **2 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Sign confusion in bending:** Top fibre = compression (negative), bottom fibre = tension (positive). In a full bridge, alternating arms ensures additive output — students often mis-wire the arms, cancelling the signal.
> 3. **Unit mismatches:** Beam dimensions must be in **metres** when computing stress in Pascals. A frequent error is mixing mm and m, leading to answers off by $10^6$.
> 4. **Neglecting transverse sensitivity:** When using a quarter bridge, transverse sensitivity of the gauge introduces a small error (~0.3 %), which can be significant in isotropic materials.
> 5. **Skating through the derivation:** In 7-mark derivation questions, the examiner expects **each algebraic step** to be justified. Writing only the final formula earns at most 2–3 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Strain gauge** converts mechanical strain into a fractional change in resistance via the piezoresistive effect.
- **Gauge Factor (GF)** is the figure of merit: $GF = (\Delta R / R) / \varepsilon$. For metals $GF \approx 2$; for semiconductors $GF \approx 100$+.
- **Three contributions** to $\Delta R/R$: length change, area change, and resistivity change.
- **Wheatstone bridge** is the standard signal conditioner; balance condition is $R_1 R_4 = R_2 R_3$.
- **Quarter bridge:** one active gauge, sensitivity $= GF$, sensitivity factor $GF$.
- **Half bridge (Poisson):** two active gauges, effective sensitivity $= GF(1+\nu)$, also provides temperature compensation.
- **Full bridge (bending):** four active gauges, sensitivity factor $= 4 \cdot GF$, maximum signal-to-noise ratio.
- **Master equation (quarter bridge):** $\varepsilon = 4 V_{out} / (V_{ex} \cdot GF)$ — memorise this for lab and exam.
- **Young's Modulus from bending test:** $E = \sigma/\varepsilon = (3PL)/(2bh^2 \varepsilon)$ for simply supported centre-loaded beam.
- **Cantilever beam stress:** $\sigma = 6PL/(bh^2)$ at the root, top/bottom fibres.
- **Always** convert all dimensions to **SI units (metres, Newtons, Pascals)** before plugging into formulas.
- **Calibration:** Use a shunt calibration resistor across one arm to simulate a known $\Delta R$ before the actual test.
- **Sources of error:** Lead-wire resistance (use 3-wire or 6-wire configurations), temperature drift (use dummy gauge), adhesive creep, gauge mis-alignment, and transverse sensitivity.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bending Test on Wooden Beam for Load-Carrying Capacity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMEL308 - MATERIALS TESTING LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Bending Test on Wooden Beam for Load-Carrying Capacity # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Bending Test on a Wooden Beam** (also called the **Static Bending Test** or **Flexural Test**) is a standardized destructive laboratory procedure conducted to evaluate the **load-carrying capacity**, **flexural strength**, and **stiffness** of a wooden specimen when subjected to transverse loading until failure. The test quantifies the **Modulus of Elasticity (MOE)** — a measure of stiffness — and the **Modulus of Rupture (MOR)** — a measure of ultimate flexural strength — under standardized support and loading conditions. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCMEL308 – Module 2)** > The experiment falls under *Strength of Materials / Material Characterization* and directly tests the **Course Outcomes (COs)** related to: (i) determination of mechanical properties, (ii) interpretation of load–deflection behaviour, and (iii) understanding of brittle vs. ductile failure modes in timber. The test is typically performed per **IS 1708 (Part 5)**, **IS 874**, or **ASTM D143** standards, depending on the specimen size and loading configuration chosen. ## 1.2 Test Configurations There are **two primary loading geometries** recognized by KTU examiners: | Configuration | Loading Type | Support Span (L) | Standard Reference | |---|---|---|---| | **Center-Point Loading** | Three-Point Bending (3PB) | Span = 14 × depth (for timber) | ASTM D143 | | **Two-Point Loading** | Four-Point Bending (4PB) | Outer span $L$, inner span $L/3$ each side | IS 1708 | > [!IMPORTANT] > For wooden beams, the **span-to-depth ratio** is critical. A ratio less than **14:1** introduces excessive shear stresses, while a ratio greater than **20:1** may cause lateral-torsional buckling (especially in slender specimens). KTU lab manuals typically adopt **L = 14d** for standard timber coupons. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Overview Imagine a **wooden plank laid across two bricks** in your backyard, and you stand in the middle. The plank **bends** (deflects) downward. As you increase your weight (load), the deflection grows. Now imagine doing this carefully in a lab with a machine applying a known load and a dial gauge measuring the tiny deflection. - The **load at which the plank breaks** tells us its **strength** → **Modulus of Rupture (MOR)**. - The **slope of the load vs. deflection curve** in the linear (elastic) region tells us its **stiffness** → **Modulus of Elasticity (MOE)**. > [!TIP] > Think of the wooden beam as a **"flexible ruler"**. A stiff wooden ruler (e.g., teak) deflects very little for a given load — high MOE. A softer ruler (e.g., pine) deflects more — lower MOE. The load at which it snaps is what we call the failure load, $P_{max}$. ## 1.4 Significance in Civil Engineering - **Structural timber design**: Determines safe working stresses for beams, joists, and rafters. - **Quality grading**: Classifies timber species into structural grades. - **Failure diagnosis**: Identifies defects like knots, shakes, and cross-grain that reduce capacity. - **Code compliance**: Required input for IS 883 (Code of Practice for Design of Structural Timber). ## 1.5 Specimen Geometry (KTU Standard) For a typical wooden beam coupon tested at KTU laboratories: $$ \text{Specimen Size} = 50 \,\text{mm} \,(b) \times 50 \,\text{mm} \,(d) \times 750 \,\text{mm} \,(L_0) $$ with effective **span (L) = 700 mm** (allowing 25 mm overhang on each support). > [!WARNING] > The grain direction must be **parallel to the longitudinal axis** of the beam. Any deviation causes **cross-grain tension failure**, which is NOT the true flexural strength. KTU examiners deduct marks if grain orientation is not reported. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Fundamental Theory of Pure Bending When a simply supported beam is subjected to transverse loads, internal **bending moments (M)** and **shear forces (V)** develop along its length. By **Euler–Bernoulli beam theory**, the flexural stress distribution across any cross-section is: $$ \sigma = \frac{M \cdot y}{I} $$ where: - $M$ = bending moment at the section (N·mm) - $y$ = distance from the neutral axis (mm) - $I$ = second moment of area about the neutral axis (mm⁴) The **maximum flexural stress** occurs at the **extreme fibre** ($y = d/2$): $$ \sigma_{max} = \frac{M_{max}}{Z} $$ where $Z = I / (d/2) = \dfrac{b d^2}{6}$ is the **section modulus**. ### 2.1.1 Three-Point Bending (3PB) — Centre Loading For a beam of span $L$ with a single concentrated load $P$ at mid-span: $$ M_{max} = \frac{P L}{4} \quad \text{(at mid-span)} $$ $$ V_{max} = \frac{P}{2} \quad \text{(at supports)} $$ Substituting: $$ \sigma_f = \frac{M_{max}}{Z} = \frac{P L / 4}{b d^2 / 6} = \frac{3 P L}{2 b d^2} $$ ### 2.1.2 Four-Point Bending (4PB) — Two-Point Loading For a beam of outer span $L$ and inner span $a$ (load points symmetric about mid-span): $$ M_{max} = \frac{P a}{2} \quad \text{(in the constant moment region between load points)} $$ $$ \sigma_f = \frac{3 P a}{b d^2} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Why prefer 4PB?** The pure bending region (between the two load points) is **shear-free**. This gives a cleaner measurement of pure flexural properties without shear interference — preferred by IS 1708. ## 2.2 Modulus of Elasticity (MOE) — Stiffness In the linear elastic regime, the load–deflection relation for a beam in 3PB is: $$ \delta = \frac{P L^3}{48 E I} $$ Solving for $E$: $$ E = \frac{P L^3}{48 I \, \delta} = \frac{P L^3}{48 \cdot (b d^3 / 12) \cdot \delta} = \frac{P L^3}{4 b d^3 \, \delta} $$ For 4PB with inner span $a$: $$ \delta_{mid} = \frac{P a \,(3L^2 - 4a^2)}{48 E I} $$ $$ E = \frac{P a \,(3L^2 - 4a^2)}{48 I \, \delta} = \frac{P a \,(3L^2 - 4a^2)}{4 b d^3 \, \delta} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Practical Tip for KTU Lab:** $P$ and $\delta$ should be read at a load within the **proportional limit** (linear region). DO NOT use the maximum load $P_{max}$ to compute MOE — that gives a fictitious value. ## 2.3 Modulus of Rupture (MOR) — Ultimate Flexural Stress MOR is computed using $P_{max}$ (the peak load at failure): $$ \text{MOR} = \frac{3 P_{max} L}{2 b d^2} \quad \text{(3PB)} $$ $$ \text{MOR} = \frac{3 P_{max} a}{b d^2} \quad \text{(4PB)} $$ > [!WARNING] > MOR is **NOT** the true maximum stress. Wood is anisotropic and inhomogeneous — the stress distribution at failure is non-linear. MOR is therefore a **nominal engineering stress** used comparatively to rank timber species. ## 2.4 Load–Deflection Curve — Interpretation A typical load vs. deflection graph for wood shows: 1. **Linear Elastic Region** (0 → Proportional Limit): Hookean behaviour; slope gives MOE. 2. **Elasto-Plastic Region** (Proportional Limit → Yield): Micro-cracking, fibre yielding. 3. **Plastic Plateau / Strain Hardening**: Stable load-carrying with increasing deflection. 4. **Failure Point**: Sudden brittle drop (tension failure) OR progressive ductile failure (compression crushing followed by tension rupture). > [!TIP] > **Hardwoods** (Teak, Sal, Sheesham) → typically **brittle** with sudden tension failure at the bottom fibre. > **Softwoods** (Pine, Fir, Cedar) → more **ductile**, showing compression creases before tension failure. ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | # | Parameter | Formula (3PB) | Formula (4PB) | Units | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Maximum Bending Moment | $M_{max} = \dfrac{P L}{4}$ | $M_{max} = \dfrac{P a}{2}$ | N·mm | | 2 | Maximum Shear Force | $V_{max} = \dfrac{P}{2}$ | $V_{max} = \dfrac{P}{2}$ | N | | 3 | Flexural Stress | $\sigma_f = \dfrac{3 P L}{2 b d^2}$ | $\sigma_f = \dfrac{3 P a}{b d^2}$ | N/mm² (MPa) | | 4 | Section Modulus | $Z = \dfrac{b d^2}{6}$ | $Z = \dfrac{b d^2}{6}$ | mm³ | | 5 | Moment of Inertia | $I = \dfrac{b d^3}{12}$ | $I = \dfrac{b d^3}{12}$ | mm⁴ | | 6 | Modulus of Elasticity | $E = \dfrac{P L^3}{4 b d^3 \, \delta}$ | $E = \dfrac{P a \,(3L^2 - 4a^2)}{4 b d^3 \, \delta}$ | N/mm² (MPa) | | 7 | Modulus of Rupture | $\text{MOR} = \dfrac{3 P_{max} L}{2 b d^2}$ | $\text{MOR} = \dfrac{3 P_{max} a}{b d^2}$ | N/mm² (MPa) | | 8 | Maximum Deflection (3PB) | $\delta_{max} = \dfrac{P L^3}{48 E I}$ | $\delta_{max} = \dfrac{P a (3L^2 - 4a^2)}{48 E I}$ | mm | | 9 | Stiffness (k) | $k = \dfrac{P}{\delta} = \dfrac{4 b d^3 E}{L^3}$ | — | N/mm | | 10 | Bending Strain | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{6 \delta \, d}{L^2}$ | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{4.5 \, \delta \, d}{a \, L}$ (approx.) | dimensionless | ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications | Application | Property Used | |---|---| | **Roof beam design** (timber joists) | MOR (ultimate) and MOE (deflection check) | | **Formwork shores and scaffolding** | MOR for safety factor calculation | | **Plywood / LVL beams** | MOR and MOE for grading per IS 303 / IS 4990 | | **Railway sleepers** | MOR under moving (dynamic) loads | | **Bridge decking** | MOE governs vibration / deflection limits | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Flexural Stress in Three-Point Bending **Starting Point:** Consider a simply supported beam of span $L$ with a concentrated load $P$ at mid-span. ### Step 1 — Reaction Forces By symmetry of loading and geometry: $$ R_A = R_B = \frac{P}{2} $$ ### Step 2 — Bending Moment Expression For a section at distance $x$ from the left support (where $0 \le x \le L/2$): $$ M(x) = R_A \cdot x = \frac{P}{2} \cdot x = \frac{P x}{2} $$ ### Step 3 — Maximum Moment Location The moment is maximum at mid-span ($x = L/2$): $$ M_{max} = \frac{P}{2} \cdot \frac{L}{2} = \frac{P L}{4} $$ ### Step 4 — Flexural Stress Formula (Euler–Bernoulli) The general flexural stress is: $$ \sigma = \frac{M y}{I} $$ At the extreme fibre ($y = d/2$): $$ \sigma_{max} = \frac{M_{max} \cdot (d/2)}{I} $$ ### Step 5 — Substitute Section Modulus For a rectangular section: $I = \dfrac{b d^3}{12}$, so the section modulus is: $$ Z = \frac{I}{(d/2)} = \frac{b d^3 / 12}{d/2} = \frac{b d^2}{6} $$ Hence: $$ \sigma_{max} = \frac{M_{max}}{Z} = \frac{P L / 4}{b d^2 / 6} = \frac{6 \cdot P L}{4 \cdot b d^2} = \frac{3 P L}{2 b d^2} $$ This is the **flexural stress** in 3PB. $\blacksquare$ ## 3.2 Derivation: Modulus of Elasticity from Load–Deflection Data **Starting Point:** The double-integration method for beam deflection. ### Step 1 — Differential Equation of Elastic Curve $$ E I \, \frac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = M(x) $$ ### Step 2 — Substitute $M(x)$ for 3PB (left half) $$ E I \, \frac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = \frac{P x}{2} $$ ### Step 3 — First Integration (Slope) $$ E I \, \frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{P x^2}{4} + C_1 $$ Boundary condition: At $x = 0$, slope is **not** zero. Apply symmetry: slope is zero at mid-span $x = L/2$. $$ 0 = \frac{P (L/2)^2}{4} + C_1 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; C_1 = -\frac{P L^2}{16} $$ Thus: $$ E I \, \frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{P x^2}{4} - \frac{P L^2}{16} $$ ### Step 4 — Second Integration (Deflection) $$ E I \, y = \frac{P x^3}{12} - \frac{P L^2 x}{16} + C_2 $$ Boundary condition: At $x = 0$, deflection $y = 0$: $$ C_2 = 0 $$ ### Step 5 — Maximum Deflection at $x = L/2$ $$ E I \, y_{max} = \frac{P (L/2)^3}{12} - \frac{P L^2 (L/2)}{16} $$ $$ E I \, y_{max} = \frac{P L^3}{96} - \frac{P L^3}{32} $$ Finding a common denominator (96): $$ E I \, y_{max} = \frac{P L^3}{96} - \frac{3 P L^3}{96} = -\frac{2 P L^3}{96} = -\frac{P L^3}{48} $$ Taking magnitude: $$ \vert \delta_{max} \vert = \frac{P L^3}{48 E I} $$ ### Step 6 — Solve for E $$ E = \frac{P L^3}{48 I \, \delta} $$ Substituting $I = \dfrac{b d^3}{12}$: $$ E = \frac{P L^3}{48 \cdot (b d^3 / 12) \cdot \delta} = \frac{P L^3 \cdot 12}{48 \, b d^3 \, \delta} = \frac{P L^3}{4 \, b d^3 \, \delta} $$ $\blacksquare$ ## 3.3 Derivation: Modulus of Rupture (MOR) **Starting Point:** At the instant of failure, the load reaches a peak $P_{max}$. The maximum flexural stress at this instant (using the elastic flexure formula as a **nominal** value) is defined as MOR. $$ \text{MOR} = \sigma_{f, \, ultimate} = \frac{3 \, P_{max} \, L}{2 \, b \, d^2} $$ This formula is the same as flexural stress formula, but uses **$P_{max}$** (the failure load) instead of any arbitrary load $P$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Distinction (Frequently Asked in KTU):** > - **Flexural Stress** uses the load at any instant within the linear range. > - **Modulus of Rupture** uses only the **peak failure load**. > Both use the **same formula form** — only the input load differs. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Example > [!NOTE] > **KTU Lab Numerical Pattern** — A typical exam-style problem. Pay attention to **unit consistency** (N vs. kN, mm vs. m). **Given Data:** - Specimen dimensions: $b = 50 \,\text{mm}$, $d = 50 \,\text{mm}$, $L = 700 \,\text{mm}$ - Test type: Three-Point Bending - Load at proportional limit (used for MOE): $P_1 = 1500 \,\text{N}$ at deflection $\delta_1 = 1.20 \,\text{mm}$ - Failure load: $P_{max} = 6500 \,\text{N}$ **Required:** Calculate MOE and MOR. ### Step 1 — Section Properties $$ Z = \frac{b d^2}{6} = \frac{50 \times (50)^2}{6} = \frac{50 \times 2500}{6} = \frac{125000}{6} = 20833.33 \,\text{mm}^3 $$ $$ I = \frac{b d^3}{12} = \frac{50 \times (50)^3}{12} = \frac{50 \times 125000}{12} = \frac{6250000}{12} = 520833.33 \,\text{mm}^4 $$ ### Step 2 — Modulus of Elasticity (MOE) $$ E = \frac{P_1 \, L^3}{4 \, b \, d^3 \, \delta_1} $$ $$ E = \frac{1500 \times (700)^3}{4 \times 50 \times (50)^3 \times 1.20} $$ $$ E = \frac{1500 \times 343 \times 10^6}{4 \times 50 \times 125000 \times 1.20} $$ $$ E = \frac{514.5 \times 10^9}{30 \times 10^6} $$ $$ E = 17150 \,\text{N/mm}^2 = 17.15 \,\text{GPa} $$ > [!TIP] > This value lies in the typical range for hardwoods (10–20 GPa), confirming the specimen is likely a structural hardwood. ### Step 3 — Modulus of Rupture (MOR) $$ \text{MOR} = \frac{3 \, P_{max} \, L}{2 \, b \, d^2} $$ $$ \text{MOR} = \frac{3 \times 6500 \times 700}{2 \times 50 \times (50)^2} $$ $$ \text{MOR} = \frac{13.65 \times 10^6}{2 \times 50 \times 2500} $$ $$ \text{MOR} = \frac{13.65 \times 10^6}{250000} $$ $$ \text{MOR} = 54.6 \,\text{N/mm}^2 = 54.6 \,\text{MPa} $$ > [!NOTE] > MOR values for structural timber typically range from **30 MPa** (softwood) to **120 MPa** (high-grade hardwood). 54.6 MPa indicates a moderate-grade structural timber. ### Step 4 — Allowable Working Stress (Design Step) $$ \sigma_{allow} = \frac{\text{MOR}}{\text{FoS}} = \frac{54.6}{3.0} = 18.2 \,\text{MPa} $$ (Factor of Safety = 3.0 as per IS 883 for timber.) ## 3.5 Summary Table — Numerical Output | Quantity | Symbol | Value | Units | |---|---|---|---| | Section Modulus | $Z$ | 20833.33 | mm³ | | Moment of Inertia | $I$ | 520833.33 | mm⁴ | | Modulus of Elasticity | $E$ | 17150 | MPa | | Modulus of Rupture | $\text{MOR}$ | 54.6 | MPa | | Allowable Stress | $\sigma_{allow}$ | 18.2 | MPa | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ##
