KTU examiners expect students to **clearly label both regions** in their lab record. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question A1** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Q: Define Modulus of Elasticity (MOE) and Modulus of Rupture (MOR) in the context of the bending test on wood. How are they experimentally determined?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Modulus of Elasticity (MOE):** It is a measure of the **stiffness** of wood in bending, representing the ratio of flexural stress to flexural strain within the elastic limit. It is determined from the slope of the **linear (proportional) region** of the load–deflection curve using the formula: $$ E = \frac{P L^3}{4 \, b \, d^3 \, \delta} $$ where $P$ and $\delta$ are read **within the proportional limit**. **Modulus of Rupture (MOR):** It is a measure of the **ultimate flexural strength** of wood, computed using the **maximum (failure) load** $P_{max}$ in the flexural stress formula: $$ \text{MOR} = \frac{3 \, P_{max} \, L}{2 \, b \, d^2} $$ MOR is a **nominal engineering stress**, not a true stress at failure. (3 marks: 1.5 each) --- ### **Question A2** [KTU University Exam – June 2024] **Q: Why is a span-to-depth ratio of 14:1 recommended for the bending test on wooden beams?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The span-to-depth ratio of **14:1** (i.e., $L = 14d$) is recommended because: 1. **Below 14:1:** Shear stresses become dominant over flexural stresses, causing premature **horizontal shear failure** at the neutral axis rather than true flexural failure. (1 mark) 2. **Above 14:1:** The slender beam may undergo **lateral-torsional buckling** or excessive deflection before the material reaches its true flexural capacity. (1 mark) 3. **At 14:1:** A balance is achieved where **flexural stresses govern** failure, and the beam fails by **tension rupture** at the bottom fibre — the true flexural mode. (1 mark) This ratio is codified in **IS 1708** and **ASTM D143** for static bending tests on timber. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **Q: A wooden beam of dimensions 60 mm × 60 mm × 800 mm is tested in three-point bending over an effective span of 720 mm. The following data is recorded:** - **Load at proportional limit:** $P = 2000 \,\text{N}$, **deflection:** $\delta = 1.50 \,\text{mm}$ - **Maximum (failure) load:** $P_{max} = 8500 \,\text{N}$ **(a)** Calculate the **Modulus of Elasticity (MOE)** and the **Modulus of Rupture (MOR)** of the wood. (7 marks) **(b)** If the same beam is used as a simply supported structural member with a uniformly distributed load over its full span, determine the **maximum permissible UDL** such that the bending stress does not exceed **40% of MOR**. Take Factor of Safety = 2.5 on MOR. (7 marks) --- #### **Solution to Part A(a) — MOE and MOR (7 marks)** **Given:** - $b = 60 \,\text{mm}$, $d = 60 \,\text{mm}$, $L = 720 \,\text{mm}$ - $P_{prop} = 2000 \,\text{N}$, $\delta_{prop} = 1.50 \,\text{mm}$ - $P_{max} = 8500 \,\text{N}$ **Step 1 — Section Properties** $$ I = \frac{b d^3}{12} = \frac{60 \times (60)^3}{12} = \frac{60 \times 216000}{12} = 1080000 \,\text{mm}^4 $$ $$ Z = \frac{b d^2}{6} = \frac{60 \times (60)^2}{6} = \frac{60 \times 3600}{6} = 36000 \,\text{mm}^3 $$ **Step 2 — Modulus of Elasticity (MOE)** $$ E = \frac{P \, L^3}{4 \, b \, d^3 \, \delta} $$ $$ E = \frac{2000 \times (720)^3}{4 \times 60 \times (60)^3 \times 1.50} $$ $$ E = \frac{2000 \times 373.248 \times 10^6}{4 \times 60 \times 216000 \times 1.50} $$ $$ E = \frac{746.496 \times 10^9}{77.76 \times 10^6} $$ $$ E = 9600 \,\text{N/mm}^2 = 9.6 \,\text{GPa} $$ **Step 3 — Modulus of Rupture (MOR)** $$ \text{MOR} = \frac{3 \, P_{max} \, L}{2 \, b \, d^2} $$ $$ \text{MOR} = \frac{3 \times 8500 \times 720}{2 \times 60 \times (60)^2} $$ $$ \text{MOR} = \frac{18.36 \times 10^6}{2 \times 60 \times 3600} $$ $$ \text{MOR} = \frac{18.36 \times 10^6}{432000} $$ $$ \text{MOR} = 42.5 \,\text{N/mm}^2 = 42.5 \,\text{MPa} $$ **Final Answers (Part a):** | Quantity | Value | |---|---| | MOE | 9.6 GPa | | MOR | 42.5 MPa | --- #### **Solution to Part A(b) — Maximum Permissible UDL (7 marks)** **Step 1 — Allowable Bending Stress** $$ \sigma_{allow} = \frac{\text{MOR}}{\text{FoS}} = \frac{42.5}{2.5} = 17.0 \,\text{MPa} $$ **Step 2 — Effective Design Stress (40% cap)** $$ \sigma_{design} = 0.40 \times \sigma_{allow} = 0.40 \times 17.0 = 6.8 \,\text{MPa} $$ **Step 3 — Maximum Permissible Moment** $$ M_{max} = \sigma_{design} \times Z = 6.8 \times 36000 = 244800 \,\text{N·mm} = 244.8 \,\text{N·m} $$ **Step 4 — UDL from Bending Moment** For a simply supported beam with UDL $w$ (N/mm) over span $L$: $$ M_{max} = \frac{w L^2}{8} $$ Solving for $w$: $$ w = \frac{8 \, M_{max}}{L^2} = \frac{8 \times 244800}{(720)^2} = \frac{1958400}{518400} $$ $$ w = 3.778 \,\text{N/mm} = 3.778 \,\text{kN/m} $$ **Final Answer (Part b):** Maximum permissible UDL = **3.778 kN/m** (≈ 3.78 kN/m). --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **Q: In a four-point bending test on a wooden beam, the following observations were made:** - Beam: $b = 75 \,\text{mm}$, $d = 75 \,\text{mm}$, outer span $L = 1050 \,\text{mm}$, inner span $a = 350 \,\text{mm}$ - Load at proportional limit: $P = 3000 \,\text{N}$, deflection at mid-span: $\delta = 2.10 \,\text{mm}$ - Failure load: $P_{max} = 11000 \,\text{N}$ **(a)** Determine the **MOE** and **MOR** using 4-point bending formulae. Compare the result with a hypothetical 3PB test on the same beam (same $L$ and same $P_{max}$). (7 marks) **(b)** List and briefly explain **four common failure modes** observed in wooden beams under bending. (7 marks) --- #### **Solution to Part B(a) — MOE and MOR by 4PB (7 marks)** **Step 1 — Section Properties** $$ I = \frac{75 \times (75)^3}{12} = \frac{75 \times 421875}{12} = 2636718.75 \,\text{mm}^4 $$ $$ Z = \frac{75 \times (75)^2}{6} = \frac{75 \times 5625}{6} = 70312.5 \,\text{mm}^3 $$ **Step 2 — MOE in 4PB** $$ E = \frac{P \, a \, (3 L^2 - 4 a^2)}{4 \, b \, d^3 \, \delta} $$ Compute $3L^2 - 4a^2$: $$ 3 L^2 - 4 a^2 = 3 \times (1050)^2 - 4 \times (350)^2 = 3 \times 1102500 - 4 \times 122500 $$ $$ = 3307500 - 490000 = 2817500 \,\text{mm}^2 $$ Now substitute: $$ E = \frac{3000 \times 350 \times 2817500}{4 \times 75 \times (75)^3 \times 2.10} $$ $$ E = \frac{2.958375 \times 10^{12}}{4 \times 75 \times 421875 \times 2.10} $$ $$ E = \frac{2.958375 \times 10^{12}}{2.6578125 \times 10^8} $$ $$ E = 11131 \,\text{N/mm}^2 \approx 11.13 \,\text{GPa} $$ **Step 3 — MOR in 4PB** $$ \text{MOR} = \frac{3 \, P_{max} \, a}{b \, d^2} = \frac{3 \times 11000 \times 350}{75 \times (75)^2} $$ $$ \text{MOR} = \frac{11.55 \times 10^6}{75 \times 5625} = \frac{11.55 \times 10^6}{421875} $$ $$ \text{MOR} = 27.38 \,\text{N/mm}^2 = 27.38 \,\text{MPa} $$ **Step 4 — Comparison with Hypothetical 3PB (Same L, Same P_max)** $$ \text{MOR}_{3PB} = \frac{3 \times 11000 \times 1050}{2 \times 75 \times (75)^2} = \frac{34.65 \times 10^6}{843750} = 41.07 \,\text{MPa} $$ **Comparison:** | Test Type | MOR (MPa) | Comment | |---|---|---| | 3PB | 41.07 | Higher — 3PB uses entire L in formula | | 4PB | 27.38 | Lower — 4PB uses only inner span $a$ | **Insight:** 4PB gives a **more conservative MOR** because the constant moment region is shorter, and stress concentration effects at load points are absent. 3PB shows higher MOR due to the longer effective lever arm in the formula. --- #### **Solution to Part B(b) — Four Failure Modes (7 marks = 1.75 each)** 1. **Tension Failure (Bottom Fibre Rupture):** The bottom fibre experiences maximum **tensile stress**. In wood, tensile strength perpendicular to grain is the **weakest link**. A **sudden brittle crack** initiates at the bottom and propagates upward. Most common in **clear, straight-grained hardwoods**. 2. **Compression Failure (Top Fibre Crushing):** The top fibre experiences maximum **compressive stress**. Visible as **wrinkling or creasing** of fibres. Common in **softwoods** and **dense hardwoods** under sustained loading. Often precedes tension failure. 3. **Horizontal Shear Failure:** Shear stress is **maximum at the neutral axis**. If the span-to-depth ratio is too small ($L/d < 14$), the beam may split **horizontally along the grain** before flexural failure occurs. 4. **Cross-Grain Tension / Splintering:** If the wood grain is not parallel to the beam axis, **tensile stresses perpendicular to grain** arise. These cause **splintering, splitting**, or **delamination**. This is a defect-driven failure mode and gives falsely low strength values. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Unit mismatch (N vs. kN):** Always convert load to **N** before substituting into N-based formulae. (–1 mark) > 2. **Span vs. Total Length confusion:** Use **effective span L** (between supports), not the total specimen length, in MOR/MOE formulae. (–2 marks) > 3. **Using $P_{max}$ to compute MOE:** MOE must use load at the **proportional limit**, not the failure load. (–1 mark) > 4. **Forgetting $a$ vs. $L$ in 4PB:** Students often use $L$ instead of $a$ (inner span) in 4PB MOR/MOE formulae. This is the **single most common error** in KTU lab exams. (–3 marks) --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [x] The bending test gives **two key properties**: **MOE (stiffness)** and **MOR (strength)**. - [x] **MOE** is computed using a load within the **proportional limit**; **MOR** uses the **maximum (failure) load**. - [x] The flexural stress formula in **3PB**: $\sigma = \dfrac{3 P L}{2 b d^2}$. - [x] The MOE formula in **3PB**: $E = \dfrac{P L^3}{4 b d^3 \delta}$. - [x] The flexural stress formula in **4PB**: $\sigma = \dfrac{3 P a}{b d^2}$. - [x] The MOE formula in **4PB**: $E = \dfrac{P a (3 L^2 - 4 a^2)}{4 b d^3 \delta}$. - [x] **Span-to-depth ratio** should be **14:1** to ensure flexural failure dominates. - [x] Section properties: $I = \dfrac{b d^3}{12}$ and $Z = \dfrac{b d^2}{6}$. - [x] MOR is a **nominal** stress, not the true stress at failure (because wood is anisotropic). - [x] **Hardwoods fail brittlely** (tension rupture); **softwoods fail more ductilely** (compression creasing then rupture). - [x] Always use **effective span L**, not total specimen length. - [x] Common failure modes: **tension rupture, compression crushing, horizontal shear, cross-grain tension**. - [x] Applicable standards: **IS 1708 (Part 5)**, **IS 874**, **ASTM D143**. - [x] Factor of Safety for timber design (as per IS 883): typically **2.5 to 3.0** on MOR. - [x] Always record and report the **grain orientation** and **failure mode** along with MOE/MOR values.
